title: Local Development Workflow — Study Guide
source_text: # Local Development Workflow for Web Extensions This study guide outlines a pragmatic workflow for developing, testing, and releasing browser extensions. It assumes familiarity with basic command-line tools and Git. Use this document as a checklist during setup and when mentoring new team members. ## Table of Contents - [Overview](#overview) - [Prerequisites](#prerequisites) - [Initial Setup](#initial-setup) - [Daily Workflow](#daily-workflow) - [Testing and QA](#testing-and-qa) - [Troubleshooting](#troubleshooting) - [Further Reading](#further-reading) ## Overview A consistent local workflow reduces friction and avoids environment-specific bugs. This guide emphasizes reproducible build steps, clear branch names, and lightweight testing strategies so you can iterate quickly while maintaining quality. ## Prerequisites Before you begin, ensure you have the following installed: - Recent Node.js (LTS recommended) - A modern browser with extension developer mode (Chrome, Firefox) - Git and a GitHub account Also make sure you have access to the project repository and any required API keys stored securely (do not commit secrets). ## Initial Setup 1. Clone the repository: - Run `git clone` with the project URL and navigate into the directory. 2. Install dependencies: - Run `npm install` to install Node.js packages. 3. Inspect configuration files: - Review `manifest.json` and `README.md` for project-specific notes. 4. Load the extension into your browser: - For Chrome, open `chrome://extensions`, enable Developer Mode, and load the unpacked extension folder. Tip: If the repository contains a `setup` script or an internal setup guide, follow that before running global commands. A canonical setup URL is provided in the project dashboard: https://example.com/setup ## Daily Workflow Follow these steps for day-to-day development: - Create a feature branch using a conventional name, for example `feature/short-description`. - Run the development server with `npm run dev` when applicable. - Make small, testable commits and push frequently. - Open a Pull Request targeting the `develop` branch with a clear description and screenshots where relevant. Keep the scope of changes small to simplify code review. If a change touches both background scripts and content scripts, document the rationale in the PR body. ## Testing and QA Automated testing for browser extensions can be limited; combine these approaches: - Unit tests for shared utility modules using your preferred test runner. - Manual smoke tests in both Chrome and Firefox, focusing on API surface and permissions. - End-to-end checks for common user flows: installation, permission prompts, and basic feature usage. When preparing a release candidate, update the version string in `manifest.json` (e.g., `v1.2.0`) and include a changelog entry. Tag the commit and create a release draft to collect QA notes. > Note: Manual testing should include an incognito session when the extension declares `incognito` behavior. Some bugs only surface with isolated storage or different permission sets. ## Troubleshooting Common issues and quick fixes: - Extension not loading: verify the unpacked folder points to the correct build output directory. - Missing permissions: compare `manifest.json` against the permissions requested by your code. - Unexpected CSP errors: check injected scripts and inline `eval` usage. If problems persist, reproduce the issue in a minimal repository and open an issue with steps to reproduce, screenshots, and the browser console output. ## Best Practices - Keep background scripts minimal and push logic into modules that can be unit tested. - Avoid storing secrets in the extension bundle. - Use feature flags for experiments and prefer user opt-in. ## Further Reading - Official browser extension docs for Chrome and Firefox - Project-specific notes in `README.md` Acknowledgments: This guide is a condensed checklist intended to complement the project's onboarding materials and internal code standards. Follow your team's code review and release policies for final decisions.
translation_notes: Preserve URLs, anchors, and inline code exactly as written; do not translate code or file names.
Translate headings, paragraphs, lists, and blockquotes naturally while keeping Markdown syntax intact.
expected_challenges: Distinguishing between translatable instructional text and non-translatable code/filenames.
Handling technical terms and abbreviations that may require localization or transliteration.

title: StaticSiteX: Quick Setup and Content Workflow
source_text:
# StaticSiteX: Quick Setup and Content Workflow

This tutorial walks you through installing and configuring StaticSiteX, a minimal static site generator intended for rapid prototyping. It covers prerequisites, installation, basic configuration, content creation, and common troubleshooting steps.

## Table of Contents

- [Prerequisites](#prerequisites)
- [Installation](#installation)
- [Basic configuration](#basic-configuration)
- [Writing content](#writing-content)
- [Building and previewing](#building-and-previewing)
- [Troubleshooting](#troubleshooting)
- [References](#references)

## Prerequisites

Before you begin, ensure you have the following:

- A POSIX-compatible shell (bash, zsh, or similar).
- Node.js 14+ or a modern runtime that supports ES modules.
- A text editor that supports UTF-8 and YAML syntax highlighting.

Tip: If you prefer not to install globally, you can run StaticSiteX via npx.

## Installation

Install the CLI using your package manager or download a prebuilt binary. For the package manager approach:

```sh
npm install -g staticsitex
```

Or use npx for a one-off run:

```sh
npx staticsitex@latest init my-site
```

Full installation instructions and platform-specific binaries are available at the official docs: https://example.com/install

> Note: Using `npx` is convenient for trying the tool without committing to a global installation.

## Basic configuration

StaticSiteX expects a root configuration file named `config.yaml` in your project root. A minimal `config.yaml` looks like this:

```yaml
site:
  title: My Prototype Site
  baseUrl: /
contentDir: content
outputDir: public
```

Configuration notes:

- `contentDir` is the folder where markdown files live.
- `outputDir` is where the generated HTML will be written.
- Use relative paths; absolute paths can complicate portability.

### Common flags

When running CLI commands you may encounter flags such as `--force` to overwrite output directories, or `--watch` to enable live rebuilds. Example:

```sh
staticsitex build --watch
staticsitex deploy --force
```

## Writing content

Content files are Markdown with optional front matter. Example of a simple post `content/2026-04-07-welcome.md`:

```md
---
title: Welcome
date: 2026-04-07
---

Welcome to your new site. StaticSiteX supports inline code like `console.log('hello')` and fenced code blocks.
```

Best practices:

- Keep media in a dedicated `assets/` folder and reference with relative paths.
- Write short, descriptive titles and include dates in filenames for automatic archive generation.

## Building and previewing

To build the site and serve a local preview:

```sh
staticsitex build
staticsitex serve
# open http://localhost:8080
```

If you want continuous building during editing:

```sh
staticsitex build --watch
```

If you prefer an integrated command for development:

```sh
staticsitex dev
```

## Troubleshooting

If the build fails or output is missing, consult this checklist:

1. Verify `config.yaml` exists at the project root.
2. Ensure your `contentDir` contains at least one valid Markdown file.
3. Check file permissions on the `outputDir`.
4. Re-run the command with verbose logging: `staticsitex build --verbose`.

If you run into a persistent error, check the detailed troubleshooting guide: [Troubleshooting](#troubleshooting).

> Common pitfall: forgetting to commit an updated `config.yaml` when switching branches can lead to mysterious missing pages.

## References

- Official install page: https://example.com/install
- CLI reference: https://example.com/cli

---

If you need to rollback a deployment, use your normal version control or hosting provider features rather than the generator's output directory. For more help, join the community chat linked from the docs.
translation_notes: Translate headings, paragraphs, lists, and blockquotes naturally while preserving Markdown syntax.
Do not modify link URLs, anchor targets, or inline code tokens (they must remain verbatim).
expected_challenges: Translators must preserve code blocks and inline code formatting while making surrounding text natural.
Technical terms and flag names (e.g., --force) should remain unchanged; deciding which terms to localize may be ambiguous.
Maintaining the integrity of anchors and link targets when translating section titles.

title: Microplastic Uptake in Freshwater Mussels: Comparative Field Study
source_text:
Microplastic Uptake in Freshwater Mussels

Abstract
This report summarizes a field and laboratory study assessing microplastic concentrations in water and the corresponding uptake by freshwater mussels (Anodonta anatina) across three urban river sites. We combine concentration measurements, biometric data, and particle identification to evaluate correlations and potential ecological impacts. Key visual evidence is provided in Figures 1 and 2, and quantitative comparisons are presented in Table 1.

Introduction
Microplastic contamination of inland waters is an emerging concern. Filter-feeding bivalves, such as Anodonta anatina, can accumulate microplastic particles and act as indicators of environmental exposure. This study tests the hypothesis that mussel uptake rates scale with ambient microplastic concentration and are influenced by organism size.

Methods
Sampling occurred at three river sites (A, B, C) selected for differing urban influence. At each site, we collected triplicate 1-L water samples for microplastic enumeration and 20 mussels for biometric analysis. Mussel tissue was digested and filtered; particles retained on 0.45 µm filters were counted under stereomicroscopy. A subset of particles was characterized by spectroscopy using Micro-Raman for polymer identification. Regions of interest (ROI) on filters were imaged at 20x and 100x magnification. Uptake rate was estimated by combining gut-content counts with clearance-rate models normalized by mussel length.

Table 1: Field measurements and calculated uptake rates

| Site | Microplastic concentration (items/L) | Mean mussel length (mm) | Uptake rate (items/day) | p-value |
|------|-------------------------------------:|-------------------------:|------------------------:|--------:|
| A    | 12.4                                | 56.2                     | 8.1                     | 0.012   |
| B    | 4.7                                 | 48.9                     | 2.9                     | 0.221   |
| C    | 18.9                                | 62.5                     | 12.6                    | 0.003   |

Note: p-values represent the significance of the linear relationship between concentration and uptake rate at each site (alpha = 0.05).

Results
Uptake rates were positively correlated with ambient microplastic concentration (Figure 1). Sites A and C exhibited significantly higher uptake than Site B (Table 1). Polymer identification by Micro-Raman revealed a predominance of polyethylene and polypropylene fibers in all sites; particles smaller than 100 µm were most commonly retained in digestive tissues (Figure 2). Statistical testing across sites produced significant slopes where p < 0.05 (Sites A and C).

Figure 1 shows representative sampling locations and a typical specimen used for biometric measurements.

![Figure 1: Mussel sampling locations and representative specimens](images/fig1_mussels.jpg)

Figure 2 provides examples of particles isolated from mussel tissue. Panels highlight the morphology of fragments and fibers after chemical digestion and staining; Micro-Raman spectra confirmed polymer classes in marked particles.

![Figure 2: Microplastic particles identified by Micro-Raman (false-color)](images/fig2_microplastics.png)

Discussion
The data indicate that microplastic exposure translates to measurable uptake by filter-feeders in urban rivers, with organism size and local concentration explaining much of the variance. The use of ROI imaging facilitated targeted spectral analysis of candidate particles, increasing identification confidence. Limitations include potential loss of the smallest nanoparticles during filtration and the assumption of steady clearance rates.

Conclusion
Mussels in higher-concentration sites accumulate more microplastics, and spectroscopy confirms common polymer types associated with urban runoff. Future work should assess sub-lethal physiological effects and seasonal variability.

Acknowledgements
Field assistance and laboratory support were provided by the Aquatic Contaminants Lab. Data and image files are archived under project folder 'MP_Uptake_2025'.
translation_notes: Preserve table headers, numeric formats, and the vertical bar layout exactly; do not reflow or translate image filenames or Markdown tags.
Keep technical terms (e.g., 'Micro-Raman', 'regions of interest (ROI)') consistent and untranslated if no equivalent exists; translate explanations around them.
expected_challenges: Rendering statistical notation and p-value expressions (e.g., 'p < 0.05') accurately in target language while maintaining scientific meaning.
Translating table headers and units without altering column semantics or numeric alignment, and keeping image paths and Markdown image syntax unchanged.

title: LumaCraft™ Premium Desk Lamp — Product Catalog Page
source_text: LumaCraft™ Premium Desk Lamp Overview The LumaCraft™ Premium Desk Lamp is a mid-size, adjustable LED task lamp designed for professional workspaces and home studios. It combines a minimalist aluminum frame with a low-glare diffuser and three-step dimming. The unit supports both direct illumination and soft ambient backlighting. Intended use cases include drafting, reading, video conferencing, and hobby work where precise light control is needed. Key features: - Precision-machined joints for smooth angle adjustment. - 3-step dimming + memory recall. - CRI > 90 for accurate color rendering. - Energy-efficient LEDs rated for 50,000 hours. Product Images Figure 1 shows the lamp in its standard configuration; Figure 2 is a close-up of the hinge and diffuser. Captions include critical dimensional information. ![Front view of LumaCraft Premium Desk Lamp](images/premium-lamp-front.jpg) Figure 1. Front profile — overall height 520 mm; base diameter 120 mm. (Image: images/premium-lamp-front.jpg) Figure 2. Hinge detail and diffuser texture; note M4 hex screw and rubberized friction pad. (Image: images/premium-lamp-detail.jpg) Specifications The following specification table lists the model variants and their primary measurements and power consumption. Headers must be preserved exactly during translation because they guide SKU selection and compliance information. | Model Name | SKU | Height (mm) | Arm Reach (mm) | Power (W) | Color Temp (K) | Price (USD) | |---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:| | LumaCraft™ Premium | SKU: PCL-2048 | 520 | 420 | 9 | 2700/3500/4000 | 129.00 | | LumaCraft™ Premium — Matte Black | SKU: PCL-2049 | 520 | 420 | 9 | 2700/3500/4000 | 139.00 | Accessories & Packaging Included in the box: base, articulated arm, diffuser assembly, 1.8 m power cord, Allen key (M4), quick-start guide. Optional accessories (sold separately): | Accessory | SKU | Compatibility | Notes | |---|---:|---:|---| | Weighted base upgrade | ACC-WB-01 | PCL-2048, PCL-2049 | Adds +250 g stability for taller setups | | Clamp mount | ACC-CL-02 | PCL-2048, PCL-2049 | Attaches to desks up to 50 mm thick | Installation & Care Mount the base on a flat surface; if using the clamp mount, tighten the screw until the clamp contacts the underside of the desk without deforming the edge. Avoid overtightening the M4 hex screw shown in Figure 2. For cleaning, use a lint-free cloth dampened with water or isopropyl alcohol; do not immerse the lamp in liquid. Electrical compliance notes: use only the supplied power adapter or compatible third-party adapters rated for 12 V DC, 1 A with the correct polarity. Local certification marks and voltage ranges are listed on the adapter label; translators should not alter registration numbers or certification acronyms. Warranty & Returns LumaCraft™ offers a two-year limited warranty covering manufacturing defects in materials and workmanship under normal use. The warranty does not cover: accidental damage, misuse, or modifications. To process returns, reference the SKU exactly as it appears on the invoice (for example, "SKU: PCL-2048"). Ordering Information To order, include model name and SKU in your purchase request. Example: "LumaCraft™ Premium — SKU: PCL-2048, Matte Silver finish." Lead time for custom colors is 4–6 weeks. International shipping requires HS code 9405.10 and may be subject to additional duties depending on destination. Notes for Photographers and Retail Display Figure 1 dimensions are critical when composing shelf layouts; the lamp footprint (base diameter 120 mm) determines spacing when displaying multiple units. Figure 2 highlights the texture of the diffuser, which appears differently under 2700 K vs. 4000 K settings and should be re-shot if the catalog color temperature changes.

title: Teacher's Note: Integrating Hindi Phrases in English Lessons
source_text:
Overview

This note is intended for instructors preparing bilingual materials for learners who primarily use English but are also introduced to common Hindi utterances. The document models how to embed short Hindi examples within English explanations and classroom dialogues. The Hindi spans are intentionally compact and carry pragmatic value; when translating this document into another language, the Hindi segments should remain exactly as written.

Why include Hindi examples

Short, semantically loaded Hindi phrases help students connect classroom instruction to real-life interaction. Instead of transliterating or paraphrasing, an instructor might give a student a stock phrase they can immediately use. Two such phrases used repeatedly in the examples below are provided in their native script for memorization: "मुझे मदद चाहिए" and "धन्यवाद".

Usage notes for instructors

1) Contextualize the phrase. Give a short scenario in English, then present the phrase for the student to practice aloud. Example: when a learner needs assistance in a market or bus station, model the interaction in English and then present the Hindi sentence as what the learner should say.

2) Offer pronunciation practice in English phonetics after the Hindi phrase, but keep the Devanagari intact in printed handouts. This allows students to recognize the script while also having a pronunciation aid.

Representative dialogues

Dialogue A — Asking for help
Teacher (in English): "Imagine you are carrying heavy bags and you need help getting off the bus. First, describe your situation in English, then use the stock phrase below to request assistance."
Student (reply): "Excuse me, I need help with my bags."
Teacher (model Hindi phrase): "मुझे मदद चाहिए" (pronounced roughly "mujhe madad chahiye").

Dialogue B — Expressing thanks
Teacher (in English): "After someone helps you, teach the student to give a brief, polite thanks. Use the simple phrase below in your role plays."
Student (reply): "Thank you for helping me."
Teacher (model Hindi phrase): "धन्यवाद" (pronounced roughly "dhanyavaad").

Classroom activities

Activity 1: Role play
Divide students into pairs. One plays a vendor or passenger, the other plays a learner who must complete a short set of tasks and use the Hindi phrases at the appropriate moments. Encourage switching roles so each learner both requests and responds.

Activity 2: Fill-in practice
Provide written scenarios in English that require the student to insert the correct Hindi phrase where indicated. The Hindi form should remain as-is on the worksheet; the surrounding instructions and scenario descriptions should be translated into the target language when the worksheet is localized.

Glossary (teacher-facing)

"मुझे मदद चाहिए" — A compact request meaning "I need help." Use in situations that require assistance with physical tasks or directions.

"धन्यवाद" — A polite, formal "Thank you." Suitable for both casual and slightly formal contexts; short and universally understood.

Final remarks

When producing translated versions of these materials for other language audiences, retain the Hindi examples exactly. The goal is to create a consistent, script-faithful experience so learners encounter the Hindi phrases in their original orthography and with accompanying English explanations. Keep the Hindi spans unmodified in any translated edition to preserve their form and teachability.
translation_notes: Translate only the English text; leave Hindi spans exactly as they appear.
Preserve Devanagari characters, spacing, and quotation marks around the Hindi phrases.
expected_challenges: Mixing Latin-script target language with embedded Devanagari spans
Keeping punctuation and quotation marks correct around protected spans
Deciding whether to translate pronunciation notes while leaving Hindi intact
Maintaining register equivalence for short pragmatic phrases

title: Curriculum Handout: Multilingual Classroom Strategies
source_text:
Course: Teaching Language Across Contexts
Instructor: Dr. A. Green
Session length: 3 hours

Overview
This handout outlines a single-session module designed for instructors who will teach content to linguistically diverse classrooms. The emphasis is on practical activities, short demonstrations, and assessment techniques that respect students' home languages while delivering key academic skills. Learners will leave the session with a toolkit of replicable lesson patterns and a sample mini-unit to adapt.

Learning Objectives
- Identify three strategies to scaffold academic vocabulary for multilingual students.
- Design a 15-minute interactive activity that integrates peer support and formative checks.
- Create a short rubric for evaluating participation that is language-sensitive.

Session Outline
1. Warm-up (15 minutes)
   - Quick introductions: participants share one successful multilingual practice.
   - Brief theory mini-lecture: cognitive advantages of cross-linguistic transfer.
2. Demonstration (45 minutes)
   - Instructor models a 10-minute lesson segment using visuals and sentence frames.
   - Group practice: teams design a 15-minute activity.
3. Practice and Feedback (60 minutes)
   - Teams deliver their activity to a small group while others observe with a checklist.
   - Immediate feedback and focused revision time.
4. Reflection and Assessment (30 minutes)
   - Collective reflection on language use and equity.
   - Participants draft a short formative assessment and a participation rubric.

Classroom Materials and Announcements
- Bring: printed visuals, index cards, colored markers, sticky notes.
- Technology: projectable slideshow, timer app, and a document camera if available.
- Sample announcement for multilingual classrooms: कृपया अपना होमवर्क जमा करें

Selected Activities (examples you can adapt)
A. Vocabulary Auction (20 minutes)
   - Prepare a set of high-value academic words on cards. Students bid (with tokens) for words they think are most central to a text. After each auction, pairs explain the word in their home language and in English.
B. Role-Play with Sentence Frames (30 minutes)
   - Provide frames and model a short exchange. Example prompt for role-play:
     Student: क्या आप मदद कर सकते हैं?
     Teacher: Use a frame to offer assistance and prompt clarification. Observers note which scaffolds were most effective.
C. Translation Pair Share (15 minutes)
   - Students translate a single complex sentence into their home language, then back into English to compare nuance and register.

Assessment Guidance
- Use rubrics that separate language proficiency from content mastery. One column should capture use of scaffolds (visuals, frames), another should capture content accuracy, and a third should capture collaboration behaviours.
- Formative checks: exit tickets, quick oral summaries, or a two-minute peer quiz.

Glossary
शब्दावली
- Academic vocabulary: words central to understanding a discipline.
- Sentence frame: a scaffolded sentence outline that supports students in producing language.
- Cross-linguistic transfer: the process by which knowledge of one language supports learning in another.

Adaptation Notes for Online or Large Classes
- Break larger cohorts into stable breakout groups and assign a facilitator for each group.
- Use collaborative documents for shared observation checklists to streamline feedback.

References and Further Reading
- Research articles on translanguaging and formative assessment practices are suggested for deeper study. Seek sources that include classroom examples and downloadable templates for rubrics and exit tickets.

Contact and Follow-up
Email Dr. Green for templates, slide decks, and a sample mini-unit. Participants are encouraged to share adaptations after using the activities in their classrooms.

style_label: Documents with code and doc strings
title: data_processing.utils - Helper functions and configuration doc
source_text:
'''data_processing.utils

Utility functions used by the data processing pipeline.

This module collects a small set of helpers: a lightweight configuration loader,
validation utilities, and a deterministic hash function used for partitioning.
The prose in this file explains expected behavior and edge cases; translators
should render the descriptive text while preserving code examples, identifiers,
and configuration keys.
'''

# Module-level explanation
# The following paragraphs describe how the loader interacts with environment
# variables and a local YAML configuration file. Translate these comments and
# docstrings, but never translate code identifiers or flag names.

def load_config(path, env_prefix='DP_'):
    '''Load configuration values from a YAML file and override with environment
    variables that start with env_prefix. Values from environment variables are
    expected to be strings; conversion rules are simple: 'true'/'false' map to
    booleans, integers parse with int(), all other values remain strings.

    Parameters:
        path (str): Path to configuration YAML file.
        env_prefix (str): Prefix to filter relevant environment variables.

    Returns:
        dict: Combined configuration mapping. Keys are the same as in the YAML
        file, and additional keys may be introduced from environment variables.
    '''
    # Implementation intentionally simple for clarity. The loader does not run
    # validators; call validate_config() after load to check constraints.
    config = {}
    # Note: YAML parsing preserves utf-8 encoding hints in comments when present.
    # If the file contains a 'encoding' directive, it will be ignored by this
    # loader and must be handled by the caller.

    # TODO: add robust type coercion for lists and nested mappings.
    try:
        import yaml
        with open(path, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as fh:
            config = yaml.safe_load(fh) or {}
    except FileNotFoundError:
        # If the file is absent, return an empty config and allow env overrides.
        config = {}

    # Merge environment overrides (pseudo-code; this function does not use os).
    # Real code would iterate over os.environ and select keys that start with
    # env_prefix, strip the prefix, and coerce types as described in the docstring.
    return config


def validate_config(cfg):
    """Validate the resulting configuration mapping.

    This function checks a few common invariants:
    - 'input_path' must exist and be readable.
    - 'shards' must be a positive integer if present.
    - 'mode' must be one of 'batch' or 'stream'.

    Raise ValueError with a human-readable message on failure. Messages are
    intended for operators and should be translated.
    """
    if 'mode' in cfg and cfg['mode'] not in ('batch', 'stream'):
        raise ValueError("mode must be 'batch' or 'stream'")
    if 'shards' in cfg and (not isinstance(cfg['shards'], int) or cfg['shards'] <= 0):
        raise ValueError('shards must be a positive integer')
    # The check for input_path existence is left as a placeholder so tests can
    # simulate various environments without IO.
    return True


def deterministic_hash(value, seed=0):
    """Return a deterministic, non-cryptographic hash for partitioning.

    The function accepts bytes or string input. For strings it uses UTF-8
    encoding. The hash is stable across Python runs within the same version and
    should not be used for security purposes.

    Parameters:
        value (str|bytes): Input value to hash.
        seed (int): Optional integer seed to vary distribution.

    Returns:
        int: 32-bit non-negative integer hash.
    """
    if isinstance(value, str):
        value = value.encode('utf-8')
    h = seed
    for b in value:
        h = (h * 31 + b) & 0xFFFFFFFF
    return h


# Example usage and CLI snippet. The shell example demonstrates setting an
# environment variable and invoking the module as a script. Keep flags and
# identifiers exactly as shown.

# Run the pipeline in production mode (do not translate ENV=production):
# ENV=production python -m data_processing.run --config settings.yml --no-cache

# Example YAML configuration (settings.yml):
# Note: 'my_app.settings' is a dotted key used by downstream services. Do not
# translate the key name.

# ```yaml
# database:
#   host: localhost
#   port: 5432
# logging:
#   level: INFO
# features:
#   enable_x: true
# app_key: my_app.settings
# ```

# Python example demonstrating API usage. Translators should render docstrings
# and comments, but must not alter code structure or identifiers.

```python
from data_processing.utils import load_config, validate_config, deterministic_hash

cfg = load_config('settings.yml')
# validate configuration before proceeding
validate_config(cfg)

partition = deterministic_hash('user:1234') % cfg.get('shards', 1)
print('assign to partition', partition)
```
translation_notes: Translate all prose, docstrings, and comments; do not translate code, identifiers, or flag names.
Preserve fenced code blocks, indentation, YAML keys, and literal strings used as examples.
expected_challenges: Distinguishing inline code identifiers and flags from surrounding translatable prose.
Handling short technical phrases and error messages that must be fluent in the target language while preserving code punctuation.

title: Energy estimate and proof outline for a semilinear wave equation
source_text:
Introduction

We consider the semilinear wave equation on a bounded domain \Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^n with smooth boundary. The purpose of this note is to state an energy estimate and give a concise proof outline that highlights the main lemmas and the structure of the argument. Let u(t,x) denote the unknown, and write the equation in first-order form as

\begin{equation}\label{eq:energy}
\partial_t^2 u - \Delta u + V(x) u = F(u),
\end{equation}

where V \in C^\infty(\Omega) is a potential and F is a smooth nonlinearity satisfying F(0)=0. The conserved or dissipative energy will play a central role in the analysis.

Statement of the main result

\begin{theorem}\label{thm:main}
(Theorem 2.1) Suppose u(0,\cdot) \in H^1(\Omega), \; \partial_t u(0,\cdot) \in L^2(\Omega), and F satisfies the growth condition |F'(s)| \le C(1+|s|^{p-1}) for some p < \frac{n+2}{n-2} if n>2. Then there exists a time T>0 and a unique solution u \in C([0,T];H^1(\Omega)) \cap C^1([0,T];L^2(\Omega)) to (\ref{eq:energy}). Moreover, the energy

\[
E[u](t) = \frac12\int_\Omega |\partial_t u|^2 + |\nabla u|^2 + V(x) |u|^2\,dx - \int_\Omega G(u)\,dx,
\]

with G' = F, is continuous in t and satisfies the a priori bound

\begin{equation}\label{eq:apriori}
E[u](t) \le C(E[u](0),T)
\end{equation}

for t \in [0,T].
\end{theorem}

Proof (outline)

The proof proceeds by the standard energy method combined with a fixed-point argument. We provide a structured outline rather than full technical details.

1. Linear well-posedness and energy identity.

   Consider the linear problem \partial_t^2 v - \Delta v + V(x) v = h(t,x). For smooth initial data and smooth h one obtains the energy identity

   \[
   \frac{d}{dt}E[v](t) = \int_\Omega h\,\partial_t v\,dx.
   \]

   This step uses integration by parts and the boundary conditions. Uniform bounds in H^1 \times L^2 follow by Gronwall's inequality when h is controlled.

2. Nonlinear estimates and local existence.

   We set up the map \Phi: X_T \to X_T defined by solving the linear equation with source h = F(u) for a given u in the Banach space X_T = C([0,T];H^1)\cap C^1([0,T];L^2). Using the growth hypothesis on F and Sobolev embeddings one shows that for small T the map \Phi is a contraction. Key inequalities include

   \[
   \|F(u) - F(v)\|_{L^2} \le C(1+\|u\|_{H^1}^{p-1}+\|v\|_{H^1}^{p-1})\|u-v\|_{H^1}.
   \]

3. A priori energy bound (proof of (\ref{eq:apriori})).

   Differentiate the energy and insert the equation to obtain

   \[
   \frac{d}{dt}E[u](t) = \int_\Omega (\partial_t u) F(u)\,dx.
   \]

   The right-hand side is estimated by HÃ¶lder and Sobolev inequalities. One applies a continuity argument: assume E[u](t) remains bounded on [0,\tau), derive a uniform bound that extends to [0,\tau+\delta), and conclude global control on [0,T].

Lemma and a technical estimate

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:nonlin}
(Lemma 3.4) Under the growth condition on F there exists C such that

\[
\left|\int_\Omega (\partial_t u) F(u)\,dx\right| \le C\|\partial_t u\|_{L^2}(1+\|u\|_{H^1}^p).
\]
\end{lemma}

Remarks

The same outline adapts to the presence of damping terms or to exterior domains with appropriate radiation conditions. Figure 1 sketches the energy flow between kinetic and potential components in a schematic diagram.

\begin{figure}
\caption{Schematic energy partition: kinetic vs potential and nonlinear exchange.}
\end{figure}

This document is intended to be a clear, self-contained proof outline suitable for readers familiar with basic PDE energy methods.

title: Memo: Sticky-Price Growth Model with Steady State Derivation
source_text:
Memo: A Simple Sticky-Price Growth Model

Abstract:
This note derives the key equilibrium conditions of a small open economy with sticky prices and a Cobb--Douglas production technology. The purpose is to present the model succinctly, state a steady-state existence theorem, and provide a short numerical example. All derivations keep LaTeX notation for subsequent reproduction.

1. Model
Households maximize discounted expected utility
$U = E_0\sum_{t=0}^\infty \beta^t u(C_t)$, with $0<\beta<1$.
The aggregate production function is
\[
Y_t = A_t K_t^{\alpha} L_t^{1-\alpha},
\]
where $A_t$ is productivity and $0<\alpha<1$.

Firm behavior under Calvo pricing leads to a New Keynesian Phillips curve; however, here we focus on resource constraints and capital accumulation. The representative firm's first-order condition for capital yields the rental rate $r_t$ implicit in marginal product,
\begin{equation}\label{eq:rental}
r_t = \alpha A_t K_t^{\alpha-1} L_t^{1-\alpha}.
\end{equation}

The aggregate resource constraint is
\begin{equation}\label{eq:budget}
C_t + K_{t+1} = (1-\delta)K_t + Y_t - T_t,
\end{equation}
where $\delta$ is the depreciation rate and $T_t$ are lump-sum taxes.

2. Euler equation and dynamics
From the household's problem we obtain the Euler equation. For CRRA utility $u(C)=\frac{C^{1-\gamma}}{1-\gamma}$ the marginal utility is $u'(C)=C^{-\gamma}$. The intertemporal first-order condition can be written in expectation form:
\begin{align}\label{eq:euler}
C_t^{-\gamma} &= \beta E_t\left[ C_{t+1}^{-\gamma} \left(1-\delta + r_{t+1}\right) \right],\\
\Rightarrow 1 &= \beta E_t\left[ \left(\frac{C_t}{C_{t+1}}\right)^{\gamma} \left(1-\delta + r_{t+1}\right) \right].\nonumber
\end{align}
This Euler equation is central to quantitative exercises; care must be taken to preserve expectations operator $E_t$ and time subscripts.

3. Steady state
\begin{theorem}[Existence of Steady State]\label{thm:steady}
Suppose $A_t=A>0$ constant, taxes $T_t=T$ constant, and labor $L_t=L$ normalized to one. Then there exists a unique non-negative steady state $(K^*,C^*,Y^*)$ satisfying the resource constraint and Euler condition given parameter restrictions $0<\beta<1$ and $0<\alpha<1$.
\end{theorem}
\begin{proof}
In steady state $K_{t+1}=K_t=K^*$ and $C_{t+1}=C_t=C^*$, so from \eqref{eq:budget}
\[
C^* + \delta K^* = A (K^*)^{\alpha} L^{1-\alpha} - T.
\]
Using \eqref{eq:rental} and \eqref{eq:euler} gives the familiar condition
\[
1 = \beta \left(1-\delta + \alpha A (K^*)^{\alpha-1} L^{1-\alpha}\right),
\]
which can be solved for $K^*$ numerically given parameters.
\end{proof}

4. Numerical example and policy implication
Consider a calibration with $\alpha=0.36$, $\beta=0.99$, $\delta=0.025$, and $A=1$. The implied steady-state capital satisfies the previous condition and generates an output level $Y^*$ that can be reported as a fraction of GDP, denoted GDP_t in simulation tables and figures.

\begin{figure}[h]
\centering
\caption{Impulse response of GDP_t to a one percent productivity shock}
\label{fig:irf}
\end{figure}

Policy implication: a small persistent shock to $A_t$ raises output and consumption; with sticky prices the adjustment is slower, implying welfare losses that can be quantified by comparing utilities in levels or via consumption-equivalent variation.

Conclusion:
This memo collected the essential derivations with explicit LaTeX equations so the reader can reproduce steady-state computations and Euler equation calibrations.

title: Proof outline for asymptotic stability of a dissipative PDE
source_text:
\section{Introduction}
This note sketches a proof outline for asymptotic stability of a simple dissipative partial differential equation on a compact Riemannian manifold. We consider a scalar field $u(t,x)$ that satisfies the evolution equation
\[
\partial_t u + Lu + N(u) = 0,
\]
where the linear operator $L$ is self-adjoint and nonnegative and the nonlinear term $N(u)$ is of lower order. The objective is to show that, for sufficiently small initial data in an appropriate Sobolev space $H^s$, the solution $u(t,\cdot)$ exists globally and converges to a steady state as $t\to\infty$.

\section{Main theorem}
\begin{theorem}\label{thm:main}
Let $(M,g)$ be a compact Riemannian manifold and let $L$ be a self-adjoint nonnegative elliptic operator of order $2m$. There exists $\varepsilon>0$ such that if $\|u(0,\cdot)\|_{H^s}\le\varepsilon$ for $s$ sufficiently large, then the solution $u(t,\cdot)$ exists globally and satisfies the decay estimate
\begin{equation}\label{eq:asymp}
\|u(t,\cdot)\|_{H^s}\le C(1+t)^{-\alpha}
\end{equation}
for some $\alpha>0$ and constant $C$ depending on the initial norm.
\end{theorem}

\section{Proof outline}
The proof proceeds in a sequence of logical steps. Below we isolate the principal estimates and the bootstrap argument that yields the decay rate in (\ref{eq:asymp}). Technical details such as mollification, domain approximation, and precise commutator bounds are omitted here but are standard.

Step 1: Linear decay. Analyze the semigroup $e^{-tL}$ generated by $-L$. Using spectral calculus and Weyl's law for eigenvalue distribution one obtains for data orthogonal to the kernel that
\[
\|e^{-tL}f\|_{H^s} \le C(1+t)^{-\alpha} \|f\|_{H^{s-2m\alpha}}.
\]
The exponent $\alpha$ reflects the low-frequency density of states; in the presence of a spectral gap one may replace polynomial decay by exponential decay.

Step 2: Energy estimates. Introduce the modified energy
\[
E_s(t)=\tfrac12\sum_{|\beta|\le s}\langle D^\beta u, D^\beta u\rangle_{L^2},
\]
and differentiate in time. Commuting derivatives with $L$ and integrating by parts yields the basic balance law
\begin{align}
\frac{d}{dt}E_s(t) + \langle Lu, u\rangle_{H^s} &= -\sum_{|\beta|\le s}\langle D^\beta N(u), D^\beta u\rangle_{L^2} \nonumber\\
&\le C\|u\|_{H^s}\|u\|_{H^{s'}}\|u\|_{H^s}, \label{eq:energy}
\end{align}
for some index $s'<s$ controlled by Sobolev embedding. The right-hand side is cubic for typical quadratic nonlinearities after applying appropriate product estimates.

Step 3: Nonlinear iteration via Duhamel. Use Duhamel's formula
\[
u(t)=e^{-tL}u(0)-\int_0^t e^{-(t-\tau)L}N(u(\tau))\,d\tau,
\]
and combine the linear decay bound with the nonlinear estimate to close a bootstrap. Concretely, assume the bootstrap hypothesis
\[
\|u(t)\|_{H^s}\le 2C\langle t\rangle^{-\alpha}
\]
and show that the contribution of the integral term is bounded by $C\langle t\rangle^{-\alpha}$ provided $\|u(0)\|_{H^s}$ is sufficiently small; this improves the constant and yields the desired decay.

Step 4: Limit and characterization of steady states. Once decay in a relatively high Sobolev norm is obtained, compact embedding implies convergence to a limit $u_\infty$ in $C^k$ for $k$ below the Sobolev threshold. Passing to the limit in the equation shows $u_\infty$ satisfies $Lu_\infty+N(u_\infty)=0$, and uniqueness near zero follows from the implicit function theorem applied to the nonlinear map $v\mapsto Lv+N(v)$.

\section{Examples and remarks}
A prototypical example is the higher-order heat equation with a small quadratic perturbation, or viscous Burgers-type models on a compact manifold. The argument above is flexible and transfers to systems with suitable structure; the main ingredients are semigroup decay, tame product estimates, and a bootstrap closure.

\section{Appendix: derivation of the energy identity}
Differentiating $\|D^\beta u\|_{L^2}^2$ and summing over multi-indices yields the identity used in Step 2:
\[
\frac{d}{dt}\|D^\beta u\|_{L^2}^2 + 2\langle LD^\beta u, D^\beta u\rangle = -2\langle D^\beta N(u), D^\beta u\rangle,
\]
and summation recovers (\ref{eq:energy}).

Concluding note: the outline above is intended to show the structure of the argument; filling in the standard technical estimates produces a complete and rigorous proof for concrete choices of $L$ and $N$.

title: Bronze Statuette of a Dancer — Exhibit Note
source_text:
Bronze Statuette of a Dancer

Accession and Identification

This note accompanies the display of a late Hellenistic bronze statuette, catalogued under Inv. No. 1976.45, recently conserved and re-mounted for the temporary exhibition "Movement and Gesture in Ancient Art". The object label reads: Artist: Unknown; Date: c. 2nd century BCE; Accession: 2021-08-15.

Figure references and images

Figure 1 illustrates the front view of the statuette and highlights the articulated right arm reattachment. Figure 2 shows the verso with evidence of inlay traces.

![Front view of statuette](./images/bronze_statuette_front.jpg)
Figure 1: Front view, bronze statuette of a dancer. Note the polished patina on the left hip and the modern cord used for support during transport.

![Back detail with inlay traces](./images/bronze_statuette_back.jpg)
Figure 2: Rear detail, showing inlay traces and a soldered repair at the lower plinth.

Object description

The statuette stands on a circular plinth and depicts a young dancer in mid-step, weight shifted onto the left foot, with a raised right arm. The modeling is cursory but conveys motion through exaggerated tilt of the torso. The hair is rendered in incised waves and a single looped chignon at the nape. Patina: dark green with localized light brown accretions.

Table 1: Technical summary

| Attribute | Details | Condition note |
|---|---:|---|
| Material | Bronze (Cu-Sn alloy) | Corrosion stable; surface encrustation removed |
| Height | 18.3 cm | Minor loss to distal right hand |
| Weight | 420 g | Internal voids visible on radiograph |
| Inlays | Possible silver on eyes and garment border | Traces remain in right eye socket |

Conservation history and radiography

The statuette underwent mechanical cleaning, mild electrolytic reduction of active chlorides, and consolidation of fragile joins. Radiography revealed a hammered two-piece casting with a seam along the left flank and a later solder repair across the plinth; see Table 2 for treatment chronology.

Table 2: Treatment chronology

| Date | Intervention | Restorer |
|------|-------------|----------|
| 1977-03 | Surface cleaning, removed thick accretions | A. Hernández |
| 1998-11 | Re-basing onto contemporary plinth, silicone sleeve added | Conservation Dept. |
| 2021-09 | Electrolytic chloride reduction; micro-solder consolidation | L. Morgan |

Comparative context

Similar statuettes from the eastern Mediterranean dated to the 3rd–1st centuries BCE commonly feature dynamic poses and limited figural detail, emphasizing movement over anatomical precision. The use of silver inlay for eyes is typical for this workshop group and aids in attribution. Comparative examples are cited in the gallery bibliography and labeled cases 5–7.

Display and interpretation notes

When translating labels and captions, maintain the object identifiers and dates exactly as printed (e.g., Inv. No. 1976.45; Accession: 2021-08-15). Do not alter numeric measurements or material abbreviations. The captions (Figure 1, Figure 2) are integral to the visitor's understanding and should appear adjacent to the corresponding image path.

Visitor engagement

The text on the gallery panel poses questions: How does pose convey purpose? Was the figure used in ritual dance or domestic display? Encourage viewers to compare the front and back images and to consult the technical tables for insights into construction techniques.

References and further reading

Selected bibliography is available on the gallery handout. For conservation protocols, see the department manual (internal use).
translation_notes: Translate captions, table headers, and surrounding prose; preserve image paths and figure numbers exactly.
Do not translate or alter object identifiers and accession codes that appear verbatim.
expected_challenges: Rendering technical conservation terms (e.g., electrolytic reduction, consolidation) accurately in target language.
Maintaining table layout and aligning translated headers with their original columns and notes.
Deciding how to translate measurement units, abbreviations, and material names while preserving precision.
Ensuring captions and figure references remain adjacent to the correct image paths and labels.

title: Data Export FAQ (v2.1)
source_text:
# Data Export FAQ — Version v2.1

This FAQ covers common questions about exporting data from the platform, formatting options, and troubleshooting steps. Use the [Export Guide](#export-guide) for a step-by-step walk-through. For the full schema reference, see the Detailed Schema: https://example.com/schema

## General

What types of exports are supported?
- Ad hoc exports for a single project.
- Scheduled exports using the CLI or API.
- Incremental exports for large datasets.

Which versions are supported?
We currently support the v2.1 export API and legacy CSV pipelines. New features will first appear on v2.1.

How is authentication handled?
All programmatic exports require an API token. Do not embed `API_KEY` values in public repositories. Tokens can be rotated from the account settings page.

## Export Guide

Anchor: #export-guide

### Supported formats
- `CSV` with configurable delimiter (`--delimiter`), quoting, and `UTF-8` encoding.
- `JSON` with optional pretty-printing.
- `NDJSON` for streaming consumers.

> Note: For massive exports we recommend `NDJSON` and using compression with `--compress` to reduce transfer times.

### CLI Examples
To run a full export using the CLI:
```bash
export-tool export --project my_project --format ndjson --output /data/exports
```

To pipe a compressed CSV and specify a comma delimiter:
```bash
export-tool export --project my_project --format csv --output - --compress | gzip > export.csv.gz
```

To authenticate using an API key (do not expose your real token):
```bash
curl -H 'Authorization: Bearer API_KEY' https://api.example.com/v2/export?project=my_project
```

### Common options
- `--project`: project identifier
- `--format`: output format (`csv`, `json`, `ndjson`)
- `--output`: file or `-` for stdout
- `--compress`: enable gzip compression

## Troubleshooting

What if a file contains garbled characters?
- Ensure you request `UTF-8` output.
- Check your downstream system's encoding expectations.

What if exports are incomplete?
- Verify that the scheduled job completed successfully.
- Check for quota limits on your account.
- If progress stalls, retry with incremental mode.

How can I validate an exported file?
- Compare a sample against the Detailed Schema: https://example.com/schema
- Use schema-aware validators or simple header checks for `CSV`.

## Examples and Edge Cases

Example: exporting nested objects to CSV often requires flattening. We suggest mapping nested keys to a dot-separated header, e.g., `user.name`, `user.email`.

Edge case: fields that contain line breaks. Wrap fields in quotes and use `CSV` option for proper quoting. Large numeric IDs should be exported as strings if the consumer treats them as identifiers rather than numbers.

## Contact

If you still need help, contact our support team at support@example.com. Provide the export job ID, project name, and a short description of the issue.
translation_notes: Preserve Markdown syntax (headings, lists, links, anchors) and link targets verbatim.
Keep inline code, CLI flags, and code blocks unchanged (do not translate content within backticks).
expected_challenges: Maintaining exact CLI flags and inline code while translating surrounding text.
Rendering links and anchors correctly in the target language without altering URLs.
Translating acronyms and technical terms like NDJSON, CSV, UTF-8 appropriately.
Preserving nuances in blockquotes and troubleshooting steps that imply tone or urgency.

title: TX-300 Battery Replacement and Maintenance Page
source_text: TX-300 Battery Replacement and Maintenance Overview This page describes the recommended procedure for replacing the internal battery of the model TX-300 field transceiver and performing the basic post-replacement checks. Technicians must follow safety precautions and torque specifications. The instructions reference illustrations and a short tools table below. When in doubt, contact the equipment owner or supervisor. Safety Precautions - Power down the unit and disconnect all external cables before beginning. - Wear ESD protection and use a grounded mat to prevent electrostatic discharge damage to internal boards. - Do not apply excessive force when disengaging clips. If resistance is unusually high, stop and inspect for hidden fasteners. Tools and Materials Refer to the tools list before starting. Use the exact toolkit recommended for best results. | Item | Specification / Part Number | Notes | |------|----------------------------|-------| | Battery pack | TXB-45, 3.7V, 2200 mAh | Replace only with manufacturer-approved cells. | | Torx driver | T6, calibrated | Torque limit 0.5 N·m for cover screws. | | Pry tool | Plastic, non-conductive | Avoid metal prying tools. | | Toolkit | ACME Toolkit v2.1 | Contains battery holder adapter and extraction hooks. | Procedure 1. Prepare the workstation: Verify you have the items listed in the Tools and Materials table and set the unit on the grounded mat. 2. External access: Remove the four cover screws (Torx T6). Keep screws in order; do not mix with other hardware. 3. Open the cover gently using a plastic pry tool to avoid damaging the top seal. Refer to Figure 1 for the internal component layout. 4. Identify the battery assembly: The internal battery is located in the lower right quadrant of the mainboard (see Figure 1). Carefully unplug the battery connector from the mainboard header. 5. Extract the battery: Use the plastic extraction hook from ACME Toolkit v2.1 to lift the battery pack. If adhesive is present, apply a slow steady pull; do not twist. 6. Install the new battery: Place the TXB-45 battery into the holder, align the connector pins, and press until the latch clicks. Verify polarity before connecting. 7. Reassembly: Close the cover and tighten screws to 0.5 N·m torque. Power the unit and run the self-test sequence. Post-Replacement Checks - Verify battery voltage in the diagnostics menu shows 3.7V nominal after a one-minute stabilization. - Confirm charge/discharge cycle status reads "Healthy" in the battery diagnostics screen. - Run a radio loopback test for 2 minutes to confirm normal RF behavior. Troubleshooting If the unit fails the self-test after replacement, consult this checklist: - Re-seat the battery connector and ensure no pins are bent. - Check for leftover adhesive causing shorting on the mainboard edges. - Restore original battery temporarily to confirm whether issue is battery-related. Maintenance Schedule | Interval | Action | Responsible | |----------|--------|-------------| | 6 months | Visual inspection of seals and connectors | Field technician | | 12 months | Replace battery if capacity < 80% | Certified technician | | 24 months | Full calibration and firmware update | Service center | Figures Figure 1: Internal battery and connector layout. See layout markers A–E for component references. Placeholder image: Figure 2: Recommended placement of tools and the kit layout before starting the operation. Markdown reference: ![Toolkit Layout](images/figure2_toolkit_layout.jpg) Notes for the translator: Translate all captions, table headers, and item notes. Preserve image paths and any model or part numbers exactly as written.
translation_notes: Translate all table headers and captions; keep units and numeric formats (e.g., 'Time (min)', '0.5 N·m') unchanged.
Preserve image paths, filenames, model and part numbers exactly as in the source (do not translate or transliterate protected spans).
expected_challenges: Technical terminology (torque units, battery specifications) and ensuring accurate localized equivalents.
Maintaining table structure and exact image/file path strings while translating surrounding text and captions.

title: Service recovery and backup runbook with scripts
source_text:
Runbook: Recovering service and restoring configuration

Overview
This runbook describes the steps to safely restore configuration files, verify backups, and restart the myapp service on a Linux host. It includes shell examples, a small Python helper script with a docstring, and a YAML snippet for an Ansible play used in automated environments.

Preconditions
- You have SSH access to the target host.
- Backups are stored in /var/backups/myapp/ and are compressed as tar.gz.
- Environment variables like MYAPP_TOKEN and MYAPP_ENV are configured on the management host.

Important: do not modify production files without approval.

Procedure
1) Verify latest backup
Check the most recent archive in /var/backups/myapp/ and list its contents to confirm integrity.

Example shell commands:

```sh
# List backups sorted by modification time
ls -1t /var/backups/myapp/*.tar.gz

# Inspect a specific archive without extracting
tar -tzf /var/backups/myapp/backup-2023-11-15.tar.gz | head -n 40
```

2) Restore configuration
If you need to restore a specific config file, create a temporary directory and extract only that file.

```sh
mkdir -p /tmp/myapp-restore
tar -xzf /var/backups/myapp/backup-2023-11-15.tar.gz -C /tmp/myapp-restore ./etc/myapp/config.yml
cp /tmp/myapp-restore/etc/myapp/config.yml /etc/myapp/config.yml
chown myapp:myapp /etc/myapp/config.yml
chmod 640 /etc/myapp/config.yml
```

Comments in these commands explain intent: do not translate command names or file paths. Translate explanatory comments.

3) Restart service
After restoring, restart the service and check the journal for any errors.

```sh
# Restart the service and show the last 50 log lines
systemctl restart myapp.service
journalctl -u myapp.service -n 50 --no-pager
```

Automated playbook example (ansible)
In automated environments use ansible-playbook with a limited set of hosts to perform the same steps atomically.

```yaml
- name: Restore myapp configuration from backup
  hosts: webservers
  become: yes
  tasks:
    - name: Extract specific config file
      unarchive:
        src: /var/backups/myapp/{{ backup_file }}
        dest: /tmp/myapp-restore
        extra_opts: [--strip-components=2]
    - name: Deploy config
      copy:
        src: /tmp/myapp-restore/etc/myapp/config.yml
        dest: /etc/myapp/config.yml
        owner: myapp
        group: myapp
        mode: '0640'
```

Python helper script
This script is a minimal helper that validates a backup path and prints the most recent archive name. Translate the docstring and inline comments, but preserve identifiers.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Utility: find_latest_backup(path)
Returns the most recently modified .tar.gz file under the given path.
"""
import os
import glob

def find_latest_backup(path):
    # List matching archives; do not change the pattern
    pattern = os.path.join(path, "*.tar.gz")
    archives = glob.glob(pattern)
    if not archives:
        # No backups found: raise a ValueError to be handled by the caller
        raise ValueError("no backups found in %s" % path)
    # Sort by modification time, newest first
    archives.sort(key=os.path.getmtime, reverse=True)
    return archives[0]

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Example usage: python3 find_backup.py /var/backups/myapp
    import sys
    print(find_latest_backup(sys.argv[1]))
```

Troubleshooting
- If systemctl fails, inspect journalctl output for precise error messages and do not guess at fixes.
- Ensure permissions on /etc/myapp/config.yml are 0640 and owned by myapp. If not, correct them before restarting.
- If ansible reports a checksum mismatch, verify the backup archive integrity locally with tar -tzf and md5sum.

Notes for operators
Always document the action id and ticket number in the incident log. When translating this runbook, preserve code fences, commands, file paths, environment variable names like MYAPP_TOKEN, and service identifiers like myapp.service.
translation_notes: Translate all narrative prose, docstrings, and inline comments; do not translate code, command names, file paths, environment variable names, or service identifiers.
Preserve fenced blocks, indentation, placeholders like {{ backup_file }}, and the exact syntax of commands and YAML keys.
expected_challenges: Distinguishing which fragments are translatable prose vs. code or identifiers that must remain unchanged.
Handling inline comments and docstrings so that translation does not break escaping, punctuation, or code semantics.
Translating technical terms, acronyms, and command output while preserving clarity for operators.
Maintaining placeholder tokens and brace syntax (e.g., {{ backup_file }}) and numeric permission formats like 0640.

title: Field Survey Summary: Coastal Wetland Vegetation and Soil Baseline
source_text: Field Survey Summary: Coastal Wetland Vegetation and Soil Baseline Executive Summary This report summarizes a two-week field survey conducted at three transects in the northern coastal wetland complex. Primary objectives were to document current vegetation composition, measure surface soil properties, and record visible erosion features. Observations emphasize areas requiring stabilization and locations suitable for pilot revegetation plots. Site Identification and Coordinates Surveyed points: Site A, Site B, Site C. GPS-based reference for the main station: GPS: 34.0522°N, 118.2437°W. The coordinates are recorded in decimal degrees and must be preserved exactly in translations to avoid geolocation errors. Methods Teams followed a standardized protocol: 50 m transects perpendicular to the shoreline at low tide, 1 m2 quadrat sampling every 10 m for percent cover estimates, and soil cores to 15 cm depth for bulk density and pH analysis. All measurements use SI units except where noted. Photodocumentation included oblique drone imagery and ground-level quadrat photos. Results Vegetation cover varied markedly between sites. Native cordgrass was dominant at Site A with sparse patches of invasive reed. Site B showed mosaic patterns of native and invasive species. Site C, the most disturbed, had extensive bare sediment and compaction. Table 1 summarizes average measurements across quadrats per site. Headers indicate what must be translated and retained structurally. Table 1: Summary of Quadrats (averages per site) | Site | Native Cover (%) | Invasive Cover (%) | Soil Bulk Density (g/cm3) | Soil pH | Erosion Index (0-5) | |------|------------------:|-------------------:|--------------------------:|--------:|--------------------:| | Site A | 72 | 12 | 1.25 | 6.8 | 1 | | Site B | 45 | 38 | 1.40 | 7.2 | 3 | | Site C | 18 | 55 | 1.60 | 6.4 | 4 | Notes: Column 'Soil pH' is a critical chemical descriptor. 'Erosion Index' follows the field scoring rubric where 0=no visible erosion and 5=severe bank collapse. Figures and Photographs Figure 1 (images/siteA_oblique.jpg): Drone overview of Site A at low tide showing intact cordgrass stands and a narrow erosion front along the northern edge. Refer to Figure 1 in the discussion for slope indicators. Figure 2 (images/quadrat_siteC_07.jpg): Ground-level quadrat photograph at Site C illustrating bare sediment, trampling, and exposed root zones. ![Figure 2: Quadrat at Site C](images/quadrat_siteC_07.jpg) Discussion Site A demonstrates the best potential for near-term stabilization using local donor material due to high native cover and low erosion index. The protected reed patches require monitoring to prevent spread. Translators should preserve species names and numeric values exactly to maintain management fidelity. Site B's mixed composition suggests targeted removal of invasives followed by replanting native cordgrass in pilot strips. Site C requires immediate intervention: regrading of collapsed edges, installation of biodegradable coir rolls, and staged revegetation where compaction is alleviated. Recommendations 1) Prioritize Site A for establishment of a 0.5 ha donor plot and test transplants in winter planting window. 2) Implement erosion control at Site C within three months; monitor soil pH monthly at disturbed plots. 3) Standardize all translated reports to retain table structure, figure references (Figure 1, Figure 2), and exact filenames for the image assets. Appendix: Field Protocol Excerpts - Quadrat sampling: 1 m2, estimate percent cover to nearest 1%. - Soil cores: 15 cm depth, dried at 105°C for bulk density. - Erosion scoring: see rubric (0-5) included in the master protocol. End of report.
translation_notes: Preserve table headers and numeric values exactly; translate headers but keep table layout.
Keep image filenames and figure references (e.g., images/siteA_oblique.jpg, Figure 1) unchanged in target text.
expected_challenges: Rendering of table alignment and numeric formatting across languages with different digit groupings.
Translating technical terms (e.g., 'bulk density', 'Erosion Index') while keeping precise measures.
Maintaining coordinates and filenames verbatim to avoid location mismatches.

title: Data Access and Retention — Policy Note
source_text: # Data Access and Retention Policy Note > This document outlines minimum standards for access, retention, and emergency procedures concerning company data. It is intended for internal teams, auditors, and third-party processors. ## Overview The purpose of this policy note is to clarify expectations for data handling across services and departments. It complements the full [Company Policy](https://example.com/policy) and specific legal guidance such as the GDPR and national data protection regulations. This note covers: - Access request procedures and approval workflows. - Data retention schedules and secure disposal methods. - Emergency access and exception handling. - Audit and compliance checkpoints. ## Scope This policy applies to all systems, applications, and services that process or store company or customer data. It includes, but is not limited to: 1. Production and staging environments. 2. Third-party processors and SaaS integrations. 3. Developer tools that contain backups, logs, or diagnostic data. Systems that expose APIs must log access attempts and maintain the minimal logs necessary for security investigations. For example, services that interact with our internal endpoints must be configured to use `api.example.com/v1` and to rotate credentials on a 90-day schedule. ## Access Requests and Approvals Access to sensitive data requires explicit, documented approval. The typical workflow is: - Request submitted via the internal ticketing system with purpose and duration. - Team lead approval documenting least privilege justification. - Security team review for cross-team or high-risk requests. Temporary access must include an expiration timestamp and an audit trail. If a request is urgent and cannot follow the standard workflow, teams should follow the Emergency Access process below. ## Data Retention Schedule Retention periods should align with legal, contractual, and operational requirements. A sample schedule: - Customer transactional records: retain for 7 years. - Authentication logs: retain for 1 year, then aggregate or purge. - Development backups: retain for 90 days unless otherwise approved. Use of the `data-retention` tag in storage metadata is required to facilitate automated purging. Refer to Section 4.2 of the full policy for additional category mappings. > Note: Shorter retention may be used where technically feasible and legally permissible. Consult Legal for jurisdiction-specific adjustments. ## Emergency Access and Exceptions Emergency access is limited to predefined roles and must be accompanied by post-access review. Steps: 1. Emergency access request via phone to Security Operations. 2. Temporary credentials issued with immediate logging enabled. 3. Post-incident report submitted within 72 hours. Exceptions are temporary and must be logged and approved by both the Team Lead and Security. Long-term exceptions require risk assessment and sign-off by Data Protection. ## Compliance and Auditing Periodic audits will verify retention enforcement, access logging, and that approved exceptions have corresponding mitigations. Auditors will be granted read-only views of logs and change histories. - Quarterly compliance checks for high-risk systems. - Annual third-party audits for major processors. Resources and contacts: - Data Protection: dataprivacy@example.com - Security Operations: soc@example.com - External authority: [Data Protection Authority](https://dpa.example.org) ## Revision History - 2024-01-15: Document created and published. - 2024-06-01: Clarified retention timelines and emergency access steps. For implementation details and templates, refer to the full policy linked above or contact the Data Protection team.
translation_notes: Preserve Markdown links, anchors (), and inline code exactly as in source_text.
Do not translate protected spans or modify URLs and code tokens; translate headings, bullets, and blockquote content naturally.
expected_challenges: Consistent rendering of legal abbreviations (e.g., GDPR) and their localized equivalents.
Keeping Markdown structure and inline code (`api.example.com/v1`, `data-retention`) intact while producing natural target-language phrasing.

title: YAML configuration and loader example with localization notes
source_text:
YAML Configuration Tutorial: service.yaml example

Overview
This document explains the recommended structure for a simple service configuration stored in a YAML file. It is intended for translators and engineers who will localize user-facing explanations and comments while leaving code and identifiers intact. Translate the human-readable explanations, docstrings and inline comments; do not change code syntax, keys, or environment variable names unless explicitly noted.

Example configuration
Below is a minimal, but realistic, service configuration. Comments beginning with # provide guidance and should be translated. Keys such as service, environment, and resource names are identifiers and must remain unchanged unless your localization policy requires otherwise.

```yaml
# Top-level configuration for the "user-service".
# Use 'service' to group runtime settings.
service:
  name: user-service
  replicas: 3  # number of instances to run
  image: "registry.example.com/user-service:1.2.0"  # container image to deploy

environment:
  LOG_LEVEL: "info"  # application log level: debug, info, warn, error
  DATABASE_URL: "postgres://user:pass@db:5432/users"  # connection string

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "256Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "128Mi"

# Optional features
features:
  enable_metrics: true  # expose /metrics endpoint for Prometheus
  enable_tracing: false  # enable distributed tracing (Jaeger)
```

Field explanations
- service.name: Human-readable name for the service. This is normally shown in dashboards; decide whether to translate it based on your product conventions.
- replicas: Integer that controls how many instances are launched. Keep numeric values unchanged.
- image: Container image reference. Do not translate registry paths or tags.
- environment: A mapping of environment variables. Variable names (for example LOG_LEVEL or DATABASE_URL) must remain exactly as shown; values may be localized if they are plain text shown to users.
- resources: CPU and memory constraints expressed in Kubernetes-style units. Preserve the units and numeric fields.
- features.enable_metrics and features.enable_tracing: Boolean toggles for optional behavior. Translate the comments that explain the feature purpose.

Usage examples
You can start the service locally for testing with a CLI flag that points to the YAML file.

```bash
# Launch the service with a specific config file
python run_service.py --config service.yaml

# Alternatively, run via Docker using the same config mounted into the container
docker run --rm -v $(pwd)/service.yaml:/etc/service.yaml registry.example.com/user-service:1.2.0 \
  --config /etc/service.yaml
```

Loader implementation (Python)
The following snippet shows a small loader that reads and validates the YAML. The docstring and inline comments are user-facing and should be translated. Preserve function names and variable identifiers to avoid breaking integrations.

```python
"""
load_config reads a YAML file and returns a dictionary with validated fields.
Translate this docstring when localizing any user-facing messages.
"""

import yaml
from typing import Dict

def load_config(path: str) -> Dict:
    # Read raw YAML and return parsed dictionary.
    with open(path, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as f:
        config = yaml.safe_load(f)

    # Validate required keys: 'service', 'environment'
    if 'service' not in config or 'environment' not in config:
        raise ValueError("Missing required top-level keys: service, environment")
    return config
```

Localization tips
- Translate all prose blocks, headings, comments, and the docstring above. These are the primary content that users and operators will read.
- Do not translate code identifiers, keys, flags, or environment variable names listed in protected spans (see list). Translating these names will break automated tooling.
- When translating short technical tokens (e.g., "info", "debug"), check whether they are part of an enum in the application; if so, prefer leaving them unchanged or consult the developer for the expected localized set.
- Keep punctuation and indentation exactly as in the examples. YAML is indentation-sensitive and code blocks must remain runnable if someone later copies them.

Additional notes
If a comment contains both natural language explanation and a code fragment, translate only the natural language portion and keep the code fragment verbatim. For example, in "# number of instances to run" translate the sentence but do not alter numeric examples like 3 or unit strings like "256Mi".
translation_notes: Translate all prose, headings, inline comments and docstrings; preserve code blocks, keys, flags and identifiers listed in protected_spans.
Do not change indentation, punctuation, or YAML syntax; when translating comments that embed code fragments, keep the code fragments verbatim.
expected_challenges: Distinguishing between translatable comments and code fragments inside comments
Preserving YAML indentation and line-sensitive syntax while translating adjacent text
Deciding whether to translate short technical tokens like log levels (info, debug)
Keeping environment variable names, flags and file paths intact to avoid breaking references

title: ProductX v2.3.0 Release Summary
source_text:
# Release Summary: ProductX v2.3.0

## Overview

ProductX v2.3.0 is a focused maintenance release that improves stability, clarifies security defaults, and introduces two minor feature enhancements requested by enterprise customers. This release is intended to be a low-risk upgrade for most deployments, with clear migration steps for environments that rely on legacy configuration formats.

For full details and the changelog, see the official release notes: [Release Notes](https://example.com/release-notes).

> Note: This release includes changes to default session handling. Review the migration guide below before upgrading production systems.

## Highlights

- Security: Session cookies are now flagged as HttpOnly by default to reduce XSS exposure.
- Packaging: Docker image layers have been optimized, reducing typical image size by ~12%.
- Observability: Added additional metrics around connection pool saturation (labels: pool, host, status).
- CLI: The `--no-cache` option is newly supported for local builds to force a fresh artifact generation.

## Detailed Changes

### Security and Defaults

- Session cookies: The `session_secure` and `session_httponly` flags are enabled by default. If your environment depends on older cookies behavior, explicitly set these values in your configuration file.
- Rate limiting: Default thresholds were adjusted to reduce false positives under bursty traffic patterns.

### Packaging and Performance

- Docker: Multi-stage builds were refined to drop unnecessary build-time dependencies. This means smaller images and faster cold-starts in serverless contexts.
- Database: Query planner hints were added for PostgreSQL 12+ to prevent a known regression in join ordering for certain workloads.

### Developer Experience

- CLI: The `pxctl build` command now accepts `--no-cache` to skip intermediate caching layers. Example:

```
pxctl build --target production --no-cache
```

- Documentation: All examples have been verified against the new defaults and updated where required.

## Migration Guide

1. Backup: Take configuration and data backups before applying the upgrade.
2. Test: Redeploy to a staging environment and run integration tests that exercise authentication and session flows.
3. Config: If you previously relied on non-HttpOnly cookies, add these lines to your configuration to preserve behavior:

```
session_httponly: false
session_secure: false
```

4. Database: If you are using PostgreSQL older than 12, evaluate the need to apply optimizer-related patches or postpone upgrade until a maintenance window.

## Known Issues

- Windows: The CLI installer may not update PATH automatically on older Windows builds. Manual PATH update may be required.
- Metrics: Some Prometheus labels changed names; dashboards that reference the old label names will need to be updated.

## Compatibility

- Backward compatible with v2.2.x for configuration files that do not rely on deprecated fields.
- Deprecated: the `legacy_auth` module is now deprecated and will be removed in a future major release. Migrate to `oauth_adapter` where possible.

## Rollback Plan

If you encounter critical issues, revert to the previous Docker tag within your orchestration system and restore the configuration backup. Ensure that any schema migrations are reversible before rolling forward.

## Notes for Translators

- Translate headings, paragraphs, bullets, and blockquotes naturally.
- Preserve Markdown structure, inline code, and link targets exactly as they appear (for example, keep `pxctl build` and flags like `--no-cache` unchanged).

## Contact and Support

If you need assistance with the upgrade, contact support@example.com or open an issue via the project tracker linked in the release notes.

Thank you for using ProductX. Your feedback helps us prioritize fixes and improvements for the next release cycle.

expected_challenges: Maintaining exact verbatim rendering of inline code and links while producing natural translations.
