title: Comparative Biofilm Formation under Variable pH and Temperature
source_text: Abstract This report summarizes a controlled laboratory study comparing biofilm formation by Pseudomonas aeruginosa across a matrix of pH and temperature conditions. The goal was to quantify biomass and structural changes using crystal violet staining and light microscopy, and to relate these observations to environmental parameters relevant to clinical and industrial settings. Introduction Biofilms are structured communities of microorganisms adherent to surfaces and embedded in extracellular polymeric substances. Variations in pH and temperature can strongly influence adhesion, matrix production, and detachment. This study explores these effects under standardized nutrient conditions and reports both quantitative optical-density measures and representative micrographs. Materials and Methods Bacterial strain: Pseudomonas aeruginosa PAO1. Growth medium: standard LB supplemented with 10 mM phosphate buffer adjusted to target pH. Cultures were incubated in 24-well polystyrene plates for 48 hours under static conditions. After incubation, planktonic cells were removed, wells were washed with PBS, and adherent biomass was stained with 0.1% crystal violet. Stain was solubilized in 30% acetic acid and quantified at OD570. Table 1: Experimental parameters and measurement details | Parameter | Levels tested | Measurement/Notes | |------------------------|-------------------------|-----------------------------------------| | pH | 5.5, 6.5, 7.4, 8.5 | Buffered; target accuracy ±0.05 pH units | | Temperature (°C) | 20, 30, 37 | Incubators calibrated within ±0.5 °C | | Incubation time | 48 hours | Static, no shaking | | Replicates | n = 6 per condition | Biological replicates | | Biomass quantitation | OD570 | Crystal violet assay, blank-corrected | Notes: Buffer composition and calibration procedures are described in the Supplementary Methods. The header "Measurement/Notes" consolidates key procedural details critical for replicability. Results Quantitative analysis showed a non-linear response of biofilm biomass to pH and temperature. Peak OD570 values were observed at pH 7.4 and 30 °C in our setup. At low pH (5.5), biomass decreased by approximately 40% relative to pH 7.4 at the same temperature. Temperature shifts from 30 °C to 37 °C produced modest reductions in adherent biomass but increased heterogeneity in microstructure as observed by microscopy. Figure 1 shows representative micrographs of crystal violet-stained wells from three conditions. The image filenames correspond to archived raw images. ![Figure 1: Representative crystal violet-stained biofilms at 48 h (left: pH 5.5, 20 °C; center: pH 7.4, 30 °C; right: pH 8.5, 37 °C).](images/fig1_placeholder.png) Figure 1 caption: Dense mat formation is apparent in the center panel (pH 7.4, 30 °C) with contiguous staining across the well bottom. Peripheral flocculation and patchy adhesion characterize the low-pH and high-temperature samples. Quantitative OD570 values associated with each image are listed in Table 2. To illustrate the experimental workflow, a schematic diagram is provided below outlining incubation, washing, staining, and quantitation steps. Discussion The observed maximum biomass at pH 7.4 supports previous findings that near-neutral pH favors matrix production in P. aeruginosa under nutrient-rich conditions. The interaction between pH and temperature suggests an optimal window for surface colonization: moderate temperature (30 °C) combined with neutral pH promotes adherence, whereas extremes reduce biomass but can alter microstructure. Translators should preserve numeric precision and units when converting this text. Limitations include static incubation (no flow) and a single strain; environmental isolates may show different optima. Future work will deploy confocal imaging to resolve three-dimensional architecture and RNA-seq to probe transcriptional responses. Conclusion This comparative analysis demonstrates that small shifts in pH and temperature materially affect biofilm formation. The combination of tabulated experimental parameters, quantitative OD measurements, and annotated images provides a multi-modal dataset for interpreting environmental influences on microbial adhesion. References Selected protocol references and buffer recipes are provided in the Supplementary Materials.
translation_notes: Translate all table headers and image captions fully into the target language; keep file paths (e.g., images/fig1_placeholder.png) unchanged.
Preserve numeric formats, units (°C, OD570), and exact chemical/strain names (e.g., Pseudomonas aeruginosa PAO1); do not translate these protected spans.
expected_challenges: Maintaining table alignment and ensuring translated headers fit without altering column structure.
Translating technical terms (e.g., 'crystal violet', 'OD570') while preserving unit consistency and measurement precision.
Handling inline image tags and file paths so they remain unchanged in the translated document.
Rendering numeric pH and temperature values with correct local conventions (decimal marks) without introducing errors.

title: reporting_module_with_usage_and_config
source_text:
'''reporting_module

This module provides utilities to collect metrics, transform data, and render a final PDF or HTML report. The documentation and inline comments are intended to be translated for international teams, while code identifiers, file names, and specific configuration keys must remain unchanged.

Overview

- Purpose: Collect runtime metrics, apply formatting rules, and generate a human-readable report.
- Audience: Developers and technical writers who will adapt templates and localize messages.

Examples include a Python API, a shell helper to set the environment, and a sample YAML config. Translators should convert user-facing messages and prose but preserve code blocks and identifiers such as generate_report and DEFAULT_TIMEOUT.
'''

# Public constants
DEFAULT_TIMEOUT = 30  # seconds to wait for external services before failing
CONFIG_PATH = '/etc/reporting/config.yaml'

def generate_report(data_source, output_path, timeout=DEFAULT_TIMEOUT):
    '''
    Generate a report from the provided data source and write it to output_path.

    Parameters:
      data_source: path or connection string for the metrics source. Translate explanation only, not path values or tokens.
      output_path: filesystem path where the final report file will be written.
      timeout: integer seconds to wait for remote calls. Uses DEFAULT_TIMEOUT by default.

    Behavior:
      - Fetches data from data_source.
      - Applies transformation pipeline.
      - Renders templates into HTML and optionally converts to PDF.

    Raises:
      IOError: when unable to write to output_path.
      ValueError: when the input data format is unrecognized.
    '''
    # Validate inputs
    if not data_source:
        # User-facing error: must be translated
        raise ValueError('No data source specified')

    # Internal token used by CI; do not translate: do_not_translate_token
    token = 'do_not_translate_token'

    # Fetch data (network or file). The comment below describes expected formats.
    # Expected formats: CSV with header, JSON array of objects, or Parquet files.
    raw = _fetch_data(data_source, timeout)
    transformed = _transform_pipeline(raw)
    _render_templates(transformed, output_path)


def _fetch_data(source, timeout):
    # Attempt to read from file or remote endpoint. Translate explanatory comments.
    # If the source looks like a URL, use HTTP; otherwise treat as filesystem path.
    # This internal helper is not intended for direct translation, but its comments should be localized.
    pass


# Usage examples

The following code block demonstrates typical usage. Translate surrounding prose but preserve code exactly.

```python
from reporting_module import generate_report

# Generate a daily report and save to local disk.
# Localize the comment above into the target language.
generate_report('/var/data/metrics/day1.csv', '/tmp/report_day1.html')
```

Shell helper

Provide these commands to set up environment variables on Unix-like systems. Filenames and flags must remain unchanged.

```sh
# Make environment variables available to the reporting process
export REPORT_ENV=production
source setup_env.sh  # this file contains secure keys and should not be translated
python -m reporting_module --config $CONFIG_PATH
```

Sample YAML configuration

Below is a minimal configuration example. Translate keys' descriptions but keep key names intact.

```yaml
# reporting configuration example
service: reporting
metrics_interval: 60  # seconds between metric collections
output: /var/reports/daily
secret_token: do_not_translate_token
```

Localization notes

- Translate all comments and docstrings that explain behavior, exceptions, and usage examples. These provide contextual meaning to users in other languages.
- Preserve code, identifiers, file names, command-line flags, and configuration keys. For example, do not translate generate_report, DEFAULT_TIMEOUT, CONFIG_PATH, or setup_env.sh.

Error handling guidance

When converting error messages, keep the technical details intact so that automated monitoring and log parsers continue to work. For instance, message templates that include machine-readable codes should maintain the codes unchanged while translating the human-readable portion.

Contributors should also check for idiomatic phrasing: short imperative comments like 'Validate inputs' can be localized to match the target language's tone.

title: Derivation of Energy Conservation for the Linear Wave Equation
source_text:
\section{Introduction}
This note presents a clear derivation of the energy conservation law for the linear scalar wave equation in a bounded domain. We assume a sufficiently smooth real-valued field $u(x,t)$ on a spatial domain $\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ with boundary $\partial\Omega$, and consider initial-boundary value problems with homogeneous Dirichlet or Neumann conditions. The methods below emphasize integration by parts, the role of boundary flux terms, and a short discussion of a conserved energy functional.

\section{The wave equation and definitions}
We study the standard second-order wave equation
\[
\partial_{tt} u - c^2\Delta u = f(x,t), \qquad (x,t)\in\Omega\times(0,T],
\]\label{eq:wave}
subject to initial conditions $u(x,0)=u_0(x)$ and $\partial_t u(x,0)=v_0(x)$. Here $c>0$ is the wave speed. The homogeneous case corresponds to $f\equiv0$.

Define the instantaneous energy density
\[
\mathcal{E}(x,t) = \tfrac12\big( |\partial_t u(x,t)|^2 + c^2|\nabla u(x,t)|^2 \big).
\]
The total energy on the domain is
\[
E(t) = \int_{\Omega} \mathcal{E}(x,t)\,dx.
\]
An elementary physical analogue is the mass–energy equivalence formula, often quoted as $E = mc^2$, which here suggests the role of $c^2$ as a coupling between kinetic and potential-like terms.

\section{Derivation of conservation law}
Multiply the homogeneous wave equation by $\partial_t u$ and integrate over $\Omega$. For smooth solutions we have
\begin{align}
0 &= \int_{\Omega} (\partial_{tt} u)\,\partial_t u\,dx - c^2\int_{\Omega} (\Delta u)\,\partial_t u\,dx \nonumber \\
&= \frac{d}{dt}\int_{\Omega} \tfrac12|\partial_t u|^2\,dx - c^2\int_{\Omega} \nabla u\cdot\nabla(\partial_t u)\,dx.
\end{align}
Integrating the second term by parts in space and using the divergence theorem gives
\begin{align}
-c^2\int_{\Omega} \nabla u\cdot\nabla(\partial_t u)\,dx &= -c^2\int_{\partial\Omega} (\partial_t u)\,\partial_n u \,dS + c^2\int_{\Omega} (\partial_t u)\,\Delta u\,dx \\
&= -c^2\int_{\partial\Omega} (\partial_t u)\,\partial_n u \,dS - \frac{d}{dt}\int_{\Omega} \tfrac12 c^2|\nabla u|^2\,dx,
\end{align}
where $\partial_n u$ denotes the outward normal derivative. Combining terms yields the identity
\[
\frac{d}{dt}E(t) = -c^2\int_{\partial\Omega} (\partial_t u)\,\partial_n u \,dS.
\]
Thus, when the boundary condition is homogeneous Dirichlet ($u|_{\partial\Omega}=0$) or homogeneous Neumann ($\partial_n u|_{\partial\Omega}=0$), the boundary integral vanishes and energy is conserved:
\[
\frac{d}{dt}E(t) = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad E(t) = E(0).
\]
This derivation makes explicit the difference between closed (energy-conserving) and open (energy-exchanging) systems.

\begin{theorem}
(Sufficient conditions for conservation.) Let $u$ be a sufficiently smooth solution of the homogeneous wave equation on a bounded domain $\Omega$ with either homogeneous Dirichlet or homogeneous Neumann boundary conditions. Then the total energy $E(t)$ is constant in time.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
The computation above proves the theorem: the boundary term vanishes under the stated boundary conditions, establishing $dE/dt=0$. The regularity assumptions ensure all integrations by parts are justified.
\end{proof}

\section{Extensions and remarks}
If a forcing $f(x,t)$ is present, the right-hand side picks up a source term
\[
\frac{d}{dt}E(t) = \int_{\Omega} f\,\partial_t u\,dx - c^2\int_{\partial\Omega} (\partial_t u)\,\partial_n u \,dS.
\]
This expression can be used to bound the growth of energy via Cauchy–Schwarz and Gronwall-type inequalities. For nonhomogeneous boundary data, the boundary flux should be interpreted as energy input or output through $\partial\Omega$.

\begin{figure}
\centering
% schematic omitted
\caption{Schematic of the domain $\Omega$ and outward normal $\mathbf{n}$.}
\end{figure}

The simple computation above is a canonical exercise in PDE lecture notes, and it generalizes to variable coefficients, damped systems, and to elastic waves where vector-valued fields and stress tensors replace scalar gradients.

expected_challenges: Distinguishing inline math from surrounding prose for accurate translation.

Close-up highlights the reinforced zipper and foam density labels. Overview The Aurora collection emphasizes modularity and durability. Each sofa consists of independent modules that can be reconfigured without tools. Customer options include fabric or leather upholstery, leg finish, and arm style. All variants are assembled with a hardwood frame and feature high-resilience foam cushions for long-term comfort. Key features: - Modular units lock together with concealed brackets for a seamless appearance. - Removable cushion covers for easy cleaning. - Bench-made construction with a 5-year structural warranty. All models comply with local fire-safety standards and include a "Made in Portugal" origin statement on the product tag. title: Getting Started with Git: A Practical Study Guide
source_text:
# Getting Started with Git: A Practical Study Guide

[Table of Contents](#table-of-contents)

## Table of Contents

- [Overview](#overview)
- [Prerequisites](#prerequisites)
- [Key Concepts](#key-concepts)
- [Setting up a repository](#setting-up-a-repository)
- [Common Commands and Examples](#common-commands-and-examples)
- [Best Practices](#best-practices)
- [Further Reading](#further-reading)

## Overview

This study guide is designed for non-developers who need to learn the core concepts of Git quickly and practically. You will find concise explanations, step-by-step instructions, and small examples to practice locally. The goal is not to replace in-depth courses but to provide a structured starting point.

> Tip: Try commands in a disposable folder to avoid altering important files.

## Prerequisites

- Basic familiarity with the command line (opening a terminal, navigating folders).
- A recent version of Git installed. On most systems you can verify by running `git --version`.
- A text editor such as VS Code, Sublime Text, or a simple editor like Notepad.

If you need a quick setup guide for your operating system, consult an authoritative source such as the official documentation or a company-approved internal page: https://example.com/setup

## Key Concepts

- Repository: A directory that Git tracks. It contains a `.git` folder holding metadata.
- Commit: A snapshot of the repository at a point in time.
- Branch: A movable pointer to commits, allowing parallel lines of development.
- Remote: A reference to a repository hosted elsewhere (for example, on GitHub or GitLab).

Understanding these building blocks will make the commands that follow easier to remember.

## Setting up a repository

Follow these steps to create a local repository and record your first commit.

1. Create a new project directory and change into it.
2. Initialize Git in the directory with `git init`.
3. Create a file named `README.md` and add a brief description of the project.
4. Stage and commit your changes:
   - `git add .`
   - `git commit -m "Initial commit"`

Example session:

    mkdir demo-project
    cd demo-project
    `git init`
    echo "# Demo Project" > README.md
    `git add .`
    `git commit -m "Initial commit"`

Subsection: Creating a remote (optional)

- Create a repository on your hosting provider (e.g., GitHub).
- Link the remote: `git remote add origin git@github.com:username/demo-project.git`
- Push your branch: `git push -u origin main`

## Common Commands and Examples

- `git status` — Check the working directory status.
- `git log --oneline` — Show commit history in a compact form.
- `git branch` — List branches; `git branch new-feature` creates a branch.
- `git checkout new-feature` or `git switch new-feature` — Move to another branch.
- `git merge main` — Merge changes from `main` into the current branch.

Small troubleshooting example:

- If you accidentally commit a file with sensitive information, use `git rm --cached filename` to remove it from the index, update `.gitignore`, and create a new commit. For complete removal from history, consult advanced tools such as `git filter-repo`.

## Best Practices

- Commit often with clear, present-tense messages.
- Keep commits focused: one logical change per commit.
- Use branches for features and bug fixes, merge frequently to avoid large conflicts.
- Review `git status` and `git log` before pushing to a remote.

## Further Reading

- Pro Git (online book): https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2
- Official Git documentation: https://git-scm.com/docs
- Interactive tutorials and sandboxes (search for "learn git branch" or similar terms)

Appendix: Quick reference

- Initialize: `git init`
- Stage: `git add <file>` or `git add .`
- Commit: `git commit -m "message"`
- Push: `git push origin <branch>`

This guide should provide a clear starting path. Practice the examples and keep a small notes file of commands you use frequently; repetition will build fluency.

Do not translate inline code, filenames, or commit messages (e.g., `git init`, `README.md`).
expected_challenges: Keeping inline code and filenames unchanged while translating surrounding text.
Rendering technical terms and commands naturally in the target language without altering syntax.
Translating imperative headings and checklist items while preserving tone and brevity.

title: Audio Guide: Gallery 4 — Ritual Objects and Inscriptions
source_text:
Welcome to Gallery 4. This audio track will guide you through three focal objects: a carved stone figure, a bound manuscript folio, and a small bronze coin. Each section begins with a short contextual introduction, followed by close listening cues and suggested prompts for further exploration at the object label. The guide is designed for quiet viewing; please lower conversational volume when others are listening.

How to use this track
- Play the numbered segment when you reach the corresponding label on the wall.
- Pause between segments to allow time for reading the labels and looking closely at the object.
- If you prefer reading, the written transcript is available at the kiosk and includes an annotated glossary.

Track 1 — Stone Figure (center podium)
Begin at the center podium where the carved stone figure stands on a low plinth. The figure depicts a river deity in ritual dress, arms raised in the blessing mudrā. Note the pattern of lotus petals carved at the hem of the garment and the traces of original pigment within the folds. Museum conservation notes indicate the surface retains mineral-based red and green pigments typical of the midlands workshops.
On the plinth you will also find a donor plaque inscribed in Hindi. Read this line aloud to appreciate its original placement and rhythm: "यह प्रतिमा 18वीं शताब्दी की है और इसे स्थानीय कारीगरों ने बनाया था।" The plaque connects the object to a specific community of makers and to local devotional practice; translators should preserve the plaque text verbatim as an inscription when creating other-language versions of the guide.
Close listening cues:
- Focus on the hands and wrists: the gestures suggest protection rather than fertility, altering how we interpret the iconography.
- Take 30 seconds to scan the base for repair marks; these indicate repeated ritual handling.

Track 2 — Bound Manuscript (glass case B)
Move to the glass case labeled B. This small bound manuscript contains devotional songs and attribution notes at the end of several folios. The paper is hand-laid rag paper with deckled edges, and the ink is carbon-based, appearing slightly raised where the scribe wrote more heavily. Open folio 12 displays an interlinear gloss in a different hand, suggesting later commentary.
One folio presents a short devotional line, framed by decorative red marginalia. The line reads: "बूँदें बनकर गिरती हैं, धरती को सींचती हैं।" Its poetic economy encapsulates a recurrent theme in the manuscript: sustenance and reciprocity. As you listen, consider how image and text interact on a single page — the marginalia often marks performance cues used by singers.
Close listening cues:
- Notice orthographic variations: the scribe uses elongated vowel markers in places where later copies simplify them.
- Inspect the ruling lines beneath the main text; they provide a rhythm that singers would have followed.

Track 3 — Bronze Coin (display pedestal)
The third object is a small bronze coin discovered in a riverbed near the region. Its obverse bears an emblematic sunburst and an abbreviated royal title; the reverse shows a stylized boat. The wear pattern suggests prolonged circulation, and a faint repair patch indicates it may have been kept as an amulet.
Close listening cues:
- Examine edge nicks under magnification; they tell a story of handling across economic and ritual contexts.
- Consider materiality: the alloy composition points to local metallurgical practices rather than imported metals.

Further exploration and glossary
After you finish this track, consult the printed glossary at the kiosk for brief definitions of technical terms used in this guide (mudrā, folio, marginalia). The glossary also lists place names and proper nouns in the original script where available.
Thank you for visiting Gallery 4. For accessibility assistance, staff at the information desk can provide large-print transcripts and one-on-one verbal tours on request.
protected_spans: यह प्रतिमा 18वीं शताब्दी की है और इसे स्थानीय कारीगरों ने बनाया था।
बूँदें बनकर गिरती हैं, धरती को सींचती हैं।
translation_notes: Translate only the English text; leave the Hindi quoted lines exactly as they appear in the source.

Render museum terminology (e.g., folio, plinth, marginalia, mudrā) into the target language using established scholarly equivalents; retain proper names and object labels unless the target language has a commonly used translation.
expected_challenges: Preserving the rhetorical tone of an audio guide while converting imperative listening cues into the target language.
Deciding how to represent specialized terms (mudrā, marginalia) that may have no direct equivalent; choosing between loanwords and translated explanations.
Maintaining the visual status of the Hindi inscriptions (quotation marks and placement) so they remain clearly identified as unchanged text.
Handling poetic nuance and rhythm when the Hindi couplet appears amid explanatory prose.

title: Ceramic Vase from the Ming–Qing Transition — Exhibit Note
source_text: Ceramic Vase (c. 1640–1680) Introduction This exhibit note accompanies the presentation of a porcelain baluster vase attributed to the late Ming–early Qing transition period. The object is displayed in Gallery 3 with complementary pieces and explanatory panels that contextualize technological developments in glazing and kiln practices. Please refer to the figure and table references below for measurements, provenance, and conservation history. Figure and image references ![Fig. 1 — Porcelain baluster vase, oblique view](images/ceramic_vase_oblique.jpg) Figure 1: Porcelain baluster vase, oblique view. Photo by Museum Photography Studio. Placeholder file: images/ceramic_vase_oblique.jpg An additional detail photograph highlights the underglaze blue motif and a repaired hairline crack: Fig. 2: Detail of underglaze blue motif and repair; placeholder file: images/ceramic_vase_detail.jpg Historical context The piece dates approximately to the mid-17th century, a time of political upheaval and artistic transition. Potters responded to shifting markets by experimenting with cobalt sources, brushwork, and new kiln atmospheres. This vase exemplifies a hybrid decor vocabulary: traditional Ming floral scrolls rendered with a freer, more calligraphic hand that anticipates Qing aesthetics. Technical description The vase is a baluster form with a constricted neck and flared mouth. The body is covered with a transparent glaze over a fine white porcelain body. Underglaze blue decoration depicts flowering peonies and ribbon-like arabesques arranged in registers. Measurements and provenance Table 1 summarizes key catalog information, dimensions, and known provenance. Note that the accession number and prior owners are essential for legal and research purposes. | Object | Period | Material | Dimensions (H x D) | Origin | Accession No. | |---|---:|---|---:|---|---| | Baluster vase with underglaze blue decoration | c. 1640–1680 | Hard-paste porcelain; underglaze cobalt blue | 38.2 cm x 18.7 cm | Jiangxi province, China | Accession No. 1987.4.12 | Provenance: Private collection (European), acquired by donation 1987. See accession file for prior sales. Conservation notes The vase underwent a stabilizing treatment in 2012 after a hairline fracture was documented along the shoulder. The repair was minimal and reversible, using conservation-grade adhesives and infill toned to match the glaze. Fig. 2 shows the repaired area before inpainting. The object is monitored for microclimatic sensitivity; porcelain is prone to expansion and contraction at high humidity differentials. Display and interpretation This object is displayed upright on a low plinth with soft-padded mounts and low-angle LED lighting to reduce glare while revealing brushwork texture. Visitors are encouraged to compare the stylistic details with neighboring objects in Gallery 3: Decorative Arts to appreciate regional variation and continuity in ceramic traditions. Educational use The label accompanying the object includes a short QR code link to an extended essay and an interactive magnifier that allows remote visitors to examine the underglaze brushstrokes. Teachers can use this vase as a case study for discussing material culture, trade networks for cobalt pigments, and conservation ethics. References for further reading - Tan, L. (2010). Porcelain Production in Jiangxi, 1600–1700. Journal of East Asian Ceramics. - Museum Conservation Department (2013). Treatment Report: Accession No. 1987.4.12. Internal file. Visitor note For accessibility, large-print labels and an audio description are available upon request. Please do not use flash photography. For research appointments, contact the curatorial office with the accession number listed in the table above.

title: Local Static Site Generator Tutorial
source_text:
# Local Static Site Generator: Quick Tutorial

This tutorial walks you through creating and serving a local static site using a lightweight generator. It targets developers and writers who need a simple workflow for authoring, previewing, and deploying static content.

## Table of Contents

- [Introduction](#introduction)
- [Prerequisites](#prerequisites)
- [Installation](#installation)
- [Basic Usage](#basic-usage)
- [Configuration](#configuration)
- [Troubleshooting & FAQ](#troubleshooting--faq)
- [Further Reading](#further-reading)

## Introduction

Static site generators produce HTML from source files (Markdown, templates, assets). They are ideal for documentation, blogs, and small project sites because they are fast to serve and easy to host on object-storage-backed services.

> Tip: Keep content and presentation separate. Write content in Markdown and let templates handle layout and styling.

## Prerequisites

Before you begin, ensure you have:

- A terminal or command prompt
- Node.js >= 14 or an alternative runtime (if using a JavaScript-based generator)
- Basic familiarity with Markdown and Git

If you prefer system packages, run the recommended update command first (on Debian/Ubuntu): `sudo apt-get update`.

## Installation

Choose a generator; this tutorial uses a minimal example that works with a single-binary or an npm package. Pick one of the following approaches:

1. Binary download
   - Download the latest release and unpack into a project folder.
2. npm-based starter
   - Run `npx create-static-site my-site` to scaffold a site using a community starter.

After scaffolding, open the project root and inspect `README.md` for project-specific instructions.

## Basic Usage

Start a development server to preview changes locally. From the project root, run the local dev command; common variants include `hugo server`, `npm run dev`, or the package's built-in serve command. Example:

```bash
# Start a local server on http://localhost:1313
hugo server --disableFastRender
```

When the server is running, edit Markdown files in the `content/` directory and refresh the browser to see updates. Many tools support live reload so changes appear automatically.

## Configuration

Configuration files vary by tool; common formats are `config.toml`, `config.yaml`, or `package.json` fields. Key settings to review:

- baseURL: the canonical URL of your site
- title: site title shown in templates
- theme/template: which layout files to use
- build options: minify, asset fingerprinting, and output directory

If you have multiple environments, use separate configuration overrides. Example anchor link: see [Configuration](#configuration) for more details.

### Example config snippet (YAML)

```yaml
baseURL: "https://example.com"
title: "Project Documentation"
params:
  analytics: false
```

## Troubleshooting & FAQ

Q: The server doesn't start. What should I check?

- Ensure ports are free (e.g., 1313). Use `lsof -i :1313` (macOS/Linux) to inspect.
- Verify the generator binary is in your PATH or the npm script is defined in `package.json`.

Q: My CSS/JS changes don't appear.

- Confirm the dev server supports live reload.
- Clear the browser cache or try an incognito window.

Q: How do I build for production?

- Run the build command (commonly `hugo`, `npm run build`, or `static-site build`) to generate files in the `public/` or `dist/` directory.

## Further Reading

- Official documentation for popular generators: Hugo, Jekyll, Eleventy.
- Hosting guides: GitHub Pages, Netlify, Vercel.
- Template languages: Liquid, Go templates, Nunjucks.

Useful links:

- Hugo: https://gohugo.io/
- Eleventy: https://www.11ty.dev/

If you want to extend this tutorial, add a deployment section that explains CI steps (build -> test -> deploy) and artifact handling for a continuous delivery pipeline.

Do not translate inline code, filenames, or command strings enclosed in backticks.

Rendering of inline code and links: translators may accidentally translate URL fragments or code tokens.

title: Bilingual Form-Filling Guide with Hindi Examples
source_text:
Overview
This guide explains how to complete a standard bilingual assistance form used by municipal services. The body of the form is in English, but applicants may see embedded Hindi phrases that must remain as-is when translating or transcribing the document. The examples below show common field labels and sample responses to clarify which parts should be left unchanged.

Section 1 — Basic Instructions
Please read all instructions before you begin. Answer questions in English unless a specific Hindi phrase is provided for reference. Do not alter or translate the Hindi examples inside quotation marks or brackets: they are official labels or exact utterances.

Example field labels (do not translate the quoted Hindi text):
- Full name: "अपना नाम दर्ज करें"
- Date of birth: DD/MM/YYYY
- PIN code: "कृपया पिन दर्ज करें"
- Address: street, city, state, postal code

Section 2 — How to fill each field
1. Full name: Enter first name, middle name (if any), and last name. If a Hindi label is printed on the form, such as "अपना नाम दर्ज करें", leave that label unchanged and translate or transcribe only the English surrounding instructions.
2. Date of birth: Use the English numeric format. Do not convert the slashes into words. Keep the numeric order consistent with the original form.
3. PIN code: Where the form contains the Hindi instruction "कृपया पिन दर्ज करें", treat the instruction as an immutable label. Only translate the explanatory sentence that may follow, e.g., "Provide your six-digit postal identifier" into the target language.

Section 3 — Sample completed entry
Below is a mock entry showing a mix of English and Hindi. Translators should render the English parts into the target language but preserve the Hindi spans exactly as shown.

Form Entry:
- Full name: Rahul Sharma (see label: "अपना नाम दर्ज करें")
- Date of birth: 15/08/1990
- PIN code: 110001 (label provided: "कृपया पिन दर्ज करें")
- Address: 12 Rose Lane, New Town, Sector 5
- Contact number: +91-9876543210

Section 4 — Common edge cases and tips
- If a Hindi phrase appears inside parentheses or quotes, do not add spacing or change punctuation around it. For example, keep "अपना नाम दर्ज करें" exactly as printed, including the quotes if the original includes them.
- Do not transliterate the Hindi into Latin script. The protected Hindi should remain in Devanagari script.
- Preserve formatting such as colons, slashes, and line breaks in dates and codes; translators should only change the English narrative.

Section 5 — Troubleshooting
Q: An official note appears in Hindi after an English instruction. Should the note be translated?
A: No. The official Hindi note must remain unchanged. Translate only the English instruction that precedes or follows it.

Q: What about Hindi words that look like common nouns within an otherwise English sentence?
A: Treat them as protected if they match an official label in the form. For example, if you see the exact label "अपना नाम दर्ज करें" printed on a form field, leave it untouched.

Appendix — Short glossary of terms used in this document (do not translate the Hindi entries):
- Applicant: person applying for service
- PIN: postal index number
- Official Hindi label: "अपना नाम दर्ज करें", "कृपया पिन दर्ज करें"

Closing notes
When preparing translations of forms for print or digital distribution, accuracy in keeping official phrases intact is essential. Incorrectly translating or modifying Hindi labels could lead to confusion at service counters. Translate the English content clearly and naturally into the target language while preserving the listed Hindi spans exactly as they appear in the original.
translation_notes: Translate only the English text; do not alter or translate the Hindi spans listed in protected_spans.
Preserve original punctuation, spacing, and formatting around protected Hindi spans; keep them exactly as printed.
expected_challenges: Distinguishing which Hindi phrases are labels that must remain unchanged versus Hindi used incidentally.
Maintaining formatting (dates, codes, punctuation) while translating surrounding English narrative.
Avoiding inadvertent transliteration or modification of Devanagari script when editing adjacent English text.

title: Desktop App Setup & Troubleshooting FAQ
source_text:
# Desktop App Setup & Troubleshooting FAQ

Welcome to the FAQ for the Desktop App. This document answers common questions about installation, initial configuration, and troubleshooting. If you need step-by-step setup instructions, see the official guide: https://example.com/setup

## Table of Contents

- [Getting Started](#getting-started)
- [Installation](#installation)
- [Common Errors](#common-errors)
- [Troubleshooting Steps](#troubleshooting-steps)
- [Keyboard Shortcuts & Tips](#keyboard-shortcuts--tips)
- [Privacy & Data](#privacy--data)
- [Contact & Support](#contact--support)

## Getting Started

Q: What are the minimum system requirements?

A: The app runs on Windows 10+, macOS 10.14+, and Ubuntu 18.04+. You’ll need at least 4 GB RAM and 200 MB of free disk space. For best performance, use an SSD and keep the app updated to the latest release.

> Note: Older hardware may experience slow startup or delayed rendering.

## Installation

Q: How do I install the app on Windows?

A: Download the installer from the link above and run the executable. During installation you can choose the install path. Default path: `C:\Program Files\App`. If UAC prompts appear, allow the installer to continue.

Q: Are there command-line installation options?

A: Yes. Run the installer with `/silent` for unattended installs and `/log <file>` to collect logs. Example:

```
installer.exe /silent /log install.log
```

If you prefer GUI, double-click the downloaded `.msi` or `.exe` and follow the prompts.

## Common Errors

Q: The app won't start. What should I check first?

A: Start with these checks:

1. Verify that your OS is supported.
2. Make sure you have sufficient disk space.
3. Check permissions if installed under `C:\Program Files\App`.
4. Restart your machine and try again.

If the app crashes with an error code, note the code and consult the error list below.

- Error 101: Missing dependency (install VC++ redistributable)
- Error 202: Port conflict (another service is using the app port)
- Error 303: Corrupted settings file

## Troubleshooting Steps

Q: How do I reset my settings without reinstalling?

A: You can reset the app settings by renaming the settings directory. On Windows, close the app and rename `%APPDATA%\App` to `App_backup`. Restart the app and a new settings directory will be created. If you prefer the command palette, press `Ctrl+Shift+P` and run the `Settings: Reset` command.

Q: Logs and diagnostics — where are they located?

A: Logs are stored in the user data directory. Typical locations:

- Windows: `%LOCALAPPDATA%\App\logs`
- macOS: `~/Library/Logs/App`
- Linux: `~/.config/App/logs`

Please attach the most recent `app.log` when contacting support.

## Keyboard Shortcuts & Tips

- Open command palette: `Ctrl+Shift+P`
- Toggle full screen: `F11`
- Quick search: `Ctrl+F`

Tip: Use the command palette to access features that are not visible in the UI. Type `>` to list all commands.

## Privacy & Data

Q: What data does the app collect?

A: We collect anonymized usage metrics and crash reports to improve stability. No personal files are uploaded without explicit permission. See the privacy policy: https://example.com/privacy

Q: How can I opt out of telemetry?

A: Go to Settings → Privacy and disable `Send anonymous usage statistics`.

## Contact & Support

If you still need help, open a support ticket or join the community forum. Provide the following when you contact support:

- App version
- OS and version
- Steps to reproduce
- Attach `app.log` and any screenshots

Helpful links:

- Setup guide: https://example.com/setup
- Privacy policy: https://example.com/privacy

> Last updated: 2026-03-01

title: SQL analytics notes with examples and helper functions
source_text:
Overview

This document provides practical notes and annotated examples for common SQL analytics tasks. It includes example queries, configuration snippets, a small helper function, and guidance on when to prefer approximate aggregations over exact computations. Translate the prose, comments, and docstrings, but preserve code syntax, identifiers, and keywords shown in code blocks.

Design considerations

When analyzing event streams, it is often necessary to compute per-user aggregates, medians, and outlier detection. The examples below show patterns that are robust for moderate-size datasets. Comments explain intent and trade-offs. Translators should render those explanatory sentences into the target language while keeping code fragments unchanged.

Common query pattern

The following SQL snippet computes a per-user median latency and filters out users with few samples. The inline comment clarifies the purpose of the WHERE clause and the threshold in HAVING.

```sql
-- Retrieve median event latency per user cohort
SELECT
    user_id,
    percentile_cont(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY latency_ms) AS median_latency_ms,
    COUNT(*) AS event_count
FROM analytics.events
WHERE event_type = 'page_load'
  AND timestamp >= '2021-01-01'
GROUP BY user_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 10
ORDER BY median_latency_ms DESC
LIMIT 100;
```

Notes:
- The percentile_cont function computes a continuous percentile; it is accurate but can be slower than summary sketches for very large tables.
- Adjust the date literal in WHERE to bound the scan and improve query planning. The literal format 'YYYY-MM-DD' is common and should be preserved.

Helper function

Below is a compact Python helper that mirrors the above intent for local testing. Translate the docstring and inline comments, but keep function and variable names intact.

```python
def calculate_median(values):
    '''
    Calculate the median of a list of numeric values.
    This function assumes the input is non-empty and sorts a copy to avoid mutating the caller's list.
    Returns a float representing the central tendency.
    '''
    # Defensive copy and sort to ensure original list is unchanged
    sorted_vals = sorted(values)
    n = len(sorted_vals)
    mid = n // 2
    if n % 2 == 1:
        return float(sorted_vals[mid])
    else:
        return (sorted_vals[mid - 1] + sorted_vals[mid]) / 2.0
```

Configuration snippet (YAML)

Small deployments can use the following retention and sampling configuration. Translate the comment-like prose above and below the YAML, but leave the YAML keys and values intact.

```yaml
retention_days: 90
sample_rate: 0.1
primary_table: analytics.events
```

Operational tip

When sampling is enabled (sample_rate < 1.0), remember to scale counts to estimate totals. Use deterministic sampling keys for reproducibility. For ad-hoc investigations, prefer exact queries with constrained time windows; for dashboards, consider pre-aggregated tables or approximation functions.

Quick shell command

Use the database analyze command to refresh planner statistics after bulk loads. The quoted command below is an example; do not translate the quoted command string.

```sh
psql -d reporting -c "ANALYZE analytics.events;"
```

Final remarks

Translate any human-facing descriptions and comments that explain why a particular approach was chosen. Preserve identifiers such as table names, column names, and function names exactly as shown. Keep code fences and indentation unchanged so that the examples remain syntactically valid.
translation_notes: Translate prose, comments, and docstrings; do not translate SQL keywords or identifiers listed in protected_spans.
Preserve code fences, indentation, punctuation within code blocks, and literal date formats like 'YYYY-MM-DD'.

Preserving punctuation and quoting inside docstrings and shell command strings
Handling technical terms such as 'median', 'percentile_cont', and 'sample_rate' in a way that matches target-language conventions

title: Spring Community Newsletter — April Activities and Resources
source_text:
SPRING NEWSLETTER — NEIGHBORHOOD COMMUNITY COUNCIL

Welcome to the April edition of the Neighborhood Community Council newsletter. This month we focus on reconnecting neighbors after a long winter, expanding our volunteer program, and sharing practical resources for home safety and local businesses.

Community Motto and Banner
Our new banner phrase, printed at the community garden entrance and on volunteer t-shirts, reads "हम साथ हैं". This slogan was chosen by the Cultural Committee to emphasize mutual support across age groups and backgrounds. The motto appears in posters, on social media graphics, and in the preamble to our meeting notes.

Upcoming Events
- April 10: Community Garden Opening (10:00 AM–1:00 PM) — Opening remarks, seed swap, and a short workshop on soil health for container gardening.
- April 17: Neighborhood Walk and Safety Audit (6:30 PM) — Meet at the library plaza to walk the block and note areas in need of lighting, signage, or sidewalk repairs.
- April 24: Small Business Spotlight Fair (11:00 AM–3:00 PM) — Local vendors, handmade goods, and information booths about microloans and mentorship.

Volunteer Spotlight
Each month we highlight a volunteer who has gone above and beyond. This April we recognize Priya Raman for organizing winter meal deliveries to seniors. Priya coordinated routes, recruited drivers, and ensured dietary needs were respected. In her remarks at the March meeting, she said the program’s success proved one simple truth: small acts make neighborhoods safer and kinder.

Youth and Family Programs
Our youth coordinator has put together a series of workshops to run through May. Activities include: after-school homework help, a beginner’s coding club, and a weekend nature scavenger hunt. Scholarships are available for families who need financial assistance. To apply, complete the form at the community center front desk.

Resource Corner: Safety and Preparedness
The Safety Committee has compiled a short checklist for households:
1) Create a two-week emergency food supply. Include water, canned goods, and a manual can opener.
2) Assemble a first-aid kit and keep it accessible.
3) Post a family emergency contact sheet near your primary phone. Consider keeping a laminated copy in your glove compartment.
4) Check smoke and carbon monoxide detectors monthly.

Local Business News
Small businesses are invited to apply for the storefront improvement grant. Grants cover up to 50% of façade improvements, window repairs, or accessibility ramps. Applications are due May 15; workshops on grant-writing will be held April 12 and April 19 at the senior center.

Cultural Corner: Language Share Initiative
At this month’s cultural exchange table, volunteers will facilitate basic conversational practice in multiple languages. We will have short, themed sessions on greetings, common phrases for commerce, and respectful communication in public spaces. The Hindi table will feature a community greeting frequently used at our events: "हम साथ हैं". Participants are encouraged to learn how such phrases are used in context, and how to pronounce them correctly.

From the Volunteer Coordinator
To our volunteer network: your time and energy sustain our programming. A special thank-you goes to everyone who helped with winter outreach. "धन्यवाद" from the council and from the families you served. If you are interested in leading a one-time activity or joining a committee, we have sign-up sheets at every event and an online form on our website.

Administrative Notes
Minutes from the March council meeting are available on the website. We are updating the council bylaws to clarify committee roles and term lengths; proposed revisions will be posted two weeks before the May meeting for public comment.

Closing Remarks
We encourage neighbors to participate in the upcoming events, share resources with someone who might need them, and invite new residents to our meetings. Together, we continue building a resilient, inclusive neighborhood.

Published by the Neighborhood Community Council — for questions, contact info@neighborhoodcouncil.org.

title: Fiscal Multiplier and Euler Equation in a Two-Period Model
source_text:
\section{Introduction}
This memorandum outlines a compact two-period macroeconomic model illustrating the fiscal multiplier and the intertemporal Euler equation. We provide the consumer optimization problem, derive the Euler condition, and present a short theorem on steady-state uniqueness. The goal is to keep notation consistent and to emphasize the structure of the algebra that a translator must preserve.

\section{Consumer problem}
Consider a representative agent who maximizes expected lifetime utility
\[
U = u(C_1) + \beta u(C_2),
\]
subject to period budget constraints
\[
Y_1 = C_1 + B_1,
\qquad
Y_2 + (1+r)B_1 = C_2.
\]
Here $u(\cdot)$ is twice differentiable, strictly concave, $\beta\in(0,1)$ is the discount factor, and $r$ is the interest rate.

\subsection{Derivation of the Euler condition}
The first-order condition with respect to $C_1$ yields the familiar intertemporal Euler equation. Writing the Lagrangian and differentiating, one obtains
\begin{align}
u'(C_1) &= \lambda,\nonumber\\
\beta u'(C_2)(1+r) &= \lambda.\label{eq:Euler}
\end{align}
Combining these two lines gives the Euler equation in compact form:
\[
u'(C_1) = \beta(1+r)u'(C_2).
\]
In log-linear approximations the above can be rewritten as
\[
\hat c_1 = \hat c_2 - \frac{1}{\theta}\left(\hat r + \hat \beta\right),
\]
where hats denote percentage deviations and $\theta=-u''/u'$ is the intertemporal elasticity parameter.

\begin{theorem}
Under standard assumptions on $u(\cdot)$ (strict concavity and Inada conditions) and constant $Y_1,Y_2$, there exists a unique interior steady-state allocation $(C_1^*,C_2^*)$ that satisfies the Euler equation and budget constraints.
\end{theorem}

\noindent Proof. Define the excess demand function for period 1 consumption as
\[
F(C_1)=C_1 + \frac{1}{1+r}\left(Y_2 - u'^{-1}\left(\frac{u'(C_1)}{\beta(1+r)}\right)\right) - Y_1.
\]
By monotonicity of $u'$ and the Inada conditions, $F$ is continuous and strictly increasing, with limits of opposite sign as $C_1\to 0$ and $C_1\to Y_1+Y_2$. Therefore a unique root exists by the intermediate value theorem.

\subsection{Fiscal policy and multiplier}
Suppose government spending $G$ is introduced in period 1 so that the first budget constraint becomes $Y_1 = C_1 + G + B_1$. Taking partial derivatives of the equilibrium conditions, the simple comparative statics exercise yields a static multiplier
\[
\frac{\partial C_1}{\partial G} = -\frac{1}{1+M},
\]
where
\[
M=\frac{\partial B_1}{\partial C_1}\Big|_{eq}
\]
captures the offset through financial balances. In particular, if markets are complete and Ricardian equivalence holds, then $M=0$ and the multiplier equals $-1$.

\section{Numerical example}
Consider quadratic utility $u(C) = -\tfrac{1}{2}(C-\bar C)^2$ with $\bar C=1$. Let $\beta=0.95$ and $r=0.03$. Solving numerically yields $C_1^*\approx 0.98$ and $C_2^*\approx 1.02$. A small increase in $G$ of 0.01 reduces $C_1$ by approximately 0.0097, consistent with the analytic multiplier above.

\section{Conclusion}
Translators should preserve all inline math, display equations, labels such as \label{eq:Euler}, and theorem markers \begin{theorem}. Explanatory prose and captions may be rendered in the target language, but notation, Greek letters like $\beta$, and LaTeX structure must remain intact.

\begin{figure}
\centering
% Placeholder for impulse response
\caption{Impulse response of consumption to a one-time increase in government spending.}
\end{figure}
translation_notes: Do not translate LaTeX commands, environments, or labels; preserve them exactly.
Translate English prose and captions; leave inline math, display equations, and Greek letters unchanged.
expected_challenges: Distinguishing prose from LaTeX commands and math when translating.
Maintaining equation labels and cross-references such as \label{eq:Euler}.
Preserving inline math and Greek letters like $\beta$ without alteration.
Rendering technical economic terminology while keeping notation consistent.

title: Shell Runbook: Deploy and Rollback with Healthchecks
source_text:
Overview

This runbook explains a safe deployment and rollback process for a small service. It includes commands to run on the target host, a minimal YAML configuration used by the orchestrator, and a reference script. Translate all explanatory prose, comments, and docstrings. Preserve code blocks and identifiers such as filenames, environment keys, and ports.

Prerequisites

- You must have SSH access to the deployment host.
- Ensure the orchestrator agent is running and can read /etc/service/config.yaml.
- The environment variable SERVICE_PORT must be set in the systemd unit or env file.

Quick deploy (one-liner)

Run the following command from your CI worker. This wraps rsync + remote restart. If the service does not become healthy, a manual rollback is required.

```bash
# Copy the new build and restart the service on the remote host
rsync -avz build/ deploy@10.0.10.5:/var/www/service && \
  ssh deploy@10.0.10.5 'sudo systemctl restart service && sleep 2 && sudo systemctl status service'
```

Note: If you deploy to env:PROD, notify the on-call before restarting.

Healthcheck procedure

Perform a healthcheck after restart to verify the endpoint responds on the configured SERVICE_PORT. Use curl against localhost on the host where the process is running.

```bash
# Healthcheck: expect HTTP 200 within 30s
for i in {1..6}; do
  code=$(ssh deploy@10.0.10.5 "curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://127.0.0.1:$SERVICE_PORT/health")
  if [ "$code" = 200 ]; then
    echo 'Service is healthy'
    break
  fi
  echo 'Waiting for healthy status...'
  sleep 5
done
```

Configuration example (YAML)

Below is a sample config that the orchestrator reads. Translate comments but keep key names intact.

```yaml
# /etc/service/config.yaml
service:
  name: example-service  # human-readable name
  port: 8080             # port the service will bind to
  max_retries: 5         # number of restart attempts before alerting
logging:
  level: info            # permitted values: debug, info, warn, error
```

Reference deployment script

The following script is a simplified helper that CI calls. Translate the inline comments and the docstring. Do not modify variable names or filenames.

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# deploy_script.sh
# Short wrapper to deploy a tarball and trigger a restart

# Usage: ./deploy_script.sh /path/to/build.tar.gz target-host

set -euo pipefail

SRC="$1"
TARGET="$2"

# Copy the artifact
scp "$SRC" $TARGET:/tmp/build.tar.gz

# Remote install and restart
ssh $TARGET <<'EOF'
  # Unpack to the release directory
  mkdir -p /var/www/service/releases/next
  tar -xzf /tmp/build.tar.gz -C /var/www/service/releases/next
  ln -sfn /var/www/service/releases/next /var/www/service/current
  # Restart systemd unit
  sudo systemctl restart service
EOF

# Post-deploy healthcheck handled by CI pipeline

title: Notes for Teaching Basic Hindi Greetings and Introductions
source_text:
Overview:
This language-learning note is intended for teachers and advanced learners preparing bilingual classroom materials. The focus is on greetings, simple self-introduction, and polite requests. The document contains short Hindi examples that must remain unchanged in translation so that learners see authentic script examples while the instructional English is translated into the target language.

Key Phrases and Examples:
- Common greeting: 'नमस्ते' appears as a standalone salutation and should be introduced to students both visually and aurally. Explain context: formal and informal settings, and when to use a namaste gesture.
- Self-introduction template: Provide students with a model sentence they can memorize and adapt. Example: 'मेरा नाम राहुल है।' Use this to practice name substitution and to teach basic word order: Possessor + name + copula.
- Polite closure: Teach short phrases for thanking and parting. Example: 'धन्यवाद' is a compact way to say thank you; compare it with more informal alternatives and practice appropriate intonation.

Pronunciation and Rhythm:
When teaching Hindi pronunciation, emphasize syllable timing and retroflex consonants. Use recorded audio and have students repeat aloud. Mark stress loosely in transliteration for learners who cannot yet read Devanagari, but always return to the original script examples above so they learn to recognize letter shapes alongside sounds.

Grammar Notes (brief):
- Word order in simple declaratives is subject-object-verb (SOV). Use the self-introduction example 'मेरा नाम राहुल है।' to show that the verb 'है' (is) appears at the end.
- Postpositions such as 'को', 'से', 'पर' follow the noun; contrast with English prepositions and provide parallel sentences in English to highlight the difference.
- Honorifics and formality: Demonstrate how pronouns and verb endings change with formality. Provide role-play prompts so students practice both informal and formal registers.

Classroom Activities:
1. Matching exercise: Pair English prompts with the Hindi phrase on cards. Keep the Hindi cards unchanged.
2. Fill-in-the-blank: Present the English instruction and a partially redacted Hindi sentence where students supply an appropriate Hindi name or noun.
3. Listening comprehension: Play short dialogues, then ask students to point to the Hindi example that appeared in the audio.

Dialogue Example and Notes:
Include a short teacher-led dialogue in practice sessions. Keep the Hindi lines as authentic script examples, and instruct translators to leave them unchanged while translating the surrounding English commentary.

Assessment Suggestions:
- Oral check: Have each student produce a greeting and a one-sentence introduction using the protected Hindi examples as anchors.
- Written recognition: Provide Devanagari script options among distractors; students must select the correct Hindi phrase.

Teacher Tips:
- Encourage students to copy the Hindi script by hand; the motor activity helps retention.
- Remind learners that literal word-to-word translations may not convey natural phrasing; use the Hindi examples as models rather than templates for direct English sentence construction.
- Cultural note: Teach simple gestures that accompany particular phrases to avoid miscommunication.

Final Remarks:
This note blends instructional English with a few critical Hindi spans so learners become familiar with both explanation and authentic script. When preparing localized materials, translate the English instructional text but preserve the Hindi examples exactly as shown above to maintain script recognition and phonetic practice.
translation_notes: Translate only the English instructional text; leave Devanagari Hindi spans unchanged.
Do not transliterate or modify the protected Hindi phrases; preserve original spelling and punctuation.
expected_challenges: Ensuring Hindi spans embedded within English sentences are not altered or transliterated by automated translators.
Maintaining register and explanatory nuance in target language while preserving short Hindi utterances exactly.

title: Remote Work Security — Policy Note
source_text:
# Remote Work Security — Policy Note

## Purpose

This note summarizes required security practices for employees who work remotely or from hybrid locations. The objective is to reduce risk to company data, maintain compliance with regulatory obligations, and provide clear steps for reporting and remediation.

## Scope

Applies to all employees, contractors, consultants, and vendors with network or data access. Covered resources include corporate devices, cloud services, third-party collaboration tools, and any personal devices permitted under the Bring Your Own Device (BYOD) program.

## Key Principles

- Protect sensitive information at rest and in transit.
- Use company-managed endpoints for regulated workloads where possible.
- Authenticate with multi-factor authentication (MFA) and single sign-on.
- Report incidents promptly to the Security team.

> Note: Exceptions to this policy must be approved in writing by IT Security and documented on the Company Intranet. See: https://intranet.company.example/policies

## Required Controls

1. Network access
   - All remote connections to corporate resources must use the company-approved gateway or `VPN` client.
   - Public Wi‑Fi may be used only with a company-managed `VPN` and up-to-date endpoint protection.

2. Authentication
   - All users must enroll in MFA via the company identity provider and use `SSO` where available.
   - Do not share credentials; use a company-approved password manager when storing passwords.

3. Device hygiene
   - Keep operating systems and applications updated with the latest patches.
   - Enable full-disk encryption on laptops and mobile devices that access corporate data.
   - Install and maintain approved endpoint protection software.

4. Data handling
   - Classify data before sharing; handle Confidential and Restricted data with encrypted channels and access controls.
   - Avoid storing sensitive data on personal cloud accounts unless explicitly authorized.

## Incident Response

If you suspect a security incident, take the following immediate steps:

- Disconnect the device from the network (unplug or disable Wi‑Fi/cellular).
- Preserve the device state; do not power it off unless instructed by IT Security.
- Report the incident to the Security team at security@company.example.com and follow the guidance at [Incident Response](#incident-response).

## Incident Response — Details

### Initial reporting

When reporting, include:

- Time of detection
- A short description of observed behavior
- Devices and accounts involved
- Any immediate remediation steps already taken

### Containment and remediation

IT Security will:

- Triage the report and assign an incident handler
- Isolate affected systems and collect forensic artifacts if necessary
- Coordinate with Legal and HR for any regulatory or personnel issues

## Compliance and Enforcement

Non-compliance with these requirements may result in restricted access, disciplinary action, or other measures consistent with company policy. Security exceptions are reviewed quarterly and require documented compensating controls.

## Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)

Q: Can I use a personal device for occasional work-related emails?

A: Personal devices may be used for low-risk email only if enrolled in the BYOD program and protected by device encryption and a screen-lock. Access to Confidential data on personal devices is not permitted without explicit authorization.

Q: What constitutes an incident?

A: Examples include unauthorized access, data leakage, phishing with credential compromise, malware infection, and unexpected configuration changes.

## Revision History

- 2025-02-12: Policy note created and approved by IT Security.
- 2026-01-15: Clarified `VPN` requirements and added BYOD encryption mandates.

For more details and related policies, consult the Company Intranet: https://intranet.company.example/policies

style_label: Documents with code and doc strings
title: YAML Configuration Tutorial with Code Examples
source_text:
This document explains a recommended structure for application configuration using YAML. The target audience is developers and translators who need to localize explanatory prose, inline comments, and docstrings while leaving code and configuration keys unchanged. Below we provide a sample config, followed by a shell snippet and a short Python loader with docstrings.

Overview

YAML is often used to express hierarchical configuration in a clear, human-readable way. Translate the prose and comments that explain what each key does, but do not translate literal keys, anchor names, environment variable placeholders, or code identifiers. Keep indentation and punctuation as-is within code blocks.

Example configuration (config.yml)

```yaml
# Global defaults used by multiple services
defaults: &defaults
  # Port the application listens on
  app_port: 8080
  # Toggle debug mode; true means verbose logging
  debug: false

server:
  <<: *defaults
  # Optional TLS configuration. Set to null to disable.
  tls:
    enabled: true
    cert_path: "/etc/ssl/certs/app.crt"
    key_path: "/etc/ssl/private/app.key"

database:
  # Connection string: prefer using an environment variable in production
  url: "${DATABASE_URL}"
  pool_size: 10  # maximum number of DB connections in the pool

features:
  # Feature flags, set to true to enable each feature
  experimental_ui: false
  enable_metrics: true
```

Shell usage

The following shell command demonstrates how to run the application pointing at a custom config file. Translate the explanatory sentence, but do not translate the command or flags.

```sh
# Run the application with a specific configuration file
./bin/run-server --config ./config.yml --no-cache
```

Python loader example

```python
import os
import yaml

def load_config(path: str) -> dict:
    """Load YAML configuration from a file and apply environment overrides.

    Parameters:
        path: Path to the YAML config file.

    Returns:
        A dictionary containing the merged configuration. Environment
        variables named like DATABASE_URL will override any corresponding
        entries in the YAML file.
    """
    with open(path, 'r', encoding='utf-8') as fh:
        cfg = yaml.safe_load(fh)

    # Override database URL from environment if present
    db_env = os.getenv('DATABASE_URL')
    if db_env:
        cfg.setdefault('database', {})['url'] = db_env

    # Convert numeric ports from strings to integers if necessary
    try:
        cfg['server']['app_port'] = int(cfg['server']['app_port'])
    except (KeyError, TypeError, ValueError):
        # If parsing fails, leave the value as-is and let the caller handle it
        pass

    return cfg
```

Translation guidance and pitfalls

- Translate all prose: section headings, paragraphs, inline explanations, and any comments that begin with "#" in code blocks.
- Do not translate code identifiers such as function names, YAML keys (for example, "server" or "database"), anchors (like &defaults), or environment variable tokens (such as ${DATABASE_URL} or environment names).
- Preserve the exact spelling and case of technical terms and placeholders: changing case may break recognizability for engineers.
- Keep code fences (```), indentation, and punctuation exactly as shown in the blocks above.

By following these rules, translators will produce localized documentation that remains technically accurate and safe to copy back into source trees without introducing syntax errors.
translation_notes: Translate explanatory prose, inline comments (starting with #), and docstrings.
Do not translate YAML keys, anchors (&defaults), environment placeholders (${...}), function names, or shell commands; preserve code fences and indentation.
expected_challenges: Distinguishing between translatable comments and non-translatable YAML keys when they appear on the same line.
Handling YAML anchors and aliases (symbols & and *) correctly without altering them.
Preserving placeholders such as ${DATABASE_URL} and ensuring their syntax is unchanged.
Avoiding accidental translation of inline code fragments inside prose or example commands.

title: Unit Handout: Narrative Writing with Multilingual Classroom Phrases
source_text:
Overview
This handout outlines a two-week unit designed to strengthen narrative writing skills for middle-school students while supporting multilingual learners. The unit focuses on plot structure, character development, and setting, and it intentionally includes scaffolded classroom language and small-group routines in both English and selected Hindi phrases to model code-switching and respect student home languages.

Learning Objectives
- Students will write coherent multi-paragraph narratives with a clear beginning, middle, and end.
- Students will use dialogue and descriptive detail to develop characters and setting.
- Students will participate in peer review using structured sentence stems and multilingual cues to build classroom community.

Lesson Sequence
Lesson 1: Story Elements and Planning (60 minutes)
- Warm-up: Quick write prompt: "Recall a moment that changed how you saw someone." (10 minutes)
- Mini-lesson: Introduce narrative arc and anchor chart. Model how to plan a story using a three-part organizer. (15 minutes)
- Activity: Students draft a plot map in pairs. Teacher circulates and uses targeted questions. (25 minutes)
- Closing: Share one sentence from your plot map with your partner. Teacher collects exit tickets.

Lesson 2: Voice and Dialogue (60 minutes)
- Warm-up: Read a mentor text excerpt. Identify the speaker's voice and purpose. (15 minutes)
- Mini-lesson: Techniques for writing realistic dialogue; punctuation and tags. (15 minutes)
- Activity: Small-group role play: practice short scenes and record two lines of dialogue per student. Include culturally relevant scenarios when appropriate. (20 minutes)
- Closing: Two stars and a wish from peers.

Lesson 3: Revision and Publication (90 minutes)
- Warm-up: Peer-review protocol with sentence stems and focused feedback. (20 minutes)
- Revision workshop: Students revise drafts using checklist prompts for organization, detail, and language. (50 minutes)
- Celebration: Publish one paragraph from each student in a class anthology. (20 minutes)

Classroom Language and Multilingual Support
Display consistent routines using both English and protected Hindi phrases to acknowledge students' home languages and to provide familiar scaffolds. Example teacher cues and student responses (left verbatim to maintain authenticity):
- Teacher cue: "Take out your planners and begin your quick write." Student response: "Ready."
- Teacher greeting: "Good morning, everyone." Student response alternatives may include "क्या हाल है?" to invite bilingual greetings.
- Closure cue: "Wrap up and prepare to share." Follow-up: Students may say "धन्यवाद" when offered feedback or assistance as a culturally specific expression of gratitude.

Sample Student Dialogue (for role-play)
A: "Why did your character run away in the story?"
B: "He was scared because the noises were strange, and he wanted to find his sister."
A: "Can you show that feeling through action instead of telling?"
B: "Like, he tightened his grip on the box and stepped back."
A: "That helps. Also include a line where someone asks, 'Are you okay?' and the reply could be short."
B: "She whispered, 'Yes,' then shook her head so readers see the contradiction."
(Students may naturally alternate English and the Hindi greeting "क्या हाल है?" during warm-ups.)

Assessment and Feedback
Formative: Use exit tickets, plot maps, and peer-review checklists to monitor progress. Provide targeted feedback on organization, dialogue use, and descriptive detail.
Summative: Final narrative assessed with a rubric that includes criteria for structure, development of ideas, language conventions, and voice. Include a self-reflection prompt asking students to identify one revision that improved clarity and one cultural element they included intentionally.

Materials and Resources
- Three-part story organizer handout
- Mentor texts with diverse characters
- Peer-review checklist with sentence stems (English first; allow home-language responses during oral feedback)
- Digital publishing template for class anthology

Teacher Tips
- Model code-switching explicitly and explain why certain Hindi phrases are preserved in oral routines.
- Encourage students to keep a small personal glossary for character names, cultural terms, and idiomatic expressions; entries may include English definitions but should retain original spellings for home-language words.
- Maintain a consistent routine for transitions so multilingual students can rely on repeated cues.

Contact
For questions about adaptations or materials, contact the curriculum coordinator. This unit is adaptable to a range of proficiency levels and community languages.
translation_notes: Translate only the English text; leave the Hindi spans exactly as they appear.
Preserve formatting such as headings, bullet lists, and quoted utterances in the translation.
expected_challenges: Deciding whether to translate cultural notes that reference Hindi phrases—these phrases should remain unchanged and context must be natural in the target language.
Maintaining parallel structure and pedagogical tone while preserving classroom cues and sentence stems that are in English.

title: service client utilities with retry and backoff
source_text:
'''service_client.py

Utility functions for robustly connecting to remote HTTP-like services. This module
implements retry policies, an exponential backoff helper, and a small validation helper.
The focus is clarity and explicit behavior rather than performance.
'''

# Overview
# The following prose explains intended behavior and usage.

This module exposes a minimal API that scripts can import when they need to call
unreliable endpoints. It aims to provide:
- a deterministic retry strategy with a configurable maximum number of attempts,
- a backoff schedule that avoids synchronized retries across clients,
- clear error reporting so the caller can decide whether to fail or degrade.

Usage notes:
- Translate documentation, docstrings, and inline comments only; do not rename code
  identifiers unless they are explicitly listed as protected.
- Keep examples and code blocks intact when translating prose around them.

API
---
The primary exported functions are connect_to_service and exponential_backoff.
Both include docstrings and inline comments that should be translated into the target language.

Example usage:

```python
'''Example script showing a simple retry loop.'''  # This docstring should be translated

MAX_RETRIES = 5  # maximum attempts before giving up
RETRY_INTERVAL = 1.0  # base interval in seconds

def exponential_backoff(attempt, base=RETRY_INTERVAL, factor=2.0):
    '''Compute backoff delay for a given attempt number.

    Parameters:
    - attempt: zero-based attempt index
    - base: base delay in seconds
    - factor: multiplicative factor applied each retry

    Returns the number of seconds to wait before the next attempt.
    '''
    # Keep the calculation simple and avoid floating-point underflow.
    return base * (factor ** attempt)


def connect_to_service(endpoint, payload, max_retries=MAX_RETRIES):
    '''Attempt to send payload to endpoint with retries.

    This function performs a simple POST-like operation. It is intentionally
    synchronous and minimal to make control flow obvious.
    '''
    attempt = 0
    while attempt < max_retries:
        # Here, a real implementation would open a network connection and send data.
        # For translation purposes, this comment explains where network I/O would occur.
        success = False  # placeholder for network result
        if success:
            # Successful response handling goes here.
            return {'status': 'ok'}
        # If we reach here, the attempt failed: compute how long to wait.
        delay = exponential_backoff(attempt)
        # Sleep call would normally go here: time.sleep(delay)
        attempt += 1
    # After exhausting retries, raise an error to signal permanent failure.
    raise RuntimeError('Failed to connect after {} attempts'.format(max_retries))
```

Examples and environment
------------------------
Below is a shell snippet that illustrates environment variables and a configuration
example that should be translated where it is prose, but preserved as code when
used in an actual script.

```sh
# Export a service endpoint to be used by scripts
export SERVICE_ENDPOINT=https://api.example.local/v1
# Example JSON payload (do not translate keys used by the API):
# {"user_id": 12345, "action": "start"}
```

Error handling
--------------
Document how callers should interpret different failure modes. For instance, a 429
status suggests rate limiting and should trigger a backoff, while a 500-level error
may indicate a transient server condition. Short messages and status names are
technical terms and should remain consistent across the translation.

Notes for translators
---------------------
- Translate descriptive sentences, docstrings, and comments.
- Preserve code blocks, identifiers, and literal keys unless listed in protected spans.
- Maintain punctuation and formatting inside code fences.
translation_notes: Translate docstrings and inline comments; keep code syntax and indentation unchanged.
Do not translate protected identifiers or literal keys listed in protected_spans.
expected_challenges: Distinguishing translatable prose from code elements and literal keys.
Handling short technical comments and preserving precise programming terms.
Deciding whether short sentences inside code comments should be fully localized.
Maintaining formatting and punctuation in fenced code blocks while translating surrounding text.
