title: Lesson Plan Handout: Introduction to Classroom Routines and Key Vocabulary
source_text:
Course Handout: Classroom Routines and Key Vocabulary

Overview
This handout outlines a 90-minute introductory lesson intended for mixed-language classrooms where English is the language of instruction but students may use Hindi during peer communication. The goal is to model clear routines, introduce essential vocabulary, and practice simple polite commands and responses. The document is designed for instructors who need a reproducible sequence of activities and assessment checkpoints.

Learning Objectives
- Students will be able to follow three basic classroom routines (arrival, participation, dismissal).
- Students will learn and use ten target vocabulary items related to classroom objects and common actions.
- Students will practice two polite commands and appropriate responses in role-play exercises.

Lesson Sequence (90 minutes)
1. Warm-up (10 minutes): Greet students, review previous material, and outline the day's agenda.
2. Presentation (20 minutes): Introduce vocabulary with flashcards and realia; demonstrate routines.
3. Controlled practice (25 minutes): Choral repetition, matching exercises, and partner practice.
4. Role-play (20 minutes): Students perform scripted exchanges that enact classroom routines.
5. Assessment and reflection (15 minutes): Short formative quiz and group reflection.

Sample Scripted Exchanges
- Teacher greeting and seating: Teacher says, "कृपया सब बैठ जाइए।" Pause for students to sit and confirm comprehension. Discuss social expectations: what it means to respond promptly and quietly.
- Student clarification: During pair work, a student might say, "मुझे समझ नहीं आया" to indicate they did not understand. Model a polite peer response and a teacher scaffold that repeats the instruction more slowly.

Materials and Preparation
- Flashcards for vocabulary: desk, chair, notebook, pencil, board, marker, door, window, teacher, student.
- Role-play cards with short scenarios for arrival, asking for help, and dismissal.
- A simple formative quiz (10 items) that combines picture labeling and short sentence completion.

Class Activities
Warm-up: Use a quick round-robin where each student says one word they remember from previous lessons. Instructor notes pronunciation challenges.
Presentation: Display each flashcard, provide an example sentence, and ask students to repeat. For example: "Place your notebook on the desk." Encourage students to use gestures when they repeat.
Controlled practice: Have students match flashcards to pictures on a worksheet. Circulate and note which words require additional emphasis.
Role-play: In pairs, students receive a scenario card and perform an exchange. Provide a model first, then have pairs practice. Record one pair for playback and class feedback.

Assessment
Formative quiz: Ten items, five pictures to label and five sentences to complete. Scoring is binary for each item (correct/incorrect). Use results to identify which vocabulary items need reteaching.
Reflection: End the lesson with a two-minute written reflection: What went well? What was confusing? Offer feedback prompts to guide student responses.

Differentiation
- For beginners: Provide visual cues and allow longer wait time. Use the role-play cards with simplified prompts.
- For advanced students: Ask for extended responses and encourage them to help peers when a request for clarification occurs.

Glossary (instructor reference)
- Arrival routine: the steps students take when entering the classroom (greet, hang bag, sit).
- Polite command example: The teacher may use short, direct phrases; the Hindi example provided above is for demonstration of real classroom language.

Notes for Instructors
- Maintain a consistent routine each class session to build predictability.
- When Hindi is used by students for clarification, model an English paraphrase to strengthen cross-linguistic transfer without correcting natural attempts to communicate.
- Record which vocabulary items students consistently miss and plan a quick recycled activity in the next lesson.

Contact
For further adaptation suggestions or additional role-play cards, contact the curriculum coordinator. This handout is intended to be adapted to specific classroom sizes and age groups.
translation_notes: Translate only the English text; do not alter or translate the Hindi spans listed in protected_spans.
Preserve punctuation, quotation marks, and placement of protected Hindi spans exactly as they appear in the source text.
expected_challenges: Ensuring Hindi spans remain untranslated and correctly integrated into the target-language sentence structure.
Maintaining instructional tone and imperative forms while adapting classroom terms and cultural references.

title: Getting Started with Local Static Site Builds
source_text:
# Getting Started with Local Static Site Builds

This tutorial walks through setting up a simple local workflow to build and preview a static site. It is aimed at translators, technical writers, and developers who need a repeatable process for creating localized documentation. The instructions are deliberately platform-agnostic and use widely available tools.

## Prerequisites

- A Unix-like environment (macOS, Linux, or WSL on Windows).
- A recent version of Git installed and accessible in your `PATH`.
- Node.js v14+ and npm (or yarn) for package management.
- A basic familiarity with the terminal and Markdown.

> Note: If you need Windows-native instructions, adjust commands accordingly and consult your package manager's documentation.

## Quick Setup (5 minutes)

1. Clone the starter repository:

   ```bash
   git clone https://example.com/your-org/static-docs-starter.git
   cd static-docs-starter
   ```

2. Install dependencies:

   ```bash
   npm install
   # or
   yarn install
   ```

3. Start the local dev server:

   ```bash
   npm run dev
   ```

Open your browser at http://localhost:3000 (or the port reported by the server) to preview the site.

## File Structure Overview

- `content/` — Markdown source files organized by language and topic.
- `templates/` — HTML templates and partials.
- `static/` — Images, fonts, and other assets.
- `build.config.js` — Simple build configuration; do not edit unless you understand the pipeline.

### Localizing Content

To add a new language:

1. Create a new directory under `content/`, for example `content/es/`.
2. Copy the relevant Markdown files and translate headings, paragraphs, and lists.
3. Preserve code blocks, inline code, links, and anchors exactly as written in the source.

Example text that should remain unchanged in translation contexts: `git clone`, `npm run build`.

Small multilingual examples may be embedded for testing purposes, such as "¡Hola, mundo!" and "中文示例". These spans are intentionally included verbatim and should be kept exactly when performing substitutions or automated checks.

## Common Tasks

- Build for production:

  ```bash
  npm run build
  ```

- Serve the built site locally:

  ```bash
  npm run serve
  ```

- Check links and anchors:

  Use the built-in link checker: `npm run check-links`. If a relative anchor like `[Jump to Troubleshooting](#troubleshooting)` fails, verify that the heading exists and is correctly formatted.

## Troubleshooting

If the dev server fails to start, consider the following checklist:

- Confirm Node.js and npm versions: `node -v` and `npm -v`.
- Ensure no other process occupies the configured port.
- Run `npm ci` to reinstall a clean dependency tree.

If translations appear clipped or formatting is lost:

- Verify that the translated Markdown preserves block-level elements such as list indentation and fenced code blocks.
- Avoid using nonstandard Markdown extensions unless the renderer supports them.

[Jump to Troubleshooting](#troubleshooting)

## Best Practices for Translators

- Translate human-readable text (headings, paragraphs, list items) while leaving code, filenames, and URLs unchanged.
- Keep sentence-level units intact where possible to simplify quality checks.
- Maintain consistent terminology: create a glossary for terms like "build", "deploy", "preview", and UI labels.

## References and Links

- Project repository: https://example.com/your-org/static-docs-starter
- Markdown guide: https://www.markdownguide.org

If you need a reproducible example, copy the repository and follow the Quick Setup steps. For advanced customization, consult `build.config.js` and the template files in `templates/`.

---

If you have questions or need a sample translation checklist, include the language code and a short description of the content type (e.g., "es — user guide").
translation_notes: Preserve Markdown syntax (headings, lists, links, anchors) and link targets exactly.
Keep inline code and fenced code blocks verbatim; do not translate protected spans like '¡Hola, mundo!' or '中文示例'.
expected_challenges: Deciding whether to translate technical terms such as "build" and "serve" vs. keeping them in English
Maintaining proper Markdown structure (list indentation, fenced code blocks) during translation
Ensuring links and anchor references remain valid after heading translation

style_label: Documents with code and doc strings
title: SQL analytics note with helper function and examples
source_text:
Overview

This document explains a small set of analytical patterns used for monthly cohort reporting and performance tuning. The prose and comments are intended to be translated, while SQL and code identifiers must remain intact. The examples show how to aggregate events, compute month-over-month changes, and annotate slow-running queries.

Design notes

- Purpose: provide a canonical approach for computing monthly metrics from an event table.
- Scope: suitable for datasets where events are partitioned by event_date and the primary key is user_id.
- Constraints: avoid wide cross joins; prefer window functions and explicit joins.

SQL example: monthly aggregation

The following SQL computes monthly active users and revenue. Translate the explanatory comments (lines starting with --). Keep SQL keywords and identifiers as-is.

```sql
-- Aggregate events to monthly granularity and compute revenue per user
WITH monthly_events AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    DATE_TRUNC('month', event_date) AS month,
    SUM(amount_usd) AS revenue_usd -- total revenue attributed to the month
  FROM sales.orders -- source table with one row per order
  WHERE event_date >= DATE_TRUNC('month', CURRENT_DATE) - INTERVAL '12 months'
    AND status = 'completed'
  GROUP BY user_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', event_date)
),
monthly_metrics AS (
  SELECT
    month,
    COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS mau, -- monthly active users
    SUM(revenue_usd) AS total_revenue
  FROM monthly_events
  GROUP BY month
)
SELECT
  month,
  mau,
  total_revenue,
  LAG(mau) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS mau_prev_month,
  CASE WHEN LAG(mau) OVER (ORDER BY month) IS NULL THEN NULL
       ELSE (mau - LAG(mau) OVER (ORDER BY month))::FLOAT / LAG(mau) OVER (ORDER BY month)
  END AS mau_growth_rate
FROM monthly_metrics
ORDER BY month DESC;
```

Notes on performance

- Use partition pruning: ensure event_date is a partition key so the planner prunes irrelevant partitions.
- Avoid repeated computation: materialize expensive CTEs if the engine does not inline them effectively.
- Monitor the query plan: look for hash joins that spill to disk; consider increasing work_mem or rewriting the join.

Python helper: post-processing and labeling

The following Python snippet demonstrates a helper function used in a scheduled notebook to compute additional derived KPIs and to format the final report. Translate the docstring and inline comments, but preserve the function name and variable names.

```python
def compute_monthly_metrics(rows):
    '''
    Given a list of rows from the monthly_metrics query, compute derived KPIs.

    Parameters:
    - rows: iterable of dicts with keys 'month', 'mau', 'total_revenue', 'mau_prev_month'

    Returns:
    - list of dicts augmented with 'mau_growth_rate' and formatted 'month_label'
    '''
    results = []
    for r in rows:
        # Defensive handling of null previous month values
        prev = r.get('mau_prev_month')
        if prev in (None, 0):
            growth = None
        else:
            growth = (r['mau'] - prev) / float(prev)
        # Format the month as YYYY-MM for human-readable reporting
        month_label = r['month'].strftime('%Y-%m')
        results.append({
            'month': r['month'],
            'month_label': month_label,
            'mau': r['mau'],
            'total_revenue': r['total_revenue'],
            'mau_growth_rate': growth
        })
    return results
```

Operational checklist

- Before running a month of backfills, validate that the source table 'sales.orders' contains no duplicate order_ids.
- Tag expensive queries in the scheduler and add a short description explaining the business context.
- Record baseline execution time and memory usage for the query and for any downstream Python processing.

Localization guidance for translators

- Translate explanatory prose, inline comments, and docstrings into the target language.
- Preserve code blocks, SQL syntax, and identifiers exactly.
- Do not change date formats inside code or replace SQL keywords.

End of note

This file is intended to be self-contained and readable in a code review. If further decomposition is needed, split the SQL and post-processing logic into separate modules and document the interface between them.
translation_notes: Translate prose, comments (lines starting with --) and docstrings; keep code, SQL, and identifiers unchanged.
Preserve formatting inside fenced code blocks and do not alter SQL keywords, punctuation, or date format strings.
expected_challenges: Distinguishing between translatable comments/docstrings and non-translatable code/identifiers.
Rendering technical terms (e.g., 'partition pruning', 'window functions') accurately while preserving concise phrasing.

title: Control Module Installation and Maintenance — Training Manual Page
source_text: Overview This page documents the installation, torque specifications, and routine maintenance checks for the control module used in field units. The module referenced throughout is Model XJ-220 and must be handled according to the safety and environmental notes below. Illustrations and tables are provided to guide technicians during on-site procedures. Safety Notices - Disconnect power and verify zero-voltage before beginning work. - Use insulated tools and eye protection for all mechanical tasks. - Avoid contaminating electrical contacts; DO NOT PAINT any surfaces labelled as electrical mating faces. Figure References and Images Figure 1 shows the front panel layout and primary cable entry points. Refer to this figure when routing cables and confirming label positions. ![Front panel layout](images/panel_front.png) Figure 1: Front panel layout. Label A = Main power inlet, Label B = Signal terminal block. For wiring details, see the schematic below (Figure 2). Pay particular attention to the color-coding of shield drains and the placement of ferrite beads at entry points. Figure 2: Wiring schematic. Shield drain wires must be terminated to chassis at a single point. Required Torque Specifications Follow the torque table when installing fasteners on the module enclosure and mounting bracket. Incorrect torque can lead to frame distortion or loose contacts. Table 1: Fastener torque specifications | Fastener | Location | Torque (N·m) | Thread Type | Remark | |----------|----------|--------------:|------------|--------| | M4 cap screw | Front panel to chassis | 2.5 | M4 x 0.7 | Hand-start then torque | | M6 hex bolt | Mounting bracket | 8.5 | M6 x 1.0 | Use threadlocker on outdoor units | | #8-32 screw | Terminal block | 1.2 | 8-32 UNC | Inspect under screw head for insulation | Installation Procedure 1. Verify the model: confirm the module label reads Model XJ-220. Check serial number against the site manifest. 2. Mount the bracket to the enclosure using the M6 hex bolts. Hand-start each bolt, then torque to 8.5 N·m in an alternating pattern. 3. Attach the front panel using M4 cap screws. Tighten to 2.5 N·m. Do not exceed specified torque; overtightening can crack the polymer spacer. 4. Route incoming power and signal cables as shown in Figure 1. Terminate wires to the terminal block, then secure with #8-32 screws torqued to 1.2 N·m. 5. Apply recommended shielding practices per Figure 2: terminate shields to chassis ground at a single point and secure ferrite clamps near the cable entry. 6. Perform a continuity check and insulation resistance test before applying power. Maintenance Schedule Perform the checks listed below at the intervals indicated. Replace any fasteners showing corrosion or stripping. - Monthly: Visual inspection of seals and cable strain reliefs; verify that labels are legible. - Quarterly: Tighten fasteners to the torque values in Table 1; inspect wiring for wear. - Annually: Full functional test including power-up diagnostics and firmware verification. Notes and Special Instructions - If the unit will be exposed to coastal environments, use stainless steel M6 bolts and apply a corrosion inhibitor to all exterior threads. - For painted enclosures, ensure electrical mating faces are masked during painting. Marked faces are labeled on the front panel template—see Figure 1. Remember: DO NOT PAINT the connector interfaces. - When translating this document, retain measurement units (N·m) and image filenames exactly as written to avoid ambiguity. Appendix: Quick Reference - Torque quick list: M4 = 2.5 N·m, M6 = 8.5 N·m, #8-32 = 1.2 N·m. - Critical images: images/panel_front.png, images/wiring_diagram.svg - Protected label text on module: "Model XJ-220"
translation_notes: Preserve image filenames and HTML/Markdown image tags verbatim.
Keep table headers and units (e.g., N·m) unchanged; do not convert units.
Do not translate protected spans such as model identifiers or explicit instructions in ALL CAPS.
expected_challenges: Maintaining table alignment and punctuation while translating header and cell content.
Deciding how to render technical abbreviations and units consistently with target language norms.
Preserving image paths and HTML/Markdown tags exactly as in source.

style_label: Documents with multilingual input and selective translation
title: Audio Guide: Gallery 1 — Early Sculpture and Sacred Labels
source_text:
Welcome to Gallery 1: The Dawn of Sculpture. This audio guide is designed to accompany your visit through the first wing of the museum, focusing on the early stone and bronze work from the subcontinent between the 2nd century BCE and the 7th century CE. Pause at each marked number and press play on the handheld device, or follow the timestamps below on your mobile player.

[00:00] Introduction
This gallery traces the development of religious and courtly sculpture, emphasizing technique, patronage, and mobility of artisans. As you enter, take a moment to read the signage to your right: a reminder for visitors in multiple languages that access is monitored and that delicate works should not be touched.

Sign on the left: “कृपया चुप रहें” — Please remain quiet. This concise Hindi instruction functions as both a practical request and a cultural cue. The phrase appears repeatedly at early medieval shrine replicas where silence was traditionally part of ritual observance.

[01:15] Exhibit 1: The Standing Buddha (circa 1st–2nd century CE)
This stone sculpture, carved from a single slab of sandstone, shows features characteristic of northern workshops. Note the interplay of stylization and naturalism: the smooth drapery folds and the geometricized hair curls. There is a short plaque at the base that reads: उकेरा गया: 2nd century CE. When translating labels, render dates and material names literally; maintain metric measurements and technical terms.

[04:30] Exhibit 2: Torana Fragment
Look above you at the wooden torana fragment. The carved narrative panels depict episodes from a local legend tied to riverine trade. One small label next to the fragment lists provenance: “उत्पत्ति: मथुरा”. This indicates the object’s origin city and is a key curatorial fact; leave the city name in Hindi script when asked to preserve original museum labels or inscriptions.

Curatorial note: Many objects in this gallery carry bilingual or multilingual inscriptions. A working principle for this guide is to reproduce original inscriptions verbatim in their source script whenever possible. Where a named patron or temple appears in the original script, that original form conveys provenance and historical attribution.

[07:00] Exhibit 3: Bronze Standing Deity
The bronze cast shows a high level of technical control in lost-wax casting. Observe the patina and the repaired rivets where arms were reattached. Our conservators have left visible repairs to document the object's history of use and restoration. The audio will now offer a short comparative listening: press the button to hear field recordings of ritual drum patterns associated with this icon type.

[10:20] Accessibility and Visitor Etiquette
Wheelchair users may follow the ramp at the east entrance; mobile guides include descriptive audio tracks that describe visual details scene by scene. For visitors reading label reproductions, the museum retains original labels, including short phrases in other languages, for authenticity. Please respect those choices and do not substitute translations for inscriptions that are displayed in their original script.

[12:45] Closing remarks for Gallery 1
Before you proceed to Gallery 2, take a moment to study the gallery map and the list of related readings. Staff members in the gallery will be wearing blue badges and can answer questions about object histories, materials, and the meaning of short inscriptions. If you need a printed brochure, request the one labeled "Historic Inscriptions" at the desk.

End of track. Proceed to the doorway marked with the lotus symbol for Gallery 2.
translation_notes: Translate only the English portions; leave the listed Hindi spans unchanged in the target language output.
Preserve timestamps, labels, and technical terms (dates, measurements) as presented; do not reformat numeric data.
expected_challenges: Deciding whether to transliterate or retain original script for proper nouns and label fragments embedded in English sentences.
Maintaining the formal, instructional tone of an audio guide while translating captions and curatorial notes accurately.

title: Application FAQ and Usage Guide
source_text:
# Application FAQ and Usage Guide

## Overview

This FAQ page provides concise answers and practical instructions for common tasks in the desktop and web versions of the Application. The document is intended for end users and technical staff who need quick guidance, troubleshooting steps, and references to configuration options.

- Latest stable release: 2.4.1
- Official documentation: [User Guide](https://example.com/user-guide)
- Support: See the Contact section below

## Getting Started

### Installation

1. Download the installer from the official site or your organization's deployment server.
2. Run the installer and follow on-screen prompts.
3. If you are using a package manager, use the recommended package name `app-suite` or the system-specific package listed in the User Guide.

Tip: If you prefer a clean configuration, rename or remove your previous `config.yaml` before first run.

### First Run

- Launch the application from the Start menu, dock, or by running `app --start`.
- Complete the initial setup wizard.
- If prompted for a sample greeting, you may see multilingual examples like "¡Bienvenido!" in the demo content.

## Common Tasks

### How do I reset cached data?

Run the reset command with elevated permissions: `app --reset-cache`. Restart the app after the command completes.

### How do I change language settings?

1. Open Settings > Preferences.
2. Select the desired language from the drop-down.
3. Restart the app to apply language and regional formats.

Note: Some embedded resource strings in third-party plugins may remain in their original language until a plugin update is available.

### How do I export logs?

- Navigate to Help > Export Logs, or use the command-line option `app --export-logs /path/to/archive`.
- Attach the generated archive file when opening a support ticket.

## Troubleshooting

### Application won't start

- Verify that system requirements are met.
- Check that no other instance is running: use your system process viewer or `ps aux | grep app` on Unix-like systems.
- Remove temporary lock files from the application data directory and retry.

> Warning: Do not delete configuration files unless instructed. Back up `config.yaml` first.

### Crashes during operation

- Attach a copy of the crash dump found in the logs folder and include steps to reproduce the issue.
- Try running with diagnostics enabled: `app --diagnostics`. This creates a verbose log suitable for developer analysis.

If the problem persists after these steps, collect logs and contact support (see Contact section).

## Security & Privacy

- The application encrypts local secrets using the OS key store where available.
- Network traffic uses TLS 1.2+ by default. Admins may enforce stricter cipher suites via `security.policy`.
- For compliance, audit logs can be forwarded to external SIEM systems.

> Privacy note: User analytics are optional. You can opt out at Settings > Privacy.

## Multilingual Examples and Protected Text

This guide contains a few protected example strings that must remain unchanged in translations to preserve test cases and sample payloads. Treat these as literal tokens when translating UI text.

Examples:

- UI sample greeting: "¡Bienvenido!"
- Do-not-translate marker for German sample: "Bitte nicht übersetzen"

## Glossary

- config.yaml: Primary configuration file stored in the user's application directory.
- Diagnostics mode: Verbose logging and additional runtime checks enabled with `--diagnostics`.
- SIEM: Security Information and Event Management.

## Contact

For unsupported issues, file a ticket through the support portal: https://support.example.com or email support@example.com. Include the export bundle and a short description of the steps to reproduce the issue.

Return to the [FAQ index](#application-faq-and-usage-guide) for quick navigation.
translation_notes: Translate all headings, paragraphs, bullets, and blockquotes naturally while preserving Markdown syntax and link targets verbatim.
Do not change inline code, filenames, command-line flags, or any protected spans; keep them exactly as in the source (e.g., `config.yaml`, `--reset-cache`).
expected_challenges: Preserving link targets and anchor references while translating surrounding link text
Deciding which UI strings are literal tokens (protected spans) and ensuring inline code and file names remain unchanged

title: Field Survey Summary: FS-20 Riparian Vegetation and Soil Metrics
source_text: Field Survey Summary — Site FS-20 Overview Site ID: FS-20 was surveyed on 2025-03-18 as part of a seasonal riparian monitoring campaign. The site is located along the North Fork at an elevation of approximately 180 m. Observations focused on vegetation composition, soil physical and chemical properties, and visible erosion features. The dominant canopy species recorded was Quercus robur with scattered Salix spp. along the bank. Methods Transect sampling: Two perpendicular transects (A and B) were established across the riparian zone. Soil samples were collected at 0–10 cm and 10–30 cm depths at each marked station. Vegetation cover was estimated using 1 m2 quadrats placed at 5 m intervals along each transect. All GPS coordinates were recorded with a handheld unit (accuracy ±3 m). Laboratory analyses: Soil pH, moisture content (gravimetric), nitrate (NO3-N, mg/kg), and bulk density (g/cm3) were measured following standard protocols. Plant specimens were identified in the field; ambiguous specimens were collected and later verified under binocular microscope. Results Summary narrative: Riparian vegetation at FS-20 shows a healthy canopy dominated by Quercus robur, with understorey dominated by Poa sp. and juvenile individuals of Salix alba near the waterline. No invasive species were detected during the visit. Soil tests indicate slightly acidic to neutral pH and moderate nitrate levels consistent with upstream agricultural influence. Table 1: Key soil measurements (averaged across transects and depths) | Parameter | Unit | Mean (Sample A) | Mean (Sample B) | Remarks | |------------------|-------------|-----------------|-----------------|----------------------------------------| | pH | — | 6.8 | 6.6 | Slightly acidic near waterline | | Moisture | % (wt) | 28 | 22 | Higher in Sample A (lower elevation) | | Nitrate (NO3-N) | mg/kg | 12 | 9 | Elevated compared to reference values | | Bulk density | g/cm3 | 1.15 | 1.22 | Compacted in boat access zone | | Organic matter | % | 4.5 | 3.8 | Correlates with canopy cover | Table 1 caption: Averaged laboratory results by transect. Units are critical for interpreting nutrient status and should be preserved in translation. Figures and imagery Figure 1 illustrates the general site layout and a representative quadrat with canopy cover estimates. ![Figure 1: Representative quadrat and canopy view at FS-20](images/FS-20_quadrat_photo.jpg) Figure 1 caption: Representative quadrat (1 m2) showing understory composition. Photo taken facing north; filename: images/FS-20_quadrat_photo.jpg. An aerial overview map is included to show access points and the location of transects. Figure 2 caption: Site map marking Transect A and Transect B, access path, and sample stations. Placeholder image: images/FS-20_site_map.png. Discussion Vegetation: The dominance of Quercus robur suggests a mature riparian band with stable canopy cover. Juvenile Salix spp. near the river edge indicate active regeneration. Management recommendations include continued monitoring of recruitment and an annual check for invasive species. Soil and nutrient status: Nitrate levels (Table 1) are higher in Sample A, likely reflecting upstream fertilizer runoff. pH values near neutral support typical riparian plant communities; however, localized compaction (elevated bulk density) at the boat access point could impair infiltration and root growth. Recommendations and next steps 1. Repeat soil sampling in the same stations during low- and high-flow conditions to capture seasonal variation. 2. Install one permanent photo-point at the quadrat location in Figure 1 to monitor understorey changes. 3. Implement a simple riparian buffer signage at the access point to reduce trampling and compaction. Notes for translators: Captions and table headers contain measurement units and site identifiers that convey essential meaning and must be translated with care. Image filenames and the string "Site ID: FS-20" should be preserved verbatim.
translation_notes: Translate table headers and captions fully; preserve units exactly (e.g., mg/kg, g/cm3, %).
Do not translate protected spans such as site identifiers and Latin species names (e.g., "FS-20", "Quercus robur").
expected_challenges: Handling of measurement units and symbols in table headers (ensure units remain consistent and clear).
Preserving image filenames and HTML/Markdown tags while translating surrounding captions and alt text.

style_label: Documents with code and doc strings
title: Service recovery runbook with config snippets and helper script
source_text:
Overview

This runbook provides step-by-step instructions to recover the "myapp" service when it fails on a Linux host or in a Kubernetes cluster. It contains shell commands, a sample YAML configuration, and a small Python helper script. Translate the explanatory prose, comments, and docstrings, but preserve code tokens, identifiers, file paths, and fenced blocks unchanged.

Preconditions

- You must have access to the host as a user in the "admin" group.
- Ensure that kubectl is configured for the target cluster context.
- Back up the current configuration at /etc/myapp/config before making changes.

Quick restart (host)

If the service is managed by systemd, try a standard restart. This does not require editing configuration files.

```bash
# Check current service status
sudo systemctl status myapp.service

# Restart the service
sudo systemctl restart myapp.service

# Verify that the main process is running
sudo systemctl status myapp.service --no-pager
```

If logs indicate a configuration syntax error, restore the previous copy from the backup and restart.

Quick restart (Kubernetes)

When running in Kubernetes, restart the deployment to force pods to pick up new configuration or secrets.

```bash
# Roll the deployment to trigger pod replacement
kubectl rollout restart deployment/myapp-deployment -n myapp-namespace

# Watch pod status until all pods are ready
kubectl get pods -n myapp-namespace -w
```

Config snippet (docker-compose compatibility)

Below is a minimal example of a docker-compose.yml fragment showing the environment variables expected by the container. Preserve key names and variable names; translate only the surrounding explanatory comments.

```yaml
version: '3.8'
services:
  myapp:
    image: myregistry/myapp:latest
    restart: unless-stopped
    environment:
      - ENV_VAR_API_KEY
      - ENV_VAR_LOG_LEVEL=info
    volumes:
      - /opt/myapp/data:/var/lib/myapp
    configs:
      - source: app.conf
        target: /etc/myapp/app.conf
configs:
  app.conf:
    file: ./config/app.conf
```

Troubleshooting tips

- If pods fail with CrashLoopBackOff, inspect the last container logs with kubectl logs --previous.
- If systemctl reports a failed status with Exit code, examine the journal with journalctl -u myapp.service --since "1 hour ago".
- For environment-specific differences, compare the host file /etc/myapp/app.conf with the config map used in Kubernetes.

Helper script

The following Python helper script validates a configuration file for required keys and prints a short report. Translate the docstring and inline comments; do not modify the function name or printed tokens that represent file paths or keys.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Validate the application configuration file.

This script checks for the presence of mandatory keys and reports missing items.
Run it locally before applying a config change.
"""

import sys
import yaml

REQUIRED_KEYS = ["server", "database", "logging"]

def validate_config(path):
    """Return a list of missing keys for the given YAML config file.

    :param path: filesystem path to a YAML config file
    :return: list of missing key names
    """
    try:
        with open(path, "r") as fh:
            cfg = yaml.safe_load(fh)
    except Exception as e:
        # Unable to read or parse the configuration file
        print(f"ERROR: cannot load config {path}: {e}")
        return REQUIRED_KEYS

    missing = [k for k in REQUIRED_KEYS if k not in cfg]
    # Print a short summary for humans and return missing keys for programmatic use
    if missing:
        print("Missing keys:", ", ".join(missing))
    else:
        print("All required keys present")
    return missing

if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) != 2:
        print("Usage: validate_config.py /path/to/app.conf")
        sys.exit(2)
    config_path = sys.argv[1]
    validate_config(config_path)
```

Post-recovery checks

After restarting, confirm that the application is serving requests and that metrics are healthy. Example checks include HTTP GET to the health endpoint and verifying that logs contain no new ERROR entries for at least five minutes.
translation_notes: Translate all prose, explanatory comments, and docstrings, but do not translate code tokens, identifiers, file paths, or command names.
Preserve fenced code blocks exactly, including indentation and punctuation; translate only the human-readable comments and docstrings inside them.
expected_challenges: Distinguishing between translatable comments/docstrings and non-translatable code or identifiers embedded in the same line.
Handling short technical phrases (e.g., CrashLoopBackOff, rollout restart) that may be left untranslated or require a standard localized term.
Ensuring YAML keys and environment variable names remain unchanged while translating nearby explanatory text.

title: How to Complete a Bilingual Registration Form (English with Hindi labels)
source_text:
Introduction
This guide explains how to fill out a standard registration form that mixes English instructions with a few Hindi labels. The form is intended for users who speak English but need to preserve specific Hindi text fields exactly as printed on the official PDF. When translating the instructions or creating a localized version, translate only the English prose and leave the Hindi labels and examples unchanged.

Why some fields remain in Hindi
Certain legal or administrative terms are left in Hindi on the form to ensure consistency with government records and supporting documents. For clarity, three Hindi spans appear as field labels and document names in the sample form below. They are semantically important and must not be altered: "पूरा नाम" (the label for the full legal name), "स्थायी पता" (the permanent address label), and "नागरिकता प्रमाणपत्र" (a required document). Translators should not attempt to translate, transliterate, or modify these exact substrings.

Before you begin
1. Read each English instruction and translate it into the target language, but do not change the Hindi spans shown in the fields.
2. Keep formatting cues such as boldface or all-caps for section headers when possible, but adapt typography to the target language conventions.
3. Preserve examples that are explicitly shown in Hindi; they serve as exact tokens for database matching.

How to complete each section
Personal details (English instructions to translate)
- Enter your full legal name in the field titled "पूरा नाम". Do not modify this label; only provide the answer in the box following the label.
- Provide your date of birth (DD/MM/YYYY) and gender as requested. If the form asks for proof of date of birth, attach scans or photocopies as listed elsewhere.

Address information
- For correspondence address, translate the English field names into the target language. However, if the form includes a line labeled "स्थायी पता", leave that label exactly as shown and enter the permanent address in the adjacent input area.
- If correspondence and permanent addresses are the same, check the English checkbox that reads "Same as correspondence address." Do not alter the Hindi label.

Supporting documents
- The list of acceptable documents will be provided in English for translation. One required document will be explicitly listed in Hindi as "नागरिकता प्रमाणपत्र". This is a document name that must remain unchanged because clerks will search for that exact string when verifying attachments.
- When listing attachments in a translated version, put the Hindi document name on a separate line exactly as printed.

Example filled form (layout)
Section: Personal Details
पूरा नाम: [Anjali Sharma]
Date of Birth: [12/07/1990]
Gender: [Female]

Section: Address
Correspondence Address: [123 Green Lane, Citytown]
स्थायी पता: [456 Old Road, Hometown]

Section: Attachments
- Passport copy
- Proof of residence
- नागरिकता प्रमाणपत्र (attach scanned copy)

Common errors to avoid
- Do not translate or change the Hindi labels. Translating "पूरा नाम" or "स्थायी पता" into the target language will break automated matching.
- Do not add diacritics, extra punctuation, or parentheses around protected Hindi spans.
- When translating the instructions that reference a Hindi label, keep the label as-is in the sentence. Example (do not change the Hindi token): "Enter your full legal name in the field titled "पूरा नाम"." Translate the surrounding English but not the quoted Hindi.

FAQ and support
Q: What if a user writes the Hindi label in Latin script? 
A: Ask them to retain the Devanagari spelling exactly as shown. The database matching uses the Devanagari token.

Contact
If you have questions about specific field translations or need clarification on formatting, contact the forms office by email. Provide translated drafts with the Hindi tokens left unchanged so reviewers can verify conformance quickly.
translation_notes: Translate only the English text; leave the listed Hindi spans exactly as they appear.
When a Hindi span appears inside an English sentence, do not alter its script, spacing, or punctuation.
expected_challenges: Distinguishing which tokens to leave unchanged when they appear inline with English sentences.
Avoiding accidental transliteration or translation of Hindi spans by overzealous tools or editors.
Preserving punctuation and spacing around Hindi spans while adapting surrounding English grammar.
Ensuring database-matching tokens remain exact (no added diacritics or invisible characters).

title: Electrochemical Stability of Thin-Film Sensors under Physiological Conditions
source_text: Abstract This report documents an experimental study on the electrochemical stability of thin-film platinum sensors immersed in buffered saline at pH 7.4. The aim was to quantify drift, noise, and response time across different fabrication batches. Sample identifiers and key conditions are reported to enable reproducibility: Sample ID: A12 was selected as a representative baseline. Introduction Thin-film electrodes are widely used in biomedical monitoring. Stability under physiological pH and ion concentration is critical for long-term implantation. We evaluated sensors fabricated with three thickness profiles and two annealing protocols to determine which combination minimizes drift while maintaining fast response times. Methods Sensors were prepared on silicon wafers using electron-beam evaporation. After patterning, devices underwent one of two annealing protocols: Rapid Thermal Anneal (RTA) at 400 °C for 60 s, or Furnace Anneal (FA) at 300 °C for 30 min. All electrochemical measurements were performed in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) at pH 7.4 and 37 °C. A three-electrode cell was used with Ag/AgCl reference and graphite counter electrodes. The data acquisition system recorded open-circuit potential (OCP) and impedance spectra from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz. Results Table 1 summarizes the primary metrics for each fabrication condition. The headers convey critical meaning: mean drift is reported in microvolts per hour (µV/h), and response time is the 90% settling time after a step change in analyte concentration. Table 1: Summary of sensor performance in PBS (pH 7.4) | Batch | Anneal | Thickness (nm) | Mean drift (µV/h) | Noise (µVrms) | Response time (s) | |-------|--------|----------------|-------------------:|--------------:|-----------------:| | A12 | RTA | 50 | 12 | 5.2 | 1.8 | | B07 | FA | 50 | 28 | 6.9 | 3.4 | | C03 | RTA | 100 | 9 | 4.7 | 2.1 | Figure 1 shows an optical micrograph and a schematic cross-section of the typical device. The micrograph highlights edge roughness after lift-off, which correlates with increased noise in some batches. Markdown image inclusion: ![Fig. 1: Optical micrograph of sensor array (left) and cross-section schematic (right)](images/figure1_optical_micrograph.png) HTML image inclusion: Discussion Batch A12 (RTA, 50 nm) displayed the best combination of low drift and fast response, likely due to improved grain boundary consolidation during rapid annealing. The presence of edge roughness visible in Fig. 1 suggests that additional photoresist optimization could reduce noise further. The impedance spectra (not shown) indicated stable double-layer capacitance over 48 hours; however, a gradual increase in charge-transfer resistance was observed for furnace-annealed devices. Practical considerations for translation: numeric units (µV/h, µVrms) and notation for pH must be rendered accurately. Captions and table headers contain key diagnostic information and should be translated with priority. Conclusion Under physiological conditions (PBS, pH 7.4, 37 °C), RTA-treated 50 nm platinum films demonstrated superior short-term electrochemical stability. Future work will evaluate encapsulation strategies to mitigate long-term drift and will report extended impedance monitoring beyond 30 days. References Representative reference: Doe J., et al., 2021. Stability of Thin-Film Electrodes. Journal of Biomedical Sensors. DOI:10.1000/abc456 Appendix: Experimental notes - All temperature values are reported in degrees Celsius. - For reproducibility, keep the image filenames and figure labels unchanged when referring to figures: images/figure1_optical_micrograph.png, images/figure1_cross_section.png.
translation_notes: Translate table headers and figure captions fully; preserve units such as µV/h and µVrms.
Do not modify image filenames or HTML/Markdown tags (e.g., images/figure1_optical_micrograph.png, ).
expected_challenges: Rendering micro-units and special characters (µ, °C) correctly in target language.
Maintaining table column alignment and numeric formatting during translation.
Translating concise technical captions without losing measurement specificity.
Preserving image tag syntax and exact filenames while translating surrounding text.

title: Remote Work Security Policy Note
source_text: # Remote Work Security: Policy Note This policy note summarizes key expectations and operational guidance for employees working remotely. It is intended to provide a concise handbook-style reference, not a full legal instrument. Use the links and internal anchors to access detailed procedures and the IT knowledge base. ## Purpose and scope The purpose of this document is to reduce security risk while enabling flexible work arrangements. Scope includes all employees, contractors, and vendors who connect to corporate resources from outside corporate-managed facilities. This covers laptops, mobile devices, and home network equipment. > Principle: protect confidential data, minimize attack surface, and ensure prompt reporting of incidents. ## Responsibilities - Employees must follow approved access methods and avoid using public or unsecured Wi-Fi for work tasks. - Managers are responsible for verifying that team members have required security controls enabled and for tracking completion of trainings. - IT maintains the endpoint management and access control systems and provides support through the IT Security Portal. ## Access and authentication - Always connect through the corporate `VPN` or an approved secure tunnel when accessing internal services. - Multi-factor authentication (`MFA`) is required for all administrative access and for access to sensitive applications. - Strong passwords and password managers are recommended; shared accounts are prohibited unless explicitly authorized. For configuration details, see the IT Security Portal: [IT Security Portal](https://intranet.example.com/security) ## Data protection and handling Employees must classify and handle data according to the data classification standard. Examples: 1. Confidential data may only be stored on encrypted, company-managed devices. 2. Sensitive information must not be transcribed into personal cloud storage or messaging apps. 3. When in doubt, consult the data owner or legal team. See [Data Classification](#data-protection) for an overview. ## Incident reporting and response If you suspect a security incident, take these steps immediately: 1. Disconnect from the network if you observe unusual activity on your device. 2. Report the incident via the incident form on the IT Security Portal and notify your manager. 3. Preserve logs and avoid attempting repairs that could destroy evidence. The incident response team will acknowledge reports within four business hours and provide next steps. Escalations follow the chain described in Section 4.2 of the full security policy. ## Approved tools and software - Use only company-approved collaboration and file-sharing tools. - Keep operating systems and anti-malware definitions up to date. - Do not install unauthorized remote access or file-synchronization software on corporate devices. ## Exceptions and enforcement Exceptions to this policy require documented approval from IT security and your business lead. Noncompliance may result in access revocation, disciplinary action, or other remedies as defined in the Terms of Service and employment policies. ## FAQ and common scenarios ### I need to use a personal device for a short-term project. What do I do? Contact IT to request a temporary access token and follow the onboarding checklist for personal devices. The device will be assessed and, if necessary, constrained with a limited session profile. ### My home router does not support enterprise-grade encryption. Is that allowed? You should update or replace home networking equipment that cannot enforce WPA2/3 or equivalent protections. IT can provide guidance and an equipment subsidy for approved cases. ## Appendix and references - Full Security Policy (internal): refer to the consolidated security playbook. - Incident report form: [IT Security Portal](https://intranet.example.com/security) - For questions, contact security-support@{{company_name}}. This note is intended as an operational summary. For legal definitions and binding contractual terms consult HR and the legal department.
translation_notes: Preserve Markdown syntax and link targets verbatim; do not alter URLs or anchor names.
Keep inline code and placeholders such as `VPN`, `MFA`, and {{company_name}} unchanged.
expected_challenges: Maintaining the structure of Markdown headings, anchors, and internal links during translation
Rendering technical acronyms and inline code consistently across languages (e.g., VPN, MFA)
Translating legal/administrative phrases like 'Terms of Service' and references to specific sections without changing numbering

title: April Community Bulletin: Events, Volunteers, and Local Resources
source_text:
Welcome to the April edition of our neighborhood newsletter. In this issue we highlight upcoming gatherings, volunteer opportunities, and small initiatives that help neighbors stay connected. Our goal is to make it easy for everyone to participate — whether you are new to the area or have been here for decades.

Editor’s note: please read through the calendar and sign up early for events that require registration. If you can help with setup or cleanup, please use the contact links at the bottom of each listing.

Upcoming events

- Spring Book Fair — Saturday, April 16, 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM
  Our seasonal book exchange returns with an expanded children’s table and a speaker corner. We’re hosting the event in the park by the community center. Featured this year is a collaboration with the local schools; teachers will run short storytelling sessions at 11:30 AM and 2:00 PM. Bring gently used books or swap freely with other attendees. This year’s fair is titled सामुदायिक पुस्तक मेले and will include a teen volunteer booth for packing leftover books for donation.

- Neighborhood Clean-Up — Sunday, April 24, 9:00 AM
  Meet at the corner of Elm and Third. Supplies are provided but feel free to bring gloves. We will have coffee and muffins for volunteers at 10:30 AM. Everyone is welcome regardless of how long you can stay.

- Community Potluck and Cultural Night — Friday, April 29, 6:00 PM
  Celebrate food and stories from our diverse neighborhood. Please label dishes with ingredients and potential allergens. We encourage a small card with the name of the dish and its origin so people can connect food to family traditions.

Welcome corner

Each month we feature a new neighbor in a short interview. This month’s informal greeting is a simple phrase many residents use to welcome each other: नमस्ते पड़ोसी. We asked five new residents what made them choose this neighborhood and how they would introduce themselves in one sentence. Answers ranged from “close to transit” to “great parks for kids.” If you’d like to be featured in a future issue, send a short paragraph and a photo to community@neighborhood.org.

Volunteer spotlight

Meet Harleen, who coordinates our food pantry deliveries. Over the last year she has created a reliable routing system and recruited weekend drivers. She credits community partnerships for making the service sustainable and often notes that small actions add up. When volunteers complete a shift, we like to offer a simple token of thanks. In community emails we frequently sign off with the phrase धन्यवाद, आपका योगदान as a visible appreciation to contributors who help maintain our services.

Local initiatives and resources

- Senior Check-In Program: A weekly phone call service for elders living alone. Call the community office to register or volunteer as a caller.
- Tool Library: Open Tuesdays and Thursdays from 4:00–7:00 PM. Membership is free for first-time users; donations accepted.
- Youth Homework Club: After-school tutoring runs Monday through Thursday at the library annex. Tutors are needed for math and reading support.

How to get involved

- Sign up on the volunteer portal for single-shift tasks or recurring commitments.
- Donate lightly used winter coats and children’s books at the community center dropbox.
- Attend the next board meeting if you want to propose an idea for a neighborhood project; meetings are held on the second Tuesday of each month.

Closing remarks

Thank you for reading and for being a part of what keeps this neighborhood supportive and resilient. We continue to rely on neighbors sharing time, skills, and kindness. If you have announcements, small business news, or items for the classifieds, please submit them by the 20th of the month to ensure inclusion in the next bulletin. For urgent community news, check our online noticeboard or sign up for SMS alerts.

Contact information

Community Center Office: 555-0147
Email: community@neighborhood.org
Online calendar and volunteer portal: www.neighborhood.org/events

We look forward to seeing you at events and hearing your ideas for making our community stronger.
translation_notes: Translate only the English text; do not translate or alter the Hindi spans listed in protected_spans.

title: AcmeDB v2.4.1 Release Summary
source_text: # AcmeDB v2.4.1 — Release Summary ## Overview AcmeDB v2.4.1 is a patch release focused on stability, security fixes, and incremental performance improvements for analytics workloads. This release includes targeted fixes for replication edge cases, an important driver update for Windows hosts, and several UI improvements in the administrative console. > Note: This patch does not introduce schema changes. If you are running a cluster on v2.3.x, the upgrade path is straightforward and reversible. See the full notes in the [Upgrade Guide](https://acme.example.com/upgrade) and the complete list of commits in `CHANGELOG.md`. ## Highlights - Security: Fixed CVE-2026-0123, a vulnerability in the HTTP handshake that could allow header injection under rare conditions. - Replication: Resolved an edge case where replica lag could be misreported after network partitions. - Drivers: Updated the Windows ODBC driver to address connection timeouts on high-latency networks. - Console: Improved search in the admin console, added keyboard shortcuts for quick navigation. - Performance: Query planner heuristics refined for window functions, yielding up to 10% faster analytic queries on large datasets. ## Upgrade notes Planned maintenance windows should account for a short rolling restart of each node. Typical upgrade steps: 1. Read the full [Upgrade Guide](https://acme.example.com/upgrade) and back up your `data/` directory. 2. Drain the node: `acmedb node drain --timeout 600`. 3. Install the new package and restart: `systemctl restart acmedb`. For automated environments, the CLI supports non-interactive upgrades: ``` acmedb upgrade --version v2.4.1 --yes --quiet ``` If you prefer to see progress, omit `--quiet`. ## Breaking changes and deprecations - The `legacy-replica` configuration key is deprecated and will be removed in v3.0. Migrate to `replica.mode = "standard"` using the migration helper script. - The old telemetry endpoint `telemetry.acme.local` has been shut down; update any firewall rules accordingly. If your deployment still relies on `legacy-replica`, plan migration during a maintenance window and verify replica health after migration. ## Known issues - On Windows hosts with both IPv4 and IPv6 enabled, the ODBC driver may prefer IPv6 even when IPv4 is specified. A workaround is to set `ACME_NETWORK_FAMILY=inet` in the environment. - Some third-party monitoring tools may misinterpret the new shortcut keys in the console; clear any custom key bindings before the upgrade. Report new issues to our tracker: https://acme.example.com/issues (include `v2.4.1` in the title). ## Migration examples Example: migrating a single-node test instance with minimal downtime 1. Backup: `tar czf /tmp/backup-$(date +%F).tar.gz /var/lib/acmedb/data` 2. Stop: `systemctl stop acmedb` 3. Replace binary and start: `dpkg -i acmedb-2.4.1-amd64.deb && systemctl start acmedb` 4. Verify: `acmedb health` and run a quick query test. For containerized deployments, update your image tag to `acmedb:2.4.1` and perform a rolling update. ## Rollout plan - Stage: apply to staging clusters first for at least 48 hours. - Canary: deploy to 10% of production nodes and monitor latency, CPU, and replication metrics. - Full: proceed with 20% increments, validating health checks between waves. Consider automating the process with your orchestration tooling and make use of the `--health-check` flag in deployment scripts. ## Support and resources - Documentation: https://acme.example.com/docs - Upgrade Guide: https://acme.example.com/upgrade - Issues and support: https://acme.example.com/issues If you require assisted upgrades for critical clusters, contact Acme Support via the portal or open a support ticket referencing this release. --- For a full changelog and contributor credits, consult `CHANGELOG.md` in the release artifacts.
translation_notes: Translate headings and prose naturally while preserving Markdown syntax (e.g., #, ##, lists, blockquotes).
Do not modify link targets or URLs; translate link text only if necessary.
Preserve inline code and code blocks exactly (e.g., `--quiet`, `acmedb upgrade`).

Deciding whether to translate UI labels and keyboard shortcut names versus keeping them in English.
Rendering configuration keys and file names (e.g., ACME_NETWORK_FAMILY, CHANGELOG.md) unchanged.

title: YAML Configuration Tutorial: Microservice Example
source_text:
YAML Configuration Tutorial: Microservice Example

Overview:
This document shows a concise example of a YAML-based configuration file for a small microservice. It includes inline comments, a Python loader with docstrings, and a shell snippet for runtime overrides. Translate the explanatory prose and the comments/docstrings; preserve code, keys, and identifiers.

Example configuration (config.yml):
```yaml
# Primary service configuration file
server:
  host: "0.0.0.0"    # Hostname or IP address the service binds to
  port: 8080         # Port number, change if default is occupied

logging:
  level: "INFO"      # Logging level: DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR
  format: "json"     # Output format for logs

database:
  host: "${DB_HOST}" # Use environment variable substitution at deploy time
  port: 5432
  user: "svc_user"
  password: "${DB_PASS}" # DO NOT commit secrets to version control
  max_connections: 20

credentials:
  api_key: "REPLACE_WITH_API_KEY" # Replace with real key stored in secrets/
  retry_policy:
    max_attempts: 3
    backoff_seconds: 2

# Runtime flags can be overridden by environment variables, e.g. APP_ENV
```

Loading the configuration:
The following Python snippet demonstrates a conventional loader that merges file configuration with environment overrides and an optional secrets file. Translate the docstring and the inline comments to the target language, but do not change variable names or function signatures.

```python
"""
config_loader.py
Simple YAML loader that merges file config with environment overrides.

Translate this docstring and inline comments, but preserve code and variable names.
"""

import os
import yaml

def load_config(path="config.yml"):
    # Load YAML configuration from disk.
    with open(path, "r") as f:
        raw = yaml.safe_load(f)

    # Apply environment overrides: if APP_ENV is set, adjust logging level.
    env = os.getenv("APP_ENV", "development")
    if env == "production":
        raw.setdefault("logging", {})["level"] = "WARNING"

    # Merge secrets injected at runtime (do not store secrets in repo).
    secret_path = os.getenv("SECRETS_DIR", "secrets/")
    secret_file = os.path.join(secret_path, "service_secrets.yml")
    if os.path.exists(secret_file):
        with open(secret_file, "r") as sf:
            secrets = yaml.safe_load(sf)
            raw.get("credentials", {}).update(secrets.get("credentials", {}))

    return raw
```

Runtime example:
```shell
# Example: set environment and run service
export APP_ENV=production
export DB_HOST=database.internal
export DB_PASS=topsecret
python run_service.py
```

Best practices:
- Translate explanatory comments so they remain helpful to local developers.
- Keep YAML keys, environment variables (like APP_ENV), and code identifiers unchanged.
- Do not translate file paths such as "secrets/" or placeholder tokens like ${DB_HOST}.
- If a comment includes a short code identifier, keep the identifier verbatim and translate surrounding text.

Troubleshooting:
If configuration changes do not take effect, verify indentation in the YAML file and confirm environment variables are exported in the runtime shell. When translating, make sure spacing and punctuation in code blocks remain identical to avoid syntax errors.

This file is intentionally concise to focus on translation decisions that affect code documentation and configuration. Translate all prose, comments, and docstrings; preserve the structure and any protected spans verbatim.
translation_notes: Translate all prose, comments, and docstrings.
Preserve code blocks, YAML keys, environment variable names, and protected spans exactly.
Do not alter indentation, punctuation, or identifier casing inside code samples.
expected_challenges: Distinguishing YAML keys (code) from inline comments (translatable prose).
Preserving indentation-sensitive YAML structure while translating adjacent comments.
Maintaining placeholder tokens like ${DB_HOST} and ${DB_PASS} intact.
Keeping short identifiers (e.g., api_key, APP_ENV) untranslated within sentences.
Ensuring triple-quoted docstrings and inline quotes are not modified in a way that breaks code syntax.

style_label: Documents with multilingual input and selective translation
title: Notes on Translating English Content with Embedded Hindi Examples
source_text:
Overview
This note is intended for translators working on documents or web content that are primarily English but intentionally include short Hindi examples, quoted phrases, or glossary entries. The goal is to translate all English passages into the target language while preserving the embedded Hindi spans exactly as they appear. These Hindi spans are semantically important: they represent greetings, culturally specific responses, or single-word terms that must remain in Devanagari script for pedagogical or branding reasons.

Context and use cases
Common scenarios include language-learning articles, customer-service templates for multilingual audiences, and cultural notes where an English explanation surrounds an example in Hindi. For instance, an English lesson might present the Hindi greeting followed by an explanation in English. In such cases, the Hindi greeting functions as an example and should not be altered during translation.

Practical example: customer greeting
Below is a short scripted interaction used on a multilingual support page. Translate only the English lines into your target language; leave the Hindi lines verbatim.

Agent: "नमस्ते" — Welcome to our support center. How may I help you today?
Customer: I received an unexpected charge on my account. Can you explain it?
Agent: Please provide your account number and the date of the transaction.
Customer: The transaction is from last Friday and the amount is $24.99.
Agent: Thank you. I'll review your transaction and follow up with details. "धन्यवाद" is a common way the customer might close the call.

Notes on the example above
- The two Hindi phrases, "नमस्ते" and "धन्यवाद", are examples of natural speech that the original document intentionally displays in Devanagari. They are not to be translated into the target language or transliterated.
- The English descriptions around the Hindi remain subject to translation, including the words that indicate why the Hindi phrase appears (for example, "is a common way the customer might close the call").

Glossary entries and short script snippets
When the source includes a glossary, treat each entry as follows: translate the definition or explanatory text, but keep the headword if it is presented in Devanagari. Example:
- नमस्ते — a traditional Hindi greeting used in both formal and informal contexts.
- धन्यवाद — literally means "thanks"; used to express gratitude.

Formatting and punctuation
Preserve punctuation that is directly attached to Hindi spans. If a Hindi phrase appears inside quotation marks or parentheses, keep it exactly as presented, including those quotation marks or parentheses if they are part of the original sentence. However, punctuation that belongs to the surrounding English sentence may need to be adjusted to match target language norms when you translate the English portion.

Register and tone
Be mindful of register. The English surrounding a Hindi phrase often explains cultural nuances or suggests appropriate usage (for example, when to use a formal vs. informal greeting). Maintain that explanatory tone in your translation so that the behavior surrounding the preserved Hindi span remains clear to readers.

Edge cases
- Do not convert Devanagari to romanization or phonetic guides unless the source text explicitly includes them as separate items.
- If the source supplies an English translation of a Hindi term in quotes immediately following it, translate the English translation but keep the Hindi term unchanged.

Checklist for submission
1) Translate all English text into target language. 2) Leave every Devanagari span exactly as in the source. 3) Preserve quotation marks and punctuation attached to Hindi phrases. 4) Maintain register and explanatory notes that contextualize the Hindi examples.

Contact
If a Hindi span appears to be corrupted or clearly a typographical error, flag it to the editor rather than attempting to correct it in translation. Maintain the integrity of the original substrings unless instructed otherwise.
translation_notes: Translate all English text into the target language; leave Hindi spans in Devanagari unchanged.
Preserve punctuation and quotation marks that directly surround Hindi spans.
expected_challenges: Deciding how to render surrounding English punctuation and sentence structure while keeping embedded Hindi unchanged.
Maintaining register and explanatory nuance when the Hindi examples imply cultural formality.

title: Study Guide: Effective Note-Taking for Research Reading
source_text:
# Effective Note-Taking for Research Reading

This study guide outlines practical strategies to read academic texts efficiently and create notes that support later writing, presentations, and literature reviews. It emphasizes structure, consistency, and the use of simple templates so notes remain searchable and reusable.

## Goals before you begin

- Define a clear purpose: Are you reading to summarize, to critique, or to extract methods? Write a one-sentence goal at the top of your file.
- Check metadata: record title, authors, journal, year, and DOI if available (for example, `DOI:10.1000/xyz123`).
- Choose a format: digital (markdown, reference manager notes) or analog (paper notebook). If digital, create a folder structure and standard filename scheme, e.g., `Author_Year_Title.md`.

> Good note-taking reduces the time spent re-reading. Capture your first impressions and a single-sentence summary immediately after reading a full section.

## Before reading: preview and plan

1. Skim the abstract, introduction, and conclusion to understand the main claim.
2. Look at figures and tables to locate key results and methods.
3. Formulate 2–3 guiding questions you expect the paper to answer.

Tip: Use a one-line working hypothesis that you can confirm or revise once you finish the full read.

## During reading: active annotation

- Use consistent symbols: `*` important claim, `?` question, `!` surprising result, and `~` tentative interpretation.
- Annotate margins with short phrases, not full sentences. Aim for keywords that map to your personal taxonomy (e.g., "population", "instrument", "limitation").
- Capture direct quotes sparingly and include page numbers or paragraph markers.

Example inline template: `Key claim:`, `Method:`, `Result:`, `Limitation:`. Maintain these headings in each note so you can quickly filter across files.

### Managing details

- For statistical results, note effect sizes and confidence intervals rather than only p-values.
- For computational methods, write the software and version; include relevant commands like `--config` or `--seed 42` as literal tokens in your notes.

## After reading: synthesize and store

- Write a 3–5 sentence synthesis that answers your initial guiding questions.
- Compose a short takeaways list: three items max, prioritized by importance.
- Link this note to related notes: use internal anchors or tags such as `#summary`, `#methods`, or `#open-questions` so you can search later.

### Writing a quick critique

- Strengths: list 2–4 concrete strengths (novelty, robustness of methods, clarity).
- Weaknesses: list 2–4 limitations and suggest follow-up experiments or analyses.
- Relevance: rate relevance to your project on a 1–5 scale and briefly justify the score.

## Templates and tools

[Jump to Templates](#templates)

- Reference managers: Zotero, Mendeley, and EndNote; export a citation record and attach the PDF.
- Note systems: plain Markdown (recommended), Obsidian, or a LaTeX-based lab notebook.
- Automated extraction: consider using OCR or text-highlighting tools for scanned materials, but always verify extracted text.

## Templates

- Minimal template (Markdown):

  ```
  # Title — Author (Year)
  - Source: [Citation Guide](https://example.org/citation)
  - Purpose:
  - Key claim:
  - Methods:
  - Results:
  - Limitations:
  - Tags: #summary #methods #open-questions
  ```

- Expanded template includes a short annotated bibliography entry and cross-references to related experiments or datasets.

## Quick workflow example

1. Skim and create guiding questions (5–10 minutes).
2. Read for structure and annotate (30–60 minutes, depending on length).
3. Synthesize and tag (10–20 minutes).
4. Add to project literature list and schedule follow-up reading if relevant.

## Final notes

Consistency is more valuable than complexity. Choose a simple system you will actually use, and refine it incrementally. If you collaborate with others, agree on a shared tagging and filename convention.
translation_notes: Preserve Markdown syntax (headings, lists, code fences, links) exactly; translate only visible text.
Do not translate link targets or inline code tokens such as `--config`, `--seed 42`, or `#summary`.
expected_challenges: Rendering concise imperative instructions into another language while keeping brevity and tone.
Maintaining technical tokens, DOI strings, and inline code verbatim within translated sentences.

title: 18th-Century Porcelain Bowl: Exhibit Note
source_text: Exhibit: 18th-Century Porcelain Bowl Overview The object on display is a porcelain bowl dated to the late 18th century, recovered from a secondary merchant context. It is catalogued under Accession No. 23-1974 and attributed to an anonymous workshop associated with the lower Yangtze trade routes. The bowl illustrates transitional decorative motifs blending traditional floral patterns with imported European chinoiserie elements. Provenance and Context Provenance summary: excavated from a riverine deposit near the port district; prior ownership recorded in an estate inventory (1898). Associated finds include glass trade beads, copper nails, and a fragmentary lead token. Current notes indicate a likely manufacturing origin at a major porcelain center (see Kiln Site: Jingdezhen). Description The bowl is wheel-thrown porcelain with underglaze cobalt decoration and an overglaze enamel palette applied in a later firing. Surface examination reveals a fine craquelure pattern concentrated around the rim and interior glaze pooling consistent with high-temperature kilns. Signs of abrasion on the exterior foot suggest prolonged handling or secondary use as a household container. Figure references Figure 1 shows the full vessel in oblique view. Figure 2 illustrates a close-up of the rim where faint factory stamps are visible under fluorescence. Images ![Figure 1: Oblique view of porcelain bowl](images/artifact_23.jpg) Image captions are essential for comparative translation: Figure 1 caption identifies the object and orientation; Figure 2 caption notes the imaging technique and targeted feature. Measurements (Table 1) Table 1 provides precise dimensional data and weight. Translate headers and units consistently. | Measurement | Value | Units | |-------------|-------:|:------| | Maximum diameter | 16.8 | cm | | Height | 6.2 | cm | | Rim thickness (average) | 0.6 | cm | | Base diameter (foot) | 7.4 | cm | | Weight (dry) | 210 | g | Table 1: Dimensions and weight of the porcelain bowl (metric units). Conservation assessment (Table 2) The following table records condition categories used for display decisions. Conservators advise relative humidity control and low-intensity lighting. | Category | Description | Intervention required | |---------|-------------|-----------------------| | Structural integrity | Stable: no active fractures | Monitor annually | | Surface condition | Moderate glaze craze, minor losses | Consolidate flaking enamel | | Residual deposits | River silt adhered in foot ring | Dry cleaning and localized aqueous treatment | Table 2: Conservation categories and recommended interventions. Display and Interpretation When preparing label text for the gallery, emphasize the object's hybrid iconography and trade context. Example interpretive points: the bowl demonstrates material exchange across maritime networks; decorative motifs signal both local production methods and foreign design influence. Handling and Imaging Guidelines - Wear nitrile gloves when handling and support the object at the foot and waist. Avoid contact with decorated surfaces. - Use raking light and UV fluorescence for documentation; do not expose to prolonged UV during display. - High-resolution images and radiographs are stored under accession-linked records (digital filenames use the accession number prefix). Bibliography and Comparative Material Comparative examples include bowls from regional museum collections dated to the same period and published in the 1999 catalog of trade ceramics. For further reading, consult the entry on late Qing export wares and kiln studies for Jingdezhen production techniques. Curatorial Notes Translate table headers and figure captions with priority. Maintain original numeric formatting and units. Preserve file path strings and accession identifiers in the translated text to avoid misrouting digital assets.
translation_notes: Preserve accession numbers, file paths, and exact place names verbatim.
Translate captions and table headers fully; keep numeric formats and units (cm, g) unchanged.
expected_challenges: Maintaining table column alignment and punctuation when translating headers and units
Rendering technical conservation terms and imaging technique names accurately
Translating provenance phrases without altering protected identifiers
Handling bilingual or specialized museum jargon (e.g., craquelure, underglaze, chinoiserie)
