title: Conversational Hindi: Classroom Handout — Introducing Yourself
source_text:
Course: Elementary Conversational Hindi (Lesson 1)
Audience: Middle school or adult learners with no prior Hindi experience
Duration: 45 minutes

Purpose
This handout supports an introductory lesson on basic greetings and self-introduction. It provides instructors with a scripted sequence of activities, sample utterances, and assessment prompts. Use this handout as a classroom guide and adapt timing to your group.

Learning objectives
- Students will recognize and respond to simple Hindi greetings.
- Students will produce a short self-introduction using a fixed template.
- Students will practice pronunciation and register appropriate for casual conversation.

Materials
- Printed phrase cards
- Whiteboard and markers
- Audio playback of model phrases
- Index cards for paired practice

Lesson sequence
1. Warm-up (5 minutes)
   - Begin with a brief introduction in English about the day's goals.
   - Model a greeting exchange with a volunteer: Instructor: नमस्ते, how are you?
     Student: Respond in English first, then try the Hindi phrase.

2. Presentation (10 minutes)
   - Display the model greeting and self-introduction on the board.
   - Play the audio model twice; students listen for rhythm and stress.
   - Write the following model phrases and have the class repeat after you (choral drill):
     - आप कैसे हैं?
     - मेरा नाम अंजली है।
   - Pause after each phrase to correct common errors in consonant clusters and vowel length.

3. Controlled practice (10 minutes)
   - Hand out phrase cards. Students work in pairs to practice the scripted dialogue.
   - Circulate and correct pronunciation and intonation.
   - Encourage students to swap names on the template, maintaining the structure.

4. Communicative activity (15 minutes)
   - Pair students and instruct them to introduce themselves to three classmates, using the template.
   - Each student should both ask and answer at least twice, changing the name and asking follow-up questions in English (e.g., What grade are you in?) to extend the exchange.

Assessment and feedback
- Formative: Observe pair interactions and annotate a checklist for accurate pronunciation of target phrases.
- Summative (exit ticket): Each student writes and pronounces one self-introduction to the teacher.
  Prompts for the exit ticket (in English): Write the Hindi template and fill in your name. Then say it aloud.

Classroom examples (teacher script)
- Teacher to class: "We will learn how to ask someone how they are and how to say your name. Listen and repeat."
- Model exchange (teacher and student): Teacher: आप कैसे हैं?  Student: मैं ठीक हूँ, धन्यवाद.  Teacher: मेरा नाम अंजली है।  Student: मेरा नाम राहुल है।

Differentiation
- For beginners: Provide name cards with common names to reduce cognitive load.
- For advanced beginners: Introduce polite/formal variations and invite students to practice both.

Homework
- Students should record a 30-second audio introducing themselves using the template.
- Encourage students to note differences between English and Hindi word order observed during practice.

Glossary (selected terms)
- greeting: a word or phrase used when meeting someone
- template: a fixed set of words used to form a short utterance
- pronunciation: the way in which a word is spoken
- model phrase examples used in class: आप कैसे हैं? , मेरा नाम अंजली है।

Notes for instructors
- Keep the Hindi phrases intact when sharing handouts with non-Hindi readers to preserve authentic script and punctuation.
- Use the audio model to ensure consistent pronunciation across sections of the course.

End of handout
translation_notes: Translate only the English portions; do not translate, transliterate, or alter the Hindi spans listed in protected_spans.
Preserve original formatting (headings, bullet lists, and example labels) and keep the Hindi spans exactly as they appear, including punctuation.
expected_challenges: Ensuring Hindi script remains unchanged while translating surrounding English content and maintaining sentence flow.
Preserving instructional formatting and the placement of Hindi example phrases within lists and dialogue contexts.

title: Project Nexus v3.1.0 Release Summary
source_text:
# Release Summary — Project Nexus v3.1.0

> This document summarizes the changes in the v3.1.0 release and provides recommended upgrade steps. It is intended for operators, integrators, and translators of user-facing materials.

## Overview

Project Nexus v3.1.0 focuses on performance improvements, expanded observability, and a few targeted breaking changes that simplify long-term maintenance. This release is the culmination of three months of work and includes contributions from 47 community members.

- Release date: 2026-04-01
- Version: v3.1.0
- Release artifacts: binaries, Docker images, and a Helm chart

For the full technical changelog and binary checksums, see the [Full changelog](https://example.com/release-notes).

## Highlights

1. Improved query planner that reduces median latency by ~20% for common workloads.
2. New metrics exported via Prometheus for request tracing and executor latency.
3. A redesigned configuration layout that groups cache and persistence settings together.
4. Updated CLI plugin `nexus-cli` to support scripted upgrades and batch operations.

## Breaking Changes

Be aware of the following breaking changes before upgrading:

- The configuration file schema has changed. `cache.size` is now nested under `performance.cache.size`. Existing `config.yml` files require migration.
- The telemetry endpoint default has moved from `/metrics` to `/v2/metrics`. If you rely on a fixed endpoint, update your scrape configuration.
- The legacy authentication token signer has been removed; any integrations relying on the old signer must migrate to the new `HS256` signer.

If you must postpone migration, refer to the rollback instructions below.

## Deprecations

- The following command options will be removed in v4.0.0: `--legacy-auth`, `--old-cache`. Avoid using these flags in new scripts.
- Deprecated settings will emit warnings on startup; review logs for `DEPRECATION` notices.

## Upgrade Steps

Follow this sequence for a safe upgrade:

1. Backup current configuration and data directories.
2. Audit `config.yml` for `cache.size`, `auth.*`, and `metrics` settings.
3. Update automation scripts to use `nexus-cli upgrade --from <old_version> --to v3.1.0`.
4. Run a dry-run: `nexus-cli upgrade --dry-run` and inspect the proposed changes.
5. Perform the upgrade in a non-production environment first.
6. For mass rollouts, use the orchestration option `--force` only after validation.

Example:

```
# Dry-run the upgrade
nexus-cli upgrade --from 3.0.4 --to v3.1.0 --dry-run

# Apply upgrade (use --force only after validation)
nexus-cli upgrade --from 3.0.4 --to v3.1.0 --apply --force
```

## Known issues {#known-issues}

- Issue #412: Under unusual high-concurrency workloads the new planner may produce a suboptimal plan. A patch is expected in a follow-up point release.
- Issue #428: Some reverse-proxy configurations rewrite the `/v2/metrics` path incorrectly. Temporary workaround: map `/v2/metrics` to the new endpoint explicitly in your proxy rules.

If you encounter problems not listed here, collect logs and relevant metrics and file an issue at the project tracker with the `v3.1.0` tag.

## Rollback

If an immediate rollback is necessary:

1. Restore data and `config.yml` from backups.
2. Redeploy the prior image tag (e.g., `project-nexus:3.0.4`).
3. Reapply any temporary compatibility shims you used during the upgrade.

Note: Downgrades that bypass migration steps may require manual state reconciliation.

## Security Notes

This release includes several security hardenings in the auth subsystem. Administrators should rotate service tokens after upgrade and verify role bindings.

## Contact & Support

- Slack: #project-nexus
- Mailing list: nexus-announce@example.com
- Bugs and feature requests: https://example.com/release-notes

For translation: preserve headings, inline code examples, link targets, and filenames exactly as they appear. Maintain concise, imperative tone used in steps and warnings.
translation_notes: Preserve Markdown syntax and link targets exactly.
Keep code spans, filenames, and version tags verbatim.
expected_challenges: Technical CLI flags and filenames must remain literal.
Differences in sentence ordering for imperative upgrade steps may affect clarity.

title: Exhibit Note: Ceramics of the Eastern Basin (Exhibit 28)
source_text: Exhibit 28: Ceramics of the Eastern Basin Overview This exhibit presents a focused selection of ceramics recovered from riverine and coastal sites in the Eastern Basin, assembled to illustrate technological developments in wheel-thrown pottery and glazing between the 9th and 15th centuries CE. The introductory panel situates the objects within trade networks and production centers; catalog entries and condition reports follow. Curators should note provenance uncertainties discussed in the Historical Context section. Historical Context Archaeological surveys along the Eastern Basin indicate a shift from hand-built coarsewares to refined wheel-thrown ceramics during the late first millennium CE. Glaze recipes and kiln types evolved concurrently, reflecting access to new mineral sources and contact with maritime traders. The objects on display were selected for their representativeness of technique and for evidence of reuse or repair. Key Objects (see Table 1) Table 1: Catalog of selected objects, essential display labels and recommended translations | Object Label | Accession Number | Material and Technique | Provenance Summary | Estimated Date | |--------------|------------------|------------------------|--------------------|----------------| | Glazed bowl (blue-green) | EX28-CER-001 | Wheel-thrown earthenware, lead glaze with copper tint | Northern estuary context, fragmentary rim rejoined | 11th–12th c. CE | | Painted storage jar | EX28-CER-002 | Coarse wheel-thrown clay, manganese paint | Riverbank kiln site, base intact | 13th c. CE | | Unglazed cooking pot | EX28-CER-003 | Hand-built, burnished surface | Domestic refuse pit, heavy soot deposits | 10th–11th c. CE | | Reconstruction sherd set | EX28-CER-004 | Mixed fabrics, adhesive joins visible | Mixed secondary deposition | 14th–15th c. CE | The table header labels above must be translated clearly to preserve curatorial categories. Accession numbers are official identifiers and should remain unchanged in any language. Figure References and Image Files Figure 1 shows the reconsolidated rim and glaze of the glazed bowl referenced above. Use the following image in exhibit labels and digital catalogs; do not rename the file path. ![Glazed bowl, front view](images/EX28_CER_001_front.jpg) Caption for Figure 1: Glazed bowl, EX28-CER-001. Front view highlighting lead-based glaze and minor copper corrosion speckling. Scale bar = 5 cm. Figure 2 demonstrates tool marks on the painted storage jar. This close-up illustrates the paint layering and post-depositional abrasion. Caption for Figure 2: Detail of painted storage jar, EX28-CER-002. Manganese-based slip visible in dashed pattern. Photograph taken under raking light to emphasize incised decoration. Conservation Notes Several objects required minimal stabilization. EX28-CER-001 was consolidated with reversible acrylic adhesive along the rim; adhesive joins are visible under UV light. EX28-CER-004 includes modern adhesive joins that are slated for conservation review prior to loan. Condition labels displayed beneath vitrines should summarize treatment status in concise terms, for example: 'Stabilized; adhesive joins visible; not recommended for handling.' Interpretive Text for Visitors Panels should explain production techniques in accessible language and include a short glossary of technical terms such as 'lead glaze', 'wheel-thrown', and 'burnishing'. Emphasize the regional trade implications and continuity of craft traditions. Cross-reference catalog table entries and figure captions for visitors who consult the printed guide. Labeling Instructions - Translate all captions, table headers, and the main section headings. - Preserve accession numbers and filenames exactly as printed here, for instance EX28-CER-001 and Accession No. 2024.028. - Maintain the image tags and markdown/html structure when integrating into the digital catalog. For reproduction and web display, use the exact image paths provided and ensure scale bars and caption text accompany each image. Curators are reminded to check provenance statements against accession records before finalizing interpretive text.
translation_notes: Translate all section headings, table headers, image captions, and in-line captions; keep technical terms consistent across languages.
Do not alter accession numbers or image file paths; preserve markdown and HTML image tag syntax exactly.
expected_challenges: Technical conservation vocabulary and material terminology (lead glaze, wheel-thrown, burnishing).
Maintaining table structure and header meanings in target language while preserving layout.
Preserving exact accession numbers, artifact codes, and image paths within translated text.

style_label: Documents with code and doc strings
title: YAML Configuration Tutorial with Code and Docstrings
source_text:
Introduction

This short tutorial explains a minimal approach to defining and loading service configuration using YAML. The target audience is developers who need a clear, translatable explanation of recommended keys, environment overrides, and runtime loader behavior. Translate the explanatory prose and any docstrings or inline comments; keep code and configuration keys intact.

1. Minimal configuration example

Below is a minimal YAML configuration for a microservice. Comments starting with "#" describe intent and should be translated along with the surrounding prose. Do not translate the keys themselves (for example, service names or environment variables).

```yaml
# Top-level version marker for compatibility checks
version: "1.0"

services:
  web:
    image: "example/web:latest"
    replicas: 3
    environment:
      APP_ENV: "development"  # Runtime environment: development, staging, production
      database_url: "postgres://user:pass@db:5432/appdb"
    logging:
      level: "info"            # Available: debug, info, warn, error
      format: "json"
    retry_backoff_seconds: 5    # Base backoff for retry logic

  worker:
    image: "example/worker:stable"
    replicas: 2
    environment:
      APP_ENV: "development"
      database_url: "postgres://user:pass@db:5432/appdb"
```

2. Loading the YAML in Python

The loader below demonstrates a simple, safe approach to parse YAML into a configuration object. The docstring explains parameters and return values; translate the docstring but leave parameter names and code intact.

```python
import yaml
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any, Dict

@dataclass
class ServiceConfig:
    name: str
    config: Dict[str, Any]

def load_config(yaml_text: str) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """
    Parse YAML configuration and return a dictionary representation.

    Parameters:
    - yaml_text: raw YAML string to parse

    Returns:
    - A dict where top-level keys are preserved exactly as in the YAML.

    Notes:
    - This loader intentionally preserves unknown keys so callers can apply custom validation.
    - Do not alter key names such as "APP_ENV" or "database_url" in translation.
    """
    data = yaml.safe_load(yaml_text)
    return data
```

3. Overriding via environment (shell example)

Sometimes you want to override a value at deployment time. The common pattern is to use environment variables to override specific keys. The exact variable names are code identifiers and must not be translated.

```sh
# Set the runtime environment to production
export APP_ENV=production
# Provide a production database URL
export database_url="postgres://user:secure@db-prod:5432/proddb"
# Start the service
./start-service --config /etc/myservice/config.yaml
```

4. Tips and common pitfalls

- Keep keys stable: translators should leave keys such as logging.level or retry_backoff_seconds unchanged; only localize comments and user-facing descriptions.
- Short imperative comments like "Enable debug logs" could be ambiguous in some languages; prefer a complete sentence for translation when possible.
- Do not translate literal tokens inside code examples (images, tags, URLs). These are identifiers, not prose.
- Maintain indentation and line breaks in YAML and code blocks. Incorrect indentation can change meaning or break parsers.

5. Example: user-facing description

When your service exposes a human-readable description field, translate that value. For example:

```yaml
service_description: "Handles incoming web requests and routes them to backend workers."
```

In the sentence above, only the quoted description should be translated, not the key name.

Conclusion

This document combines prose, YAML, shell, and Python examples to show best practices for configuration files. Translators should focus on the explanatory text, comments, and docstrings while preserving all code, keys, and identifiers exactly as shown.
translation_notes: Translate all explanatory prose, comments (lines starting with '#'), and docstrings; do not translate code, keys, identifiers, or protected spans.
Preserve code block syntax, indentation, punctuation, and literal tokens such as URLs and image tags.
expected_challenges: Distinguishing between translatable comments/docstrings and non-translatable code or keys
Handling dotted keys (e.g., logging.level) that may be mistaken for sentences
Short imperative comments that lack context and could be translated ambiguously
Maintaining exact indentation and punctuation in YAML and code blocks while translating surrounding text

title: Audio Guide: South Asian Sculpture Gallery - Visitor Orientation and Highlights
source_text:
Welcome to the South Asian Sculpture Gallery. This audio guide will lead you through a curated path of seven key objects, highlighting historical context, materials, and moments of ritual significance. Please begin at the entrance alcove, where the introductory panel and the gallery map are mounted. The greeting at the entrance is नमस्ते, rendered in painted calligraphy to evoke the feel of a temple threshold. Take a moment to look at the map and orient yourself: the route is one-way and designed to progress chronologically from the third century BCE through the late medieval period.

0:00 — Orientation and Entrance Alcove
This section offers an overview of the political and religious currents that shaped the collection. Listen for references to dynasties, trade networks, and liturgical change. The timeline bar beneath the map compresses centuries into a readable visual: each interval highlights a different material—stone, bronze, terracotta—that you will encounter in subsequent displays. Note the museum’s lighting policy here: warmer light for polychrome elements and low glare for metalwork.

1:30 — Object One: Terracotta Mother Goddess (circa 200 BCE)
This modest figure sits in a low vitrine at eye level. The audio description emphasizes her stylized posture and painted traces. Attention to scale is important: although small, the piece served a domestic cultic function, likely positioned on a household shrine. Pay particular attention to the incised pattern on the headdress; this pattern recurs on several figurines in case gallery 4.

3:00 — Object Two: Stone Buddha from the Early Gupta Period
This sculpture stands in a brighter niche. The form emphasizes idealized anatomy and the serene, inward gaze characteristic of the age. The plaque explains the iconographic program—mudrā, urna, ushnisha—and how patrons would have commissioned such images for monastery installation. A short excerpt from a donor inscription appears on the label; translators should preserve script examples verbatim when asked to reproduce text samples.

5:15 — Object Three: Bronze Stupa Reliquary Lid
The lid is displayed slightly elevated to suggest its original placement. The guide describes lost polychromy and evidence of repair. Conservators have provided an audio note on how bronze disease is treated; you will hear a brief technical explanation using accessible language for visitors who wish to understand conservation without specialist jargon.

7:00 — Ritual Practices and Soundscape
At this point in the loop, subtle recorded chant underscores the gallery. The curators have placed a moderation notice near the benches: throughout the space, signs remind visitors of respectful behavior, with a simple phrase in Hindi: कृपया चुप रहें. Where appropriate, visitors are invited to sit and reflect for sixty seconds while the track plays. If you are visiting with children, please supervise them so that the atmosphere of contemplation is maintained.

8:30 — Object Four: Temple Panel Depicting a Narrative Scene
This high-relief panel is packed with action: processions, offerings, and mythic animals. The audio identifies named figures and gives a short synopsis of the scene’s literary source. Translators should preserve capitalized names and titles as proper nouns. Where inscriptions appear on the panel edge, they are transcribed in the catalogue but are not reproduced here.

10:45 — Accessibility and Visitor Tips
If you require a tactile description or large-print materials, request these items at the front desk. The assistant will provide a printed transcript of this audio guide on request. For visitors using headphones, please adjust volume to avoid overlapping with other interpretive tracks. Children’s labels are available along the lower ledge to encourage family engagement.

12:00 — Further Reading and Exit
Before you exit, a small reading table houses catalogue essays and laminated maps with suggested extended routes through the museum. Curators recommend the catalogue essay on transregional exchange for readers seeking deeper context. Thank you for visiting; we hope this presentation has deepened your appreciation of the objects and the communities that made them.
translation_notes: Translate only the English text; leave the Hindi spans exactly as they appear.
Preserve timestamps, labels, and technical terms (e.g., mudrā, urna, ushnisha) in transliteration when appropriate.
expected_challenges: Deciding whether to transliterate specialized Sanskrit terms versus leaving them unchanged; maintain consistency.
Rendering instructions and signage tone (polite imperative) appropriately in the target language while keeping the embedded Hindi intact.

title: Model X-12 Field Controller — Installation and Maintenance
source_text: Overview The Model X-12 field controller is a compact industrial controller used for pump station monitoring and basic motor control. This page covers installation, wiring, and routine maintenance. Before beginning, verify you have the correct unit by checking the label on the rear panel: Model X-12, firmware revision printed on the sticker, and the IP65 rating for outdoor enclosures. Safety Warnings - WARNING W-204: Isolate mains power before servicing. Failure to follow this instruction may result in injury or equipment damage. - Do not open the enclosure while energized. - Do not submerge the device; IP65 protects against water jets but not full immersion. Figure 1 shows the front panel layout and key controls. Figure 1: Front panel of Model X-12 showing status LEDs and the programming port. Tools and Fasteners Required tools include a Phillips #2 screwdriver, torque driver, and wire stripper. Fasteners: use M4x0.7 screws for the mounting flange and torque to 2.0 N·m. Mounting Instructions 1. Select a location that provides adequate ventilation and is within 1 m of the control station to simplify wiring runs. 2. Mount the controller on a flat surface using the four mounting holes on the rear flange. 3. Keep the controller upright as shown in Figure 2; do not invert the unit. ![Mounting Orientation](images/mounting_orientation_x12.png) Figure 2: Recommended mounting orientation (do not invert). Electrical Connections Shut off power before making connections. The terminal block is accessible after removing the front bezel. Refer to the table below for connector assignments—headers are critical to correct operation. | Connector | Pin | Signal | Wire Color | |-----------|-----:|---------------------------|------------| | J1 | 1 | L (AC line) | Brown | | J1 | 2 | N (AC neutral) | Blue | | J2 | 1 | Digital Input 1 (DI1) | White | | J2 | 2 | Digital Input 2 (DI2) | Gray | | J3 | 1 | Relay Output (NO) | Red | | J3 | 2 | Relay Common (COM) | Black | Note: The table headers "Connector", "Pin", "Signal", and "Wire Color" must remain with exact meaning during translation. Colors reference IEC standard wiring where applicable. Configuration and Commissioning - Power up the unit and observe the PWR LED. If the LED does not illuminate, re-check J1 connections. - Enter configuration mode via the programming port (use the supplied USB adapter cable). The default baud rate is 115200, 8N1. - Set digital input parameters according to the site logic. For floating inputs, enable internal pull-ups in the configuration menu. Maintenance Schedule Perform visual inspection quarterly. Look for loose wires, corrosion, or ingress of dust. Clean with a dry brush; do not use solvents. Replace the gasket if the IP65 seal shows wear. Troubleshooting - Symptom: Controller does not power up. Check mains at J1 pins 1 and 2 and verify fuse integrity. - Symptom: Relay not actuating. Confirm jumper placement for relay mode and check J3 wiring. - If the device displays error code E-08, cycle power and consult the service log. If E-08 persists, contact technical support and reference WARNING W-204. Spare Parts and Ordering Order replacement front bezels as PN: X12-BEZEL and fuses as PN: FUSE-250mA. When ordering, specify model and firmware revision. Appendix: Example Wiring Diagram For a sample single-pump controller wiring, see the schematic file: diagrams/x12_single_pump.pdf (attached in the product package). Ensure all connectors match the table above before powering the system.
translation_notes: Translate all visible text, including captions and table headers; keep image file paths and filenames unchanged.
Preserve technical codes, part numbers, and warning codes verbatim (e.g., Model X-12, W-204, E-08).
Keep table layout intact and maintain units and screw torque format (e.g., 2.0 N·m).
expected_challenges: Rendering table headers and alignment correctly while translating compressed technical terms.
Handling protected technical codes and measurements that must not be altered.
Conveying warning tone and imperative safety instructions concisely in target language.
Deciding whether to translate IEC color names versus local wiring color conventions.

title: Collaborative Writing with Git and Markdown: A Study Guide
source_text:
# Collaborative Writing with Git and Markdown: A Study Guide

## Overview

This study guide explains practical workflows for collaborative writing using Git and Markdown. The target reader is anyone familiar with basic text editing who wants to publish or maintain documents collectively—research notes, documentation, or course materials.

Key objectives:

- Understand a simple branching workflow for writers.
- Learn document organization conventions in Markdown.
- Practice common Git operations and conflict resolution.

> Tip: Start small. Use a single repository for one project rather than multiple sparse repos.

## Prerequisites

Before following the exercises you should have:

1. A Git client installed and configured.
2. A text editor that supports Markdown preview.
3. A remote repository (e.g., on GitHub, GitLab, or a self-hosted server).

Links and resources:

- Official Git documentation: https://git-scm.com/doc
- Markdown guide: https://www.markdownguide.org

## Quick Setup (anchor: setup)

Follow these steps to create your local copy:

```bash
# Clone the repository
`git clone` https://example.com/your-repo.git
cd your-repo
```

Create or update the main documentation file, typically `README.md` at the repository root. Keep this file concise: project purpose, how to contribute, and pointers to deeper sections.

## Branching workflow

A simple branching model for writing is:

- main: stable published text.
- draft/*: working drafts and edits.
- review/*: proposed revisions awaiting review.

Example sequence:

1. Create a draft branch: `git checkout -b draft/section-3`
2. Write and commit changes locally.
3. Push and open a merge request / pull request for review.

When reviewing, use inline comments in the hosting service and update the draft branch with suggested edits, or discuss until consensus.

## Writing conventions and Markdown tips

Use clear, consistent conventions so multiple contributors produce uniform output.

- Headings: Use thematic headings (#, ##, ###) to represent the document hierarchy.
- Code and filenames: Use inline code for commands and filenames, e.g., `config.yml` or `make build`.
- Links and anchors: Keep targets intact; do not localize URLs or anchor names.
- Lists: Prefer bulleted lists for unordered items and numbered lists for procedures.

Example: Keep admonitions short.

> Warning: Avoid mixing presentation markup (HTML) and Markdown unless necessary for specific layout needs.

## Resolving conflicts and best practices

Conflicts commonly occur when multiple writers edit the same paragraph. Best practices:

- Communicate intent in the merge request description.
- Break large edits into smaller, focused commits.
- Rebase or merge promptly to minimize diverging histories.

A typical conflict resolution workflow:

1. Update your branch from `main` (`git fetch` + `git rebase origin/main` or `git merge origin/main`).
2. Resolve conflicts in your editor, test the rendered Markdown locally.
3. Run a quick preview (editor preview or `pandoc`/static site build) to confirm layout.

## Accessibility and localization

When preparing documents for translation:

- Keep sentences concise and avoid idiomatic expressions that do not translate well.
- Mark protected or untranslated spans explicitly. For example, short in-line phrases or labels may be left in the original language: Por favor revisa (Spanish fragment often used as a placeholder).
- Maintain locale identifiers (e.g., en-US) and filenames exactly.

## Example checklist before merging

- [ ] Spelling and grammar checked.
- [ ] Headings reflect the intended hierarchy.
- [ ] Links and image paths valid.
- [ ] Code snippets tested where applicable.
- [ ] Merge request description explains the purpose and scope.

## Further reading and templates

For document templates and contributor guides, collect examples in a `docs/` directory and reference them from `README.md`. A CONTRIBUTING.md should describe the workflow, formatting rules, and review expectations.

If you're managing translations, include a localization matrix and style guide for translators.

---

If you have questions about a specific step, refer to the linked resources or open an issue in the repository to start a discussion.
translation_notes: Do not translate text inside backticks (inline code) or fenced code blocks.
Preserve Markdown structure, link targets (URLs), and anchor names exactly.
Do not translate filenames or locale tags such as README.md or en-US.
Leave protected spans unchanged.
expected_challenges: Translating technical terms while preserving Markdown syntax and inline code.
Maintaining the imperative tone of instructions and checklist items.
Handling short embedded multilingual fragments (e.g., 'Por favor revisa') without altering meaning.
Ensuring links, anchors, and filenames remain exact and functional after translation.

title: Proof outline: Spectral theorem for compact self-adjoint operators
source_text:
This document outlines a standard proof of the spectral theorem for compact self-adjoint operators on a separable Hilbert space. The goal is to convey the key steps and variational arguments while preserving the essential equations and labels used in a formal proof.

Let H be a separable Hilbert space with inner product \(\langle \cdot,\cdot\rangle\) linear in the first argument. Let \(T: H\to H\) be a compact, self-adjoint operator. We recall the Rayleigh quotient
\begin{equation}\label{eq:rayleigh}
R(x) = \frac{\langle Tx,x\rangle}{\langle x,x\rangle}, \qquad x\in H\setminus\{0\}.
\end{equation}
The extremal values of \(R\) on finite-dimensional subspaces play a central role. The proof proceeds in several structured steps.

Theorem 1 (Spectral theorem, outline). Let \(T\) be compact and self-adjoint. Then there exists an orthonormal sequence \(\{e_n\}_{n=1}^\infty\) of eigenvectors of \(T\) with corresponding real eigenvalues \(\{\lambda_n\}_{n=1}^\infty\) such that \(\lambda_n\to 0\) and
\[
T = \sum_{n=1}^\infty \lambda_n \langle \cdot,e_n\rangle e_n
\]
in the operator norm topology on the closure of the span of \(\{e_n\}\). Furthermore, the nonzero spectrum consists of the \(\lambda_n\) with finite multiplicity.

Lemma 2 (Existence of a maximal Rayleigh quotient). For the compact self-adjoint \(T\), the supremum
\[
\lambda_1 = \sup_{\|x\|=1} \langle Tx,x\rangle
\]
is attained, and the maximizer is an eigenvector of \(T\).

Proof outline. Take a maximizing sequence \(\{x_k\}\) with \(\|x_k\|=1\) and \(\langle Tx_k,x_k\rangle\to\lambda_1\). Compactness of \(T\) implies \(Tx_k\) has a convergent subsequence. Use polarization and weak compactness to show the subsequence converges strongly to some \(x_1\) with \(\|x_1\|=1\), and then verify \(Tx_1=\lambda_1 x_1\). See labels \eqref{eq:rayleigh} and \eqref{eq:orthogonality} for the relevant relations.

Orthogonality and reduction. Once \(e_1\) is produced, restrict \(T\) to the orthogonal complement \(\{e_1\}^\perp\). The restricted operator is again compact and self-adjoint; apply the same variational argument to obtain \(e_2\), then iterate. One must show eigenvalues satisfy
\begin{equation}\label{eq:ordering}
|\lambda_1| \ge |\lambda_2| \ge \cdots, \qquad \lambda_n\to 0.
\end{equation}
This monotonicity follows from the minimax characterization: define recursively
\[
\lambda_k = \sup_{\substack{\|x\|=1,\\ x\perp e_1,\dots,e_{k-1}}} \langle Tx,x\rangle.
\]

Completeness. To prove that the span of eigenvectors is dense in the closure of the range of \(T\), assume by contradiction that there exists \(y\) orthogonal to all eigenvectors but such that \(Ty\neq 0\). Use compactness to construct a sequence of finite-rank approximations \(T_n\to T\) in operator norm. Each \(T_n\) has a finite orthonormal eigenbasis; project \(y\) and derive a contradiction with orthogonality and approximation.

Corollary 3 (Spectral decomposition for finite multiplicity). If zero is the only accumulation point of the spectrum, then \(T\) admits the decomposition
\[
T = \sum_{n=1}^\infty \lambda_n P_n,
\]
where \(P_n\) are the orthogonal projections onto the eigenspaces corresponding to \(\lambda_n\).

Sketch of derivations. Many technical steps reduce to careful estimates: use the identity
\begin{equation}\label{eq:orthogonality}
\langle T(x+y),x+y\rangle - \langle Tx,x\rangle - \langle Ty,y\rangle = 2\Re\langle Tx,y\rangle
\end{equation}
and polarization to transfer quadratic estimates to bilinear ones. Compactness is invoked precisely to upgrade weak convergence to strong convergence for subsequences of \(\{Tx_k\}\).

Figure 1: A schematic spectrum of a compact self-adjoint operator showing a discrete set of nonzero eigenvalues accumulating only at 0.

This outline omits several epsilon arguments and multiplicity bookkeeping; consult a full text for detailed epsilon-delta steps. Nevertheless, the variational characterization, orthogonality reduction, and compactness-based extraction are the core mechanisms that make the spectral theorem work in this setting.
translation_notes: Preserve all LaTeX math, commands, labels (e.g. \begin{equation}, \label{...}, \lambda_n) exactly; translate only surrounding prose.
Do not alter or translate mathematical expressions inside \( ... \), \[ ... \], or equation environments; keep environment names (theorem, lemma, proof) and labels intact.
expected_challenges: Maintaining LaTeX command integrity while translating adjacent English sentences.
Rendering mathematical terminology (e.g. "compact", "self-adjoint", "orthogonality", "Rayleigh quotient") accurately in target language without disturbing formulas or labels.

title: my_service module: client utilities and configuration
source_text:
'''my_service.client

Client helpers and utilities for interacting with the MyService API.

This module provides a lightweight wrapper around HTTP calls, built-in retry
logic, and a small configuration loader. Translate the prose, docstrings, and
inline comments; do not translate code identifiers or literal configuration keys.
'''

# Module-level constants
CONFIG_PATH = '/etc/my_service/config.yaml'  # path to the main configuration file
DEFAULT_TIMEOUT = 30  # default network timeout in seconds

class MyServiceError(Exception):
    """Base exception for MyService-related errors.

    Translate this docstring. Explain what situations may raise this error,
    e.g., configuration problems or network failures.
    """
    pass


def load_config(path=CONFIG_PATH):
    '''Load YAML configuration from the given path.

    Parameters:
    - path: str, path to the YAML configuration file. Use CONFIG_PATH for the
      default system location.

    Returns:
    - dict: parsed configuration mapping. Keys include 'endpoint' and
      'api_key'.

    Raise MyServiceError on missing or invalid configuration.
    '''
    # Note: this loader intentionally does not validate all fields. Callers
    # should inspect the returned dict and provide defaults as needed.
    import yaml
    try:
        with open(path, 'r') as fh:
            cfg = yaml.safe_load(fh)
    except FileNotFoundError:
        raise MyServiceError(f'Configuration file not found: {path}')
    except Exception as exc:
        raise MyServiceError(f'Error loading configuration: {exc}')
    return cfg or {}


def request_with_retry(session, method, url, retries=3, timeout=DEFAULT_TIMEOUT):
    """Perform an HTTP request with simple exponential backoff retry.

    Parameters:
    - session: requests.Session-like object with .request(method, url, ...)
    - method: HTTP method string, e.g., 'GET' or 'POST'
    - url: full request URL
    - retries: number of retry attempts on transient errors
    - timeout: request timeout in seconds (DEFAULT_TIMEOUT used by default)

    Returns the response object on success. Raises MyServiceError on permanent
    failure.
    """
    backoff = 1
    for attempt in range(1, retries + 1):
        try:
            # Perform the HTTP request. Preserve headers and payload handling
            resp = session.request(method, url, timeout=timeout)
            if 200 <= resp.status_code < 300:
                return resp
            # Treat 5xx responses as transient
            if 500 <= resp.status_code < 600:
                raise RuntimeError(f'Server error: {resp.status_code}')
            # Other non-2xx responses are considered permanent failures
            raise MyServiceError(f'Unexpected response: {resp.status_code}')
        except (MyServiceError,):
            # re-raise known MyServiceError without retrying
            raise
        except Exception as exc:
            # Translators: this comment explains the retry behavior and should
            # be translated along with surrounding text. The code logic must
            # remain unchanged.
            if attempt == retries:
                raise MyServiceError(f'Failed after {attempt} attempts: {exc}')
            # simple sleep for backoff; keep numeric logic as-is
            import time
            time.sleep(backoff)
            backoff *= 2


# Example usage and CLI snippet to demonstrate integration
# Translate the prose but keep the command exact.

# Installation
# Run the following command in your shell to install the package:
# ```shell
# pip install my-service>=1.2.0
# ```

# Configuration example (YAML):
# ```yaml
# endpoint: "https://api.example.com/v1"
# api_key: "REPLACE_WITH_KEY"
# timeout: 20
# log_level: info
# ```

# Quick example of client code (do not execute as-is):
# ```python
# from my_service.client import load_config, request_with_retry
# import requests
#
# cfg = load_config()
# session = requests.Session()
# url = cfg.get('endpoint') + '/status'
# resp = request_with_retry(session, 'GET', url)
# print(resp.json())
# ```

# Notes for translators:
# - Translate explanatory prose, comments, and docstrings.
# - Preserve code blocks, identifiers, and literal keys such as CONFIG_PATH
#   and DEFAULT_TIMEOUT.
translation_notes: Translate all prose, docstrings, and inline comments; do not translate code identifiers.
Preserve code syntax, indentation, fenced code blocks, YAML keys, and literal command lines.

title: Instructions for Completing the Community Enrollment Form (Bilingual Examples)
source_text:
Overview
This document explains how to complete the Community Enrollment Form that supports both English and Hindi examples. The main body of the form should be filled in English unless a field specifically requests or shows a Hindi example. Two Hindi example entries are included below as reference and should be copied exactly when present as examples for the local data sample.

Before you begin
Read each field label carefully. Some fields are mandatory, some are optional. If an example shows a Hindi entry, that example demonstrates how a respondent in Hindi might write their information; do not change the Hindi script when you reproduce sample entries.

Key examples (do not alter these Hindi samples)
For NAME field, an example entry is shown as: नाम: राजेश कुमार
For ADDRESS field, an example entry is shown as: पता: १२३ मुख्य सड़क, दिल्ली
These samples are intentionally in Devanagari to illustrate local formats for names and addresses. When translating the English help text into another language, keep these two Hindi strings exactly as shown. Do not transliterate or translate the names or the address components.

Field-by-field guidance
1. Name (required)
- Enter the full name as it appears on ID documents. Use uppercase for the given name and family name if your system style requires it.
- If a Hindi name example appears, copy it verbatim into example boxes and do not change its script.

2. Date of birth (required)
- Use the format DD/MM/YYYY unless another format is specified on the form. Avoid month names unless explicitly requested, to reduce ambiguity for multilingual users.

3. Address (required)
- Provide the full postal address including house number, street, locality, city, and postal code.
- The Hindi example demonstrates typical ordering and script for local entries. Again, do not modify the Hindi example when including it in translated help text.

4. Contact phone and email (conditional)
- List a primary phone number and an alternate. Use international format if available (+CountryCode LocalNumber).
- Email should be provided in ASCII characters and left lowercase if possible.

5. Emergency contact (recommended)
- Provide name and phone number; indicate relationship. This is particularly helpful in case of health or safety incidents during program activities.

Special cases and tips
- Multiple names: If a person uses both a formal legal name and a commonly used name, include both fields when available. For instance, Legal Name (as on ID) and Also Known As (AKA).
- Diacritics and non-Latin scripts: Preserve the original script in example sections. Do not attempt to convert Devanagari to Latin characters in examples provided by respondents.
- Abbreviations: Avoid ambiguous abbreviations in address lines (e.g., use “Street” instead of “St.” unless the local convention clearly prefers the abbreviation).

Common errors to avoid
- Leaving required fields blank. The form will validate some fields, but not all; always verify completeness.
- Mixing date formats. Select a single date format and apply it consistently across all instructions and samples.
- Altering sample scripts. The Hindi samples above are intentionally preserved to show native script usage. Translators and localizers should keep them exactly as given.

Glossary (terms used in the form)
- Primary contact: the first person we will contact in case of need.
- Alternate contact: backup contact if the primary is unavailable.

If you are preparing localized help materials, translate all English paragraphs but leave the two Hindi example strings unchanged. Doing so will ensure clarity for users who read both languages and will preserve the cultural and script-specific examples needed for accurate data entry.
translation_notes: Translate only the English sentences; do not alter or translate the listed Hindi example strings.
Preserve punctuation, line breaks, and formatting around protected Hindi spans exactly as in the source.
expected_challenges: Deciding whether to transliterate Hindi names or keep them in Devanagari—must remain unchanged.
Consistent date and number formatting across locales when translating instructions.
Maintaining clarity of field order and labels when target language has different syntactic norms.

title: Spring 2025 Wetland Field Survey Summary
source_text: Field Survey Summary: Spring 2025 Wetland Assessment Overview This document summarizes field observations collected during the spring 2025 wetland assessment across three monitored sites. The survey team recorded vegetation composition, basic water chemistry, and microtopography along fixed transects. Results are intended to inform a short-term management plan and to provide baseline data for subsequent seasonal monitoring. Methods Sampling took place between 2025-04-10 and 2025-04-18. At each site we established a 50 m transect, with sample points every 5 m. At each point we recorded: dominant vegetation, water depth (m), surface pH (portable meter), and a brief photographic record. Field tags and short hand notes were entered on waterproof forms; see tag prefix "Transect_Notes" used in the field notebooks for quick cross-reference. Equipment: handheld GPS (accuracy ±3 m), calibrated pH meter (last calibrated 2025-04-09), handheld depth rod, digital camera. Summary Table of Observations | Site | Coordinates (WGS84) | Dominant Vegetation | Water pH | Max Depth (m) | Field Notes | |--------|------------------------|----------------------------|----------|---------------|-------------------------------------| | Site A | 45.1230 N, -71.4560 W | Carex spp., Juncus effusus | 6.8 | 0.45 | Fringe marsh; sediment organic-rich | | Site B | 45.1255 N, -71.4592 W | Phragmites australis | 7.1 | 0.60 | Dense invasive stands present | | Site C | 45.1278 N, -71.4521 W | Sphagnum, Eriophorum | 5.9 | 0.20 | Bog-like conditions, acidic | Please preserve the table layout and headers when translating; headers carry technical meaning (e.g., "Water pH", "Max Depth (m)"). The coordinate format (WGS84) should remain unchanged. Figures and Photographic Records Figure 1 shows a representative transect photo taken at Site B. Observers noted the dominance of tall Phragmites stems encroaching on the open water channel in the foreground. The image file name is included to allow cross-referencing with the raw image archive. Figure 1: Wetland Transect Photo (file: images/transect1.jpg) — invasive Phragmites australis in foreground; water channel visible at center-left. Elevation and microtopography were profiled at 1 m increments at the marsh centerline. A simple elevation profile chart is provided for orientation; see the schematic image and caption. ![Elevation profile](images/elev_profile.png) Figure 2: Elevation profile schematic (file: images/elev_profile.png). Vertical exaggeration 3x; datum = local benchmark BM-2025. Key Observations - Vegetation: Site B shows heavy Phragmites cover likely reducing open-water habitat. Site C retains peat-forming Sphagnum mats and has the lowest measured pH, consistent with bog conditions. - Water chemistry: pH ranges from 5.9 (Site C) to 7.1 (Site B). Values were measured in situ with a portable meter; field blanks and calibration logs are available in the project folder. - Hydrology: Maximum depths are shallow (0.2–0.6 m). Surface connectivity to the downstream channel appears limited at Site C during the sampling window. Recommended Actions 1. Prioritize mechanical thinning and follow-up herbicide treatment for Phragmites at Site B to restore open-water habitat. 2. Install three permanent staff gauges at Sites A–C to improve temporal resolution of depth measurements. Record gauge IDs and positions in all future visits using the Transect_Notes prefix. 3. Repeat pH and depth sampling in midsummer and autumn to assess seasonal variation. Appendix: Field tags and file references All field photos and raw data are stored using the file naming convention: SITEID_YYYYMMDD_point#. Example: "B_20250412_05.jpg". Cross-reference photographic records with table entries and the captions that include the image file paths.
translation_notes: Translate all captions and table headers; preserve file paths and coordinate format exactly.
Keep measurement units (e.g., "m", "pH") as-is and convert only numeric formatting if target language requires (e.g., decimal comma).
expected_challenges: Rendering table markup and preserving header-cell alignment during translation
Translating botanical names (Phragmites australis, Carex spp.) — keep Latin names unchanged
Handling embedded file paths and image tags without altering them
Maintaining technical units and coordinate notation (WGS84) while adapting surrounding text

title: April Community Newsletter: Village Festival & Volunteer Spotlight
source_text:
Community Association Monthly — April Edition

Welcome to the April issue of our neighborhood newsletter. This month we focus on the preparations for the spring harvest festival, ongoing volunteer projects, and a short language corner that highlights a community motto. Please read the full schedule and consider signing up for tasks where help is needed.

Upcoming Events

1) Spring Harvest Festival — Our signature annual event is returning on the first Saturday of May. Event name: हमारा गाँव उत्सव. The festival will be held at Riverside Park from 10:00 AM to 6:00 PM and will include craft stalls, a food fair, children's activities, and a local music stage. Local vendors are encouraged to apply for booths; priority will be given to small businesses from within the neighborhood. We are coordinating with the parks department for additional waste stations and extra seating.

2) Community Clean-Up — Saturday, April 20, 8:00 AM. Meet at the community center for sign-in and supply pickup. We will divide teams to cover the riverbank, playground, and main street. Gloves, bags, and high-visibility vests will be provided. Refreshments and a small appreciation breakfast will be served to volunteers.

3) Senior Tech Hour — Weekly on Wednesdays, 2:00–4:00 PM. Drop-in help for seniors who want one-on-one assistance with smartphones, video calls, or setting up online appointments.

Volunteer Spotlight

This month we celebrate Anika Shah, who has coordinated our tutoring program for the last two years. Anika started a weekend literacy circle that now serves over 40 children across three age groups. Her approach blends reading aloud, paired reading, and interactive storytelling. Anika organizes mentor training sessions and tracks progress through a simple workbook system. If you have materials to donate — books for ages 4–12, markers, or notebooks — please bring them to the community center labeled "Tutoring Materials."

Feature: How to Run a Successful Stall at the Festival

- Plan your layout: 6 by 8 feet is a comfortable stall size; include a clear area for point-of-sale and a display area.
- Pricing: Offer at least one 'budget' option under $5 to keep things accessible.
- Permits and labels: Ensure food vendors have up-to-date health permits and clear ingredient labels for allergy awareness.
- Volunteer shift management: Limit shifts to 3–4 hours to avoid burnout, and arrange a 30-minute overlap for smooth handoffs.

Language Corner

We include a small bilingual element each month to reflect our diverse community. Two phrases we often hear around the neighborhood are the festival title and a guiding principle. Please note these phrases are presented in their original script to honor local usage and should remain unchanged in translation.

Festival title: हमारा गाँव उत्सव
Community motto: "अतिथि देवो भव" — a traditional phrase often invoked to express hospitality. When translating the newsletter into another language, translators should preserve these original script phrases exactly as written here.

Resources and Contacts

- Community Center: 123 Main Street, (555) 234-7890, info@community.org
- Volunteer Coordinator: Maya Li, volunteer@community.org
- Event Registration: forms.community.org/ourvillage

Closing Remarks

As we prepare for warmer days and more outdoor gatherings, your participation makes a tangible difference. Whether you can donate an hour to the clean-up, set up a stall at हमारा गाँव उत्सव, or simply stop by Senior Tech Hour, every bit of involvement strengthens our neighborhood. We will publish a post-event recap and photo gallery in the June issue; please share your best photos with the communications team.

Thank you for being an engaged neighbor. See you around the park, and remember the spirit that guides many of our volunteers: "अतिथि देवो भव."

style_label: Documents with code and doc strings
title: Sessionization and Aggregates: SQL Analytics Note with Example Queries
source_text:
/*
Analytics note: sessionization patterns for web events

This file documents a recommended approach to generate session identifiers
for event streams stored in a typical analytics table. The prose and
comments explain intent and assumptions; translators should translate
explanatory text but preserve SQL keywords, table names, column names,
string literals, and code formatting.
*/

-- Table assumptions:
--   Table name: user_activity
--   Primary event timestamp column: EVENT_TIME (UTC, type TIMESTAMP)
--   User identifier column: user_id
--   Session break threshold: 30 minutes

-- The query below builds session_id using a gap-and-island approach.
-- It relies on window functions and a cumulative sum to assign increasing
-- session buckets when the gap exceeds the threshold.

```sql
-- Generate session identifiers per user based on a 30-minute inactivity gap
WITH events AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    EVENT_TIME,
    LAG(EVENT_TIME) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY EVENT_TIME) AS prev_time
  FROM user_activity
), flagged AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    EVENT_TIME,
    CASE
      WHEN prev_time IS NULL THEN 1
      WHEN EVENT_TIME > prev_time + INTERVAL '30' MINUTE THEN 1
      ELSE 0
    END AS is_new_session
  FROM events
), sessionized AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    EVENT_TIME,
    SUM(is_new_session) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY EVENT_TIME ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) AS session_id
  FROM flagged
)
SELECT
  user_id,
  session_id,
  MIN(EVENT_TIME) AS session_start,
  MAX(EVENT_TIME) AS session_end,
  COUNT(*) AS event_count
FROM sessionized
GROUP BY user_id, session_id
ORDER BY user_id, session_start;
```

"""
Python helper: consolidate_sessions
This helper consumes rows from the SQL query above and emits a summary
object per session. The docstring and inline comments explain expected
fields and edge cases. Translators should translate prose but leave code
structure and identifiers intact.
"""

def consolidate_sessions(rows):
    """Consolidate SQL rows into session summaries.

    Args:
        rows: iterable of dict-like objects with keys: user_id, session_id,
              session_start, session_end, event_count.

    Returns:
        A list of dictionaries where each dictionary contains:
          - user_id: original user identifier
          - session_id: numeric bucket per user (1-based)
          - duration_seconds: session_end - session_start in seconds
          - event_count: number of events in the session

    Notes:
        If session_end equals session_start, duration_seconds will be 0.
        Do not attempt to merge sessions across users.
    """
    summaries = []
    for r in rows:
        # Normalize missing values; treat NULL as zero-length session
        start = r.get('session_start')
        end = r.get('session_end')
        if start is None or end is None:
            duration = 0
        else:
            duration = int((end - start).total_seconds())
        summaries.append({
            'user_id': r.get('user_id'),
            'session_id': r.get('session_id'),
            'duration_seconds': duration,
            'event_count': r.get('event_count', 0)
        })
    return summaries

# Example usage (shell):
# Run the SQL query in your analytics warehouse and then pipe results into
# a small Python script that calls consolidate_sessions.
# psql -d analytics_db -f session_query.sql | python process_sessions.py

-- Additional considerations:
-- 1) Time zones: EVENT_TIME is assumed to be normalized to UTC. If your
--    data contains local timestamps, convert them to UTC before sessionizing.
-- 2) Out-of-order events: If events can arrive out of order, consider
--    applying a watermark or re-windowing step before computing session_id.
-- 3) Large users: For users with extremely high event volumes, you may
--    want to materialize intermediate results to avoid window-function
--    memory pressure.

-- Quick sanity check: return one sample user with up to 100 recent events
SELECT * FROM user_activity WHERE user_id = 'sample_user' ORDER BY EVENT_TIME DESC LIMIT 100;
translation_notes: Translate all explanatory prose, comments (/* ... */ and -- ...), and docstrings, but do not translate code, SQL keywords, identifiers, or string literals.
Preserve code block fences, indentation, punctuation, and any inline examples exactly as in the source.
expected_challenges: Distinguishing between text that should be translated (comments and docstrings) and code elements that must be preserved (SQL keywords, identifiers like user_id and session_id).
Rendering precise technical terms such as 'gap-and-island', 'window functions', and 'watermark' accurately in the target language while keeping original code intact.

title: Growth Response of Marine Microalgae to Controlled Thermal Gradients
source_text:
Abstract
This report summarizes a controlled laboratory study of the growth response of a marine microalga species under incremental temperature increases. The goal was to quantify short-term changes in growth rate and pH stabilization across a 5°C gradient and to document the experimental setup for reproducibility. Results are presented in narrative form, supported by a summary table and a schematic image of the apparatus.

Introduction
Marine microalgae play a critical role in coastal carbon cycling. Small shifts in temperature can alter metabolic rates and affect community composition. We hypothesized that a 5°C increase would produce measurable changes in growth rate and pH buffering within 72 hours.

Methods
Cultures were acclimated for 48 hours at baseline temperature (18°C) in continuous light (100 μmol photons m−2 s−1). Triplicate flasks for each temperature treatment (18°C, 20°C, 22°C, 24°C, 23°C) were incubated in a gradient-controlled chamber. Temperature increments were imposed sequentially to avoid thermal shock. Measurements were taken at 24, 48, and 72 hours.

Instrumentation and setup are shown in Figure 1. Temperature control used a PID-regulated heater and thermometer probe (± 0.1°C accuracy). Dissolved pH was measured with a calibrated glass electrode; growth rate was determined from optical density (OD750) converted to biomass using a species-specific calibration curve.

![Figure 1: Experimental setup with gradient chamber and sampling ports.](images/fig_experiment_setup.png)
Figure 1: Schematic of the gradient chamber and sampling configuration. The layout indicates inlet/outlet ports, heater placement, and probe locations to ensure consistent sampling across replicates.

Results
Table 1 summarizes the mean growth rates and pH values recorded at 72 hours. Reported values include variability across triplicates expressed as Mean ± SD, which is critical for interpreting biological significance.

| Sample ID | Temperature (°C) | pH (72 h) | Growth rate (mm/day) (Mean ± SD) |
|-----------|------------------:|----------:|----------------------------------:|
| A1        |             18.0  |      8.10 |                         0.45 ± 0.03 |
| B1        |             20.0  |      8.05 |                         0.52 ± 0.04 |
| C1        |             22.0  |      7.98 |                         0.61 ± 0.05 |
| D1        |             23.0  |      7.92 |                         0.68 ± 0.06 |
| E1        |             24.0  |      7.85 |                         0.72 ± 0.08 |

Table 1: Summary of measurements at 72 hours for each temperature treatment. Note that growth rate increases nonlinearly with temperature, while pH shows a gradual decline.

Discussion
The data indicate a positive correlation between temperature and growth rate within the experimental range. The largest relative increase occurred between 20°C and 24°C, suggesting a thermally driven acceleration of metabolic activity. The pH decline is consistent with increased respiration and CO2 production at higher temperatures; however, the changes are modest and remain within the buffering capacity of the medium.

Potential artefacts include slight heterogeneity in light distribution across the chamber and minor temperature drift at the highest setpoint. These were monitored and logged; timestamps and raw probe outputs are included in the supplemental files.

Conclusion
This controlled experiment demonstrates that small thermal increments can substantially affect short-term growth rates of the tested microalga. The combination of clearly labeled apparatus imagery and the tabular presentation of Mean ± SD values supports reproducibility and facilitates translation of protocols to different laboratory settings.

Acknowledgments
We thank the facility technical staff for assistance in maintaining the gradient chamber and the analytical staff for pH probe calibration.

References
Selected references on microalgal temperature responses and methods are provided in the bibliography. Raw datasets and calibration curves are available upon request.
translation_notes: Preserve file names, figure labels, and table identifiers exactly as written (e.g., image filenames).
Keep scientific units and symbols unchanged (°, μmol, mm/day, ±, etc.) and translate surrounding words only.
expected_challenges: Rendering the table layout and preserving the 'Mean ± SD' notation while translating headers and units.
Translating figure caption and technical terms without altering file paths or schematic references (e.g., image filenames and Figure 1).

title: Getting Started Tutorial: Project Phoenix CLI
source_text:
# Getting Started with Project Phoenix CLI

This tutorial walks you through installing, configuring, and running the Project Phoenix command-line interface (CLI). It is intended for developers and operators who need a lightweight workflow for local development and simple deployments.

> Tip: read the official documentation for advanced topics: https://project-phoenix.example/docs

## Table of contents

- [Prerequisites](#prerequisites)
- [Install](#install)
- [Configuration](#configuration)
- [Basic commands](#basic-commands)
- [Examples](#examples)
- [Troubleshooting](#troubleshooting)
- [Further reading](#further-reading)

## Prerequisites

Before you begin, ensure you have the following:

- A POSIX-compatible shell (bash, zsh) or Windows PowerShell
- Git installed and available on your PATH
- Node.js v14+ or an alternative runtime if you use the JavaScript tooling
- Approximately 200 MB free disk space

If you are behind a corporate proxy, configure your environment variables (`HTTP_PROXY`, `HTTPS_PROXY`) before running the installer.

## Install

Installation is intentionally simple. Clone the repository and run the setup script:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/example/project-phoenix.git
cd project-phoenix
./scripts/setup.sh
```

The setup script will place the `phoenix` binary into `~/.local/bin` by default. Make sure that directory is on your PATH.

If you prefer a single-file installer, download from the releases page and make the binary executable:

```bash
curl -L -o phoenix https://project-phoenix.example/releases/phoenix-latest
chmod +x phoenix
mv phoenix ~/.local/bin/
```

## Configuration

Configuration is read from a YAML file named `config.yaml` in your project root or from `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/phoenix/config.yaml`.

[Configuration](#configuration) supports these top-level keys:

- `server`: host/port settings
- `database`: connection strings and pooling options
- `features`: feature flags (boolean)

Example `config.yaml` snippet:

```yaml
server:
  host: "127.0.0.1"
  port: 8080
database:
  url: "postgres://user:pass@localhost:5432/phoenix"
features:
  new_ui: true
```

Inline overrides are available with the `--set` flag for quick experiments: `phoenix --set server.port=9090 start`.

## Basic commands

Here are the common commands you will use frequently:

1. `phoenix start` — Start the local server in foreground.
2. `phoenix stop` — Stop a running server.
3. `phoenix status` — Show current status and active configuration.
4. `phoenix migrate` — Apply database migrations.

Example: start in production mode with a specific environment variable:

```bash
PHOENIX_ENV=production phoenix start --env=production --detach
```

Common flags:

- `--env` — choose environment (development, staging, production)
- `--detach` — run as a background process
- `--verbose` — enable verbose logging

## Examples

Local development workflow:

- Create a new branch, edit `config.yaml` with a local DB URL.
- Run `phoenix migrate` to apply schema changes.
- Launch with `phoenix start` and visit `http://localhost:8080`.

Deploying a simple containerized app:

- Build the image using the provided Dockerfile: `docker build -t phoenix-app .`
- Run with `docker run -e PHOENIX_ENV=production -p 8080:8080 phoenix-app`

## Troubleshooting

If the server fails to start, check the following:

- Is `config.yaml` syntactically valid YAML? Use a linter or `yamllint`.
- Are required ports already in use? Use `lsof -i :8080` to inspect.
- Does the database allow connections from the host? Check firewall and credentials.

If logs are not helpful, start with `--verbose` and consult `~/.local/share/phoenix/logs/phoenix.log`.

> If you encounter permission errors during install, try installing to a directory you own or use a dedicated user account.

## Best practices

- Keep sensitive credentials out of `config.yaml`; use environment variables or a secrets manager.
- Pin migration versions in CI to avoid accidental upgrades.
- Run health checks using `phoenix status` in monitoring scripts.

## Further reading

- API reference: https://project-phoenix.example/docs/api
- Contribution guide: https://github.com/example/project-phoenix/CONTRIBUTING.md

If you need help, open an issue on the repository or join the community chat linked in the documentation.

Preserve inline code, code blocks, links, and anchors exactly as they appear (do not translate `phoenix start`, `config.yaml`, URLs, or Markdown link targets).
