title: Maritime Trade and Ceramics: Quanzhou Harbor Assemblage
source_text: Maritime Trade and Ceramics: Quanzhou Harbor Assemblage Overview This gallery note introduces a selection of ceramic wares recovered from a 14th-century merchant context associated with the port of Quanzhou. The objects illustrate the cross-cultural exchange of forms, glazes, and motifs across the South China Sea and into the Indian Ocean trade network. Visitors should pay particular attention to the manufacturing techniques and inscriptions that reveal origin and date. Context and Provenance Excavation records and ship manifests indicate that the objects on display were part of a mixed cargo destined for Southeast Asian ports. Comparative analysis of kiln technologies suggests production centers in Fujian and Zhejiang provinces. The site label frequently references the historical toponym 泉州港 (Quanzhou Harbor), which appears on several fragmentary crate markings. Highlights - Blue-and-white export wares with Kraak-style panels. - Sancai-glazed bowls with iron-painted inscriptions. - Stoneware jars fitted with lead-resin shipping seals. Figure references See Figure 1 for the complete dish, and Figure 2 for a close detail of the shipper's ink stamp. ![Figure 1: Blue-and-white Kraak-style dish (Accession No. AQ-1407)](images/aq1407_dish.jpg) Figure 1 caption: Blue-and-white Kraak-style dish, porcelain, 14th century. Diameter: 28 cm. Accession No. AQ-1407. Figure 2 caption: Shipper's ink stamp on crate fragment recovered adjacent to the primary assemblage. Inked characters include a place-name and a merchant mark. Display Table: Catalog Summary | Catalogue ID | Object Type | Date (c.) | Origin | Material | Display Label | |--------------|-------------|-----------:|--------|----------|---------------| | AQ-1407 | Dish | 14th C | Fujian | Porcelain| Blue-and-white Kraak-style dish (Figure 1) | | AQ-1421 | Jar | 14th C | Zhejiang| Stoneware| Transport jar with lead-resin seal | | AQ-1435 | Bowl | 13th–14th C| Fujian | Sancai-glazed earthenware| Painted inscription on rim | Table notes: Translate headers precisely; keep Catalogue ID and Accession numbers unchanged. Technical Observations The glaze on AQ-1407 exhibits underglaze cobalt that has spread anisotropically during firing, creating the characteristic 'blossomed' outlines seen in many export wares. Microscopic analysis reveals traces of iron-rich temper in one rim fragment, indicating a secondary repair prior to shipping. The shipper's ink stamp (Figure 2) uses a quick-drying carbon ink; its characters are faint but legible under raking light. Interpretive Remarks These objects illustrate both local ceramic traditions and their adaptation for long-distance trade. The presence of repair and resealing suggests intentional reuse and cost-management practices by merchants. Cross-referencing crate inscriptions with port logs reveals recurring merchant family names and the repeated use of certain seals across voyages. Visitor guidance - When translating labels, preserve all numerical and alphanumeric identifiers (e.g., AQ-1407). - Do not translate place names that are shown in their original script as part of the material culture; instead, provide a transliteration and brief gloss. Acknowledgments This exhibit was organized in collaboration with maritime archaeologists, conservators, and local historians. High-resolution photographs were produced for documentation purposes; image filenames and paths in captions are placeholders and should be preserved in translation for archival reference.
translation_notes: Translate all prose, captions, and table headers, but preserve catalogue/accession codes (e.g., AQ-1407) and file paths exactly.
Keep table layout and column order intact; translate cell contents and units but do not alter punctuation that separates columns.
expected_challenges: Rendering and preserving Markdown/HTML image tags and file paths while translating surrounding text.
Translating specialized material terms (e.g., 'Sancai', 'Kraak-style') that may be transliterated or treated as technical terms.
Maintaining the integrity of the table structure and aligning translated headers with corresponding cells.
Deciding how to present original-script place names (泉州港) — whether to transliterate, translate, or both.
Preserving accession numbers and measurement units exactly while translating adjacent descriptive text.

title: SyncBridge FAQ and Troubleshooting Guide
source_text:
# SyncBridge — FAQ and Troubleshooting

Welcome to the SyncBridge FAQ. This page collects common questions, setup notes, and solutions to frequent issues for the SyncBridge file synchronization client. Use the quick links below to jump to a section.

- [Overview](#overview)
- [Setup](#setup)
- [Common Problems](#common-problems)
- [Security & Privacy](#security--privacy)
- [Billing & Plans](#billing--plans)
- [Contact & Links](#contact--links)

## Overview

SyncBridge keeps files synchronized between devices and cloud storage providers. It supports incremental sync, file versioning, and conflict resolution. SyncBridge is designed for desktop and server environments and works with a command-line interface and a GUI.

> Tip: If you prefer a quick start, check the `README.md` in the installer package for platform-specific notes.

## Setup

### System Requirements

- macOS 10.14+ or Windows 10+ or Linux (glibc 2.17+)
- Minimum 2 GB RAM, 100 MB disk for client
- Internet connection for cloud access

### Installation Steps

1. Download the installer for your platform from the official site.
2. Run the installer and follow on-screen instructions.
3. Open the SyncBridge app and go to Settings → Accounts.
4. Add a cloud provider and paste your `api_key` when prompted.

Anchor to this section: see [Setup](#setup) for the full steps.

## Common Problems

### Sync speeds are slow

- Check your network connection and firewall settings.
- Reduce the number of parallel transfers in Settings → Network.
- If you are on a metered connection, enable "Limit bandwidth" to avoid throttling.

### Files appear out of date

1. Ensure SyncBridge is running on all devices.
2. Confirm there are no conflicting edits; open the conflict viewer from the tray menu.
3. If timestamps are incorrect, enable "Preserve original mtime" in advanced preferences.

### Conflict resolution

- By default SyncBridge creates a conflict copy and preserves both versions.
- Use the conflict editor to merge text files, or choose the preferred version to retain.

## Security & Privacy

We consider security a priority. Below are common security questions.

### How is my data protected?

- Data in transit is encrypted with TLS 1.2+.
- Optional end-to-end encryption (E2EE) can be enabled per folder.

### Should I share my `api_key`?

Never share your `api_key` or account password. Treat keys like credentials and rotate them periodically.

> Warning: SyncBridge support will never ask you to paste your password into a chat.

## Billing & Plans

SyncBridge offers a free tier and paid plans with extended history and team features.

- Free: 5 GB storage, single-device support
- Pro: 1 TB, multiple devices, priority support
- Team: centralized administration, SSO support

For details and current pricing, visit the pricing page or the Support portal: https://example.com/faq

Payment methods supported: credit card, invoice (for Team plans), and bank transfer.

## Contact & Links

If you cannot resolve an issue using this FAQ:

- Check the full documentation at the Support portal: https://example.com/faq
- Open a ticket via the app (Help → Report an issue).
- Email support: support@syncbridge.example

When filing a ticket, include:

- A brief description of the problem
- Steps to reproduce
- Logs from Help → Export logs

## Troubleshooting Checklist (Quick)

1. Restart SyncBridge.
2. Ensure latest client version is installed.
3. Verify account credentials and `api_key`.
4. Export logs and contact support if problem persists.

## Version History

- 3.2.1 — Bug fixes for large-file uploads
- 3.2.0 — Added E2EE and improved conflict editor
- 3.1.0 — Performance improvements on Linux

If you need localized help or have accessibility requirements, include those details when contacting support so we can assist you efficiently.
translation_notes: Preserve Markdown syntax (headings, lists, blockquotes, inline code, links) unchanged.
Do not translate link targets or inline code; translate only visible text.
Maintain formal tone and imperative instructions for steps and warnings.
expected_challenges: Technical terms like "end-to-end encryption", "mtimes", and "conflict resolution" may have multiple valid translations.
Keeping inline code and links intact while translating surrounding punctuation and sentences.
Translating UI labels and menu paths (e.g., Settings → Accounts) consistently without altering path symbols.

style_label: Documents with multilingual input and selective translation
title: Notes on Basic Conversational Hindi with Embedded Examples
source_text:
Overview:
This language learning note is intended for translators working with English source materials that include selected Hindi phrases. The document outlines realistic usage scenarios, highlights phrases that should be preserved verbatim, and offers contextual guidance so that a translator retains the intended meaning while translating the surrounding English into the target language.

Key Phrases and Their Contexts:
- Everyday request: मुझे किताब चाहिए।
  Context: Use this sentence when a speaker literally wants to express the need for an object. It functions as a simple SOV construction and is used in informal and formal contexts depending on intonation. Preserve this line exactly as written when translating the rest of the document.

- Casual greeting: क्या हाल है?
  Context: A short, informal way to ask 'How are you?'. It can appear inside quoted dialogue or as part of a list of expressions. Keep the phrase unchanged; its tone and register are important to the interaction.

- Politeness marker: धन्यवाद
  Context: Literally 'thank you'. Often used alone as a response to help or a compliment. Because of cultural recognition, translators should not replace it with a romanization or translate it into the target language when it appears as an interjection.

Usage Notes:
1. Preserve Hindi spans exactly. The three Hindi examples provided above are semantically important: the first expresses need, the second is a social opener, and the third marks gratitude. They often carry cultural weight that an equivalent in another language might not convey in the same register.

2. When these Hindi phrases appear in dialogue, maintain original punctuation and capitalization conventions for English around them. For example, if a sentence begins in English and ends with a Hindi phrase, translate the English portion fully and leave the Hindi phrase untouched.

Grammar and Register:
- Word order: The Hindi example in 'मुझे किताब चाहिए।' demonstrates the dative experiencer construction common in Hindi (literally: 'To me a book is needed'). Translators should avoid restructuring the English translation in a way that suggests the Hindi phrase should be syntactically altered. Since the Hindi remains unchanged, ensure the translated English surrounding text still flows logically.

- Formality: 'क्या हाल है?' is casual. If surrounding English text signals a highly formal context, make that contrast explicit in the translation (for example by rendering the English as more formal while leaving the Hindi greeting as an intentionally casual interjection).

Practice Exercises (for editors/translators):
A. Translate the following paragraph into the target language, preserving Hindi spans verbatim:
   "When you enter a small bookstore in Varanasi, you might hear a patron whisper, 'क्या हाल है?' to an old friend. The customer then asks the shopkeeper, 'मुझे किताब चाहिए।' After the sale, someone nearby might say, 'धन्यवाद' as they leave. The scene is friendly and immediate."

B. Provide two variants for the exercise: one where the target audience is a casual reader, and one where the reader is a formal academic audience. In both variants, leave the Hindi phrases unchanged.

Editorial Checklist:
- Do not transliterate Hindi examples into Latin script.
- Do not translate Hindi words into the target language; they must remain in Devanagari as shown.
- Check spacing and punctuation immediately adjacent to Hindi spans to ensure no accidental alterations were made.

Cultural Note:
Small phrases like 'धन्यवाद' often carry concise social functions and are frequently used independently. Retaining them can preserve cultural authenticity and cadence in translation. Similarly, keeping a casual greeting like 'क्या हाल है?' unaltered may preserve a conversational register that would be lost if fully localized.

Summary:
This note is designed to guide translators to preserve a handful of meaningful Hindi examples embedded in otherwise English instructional or narrative text. Translate all English content into the target language, but keep the listed Hindi spans exactly as they appear here to preserve tone, meaning, and cultural nuance.
translation_notes: Translate only the English portions; do not modify or translate the Hindi spans.
Retain exact Devanagari script, punctuation, and spacing for protected Hindi substrings.
expected_challenges: Avoiding accidental transliteration of Devanagari when using automated tools.
Maintaining register contrast when Hindi phrases are casually informal within formal English context.
Ensuring punctuation and spacing around Hindi spans remain unchanged during sentence restructuring.

title: AC-2200 Controller — Maintenance and Troubleshooting (Training Manual Page)
source_text:
AC-2200 Controller — Maintenance and Troubleshooting

Overview
The AC-2200 programmable controller is designed for medium-duty conveyor systems. This page covers routine maintenance, a standard restart procedure, and first-line troubleshooting for common error codes. Technicians must follow the safety requirements before attempting any service.

Safety Precautions
- Disconnect power and lock out/tag out according to company procedure.
- Allow capacitors to discharge for at least 5 minutes before touching internal components.
- Wear ESD wrist strap when handling circuit boards.

Tools Required
- Insulated screwdriver set
- Multimeter (0.1% accuracy recommended)
- 2.5 mm hex key
- Anti-static bag

Figure 1 shows the front panel layout with the primary connectors and indicator LEDs. Refer to the caption and image filename when ordering replacement parts.

![Figure 1: Front panel layout of AC-2200 controller](images/controller-diagram.png)

Figure 1 — Front Panel Layout (file: images/controller-diagram.png)

Quick Restart Procedure
Follow these steps for a controlled restart after clearing an upstream fault. Improper sequencing may trigger ERR42. If ERR42 persists after restart, escalate to Level 2 support.

1. Verify that the conveyor is clear and no pinch points are active.
2. Switch MAIN POWER to OFF and wait 10 s.
3. On the front panel, press and hold RESET for 3 s until the STATUS LED cycles green.
4. Turn MAIN POWER to ON and observe boot sequence (0–60 s).
5. If boots normally, place the controller back into RUN mode.

Table 1 lists standard restart steps and expected indicators.

| Step | Action | Expected Indicator | Time Window |
|------|--------|--------------------|-------------|
| 1 | MAIN POWER OFF | All LEDs off | immediate |
| 2 | Hold RESET | STATUS LED blinks amber | 3 s |
| 3 | MAIN POWER ON | STATUS LED solid green then amber | 0–60 s |
| 4 | ENTER RUN | RUN LED green | immediate |

Routine Maintenance Checklist
- Monthly: Inspect connectors for corrosion; torque mounting screws to 1.2 N·m.
- Quarterly: Replace air filter in nearby enclosure; verify fan RPM at 2600 ±100.
- Annually: Update firmware to the latest approved release and record version in maintenance log.

Firmware Update Notes
When performing a firmware update, use only signed packages provided by the vendor. The update utility will show the current firmware version and prompt to backup settings. If the device reports an unexpected checksum error, abort and verify package integrity.

Troubleshooting: Common Errors
- ERR42 — Overcurrent on output channel 2. Check motor wiring and motor brake. Typical causes include jammed load or shorted cable.
- ERR17 — Temperature sensor open. Replace sensor or check connector P3.

If ERR42 is logged:
- Confirm load is free to move; clear jams.
- Measure output current with clamp meter (expected idle < 0.5 A).
- If current exceeds threshold, isolate motor and measure winding resistance.

Example: resetting to factory defaults
To reset settings to factory defaults use the front-panel sequence: press and hold RESET while cycling MAIN POWER from OFF to ON; continue holding RESET until STATUS LED flashes blue three times. Note: this will erase all user profiles and network settings. For remote units, ensure you have backup configuration.

Advanced Notes for Translators
Image filenames and on-panel labels (e.g., MAIN POWER, RESET, STATUS LED) are critical for field guides and must remain consistent with printed labels. Table headers carry specific procedural semantics and should be translated with terminology that reflects commands (e.g., "Action" as an imperative noun). Preserve numeric ranges and symbols exactly (for example, "0–60 s", "1.2 N·m", "2600 ±100").

Revision History
Document version: 3.1
Last updated: 2025-01-15
Author: Technical Publications
translation_notes: Translate table headers and image captions; keep file paths and label tokens unchanged.
Preserve numeric ranges, units, and symbols exactly (e.g., "0–60 s", "1.2 N·m").
expected_challenges: Consistent translation of imperative labels and UI text (MAIN POWER, RESET, STATUS LED).
Rendering table structure and alignment while translating headers and cell content.
Handling mixed alphanumeric error codes and model names that must remain unchanged.
Translating technical units and tolerances without altering symbols or ranges.

title: Remote Work and Data Security: Policy Note
source_text:
# Remote Work and Data Security: Policy Note

## Purpose

This policy note clarifies expectations for remote work environments and outlines practical steps to protect company data, personal information, and network integrity. It supplements the Corporate Security Policy and applies until superseded by formal policy revision.

For the canonical policy, see: https://intranet.example.com/policies

## Scope

This note applies to all employees, contractors, and third parties who access company systems or handle company data remotely. It covers device requirements, acceptable locations for remote work, secure connectivity, and incident reporting.

## Policy Statement

- All devices used to access company systems must be approved and managed by IT.
- Remote connections must use the company VPN (`VPN-Access`) or an approved secure alternative.
- Sensitive information must never be stored on personal cloud services unless explicitly authorized.
- Work must not be conducted from public computers or unsecured networks.

### Definitions

- Sensitive Data: any information classified as Confidential or Restricted under the Data Classification Standard.
- Approved Device: device enrolled in Mobile Device Management (MDM) and running supported OS versions.

## Procedures

1. Device Enrollment

   - Register new devices with the IT Service Desk. Include device make, model, and OS version.
   - Enroll devices in MDM and enable full-disk encryption.

2. Network and Connectivity

   - Always connect through the company VPN (`VPN-Access`) when accessing internal resources.
   - If on an untrusted network, enable the personal firewall and verify TLS certificates before proceeding.

3. Data Handling

   - Use company-approved collaboration tools for file sharing. Do not attach large confidential files to regular email.
   - If you must print confidential material, secure it immediately and follow local disposal procedures for printed documents.

> Note: If you are unsure whether data is classified as Sensitive Data, contact Data Protection at mailto:security@example.com for guidance.

## Compliance and Monitoring

The company reserves the right to monitor and audit remote connections and device compliance. Non-compliance may result in restricted access, mandatory remediation, or disciplinary action according to HR policy.

### Enforcement Example (Section 3.2)

- Repeated failure to enroll devices or to apply security updates will trigger a stepwise enforcement process described in Section 3.2 of the Corporate Security Policy.

## Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)

Q: Can I use a personal laptop for occasional remote work?

A: Only if it is enrolled and meets minimum security requirements. Contact IT to request enrollment.

Q: What if I need to travel and use hotel Wi‑Fi?

A: Use the company VPN and avoid accessing confidential resources unless necessary. Consider using a mobile hotspot for sensitive tasks.

Q: How do I report a suspected breach?

A: Report immediately via the incident portal and inform the Security Team at mailto:security@example.com. See [Incident Reporting](#incident-reporting) for details.

## Incident Reporting

- Immediate containment steps: disconnect the device from the network, preserve logs if possible, and follow the checklist on the incident portal.
- Complete the incident report within 24 hours. Failure to report in a timely manner may affect remediation support.

[See Section 3](#section-3) for escalation pathways.

## Revision History

- 2024-01-15: Updated requirements for encryption and MDM enrollment.
- 2023-07-10: Clarified guidance on use of personal devices during the COVID-19 remote surge.

## Contact

For questions about interpretation or implementation, contact the Security Team at mailto:security@example.com or submit a request through the IT Service Desk portal.

---

This policy note is intended as operational guidance. It does not replace contractual obligations or local laws. Always consult Legal for jurisdiction-specific concerns.
translation_notes: Do not translate URLs, email addresses, anchors, or inline code; preserve them verbatim.
Keep Markdown structure (headings, lists, blockquotes, links) intact while translating content.
Translate legal and technical terms consistently; do not add or remove procedural steps.
expected_challenges: Rendering of inline code and anchors within translated sentences without altering syntax.
Consistent translation of technical terms (e.g., 'MDM', 'VPN', 'Sensitive Data') and acronyms.
Maintaining formal policy tone and imperative instructions across languages.
Handling culture- or law-specific references like 'COVID-19' guidance and local disposal procedures.

title: Field Survey Summary: Site A Vegetation and Soils
source_text: Executive Summary This field survey report summarizes vegetation cover, soil observations, and photographic documentation collected at Site A during the summer baseline survey (July 12–16). Teams focused on three fixed transects along the southern slope. Key findings include a dominance of early successional grasses in disturbed patches, an emerging Quercus sapling cohort on the ridge, and localized soil compaction near the access road. See Table 1 and Figures 1–2 for quantitative and visual references. Survey Methods Methods followed the standard 0.5 m2 quadrat protocol. Each transect consisted of ten quadrats placed at 5 m intervals along a 45 m line. Percent cover, species frequency, and canopy height were recorded for vascular plants; soil texture was assessed by hand-texture and Munsell color was noted. GPS waypoints were taken at the beginning and end of each transect. For example, Site A - Transect 3 began at GPS: 38.8977° N, 77.0365° W and proceeded upslope to the ridge crest. Vegetation Results Table 1: Vegetation cover summary | Species/Common Name | Percent Cover (%) | Frequency (# quadrats) | Mean Height (cm) | |---------------------|-------------------:|------------------------:|-----------------:| | Poa pratensis (Kentucky bluegrass) | 28 | 9 | 12 | | Schizachyrium scoparium (Little bluestem) | 18 | 7 | 34 | | Quercus rubra (Red oak sapling) | 12 | 5 | 85 | | Plantago major (Broadleaf plantain) | 6 | 4 | 8 | | Bare ground / rock | 24 | -- | 0 | | Other (combined species) | 12 | -- | -- | Notes on Table 1: Percent cover values are averaged across all quadrats within Site A. Frequency reflects the number of quadrats in which the species was observed; "--" indicates not applicable. Table 1: Vegetation cover summary is the primary quantitative summary used to compare transects. Soil and Disturbance Observations Soils on the lower slope were loamy with frequent small stones; Munsell topsoil color was 10YR 4/3. Compaction probes indicated reduced infiltration rates within 5 m of the service road where bare ground increased. Evidence of recent grading was observed at the southernmost sampling point of Transect 1. Small mammal burrows were common in the mid-slope grassland patches. Photographic Documentation Refer to figures below for context images and site map. Photographs were taken with a calibrated scale bar included in each plot photo when possible. Captions provide orientation and relevant microhabitat notes. ![Site Map](images/site_map.png) Figure 1: Site map for Site A showing transect locations, access road, and key physiographic features (path: images/site_map.png). Transect lines are labeled 1–3; north arrow indicates map orientation. Figure 2: Plot 12 (mid-slope) photo demonstrating dominant Poa pratensis cover and exposed mineral soil along the eastern margin. Photo filename: images/plot_photo.jpg. Scale bar = 30 cm. Discussion The dominance of cool-season grasses suggests either past seeding or preferential colonization after disturbance; Quercus rubra saplings on the ridge indicate potential for woodland recovery if herbivore pressure remains low. Management recommendations include: limiting vehicle access to reduce compaction, creating small refugia for woody seedlings, and follow-up monitoring in 12 months to assess sapling survival. When translating this document, pay attention to plant scientific names and units (percent, cm) to preserve meaning. Appendix: Raw Quadrat Notes (excerpt) - Transect 3, Quadrats 4–6: highest frequency of Schizachyrium scoparium; soils slightly coarser. - Transect 2: localized pocket of Plantago major near footpath; likely associated with higher soil moisture. End of report.
translation_notes: Preserve table headers and numeric formats exactly; do not reorder columns or change percent and unit notations.
Keep image filenames and paths verbatim (e.g., images/site_map.png, images/plot_photo.jpg); translate captions but retain file references.
expected_challenges: Rendering species binomials and common names: ensure Latin names remain italicized or left as plain text depending on target language conventions while keeping capitalization.
Maintaining table alignment and the meaning of 'Frequency' vs. 'Percent Cover' during translation where terms may be non-equivalent.

title: Module 4 Handout: Translating Code-Switched Examples and Cultural Terms
source_text:
Module 4: Translating Code-Switched Examples and Cultural Terms

Overview:
This handout is designed for a single 90-minute class session in a course on practical translation strategies. The goal is to give students experience working with mostly English source materials that contain embedded Hindi examples and culturally loaded single-word items. Students should practice translating the English portions while recognizing and preserving the integrity of Hindi spans that function as examples, quoted utterances, or glossary entries.

Learning objectives:
- Identify Hindi spans embedded in English texts and determine which spans must remain unchanged in the translation.
- Apply consistent formatting rules so that multilingual items remain visually distinct in the target language text.
- Discuss strategies for handling culture-specific terms that may require footnotes or an explanatory parenthetical.

Class structure and timing:
- 0–10 minutes: Brief lecture on code-switching and the pedagogical reasons to protect certain spans.
- 10–40 minutes: Guided reading of example passages and instructor-led annotation.
- 40–70 minutes: Small-group translation exercise where each group translates the English only, preserving Hindi examples.
- 70–90 minutes: Group presentations and instructor feedback.

Example Passage for in-class exercise:
Read the passage below, then produce a translation that renders only the English into the target language. Leave embedded Hindi examples unchanged and keep their formatting aligned with the surrounding text.

"Consider a short illustrative sentence used in the grammar exercise: राम ने खाना खाया। This sentence demonstrates ergative marking in simple past tense and is an important test case for students learning to identify agent and patient roles. In the classroom, instructors may write the Hindi sentence on the board and then discuss word order and case marking in English. During translation practice, the Hindi sentence should be preserved exactly as written, while the explanatory English surrounding it must be translated."

Glossary and culturally loaded single words:
Some curriculum items include single-word concepts that are better kept in the original script because they carry cultural content that resists direct translation. For this module we will treat selected glossary items as protected.
- धर्म — A complex term frequently discussed in comparative ethics and religion modules. Students should not translate this string when it appears in the source text; instead, they may add a brief translator's note in the target language explaining its connotations.

Instructor guidance and grading rubric:
When grading the exercise, focus on three things: fidelity to the source English meaning, correct preservation of protected Hindi spans, and clarity of any added translator notes. Award partial credit if a student accurately translates the English but alters an embedded Hindi example. Reserve full credit for translations that render the English fluently while leaving protected spans unchanged and providing helpful, concise annotations when necessary.

Teaching tips and common pitfalls:
- Do not assume that every non-Latin script is a protected span; instruct students to check the syllabus or handout markers for explicit instructions.
- Maintain punctuation adjacent to Hindi spans as it appears in the original; if a protected Hindi sentence includes a danda (।), retain it.
- If a culturally specific word such as धर्म appears in running text, translators may either leave it unchanged or provide a parenthetical explanation in the target language, but they must not alter the original Devanagari token.

Follow-up assignment:
For homework, students will be given a 300-word English article with three embedded Hindi examples. They must translate the English portions only, leave Hindi spans unchanged, and submit a short explanation (50–100 words) of any choices made regarding cultural terms.

Recommended readings:
- Introductory chapters on code-switching and translation ethics.
- Articles on annotation strategies for multilingual classroom materials.

End of handout.
translation_notes: Translate only the English text; do not translate or modify the exact Hindi spans listed under protected_spans.
Preserve punctuation and script of protected Hindi spans; explanatory notes in the target language are permitted but must not alter the protected substrings.
expected_challenges: Identifying the precise boundaries of Hindi spans embedded in English prose
Deciding how to present cultural concepts like धर्म without modifying the protected token
Maintaining original punctuation (for example the danda character) adjacent to protected spans

title: Release Summary v3.2.0
source_text: # Release Summary — v3.2.0 This document summarizes the notable changes, upgrade guidance, and known issues for the v3.2.0 release. It is intended for operators, integrators, and platform engineers who manage deployments and automation in production environments. ## Quick links - [Full Changelog](https://example.com/changelog/v3.2.0) - [Upgrade Notes](#upgrade-notes) - [Security Advisories](https://example.com/security) ## Highlights - Performance: Average API latency reduced by 25% under typical workloads. - Storage: Snapshot replication now supports incremental deltas between minor versions. - Usability: The CLI has new subcommands for bulk operations and interactive help. > Note: This release focuses on stability and incremental feature expansion rather than a large set of new APIs. ## Notable changes 1. New bulk import CLI The `bulk-import` subcommand accepts a directory of configuration bundles and applies them in parallel, respecting dependency ordering. Example: ```bash toolctl bulk-import ./bundles --concurrency 8 ``` 2. Config schema redesign BREAKING CHANGE: The configuration schema for cluster resources has been redesigned to unify labels and selectors. Operators must run the migration utility before upgrading. 3. Storage replication Replication now supports incremental snapshots. This reduces bandwidth and storage use during regular backups. ## Upgrade notes Before upgrading, read these steps carefully to avoid service disruption. - Back up your control-plane database and snapshots. - Run the migration tool: ```bash ./migrate-config --from 3.1.x --to 3.2.0 --output migrated-config.yaml ``` - If you use Docker Compose, update `docker-compose.yml` to the new image tags and the revised volume mounts. - When using Kubernetes, apply the updated manifests with: ```bash kubectl apply -f k8s/manifests/v3.2.0/ ``` ### Rolling upgrade strategy For high-availability clusters, perform a rolling upgrade by restarting one control-plane node at a time and verifying health checks before proceeding. ## Deprecations and removals - The legacy `sync-agent` is deprecated and will be removed in v4.0. Migrate to `replicator-v2`. - Deprecated configuration keys continue to function but will emit warnings during startup. ## Security fixes - CVE-2025-0101: Fixed an authentication bypass in the token refresh endpoint. - Improvement: Hardened TLS negotiation to prefer AEAD ciphers by default. Operators should review the Security Advisories link above and apply patches as soon as practical. ## Known issues - On some older kernels, snapshot restore may take longer than expected; a progress indicator has been added to the restore command but may not reflect exact timings. - The admin UI may show stale metrics for up to 30 seconds due to a caching optimization; this is intentional to reduce API load. ## Troubleshooting tips - If the migration tool reports schema mismatch, verify that all plugins are at least at version 2.5. - For permission errors on Kubernetes, ensure the `serviceaccount` has the `cluster-admin` role during migration and then reduce privileges afterwards. - Log collection: ```bash toolctl collect-logs --since 2h --output support-bundle.tar.gz ``` ## Developer notes - New contributors: see CONTRIBUTING.md in the repository for local dev setup and unit test guidelines. - Tests: Several end-to-end tests have been added; maintainers should expect longer CI runtimes for coverage on integration suites. ## Acknowledgements Thanks to the community contributors who reported bugs, provided patches, and helped validate the release candidates. For a full list of changes, contributors, and commit details, see the [Full Changelog](https://example.com/changelog/v3.2.0).
translation_notes: Translate headings, paragraphs, lists, and blockquotes naturally while preserving Markdown structure.
Do not translate link targets, anchors, or inline code (preserve every `code` span and URL verbatim).
expected_challenges: Handling of technical terms, acronyms, and command-line examples without altering syntax.
Translating the imperative upgrade instructions and warnings accurately and concisely.
Preserving emphasis and exact phrasing for deprecations and security advisories.

style_label: Documents with code and doc strings
title: SQL analytics note: aggregation patterns and partition maintenance
source_text:
/*
SQL Analytics Note: Aggregation patterns and partition maintenance

This document summarizes common aggregation patterns used in our analytics layer,
explains the rationale for partition maintenance, and provides example queries and
scripts that data engineers and analysts can adapt. Translate explanatory prose
and comments/docstrings while preserving code, identifiers, and SQL syntax.
*/

Overview

This note covers three areas:
1. Rolling daily aggregation for event metrics.
2. Incremental update strategies for a time-partitioned table.
3. Maintenance commands and a small automation snippet.

Data model

We assume a raw events table called staging.sales_events that contains one row
per user action. Key columns:
- event_id: unique event identifier
- occurred_at: timestamp of event
- user_id: identifier for the user
- event_type: type of event (purchase, view, click)
- amount: numeric amount for purchase events

Typical aggregation: daily metrics

The following SQL produces daily aggregated metrics. Translate the SQL comments
and block-level explanations but do not change identifiers.

```sql
-- Aggregate daily counts and revenue by event_type
CREATE TABLE analytics.daily_agg PARTITION BY RANGE (day);

INSERT INTO analytics.daily_agg (day, event_type, event_count, total_amount)
SELECT
  date_trunc('day', occurred_at) AS day,
  event_type,
  count(*) AS event_count,
  sum(amount) FILTER (WHERE event_type = 'purchase') AS total_amount
FROM staging.sales_events
WHERE occurred_at >= current_date - interval '30 days'
GROUP BY 1, 2;
```

Notes on correctness and idempotency:
- The query uses a date_trunc on occurred_at so that timezone handling is explicit.
- If you re-run the INSERT for the same day range, consider using a MERGE or
  delete-then-insert pattern to avoid duplicates.

Partition maintenance

Time-partitioned tables require periodic pruning and vacuuming to keep scans fast
and storage compact. The following maintenance actions are recommended:
- Drop partitions older than the retention period.
- VACUUM partitions after large deletes.
- Reindex if index bloat is suspected.

Example maintenance script (shell + SQL):

```bash
# Remove partitions older than 90 days and VACUUM them
psql -d analytics_db -c "BEGIN;"
psql -d analytics_db -c "DELETE FROM analytics.daily_agg WHERE day < current_date - interval '90 days';"
# Run a full vacuum for reclaimed space
psql -d analytics_db -c "VACUUM FULL analytics.daily_agg;"
psql -d analytics_db -c "COMMIT;"
```

Automation example: small Python helper

The helper below shows a minimal pattern to run the aggregation and then trigger
partition maintenance. Comments and the docstring should be translated; code
identifiers and SQL fragments must not be changed.

```python
def run_daily_aggregation(conn):
    '''
    Run the daily aggregation job.

    This function inserts aggregated rows for the last 30 days into
    analytics.daily_agg. It expects an open DB connection object with an
    execute(sql) method. It does not commit automatically; caller controls
    transactions.
    '''
    sql = """
    INSERT INTO analytics.daily_agg (day, event_type, event_count, total_amount)
    SELECT date_trunc('day', occurred_at) AS day,
           event_type,
           count(*) AS event_count,
           sum(amount) FILTER (WHERE event_type = 'purchase') AS total_amount
    FROM staging.sales_events
    WHERE occurred_at >= current_date - interval '30 days'
    GROUP BY 1, 2;
    """
    # Execute the aggregation SQL
    conn.execute(sql)

# Example configuration snippet for pipeline tools
# dbt_project.yml or other orchestrator files can reference the above job

pipeline:
  name: daily_rollup
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"
  description: nightly aggregation and maintenance

Final notes

When translating, be careful with units (days, intervals) and with phrases that
refer to implementation constraints such as idempotency and partition retention.
Do not translate code identifiers like table names or SQL keywords. Preserve
fenced blocks and indentation exactly as shown.
translation_notes: Translate prose, comments, and docstrings; leave code, SQL keywords, and identifiers unchanged.
Preserve fenced blocks, indentation, and punctuation in code examples.
expected_challenges: Distinguishing which words are part of code/identifiers versus translatable narrative.
Handling SQL-specific terms and filters like FILTER (WHERE ...) that mix natural language.
Preserving date/time expressions and interval syntax without altering meaning.
Translating comments that include technical constraints such as idempotency and retention.

title: Kinetic Analysis of Enzyme-Catalyzed Hydrolysis in Microreactors
source_text: Abstract This report presents a kinetic analysis of an esterase-catalyzed hydrolysis reaction performed in a continuous microreactor. The study combines time-resolved spectroscopy with microscopy to correlate macroscopic conversion rates and microscale spatial heterogeneity. Key observations are summarized in Table 1 and visualized in Figures 1 and 2. Introduction Microreactor environments can produce gradients in substrate concentration, temperature, and enzyme activity that are not apparent in batch experiments. We investigated how flow rate and enzyme loading influence initial rate constants and conversion efficiency. The experimental design prioritized reproducibility and direct comparison with standard Michaelis-Menten kinetics. Methods Reagents: substrate solution (10 mM), esterase enzyme (0.2 mg/mL), buffer (50 mM phosphate, pH 7.4). All solutions were prepared using H2O and analytical-grade salts. Reactions were initiated by introducing the substrate stream into a T-junction connected to a silicon-glass microreactor with 200 µm channel width. Apparatus: A diode-array spectrometer recorded absorbance at 405 nm with 1 s temporal resolution. Flow rates were controlled by syringe pumps; flow-rate-dependent residence time was calculated from channel volume. Images were collected using an epifluorescence microscope (60× objective) and captured as 2048×2048 PNG files. Image references ![Figure 1: Experiment setup and flow schematic](images/experiment_setup.png) Figure 1: Experimental setup showing reagent reservoirs, T-junction, and microreactor chip. Flow direction is left-to-right; sample extraction port located after the outlet. Figure 2: Representative fluorescence micrograph of enzyme distribution at 2 min after start. Bright regions correspond to higher enzyme adsorption on channel walls. Results Figure-level summaries and the tabulated kinetic parameters are presented below. Initial rates were determined by linear regression of absorbance increase within the first 30 seconds of residence time. For low flow rates, we observed deviation from classical Michaelis-Menten behavior attributed to wall adsorption and substrate depletion near the channel walls. Table 1: Summary of kinetic parameters and conversion metrics for selected flow rates | Flow rate (µL/min) | Residence time (s) | Initial rate (µM/s) | Conversion at outlet (%) | Observed k_obs (s^-1) | |--------------------:|-------------------:|--------------------:|------------------------:|---------------------:| | 5 | 240 | 0.32 | 78 | 0.0013 | | 20 | 60 | 0.28 | 64 | 0.0011 | | 80 | 15 | 0.12 | 22 | 0.0005 | Discussion The table headers (Flow rate, Residence time, Initial rate, Conversion at outlet, Observed k_obs) are critical for translation because they define experimental axes and conclusions. At 5 µL/min, a high conversion was achieved despite modest initial rates, indicating effective residence time compensation. Conversely, at 80 µL/min conversion drops markedly, consistent with limited substrate-enzyme contact. Microscopy (Figure 2) reveals nonuniform enzyme coverage, suggesting surface interactions contribute to the observed kinetics. Translators should preserve image filenames and captions verbatim while rendering captions into the target language with attention to technical terms like 'adsorption' and 'residence time'. Conclusion Continuous microreactors offer controllable residence times that can be tuned to optimize conversion for enzyme-catalyzed reactions. However, microscale heterogeneities and wall interactions can lead to departures from bulk kinetic models. Future work will quantify adsorption isotherms and test surface passivation strategies. References Selected experimental protocols and raw data are stored as: data/kinetics/run_051.dat and images/experiment_setup.png. Raw microscopy images are archived under figures/microscopy_A.png.
translation_notes: Preserve image file paths and HTML/Markdown tags exactly as they appear (e.g., images/experiment_setup.png, ).
Do not translate chemical formulas, buffer designations, or numeric unit expressions (e.g., H2O, pH 7.4, µL/min).
expected_challenges: Translating table headers without altering numeric units or scientific notation.
Rendering figure captions and maintaining image tag syntax and file paths.
Handling SI units and special characters (µ, °) consistently in the target language.
Translating technical terms like 'residence time', 'adsorption', and 'initial rate' while preserving precise meaning.

title: Audio Guide: Contemporary South Asian Galleries — Visitor Script
source_text:
Welcome to the Contemporary South Asian Galleries. This audio guide is designed for independent visitors and small groups who want contextual orientation at each station. Please follow the floor plan in the brochure and pause between tracks if you wish to examine objects more closely.

[Entrance — 0:00]
The lobby installation greets visitors with a wall-mounted plaque. The plaque displays a single word in Devanagari at its center: नमस्ते. Curators chose this word to emphasize hospitality and the performative moment of entering a domestic or communal space. When you approach the installation, a motion sensor will trigger a short recording of local voices responding to the greeting. Note: translate all English signage, wall labels, and audio narration, but leave Devanagari script intact where it is presented as part of the artwork.

Exhibit 1 — Mandala of Everyday Objects [0:45]
Artist statement (transcribed from the audio guide at the exhibit): "I construct mandalas from objects of daily use to map memory and labor. These concentric arrangements are not sacred in a traditional sense, but they function as sites for ritualized remembrance." A small brass plaque beside the composition contains the artist's short aphorism in Hindi: मेरा काम जीवन है. This phrase is integral to the interpretive frame: the artist equates creative practice with sustained life work. In translation, retain the Hindi phrase verbatim and translate the surrounding English commentary into the target language. The audio accompaniment includes the artist speaking in English, interrupted by a recurring refrain of the phrase मेरा काम जीवन है, looped as an evocative motif.

Exhibit 2 — Textile Narratives [2:30]
This room presents hand-stitched panels documenting migration stories. Labels identify provenance, material, and donor histories. Where donor placards include family names in native scripts, leave those scripts unchanged; translate the explanatory prose only. A listening alcove in the rear of the room hosts field recordings and a curated playlist; the archive identifies one track by its folk title, shown as राग-रंग. This title signifies a hybrid of melodic mode and color symbolism and is displayed on the digital kiosk in Devanagari. Do not translate or transliterate the song title; leave it as a visible, protected text element.

Exhibit 3 — Interactive Projection [4:10]
Stand at the floor marker and activate the projection with the push button. The projection overlays English captions onto archival footage and occasionally superimposes Hindi captions when participants speak in that language. For translation, render the English captions into the target language while preserving all Hindi captions exactly as they appear on-screen. The interactive also includes a short, subtitled interview with a community elder; when the elder switches to Hindi for a proverb, the original Devanagari line is reproduced in the exhibit transcript and should remain untouched.

Visitor Notes and Accessibility
This guide includes audio cues, tactile markers, and short text panels. Translate all descriptive English text, wayfinding instructions, and emergency notices. Leave any embedded Hindi phrases, song titles, or artist quotes in Devanagari unchanged. For visually impaired visitors we provide an extended audio track; the extended narration expands on materials and donor stories and will reference the Hindi phrases as distinct verbal items — treat them as non-translatable tokens in the translated audio.

Curatorial Credits and Further Reading
At the end of the tour, a reading list and bibliography are available. Titles published in Hindi and presented in Devanagari should remain in their original script. English-language titles should be translated according to the target audience. If a catalog entry includes mixed-language quotations, translate only the English parts; keep Hindi quotations and song titles as they appear.

Thank you for visiting. If you have questions about the display labels or wish to report a translation issue on a label, contact the front desk specialist. Enjoy the rest of your visit and take time to listen to the embedded audio loops that preserve the linguistic diversity of the communities represented here.
translation_notes: Translate only the English text; leave the three Devanagari spans unchanged.
Do not transliterate protected Hindi spans—preserve exact script and spacing as given.
expected_challenges: Identifying and preserving short Hindi spans embedded in flowing English sentences without altering punctuation or spacing.
Maintaining register and tone of an audio guide while integrating untranslated Hindi phrases that are semantically important.
Deciding how to handle surrounding punctuation and quotation marks for Hindi spans in target language typography.

title: Study Guide: Getting Started with Local Project Workflows
source_text: # Getting Started with Local Project Workflows This study guide is intended to help developers and technical writers establish a consistent local workflow for small to medium projects. It covers prerequisites, initial setup, common commands, best practices, and troubleshooting tips. Use the examples and checklists below to create a repeatable process for new projects. ## Prerequisites Before you begin, make sure you have the following installed and configured: - A recent version of Git (2.20+ recommended). - A package manager appropriate for your language (e.g., npm, pip, cargo). - An editor or IDE with basic linting support. - Access to any private registries or credential stores needed by your organization. > Note: If you work behind a corporate proxy, verify network settings and credential helpers first. This prevents failures during package installation and remote operations. Useful reference: [Official Guide](https://example.com/guide) ## Quick-start checklist 1. Clone the repository and create a feature branch: - `git clone ` - `cd ` - `git checkout -b feature/my-task` 2. Install dependencies: - For Python: `pip install -r requirements.txt` - For Node.js: `npm ci` 3. Run the test suite locally and fix lint issues before committing. Inline commands to remember: - Initializing a new repository: `git init` - Creating a virtual environment: `python -m venv .venv` ## Development workflow (step-by-step) ### 1. Branch strategy Follow a simple branch naming convention that includes the issue tracker ID and short description, for example: - `feature/1234-add-user-login` - `fix/2345-typo-in-doc` This makes it easy to review history and to search for related work. ### 2. Writing code and tests - Write unit tests that cover critical logic branches. - Keep changes small and focused; prefer multiple small PRs over a single large one. - Run `pytest -q` or `npm test` depending on the stack. #### Example: adding a small function 1. Write the function in the source file. 2. Add tests that assert expected behavior, including edge cases. 3. Run the test runner and correct any failures. ### 3. Committing and pushing - Use concise, imperative commit messages, e.g., `Add user login validation`. - Squash or rebase as required by your team policy before merging. - Push the branch and open a pull request referencing the issue tracker. ## Best practices - Keep documentation close to code: update README and inline docstrings. - Use small, descriptive PR titles and include context in the description. - Automate repetitive tasks via scripts in `scripts/` or Makefiles. - Configure CI to run tests and linters on every push. ## Troubleshooting If tests fail locally but pass in CI, check the following: - Environment differences (Python/Node versions, OS-specific behavior). - Missing environment variables or secret files. - Database migrations or seed data requirements. Common error resolution steps: - Recreate the environment: delete virtual env or `node_modules` and reinstall. - Run with verbose logging to capture stack traces: e.g., `pytest -k name -vv`. - Verify that `pip` or `npm` is pulling from the expected registry. ## Further reading and resources - Project templates and starter repos in your organization. - Style guides for commit messages and code formatting. - Links to onboarding documents and external tutorials. See also: [Jump to Troubleshooting](#troubleshooting) > Pro tip: Keep a personal checklist for onboarding new projects. It accelerates getting productive and reduces context-switching. Appendix: common commands summary - Initialize: `git init` - Install (Python): `pip install -r requirements.txt` - Install (Node): `npm ci` - Create venv: `python -m venv .venv`
translation_notes: Preserve Markdown structure and keep link targets (URLs) verbatim.
Do not translate protected spans; keep inline code exactly as shown.
expected_challenges: Translating technical terms and command syntax without altering code elements.
Maintaining Markdown anchors, links, and the integrity of inline code during translation.

title: Service deployment and troubleshooting runbook with scripts
source_text:
Overview

This runbook explains how to deploy and troubleshoot the "myapp" service using Docker Compose and a lightweight systemd unit. It focuses on common operational tasks: start, stop, view logs, and rotate configuration without downtime. The prose and inline comments are intended to be translated; code, filenames, service names, and variable identifiers must remain unchanged.

Prerequisites

- Access to the host with sudo privileges.
- Docker and docker-compose installed.
- Environment variables configured: DOCKER_REGISTRY and APP_ENV.
- A configuration file at /etc/myapp/config.yaml

Quick start (shell)

Run these commands to bring the application up quickly. Lines starting with # are comments and should be translated.

```bash
# Pull the latest images from the registry
docker-compose -f docker-compose.yml pull

# Start containers in detached mode
docker-compose -f docker-compose.yml up -d

# Check the service status under systemd
sudo systemctl status myapp.service

# Tail the main service log
journalctl -u myapp.service -f
```

Configuration snippet (docker-compose.yml)

The YAML block below defines a single service. Only translate human-readable comments and descriptions; preserve indentation, keys, and image names.

```yaml
version: '3.8'
services:
  web:
    image: "${DOCKER_REGISTRY}/myorg/myapp:${APP_ENV}"
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    volumes:
      - /etc/myapp/config.yaml:/app/config.yaml:ro  # bind the host config file into the container
    environment:
      - ENV=${APP_ENV}  # application runtime environment (translate this phrase if needed)
```

Python healthcheck script

The following Python snippet is used as a simple healthcheck. Translate the docstring and inline comment, but do not change function or variable names.

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3

'''Simple HTTP healthcheck used by the orchestration layer.

This script attempts a GET on the configured health endpoint and exits
with a non-zero status if the check fails. Keep the exit codes intact.
'''

import sys
import requests

HEALTH_URL = 'http://127.0.0.1:8080/health'

# Attempt the request and interpret the response
try:
    resp = requests.get(HEALTH_URL, timeout=3)
    if resp.status_code != 200:
        # Non-200 is considered unhealthy
        sys.exit(2)
except requests.RequestException:
    # Network errors should cause a failure
    sys.exit(1)

sys.exit(0)
```

Rolling configuration update

To update configuration without restarting all containers, follow these steps:
1. Edit /etc/myapp/config.yaml and increment the config_version value. This allows the runtime to detect changes.
2. Execute the reload endpoint inside the container.

```bash
# Copy new config into place (example)
sudo cp config.new.yaml /etc/myapp/config.yaml

# Trigger an in-process reload via curl
docker exec -it myapp_web_1 curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/-/reload

# If reload fails, fallback to a graded restart
sudo systemctl restart myapp.service
```

Troubleshooting tips

- If containers fail to start, run docker-compose -f docker-compose.yml up without -d to view immediate errors.
- Check journalctl -u myapp.service for systemd-level failures.
- Verify that DOCKER_REGISTRY is reachable from the host; network DNS issues are a common cause.

Notes for translators

Translate all explanatory prose, comments, and docstrings into the target language. Preserve code fences, YAML and shell syntax, file paths like /etc/myapp/config.yaml, service names such as myapp.service, and environment variable names like DOCKER_REGISTRY. Placeholders in image names and environment values (for example, ${APP_ENV}) should remain unchanged unless explicitly indicated.
translation_notes: Translate prose, comments, and docstrings only; do not translate code identifiers, file paths, or environment variable names.
Keep code fences, indentation, YAML keys, and shell syntax exactly as in the source.
expected_challenges: Distinguishing which words inside code blocks are translatable (comments/docstrings) versus code or identifiers that must be preserved.
Preserving placeholders like ${APP_ENV} and avoiding accidental translation of environment variable names such as DOCKER_REGISTRY.
Maintaining indentation and line breaks in YAML and Python docstrings while translating the human-readable parts.

title: EcoHome™ AX-4521 Wall-Mounted Air Purifier — Product Catalog Page
source_text: EcoHome™ AX-4521 Wall-Mounted Air Purifier Overview The EcoHome™ AX-4521 is a compact, energy-efficient wall-mounted air purifier designed for residential use. It combines a HEPA-13 filter, activated carbon layer, and a low-noise variable-speed fan to deliver continuous filtration in living rooms, bedrooms, and home offices. The unit emphasizes simple installation, intuitive touch controls, and Wi-Fi connectivity for smart home integration. Key features - True HEPA-13 filtration (captures 99.97% of particles ≥0.3 μm) - Activated carbon module for VOC and odor reduction - Quiet mode: <24 dB at minimum speed - Smart app control (iOS and Android) - Child-lock and filter-replacement indicator - Energy Star certified motor Product images Figure 1: Front view of the AX-4521 unit with touch-control panel and indicator LEDs. Figure 2: Typical wall-mount installation. Mounting bracket (A), power inlet (B), and clearance recommendations are shown. Model comparison table | Model | SKU | Coverage (sq ft) | Dimensions (W × H × D, inches) | Weight (lbs) | MSRP (USD) | |-------|-----|------------------:|-------------------------------:|-------------:|-----------:| | AX-4521 | AX-4521 | 500 | 18.0 × 12.5 × 5.2 | 9.8 | 249.00 | | AX-4521 Pro | AX-4521-P | 800 | 22.0 × 14.0 × 5.5 | 12.1 | 349.00 | Table 1: Comparison of AX-4521 family models. Headers indicate the fields that must be translated exactly: 'Model', 'SKU', 'Coverage (sq ft)', 'Dimensions (W × H × D, inches)', 'Weight (lbs)', 'MSRP (USD)'. Technical specifications - Power: 24 W (nominal) - Voltage: 100–240 VAC, 50/60 Hz - Filter life: 6–12 months (dependent on usage) - Noise levels: 24–55 dB (min–max) - Connectivity: 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi, Bluetooth LE Installation notes Refer to Figure 2 for bracket positioning. Minimum clearances: top 6 in (15 cm), bottom 6 in (15 cm), and 3 in (8 cm) from side walls. Use the included wall anchors for drywall installations, and ensure the mounting bracket is mounted to wood studs or equivalent support when possible. If the unit will be mounted above 8 ft (2.4 m), consider an installation service for safety. Accessories and spare parts | Accessory | Part number | Notes | |----------|-------------|-------| | Replacement HEPA-13 filter | HEPA-AX4521 | Rated for 6–12 months | | Activated carbon cartridge | CARB-AX4521 | Snap-in, replaceable | | Mounting bracket kit | BRKT-AX4521 | Includes screws and anchors | Figure captions and labels are important: the accessory table header 'Accessory', 'Part number', and 'Notes' should be translated and preserved in layout. Warranty and support EcoHome™ offers a two-year limited warranty covering defects in materials and workmanship under normal household use. Warranty does not cover damage from improper installation, misuse, or unauthorized repairs. For warranty service, include the SKU and proof of purchase when contacting EcoHome customer support. Support hours: Monday–Friday, 9:00–17:00 local time. Additional remarks for translators - Preserve product codes and filenames verbatim where shown (e.g., AX-4521 and image paths). - Translate measurement units and provide local equivalents where appropriate, but keep the original parentheses (e.g., "6 in (15 cm)"). - Maintain table structure and caption associations so the relationship between figures/tables and text remains clear. Contact For bulk orders and reseller pricing, contact sales@ecohome.example or call +1-800-555-0199. Include model reference 'AX-4521' in the subject line when applicable.

Do not translate protected spans (product codes, brand names, and image filenames); preserve exact punctuation and symbols.
expected_challenges: Handling units and parenthetical local equivalents (e.g., "6 in (15 cm)") without altering meaning or layout.
Preserving table alignment and headers while translating short header phrases and units.

title: Tutorial: Building a Minimal Static Site with Hugo
source_text:
# Tutorial: Building a Minimal Static Site with Hugo

This tutorial walks you through creating a small, maintainable static website using Hugo. It targets users who are comfortable with the command line and basic Git workflows but are new to static site generators.

- Estimated time: 30–60 minutes
- Requirements: Git, Hugo installed, basic Markdown knowledge

> Note: This guide uses Hugo as an example. Many principles apply to other static site generators.

## Overview

Hugo is a fast static site generator that converts Markdown content into a complete website. The key steps are:

1. Create a project scaffold.
2. Add content and templates.
3. Configure site settings in `config.toml`.
4. Build and preview locally with `hugo serve`.

For official documentation see https://gohugo.io.

## Prerequisites

Before you begin, install Hugo and Git. Verify installations:

- Run `git --version` to check Git.
- Run `hugo version` to check Hugo.

If you need a quick start guide, the Hugo site has detailed installation instructions.

## Installation and Project Setup

1. Create a new directory for your site and initialize Git:

   ```bash
   mkdir my-site && cd my-site
   git init
   ```

2. Create a new Hugo site skeleton:

   ```bash
   hugo new site .
   ```

3. Add a theme or starter template. You can clone an official starter or use a minimal theme from the community. Example:

   ```bash
   git submodule add https://github.com/theNewDynamic/gohugo-theme-ananke.git themes/ananke
   ```

4. Update `config.toml` to set site title, baseURL, and theme:

   ```toml
   baseURL = "https://example.com/"
   languageCode = "en-us"
   title = "My Minimal Site"
   theme = "ananke"
   ```

## Creating Content

Hugo organizes content in the `content/` directory. Create a new post:

- Command: `hugo new posts/welcome.md`
- Edit the front matter and body in Markdown. Use headings, lists, and code fences as needed.

Example front matter:

```yaml
---
title: "Welcome"
date: 2024-01-01T12:00:00Z
---
```

Write a few paragraphs of Markdown to introduce your site. Keep lines reasonably short for readability.

## Local Development and Preview

Run a local server to preview changes interactively:

- Start server: `hugo serve`
- Open http://localhost:1313 in a browser.

The server auto-reloads when you edit content or templates.

## Deployment Basics

Several deployment strategies are possible:

- Push the `public/` folder to a static hosting provider.
- Use Continuous Integration to build and deploy from your repository.

A simple manual deploy:

1. Build the site: `hugo -D` (the `-D` flag includes drafts).
2. Copy the `public/` output to your host.

For Git-based hosting (GitHub Pages, Netlify), connect the repository and configure the build step.

## Best Practices

- Keep content in Markdown; separate presentation into themes.
- Use partial templates for repeated elements (header, footer).
- Version control your `config.toml` and content, but typically ignore the `public/` output directory.

> Tip: Use meaningful slugs and keep titles short for better URLs.

## Troubleshooting

- If pages don't render, check front matter syntax and indentation.
- If the server doesn't start, confirm that the port 1313 is free.
- For theme issues, inspect `layouts/` and override specific templates.

## Links and Further Reading

- Hugo documentation: https://gohugo.io
- Example starter: https://example.com/hugo-starter

## Appendix: Quick Commands

- Initialize repository: `git init`
- Create new content: `hugo new posts/title.md`
- Serve locally: `hugo serve`
- Build output: `hugo -D`

This tutorial focused on a minimal workflow to get a static site online quickly. Once comfortable, explore more advanced features like taxonomies, shortcodes, and custom output formats.
translation_notes: Translate all visible text (headings, paragraphs, lists) but keep link targets (URLs) and inline code exactly as written.
Preserve Markdown syntax: headings, lists, blockquotes, anchors, and code fences must remain intact.
expected_challenges: Deciding whether to translate technical terms, filenames, and commands (often keep them unchanged).
Maintaining natural target-language phrasing while preserving links, anchors, and inline code structure.

title: Bilingual Form Completion Guide (English with Hindi Examples)
source_text:
Overview:
This document explains how to complete the citizen service intake form that contains English field labels but also shows a small number of Hindi prompts. Translators should convert the English instructions and labels into the target language while leaving embedded Hindi phrases exactly as they appear. The Hindi snippets are intentionally present because some official paper forms include them and they carry legal or administrative meaning.

Purpose and audience:
The guide targets front-desk staff, translation teams, and quality reviewers who must produce a localized form from this English source. It assumes the final output will be presented to speakers of the target language who may also recognize familiar Hindi labels. Keep layout and punctuation consistent; a change in punctuation next to a protected Hindi label can change meaning.

Filling individual fields:
- Full name field (example): provide the given name, middle name(s), and family name in the order requested. In the example form we include the following prompt as the authoritative Hindi label for the name field: कृपया अपना पूरा नाम दर्ज करें: This specific Hindi sentence must remain unchanged in the translated version. Place the translated English label above or beside it as required by layout.

- Identification number field: where the original form shows an official label in Hindi, preserve that label. Example: आधर_NUMBER in English should be translated as needed, but the official Hindi label appears exactly as: आधार संख्या: Do not alter this string; keep it verbatim in the localized form.

- Address and contact details: translate field labels such as "Permanent address," "Correspondence address," "City/Town/Village," and "Phone number" into the target language. If the original includes any Hindi guidance within a field (for instance, a short clarifying phrase), that Hindi text is protected and must remain in Devanagari script.

Examples of acceptable rendering:
1) [Translated label for Full Name]
   कृपया अपना पूरा नाम दर्ज करें:
   [input box]

2) [Translated label for Aadhaar]
   आधार संख्या:
   [input box]

Dialogues and quoted utterances:
When the source includes short quoted examples of how a clerk might speak to an applicant, translate the English utterances but leave any quoted Hindi phrases untouched. For instance, if the clerk says, 'Please show your ID and write your address on the form,' translate that entire sentence. If the clerk also demonstrates the official Hindi phrase as an example, keep that phrase in its original script.

Formatting and punctuation rules:
- Maintain the placement of colons, slashes, and parentheses associated with protected Hindi spans.
- Do not transliterate protected Hindi phrases into Latin script; preserve Devanagari exactly as shown.
- Keep numeric formats (e.g., 10/04/2026) consistent with the target locale unless an explicit reformatting instruction is provided.

Glossary and field mapping:
Include a short glossary at the end of the translated form that maps English field names to their target-language equivalents. Also list the preserved Hindi labels and explain (in the translated text) that these labels are official and therefore unchanged.

Quality checklist for reviewers:
- Verify that each occurrence of the protected Hindi phrases remains exactly as in the source.
- Check surrounding translated labels for naturalness and clarity in the target language.
- Confirm that no additional Devanagari characters were introduced or that protected spans were accidentally altered.

Contact for queries:
If there is uncertainty about leaving a specific Hindi phrase unchanged, consult the supervising linguist or the legal team before making changes. Small variations can have administrative consequences.

title: YAML Configuration Tutorial with Docstrings and Examples
source_text:
YAML Configuration Tutorial: service example

This tutorial explains a compact YAML configuration used for a microservice. Translate the explanatory prose, comments, and docstrings. Preserve code, keys, identifiers, boolean values, and protected placeholders.

Example configuration (config.yaml):

```yaml
# Top-level configuration for the example service.
version: 1.0
service:
  name: user-profile
  listen: 0.0.0.0:8080
  debug: false

logging:
  level: INFO
  rotate: daily
  # Keep format tokens intact: do_not_translate: true
  format: "%(asctime)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s"

credentials:
  # API key is a placeholder and must not be replaced during translation.
  api_key: "API_KEY_PLACEHOLDER"
  # Environment variable name used in deployment
  env_name: "ENV_VAR_NAME"
  timeout_seconds: 30

features:
  enable_signup: true
  experimental:
    feature_x: false
    feature_y: true
```

Explanatory notes:
- The "version" value is used by the deployment tool to validate compatibility.
- "service.name" is an internal identifier and should not be localized; translators should leave it unchanged.
- Comments beginning with "#" and docstrings in code blocks below are translatable. Translate them into the target language, keeping line breaks and sentence structure where appropriate.

Parsing example in Python:
```python
"""
load_config(path) -> dict
Load the YAML configuration from the given path and normalize defaults.

Parameters:
    path (str): Filesystem path to the YAML file.
Returns:
    dict: Normalized configuration dictionary with defaults applied.

Notes:
    - This function does not perform network calls.
    - Exception messages should be translated by the user of this sample.
"""
import yaml
import os

def load_config(path):
    # Read file contents and parse YAML.
    with open(path, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        cfg = yaml.safe_load(f)

    # Apply defaults if keys are missing.
    if "logging" not in cfg:
        cfg["logging"] = {"level": "WARNING", "rotate": "weekly"}
    # Ensure credentials block exists.
    cfg.setdefault("credentials", {})
    # Keep placeholders unchanged.
    if "api_key" not in cfg["credentials"]:
        cfg["credentials"]["api_key"] = "API_KEY_PLACEHOLDER"
    # Convert timeout to int if necessary.
    cfg["credentials"]["timeout_seconds"] = int(cfg["credentials"].get("timeout_seconds", 30))

    return cfg

# Example usage:
# cfg = load_config("config.yaml")
# print(cfg["service"]["name"])
```

Translation guidance and edge cases:
- Translate all prose, comments, and docstrings (including triple-quoted strings), but do not translate YAML keys, code identifiers, boolean literals (true/false), numeric values, or protected placeholders such as API_KEY_PLACEHOLDER and ENV_VAR_NAME.
- Preserve indentation, punctuation inside code blocks, and YAML structure. Do not modify quoted strings that are intended as identifiers unless they are natural language descriptions.
- For compound nouns like "service.name" and "timeout_seconds", prefer leaving the identifier form unchanged; if a natural language equivalent is required, provide it in parentheses after the identifier.

Small troubleshooting section:
- If YAML parsing raises a TypeError, check for stray tabs or Python-specific indent issues.
- If a translated comment introduces punctuation that breaks YAML (for example, newlines inside a quoted string), keep the original quoted YAML value unchanged or consult the developer.

End of tutorial.

Preserve YAML keys, code identifiers, boolean/numeric literals, punctuation inside code blocks, and protected placeholders unchanged.
expected_challenges: Deciding which quoted strings are natural language vs. identifiers (e.g., service.name vs. format strings).
Keeping YAML structure and indentation intact while translating adjacent comments or docstrings.
Preserving placeholders and environment variable names without altering their casing or punctuation.

title: Seaside Interlude — Exhibit Note and Object Data
source_text:
Seaside Interlude

Artist: Eleanor Pratt (1889–1964)
Accession No. MFA-1921.45

Overview

Seaside Interlude is an oil on canvas painting completed in 1920 during the artist's extended stay on the southern coast. The work is displayed in Gallery 7 as part of the thematic exhibition 'Coastal Modernisms'. Visitors should refer to the figure and table references below for measurements, provenance, and conservation history.

Figure

Figure 1 shows the painting in its current frame and lighting. The image file is a reproduction for reference only; color and texture may vary from the original in situ.

![Figure 1: Seaside Interlude, Eleanor Pratt, 1920. Placeholder image file: images/seaside_interlude_MFA-1921.jpg](images/seaside_interlude_MFA-1921.jpg)

Caption: Seaside Interlude, oil on canvas, framed. Accession No. MFA-1921.45. Photograph by Museum Imaging Services, placeholder path images/seaside_interlude_MFA-1921.jpg.

Object Data

| Attribute | Details | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Title | Seaside Interlude | Primary title as cataloged |
| Artist | Eleanor Pratt (1889–1964) | American, active 1910–1958 |
| Accession number | Accession No. MFA-1921.45 | Do not alter; institutional identifier |
| Date | 1920 | Inscribed lower right with year |
| Medium | Oil on canvas | Ground layer of lead white and oil binder |
| Dimensions | 76.2 x 101.6 cm (30 x 40 in) | Height x width; framing adds 5 cm each side |
| Provenance | Private collection of J. Harrow (acquired 1921); Gift to the Museum, 1921 | See provenance notes below |

Provenance and Exhibition History

Provenance is concise yet important for legal and interpretive reasons. The canvas entered the museum collection as a gift cataloged under Accession No. MFA-1921.45. Early exhibition records list the painting in the 1923 summer loan display at the Coastal Arts Society.

Interpretation and Context

Pratt's Seaside Interlude exemplifies the artist's shift from academic figure work to looser brushwork influenced by contemporary European colorists. The composition centers on a solitary figure at the water's edge, framed by a tilted horizon that disrupts classical perspective. The palette is dominated by cool grays and muted blues, with an accent of cadmium orange applied in quick, visible strokes.

The title implies a narrative pause rather than cinematic action: a brief encounter between observer and landscape. Interpretive labels in the gallery highlight Pratt's interest in time and memory, and include a short translated phrase for bilingual visitors. Example bilingual label: Français: Une fenêtre sur la mer.

Materials and Technique

Conservators sampled the ground and paint layers in 2018 to document stratigraphy. Analysis revealed a conventional priming of rabbit-skin glue beneath a lead white ground, and a later varnish layer with traces of natural resin.

Conservation Record

| Treatment Date | Action | Conservator |
|---|---|---|
| 1965-06 | Relined canvas; restretched | L. Morales |
| 2018-11 | Surface cleaning and varnish removal; inpainting of flaking areas | T. Okoro |
| 2020-03 | Stabilization of stretcher bars; humidity monitoring installed | T. Okoro |

Display Recommendations

Lighting should be calibrated to a maximum of 50 lux to minimize cumulative light exposure. Relative humidity is recommended to remain between 45 and 55 percent. Any reproduction for publication must cite the accession number exactly as cataloged: Accession No. MFA-1921.45.

References and Further Reading

Museum accession files; Conservation report 2018, Museum Technical Archives; exhibition catalogue: Coastal Modernisms, 2021. For questions about handling, requests for high-resolution images, or scholarly access to condition reports, contact the Collections Manager with the accession number provided above.
