title: Account & Billing FAQ
source_text:
# Account & Billing FAQ

[Back to top](#account--billing-faq)

## Table of Contents

- [Account creation](#account-creation)
- [Billing cycles and invoices](#billing-cycles-and-invoices)
- [Payments and accepted methods](#payments-and-accepted-methods)
- [Refunds and disputes](#refunds-and-disputes)
- [Data export and migration](#data-export-and-migration)
- [Contacting support](#contacting-support)

---

## Account creation

Q: How do I create a new account?

A: To create an account, navigate to the Sign Up page via the main menu. You'll need a valid email address and a secure password (minimum 12 characters). After registering, verify your email by clicking the link we send. If the verification email doesn't appear, check your spam folder or resend verification from your profile settings.

- Requirements:
  - Unique email address
  - Password with at least one numeric character and one symbol
  - Acceptance of the Terms of Service

> Note: Disposable or temporary emails are blocked during registration to reduce fraudulent accounts.

## Billing cycles and invoices

Q: When am I billed and how can I access invoices?

A: Billing occurs on the subscription anniversary date unless you select a custom billing cycle during checkout. Invoices are available in PDF format from your Account > Billing page. Each invoice includes a unique invoice number and a breakdown of charges.

- Invoice format details:
  - Line-item breakdown
  - Tax and discount adjustments
  - Invoice reference (e.g., INV-2024-XXXX)

If you need an invoice in a different language or with a purchase order number, contact billing using the address below.

## Payments and accepted methods

We accept the following payment methods:

- Major credit cards (Visa, MasterCard, American Express)
- ACH transfers (for eligible accounts)
- Wire transfers for enterprise customers
- Corporate purchase orders (POs) upon pre-approval

To update your payment method, visit Account > Payment Methods. For CLI-driven accounts, you can also register a billing token by running the admin tool with the `--force` flag when performing bulk updates. Example:

```
billing-tool update --token <token> --force
```

Please keep your card details up to date to avoid service interruption.

## Refunds and disputes

Q: What is the refund policy?

A: Refunds are evaluated on a case-by-case basis. Pro-rated refunds may be issued for annual plans cancelled mid-term. For charge disputes, submit a request with your invoice number and a description of the issue.

- To request a refund:
  1. Open a support ticket with the invoice number.
  2. Provide the reason and any supporting evidence.
  3. Allow up to 10 business days for review.

> Excerpt from policy: Refunds do not cover third-party fees, such as wire or currency conversion costs.

## Data export and migration

Q: How can I export my account data?

A: You can export data from the Account > Data Export section. Exports are provided as compressed archives (ZIP) and include JSON and CSV representations of your resources. For API-driven exports, use the endpoint `api/v1/export` with your API key. Example curl command:

```
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <API_KEY>" https://api.example.com/api/v1/export
```

Developers should consult the `README.md` in the repo for field mappings and schema definitions. If you plan a large-scale migration, request a temporary bulk-export token from support.

## Contacting support

If you still have questions or need assistance, please:

- Visit the [Support Portal](https://support.example.com)
- Email our team at support@example.com
- For enterprise customers, use your assigned account manager

Support hours are Mon–Fri, 09:00–18:00 local time. Typical response times are within 24 hours for priority tickets and 72 hours for standard tickets.

---

If you need step-by-step help for a particular scenario (for example, transferring billing to a new legal entity or consolidating multiple accounts), include as much detail as possible in your support request so we can route it correctly.

title: April Community Newsletter
source_text:
समुदाय समाचार

Welcome to the April edition of our neighborhood newsletter. This month we focus on strengthening local networks, expanding volunteer opportunities, and supporting small businesses as they prepare for the spring season. Please read through the events calendar, volunteer spotlight, and resources sections to find ways to participate—either in person or remotely.

What's New

Our community garden project completed its first winter repairs and is now accepting plot applications. We launched a seed swap shelf at the community center and hosted a workshop on pollinator-friendly planting. If you would like to reserve a plot or volunteer for Saturday maintenance, email gardens@neighborhood.org. Note deadlines for plot applications are listed in the Events Calendar below.

Events Calendar

- April 10, 10:00–12:00: Seed Swap and Planting Tips at the Community Center
- April 16, 18:30–20:00: Neighborhood Safety Forum (open to all residents)
- April 22, 09:00–12:00: Community Clean-Up and Recycling Drive
- April 29, 15:00–17:00: Family Art Day (materials provided; register in advance)

Please be aware that most events require pre-registration due to space limits. If you need translation assistance or transportation, contact coordinator@neighborhood.org at least five days in advance.

Volunteer Spotlight

Each month we highlight a volunteer who has made a measurable difference. This month we celebrate Priya R., who coordinated the winter coat drive and helped distribute items to over 120 families. Priya shared her reason for volunteering: "मैं यहाँ मदद करूँगा।" Her leadership demonstrates how a single volunteer can organize donations, recruit helpers, and ensure equitable distribution.

Call for Volunteers

We have a recurring need for volunteer coordinators for food distribution, after-school tutoring, and our elder visitation program. New volunteer orientation will be held on April 12 at 19:00 in the main hall. An additional meeting labeled "सहयोगी बैठक" will be held on April 20 to plan summer programming—this meeting is specifically for team leads and long-term volunteers. All volunteers must complete a basic background check and attend orientation before leading activities.

Local Resources

- Food Pantry: Open Tuesdays and Thursdays, 10:00–14:00. Please bring ID and proof of address. Emergency boxes available for same-day pickup when supplies permit.
- Legal Aid Clinic: Free consultations on tenant rights and immigration on the third Wednesday of each month. Appointments required.
- Mental Health Support: Weekly group sessions and one-on-one counseling available; contact wellness@neighborhood.org for referral information.

Small Business Corner

Several local businesses have joined a neighborhood discount program. Present your community newsletter or sign up online to receive discounts at participating cafes, bike shops, and home services. If you operate a small business and would like to enroll, email business@neighborhood.org with a brief description and proposed discount.

Language Corner

We continue to offer conversational English classes and beginner computer literacy sessions for adult learners. Class schedules are posted online and at the community center. When preparing bilingual materials, please leave quoted or script-based examples in their original language. For instance, the literal Hindi examples and titles used in this newsletter should remain exactly as printed to preserve meaning for native readers.

How to Stay Connected

Sign up for our weekly updates, follow us on social media, or drop by the community center during open hours. If you have suggestions for future issues or would like to contribute an article, send your submission to editor@neighborhood.org. We aim to reflect diverse voices and practical information that helps neighbors connect and thrive.

Thank you for being part of our community. Together we build safer streets, greener public spaces, and more resilient support networks for everyone.

title: Hydraulic Control Panel — Maintenance and Troubleshooting (Page 54)
source_text:
Hydraulic Control Panel — Maintenance and Troubleshooting

Overview
This page describes routine maintenance, common faults, and step-by-step corrective actions for the Model HCP-400 hydraulic control panel. Technicians should consult the full system manual for electrical diagrams and fluid specifications before performing work. All times and torque values assume dry-room conditions (20–25°C).

Safety
Always follow site safety rules. Do not service the panel while the system is pressurized. Lockout/tagout procedures must be completed prior to disassembly. Observe the following warning label on the primary pressure line:

CAUTION: HIGH PRESSURE

Personal protective equipment (PPE) required: safety glasses, pressure-rated gloves, and hearing protection if pumps are running in the vicinity.

Figure references
See Figure 1 for the front layout and Figure 2 for the valve subassembly orientation. Remove the access cover by releasing the four captive screws indicated in Figure 1.

![Front view of HCP-400 control panel](images/control_panel_front.png)
Figure 1: Front view of HCP-400 control panel. Note the position of the pressure gauge (PG), emergency stop (E-STOP), and filter bypass valve (FV).

Procedure: Access and Visual Inspection
1. Lockout/tagout main power and bleed hydraulic pressure at the bleed valve.
2. Verify pressure at the gauge reads zero before opening the cover (see Figure 1).
3. Inspect hoses, fittings, and filter element for leaks or contamination.
4. Photograph any anomalies for the maintenance log.

Valve Subassembly Removal
Refer to Figure 2 for the valve orientation. Remove the retaining bolts in a star pattern to avoid distorting the gasket surface. Keep components organized and label Valve A and Valve B per the panel ID.

![Valve assembly exploded view](images/valve_subassembly_diagram.png)
Figure 2: Exploded view of valve subassembly. Use gasket kit part number GKT-400 for resealing.

Troubleshooting Matrix
Use the table below to identify common faults. Table 1 lists symptoms, likely causes, and recommended corrective actions. Translate all header labels literally to preserve the troubleshooting workflow.

Table 1: Troubleshooting Matrix

| Symptom | Likely Cause | Immediate Action | Follow-up Check |
|---------|--------------|------------------|-----------------|
| Low system pressure | Clogged suction filter | Replace filter element, prime pump | Verify pressure reaches spec within 2 minutes |
| Erratic actuator motion | Contaminated fluid or air in lines | Flush lines, bleed air, replace fluid if contaminated | Perform 3 cycle test at no-load |
| Panel alarm E23 | Pressure sensor fault | Replace sensor or check wiring harness | Confirm alarm clears and logs reset |

Preventive Maintenance Schedule
The schedule below defines intervals for inspection and parts replacement. Preserve the column headers during translation to avoid ambiguity.

| Item | Interval (hours) | Action | Replacement Part |
|------|------------------:|--------|------------------|
| Filter element | 500 | Replace and inspect seating | FE-500 |
| Hydraulic oil | 2000 | Drain and replace, analyze sample | HO-32 |
| Pressure sensor | 1000 | Calibrate; replace if out of tolerance | PS-100 |

Notes and Best Practices
- Use only manufacturer-approved hydraulic fluids (see full manual). Mixing fluid types can cause seal swelling and component failure.
- Record all maintenance in the logbook and include the serial number of replaced parts.
- When translating this page, retain technical identifiers such as model numbers, part numbers, and image filenames exactly as shown (e.g., images/control_panel_front.png and images/valve_subassembly_diagram.png) to avoid supply chain errors.

Appendix: Torque and Fastener Table
Fasteners securing the access cover are M6x1.0; tighten to 7 N·m in a cross pattern. For the valve subassembly, use 14 N·m for the M8 bolts and apply Loctite 243.

End of Page 54

title: Remote Work Data Handling — Policy Note
source_text:
# Remote Work Data Handling — Policy Note

## Overview

This policy note outlines updated expectations for handling company data while working remotely. It complements existing security guidance and should be read alongside the full Data Protection Policy available at https://www.example.org/policy. The objective is to provide clear, actionable instructions for employees, contractors, and partners who access, process, or store company information away from corporate premises.

> All staff must treat company data with the same care remotely as they would in the office. Exceptions require documented approval from Information Security.

## Scope

Applies to:

- All employees, contractors, and temporary workers.
- Personal devices used for work tasks.
- Third-party services that process company data.

This note does not replace role-specific procedures; rather, it highlights baseline requirements for remote operations.

## Key Definitions

- Confidential data: Financial records, customer personal information, authentication credentials, and proprietary source code.
- Personal device: Any non-company-issued hardware used to access company systems.

See Section 4.2 for a definition of "sensitive processing" and examples.

## Policy Statement

1. Devices and Encryption

   - Company-issued devices must use full-disk encryption and screen lock. For non-company devices, employees must enable industry-standard protections such as `encrypt-at-rest` where supported.
   - Do not store confidential data on unencrypted removable media.

2. Network and Access Controls

   - Always use the company VPN when accessing internal services from public or untrusted networks.
   - Multi-factor authentication (MFA) is mandatory for all remote access to internal systems.

3. Data Minimization and Retention

   - Only download the minimum data required to complete a task. Follow the `data-retention` schedule in the Data Protection Policy for local copies.
   - Use approved cloud storage and collaboration tools; avoid ad hoc file sharing services.

## Responsibilities

- Employees: Follow procedures, report incidents promptly, and attend required training.
- Managers: Ensure team compliance and document any approved exceptions.
- Information Security: Maintain tooling, review exception requests, and produce quarterly compliance reports.

## Reporting and Incident Response

If you suspect a data breach or device compromise:

1. Immediately disconnect the device from the network.
2. Notify Information Security via security-incident@company.example or use the emergency hotline.
3. Preserve logs and provide a written account of the events.

Timely reporting accelerates containment and reduces potential harm to data subjects.

## Compliance and Legal Considerations

Remote work practices must align with applicable regulations, including GDPR and local privacy laws. Legal holds and litigation requests may override standard `data-retention` schedules; consult Legal if you receive any subpoena or preservation notice.

## Review Cycle and Exceptions

This policy note will be reviewed annually, or sooner if there are significant changes in threat landscape or regulation. Exceptions are granted only after documented risk assessment and approval from Information Security and Legal.

## Practical Examples

- Example 1: A marketing analyst must analyze customer segments. They should request a filtered dataset through the analytics team rather than downloading the full production database.
- Example 2: A contractor using a personal laptop must connect through the company VPN and enable disk encryption; temporary credentials are time-limited.

## Appendix A — Quick Links

- Full Data Protection Policy: https://www.example.org/policy
- Incident reporting: mailto:security-incident@company.example
- IT support portal: https://support.company.example

## Contact

For questions about this note, contact Information Security or your manager. For legal interpretations, contact the Legal team.

[Back to top](#remote-work-data-handling-—-policy-note)

title: DataTransformer module: stream processing utilities
source_text:
'''Module: DataTransformer

This module provides a small, illustrative set of utilities for transforming streaming
records. It is intended as a readable example that mixes explanatory prose, docstrings,
comments, and code snippets. Translators should convert all human-facing text while
preserving code identifiers, inline flags, and configuration keys.
'''

DEFAULT_TIMEOUT = 30  # Default timeout in seconds for external requests

class DataTransformer:
    '''Simple transformer for record dictionaries.

    The transformer normalizes keys, applies type conversions, and can add
    computed fields. It accepts a 'schema' mapping that defines expected types
    and default values for missing keys.

    Parameters:
    - schema: a dict mapping field names to types or callables used to coerce values.
    - strict: if True, unknown fields raise ValueError; otherwise they are kept.

    Example:
        transformer = DataTransformer({'id': int, 'price': float})
        transformer.transform({'id': '42', 'price': '3.50'})
    '''

    def __init__(self, schema=None, strict=False):
        self.schema = schema or {}
        self.strict = strict

    def transform(self, record):
        '''Transform a single record in place.

        This method performs the following steps:
        1. Normalize keys to lowercase strings.
        2. Apply type coercion according to the schema.
        3. Fill defaults where applicable.

        Returns the transformed record.
        '''
        # Normalize keys
        normalized = {str(k).lower(): v for k, v in record.items()}

        # Apply schema coercion
        for key, spec in self.schema.items():
            if key in normalized and normalized[key] is not None:
                try:
                    normalized[key] = spec(normalized[key])
                except Exception:
                    # If coercion fails, keep original value and attach warning
                    normalized[key + '_coercion_failed'] = True

        if self.strict:
            # Remove unknown keys when running in strict mode
            unknown = set(normalized) - set(self.schema)
            for k in unknown:
                del normalized[k]

        return normalized


def process_stream(source, transformer, timeout=DEFAULT_TIMEOUT):
    '''Process an iterable source of records using a DataTransformer.

    Parameters:
    - source: an iterable that yields mapping-like objects (dicts).
    - transformer: an instance of DataTransformer or any object with a 'transform' method.
    - timeout: maximum time in seconds to wait for external resources per batch.

    Yields transformed records one by one. Errors are logged but do not stop the stream.
    '''
    for raw in source:
        try:
            yield transformer.transform(raw)
        except Exception as e:
            # Log the error and continue processing next record
            # Real implementations should use the logging module; here we attach an error field
            err = {'error': str(e), 'original': raw}
            yield err

# Usage example and small CLI demonstration. Translators should translate the descriptive
# text below, but leave the code and CLI flags unchanged.

# Example usage in a script:

```python
from data_transformer import DataTransformer, process_stream

# Define a simple schema
schema = {'id': int, 'value': float}
transformer = DataTransformer(schema)

# Simulated source of records
source = [{'ID': '1', 'Value': '10.0'}, {'ID': '2', 'Value': 'invalid'}]

for item in process_stream(source, transformer):
    print(item)
```

# Configuration file (config.yaml) example. Keys in YAML are significant and should not be translated.

```yaml
# config.yaml
input:
  type: s3
  bucket: my-data-bucket
processing:
  schema:
    id: int
    value: float
  strict: false
cli:
  flags:
    - --dry-run
```

Notes:
- Keep identifiers such as DataTransformer, DEFAULT_TIMEOUT, and process_stream unchanged.
- Translate user-facing comments and docstrings into the target language, including the
  prose in the module header and the explanatory paragraphs above.
- Do not translate configuration keys (for example, input, processing) or CLI flags like
  --dry-run. The filename config.yaml should also remain literal when referenced.
translation_notes: Translate all docstrings and comments, preserving code blocks and identifiers.
Do not translate configuration keys, CLI flags, filenames, or code identifiers listed in protected_spans.
expected_challenges: Deciding whether short technical phrases are part of code or translatable prose (e.g., 'type coercion').
Handling inline code fragments and flags within sentences without altering punctuation.
Maintaining consistency of schema key names and YAML keys that must remain unchanged.
Translating error message fragments while not changing underlying JSON-like structures.

style_label: Documents with multilingual input and selective translation
title: Note on Translating English Documents with Embedded Hindi Examples
source_text:
Overview
This note is intended for translators working on predominantly English documents that include targeted Hindi examples, quoted utterances, or short glossary entries. The purpose is to provide clear guidance and contextual examples so the Hindi fragments remain unchanged in the translated output while the surrounding English is rendered accurately into the target language. Pay attention to how Hindi spans are integrated syntactically: sometimes they function as full clauses, other times as fixed formulaic expressions.

Why such selective preservation matters
Many learners and bilingual readers rely on exact Hindi forms within an English text to teach grammar, illustrate pronunciation, or preserve cultural nuance. For instance, a textbook might include the example मुझे पानी चाहिए embedded in an English explanation to show a common verb-object structure. Altering spelling, punctuation, or transliteration of those Hindi fragments would defeat the pedagogical intent. Therefore, the translator must leave the Hindi substrings exactly as they appear while translating all English prose that surrounds them.

Examples and commentary
Dialogue 1 (classroom example):
Teacher: Explain to your partner how to ask for water politely in Hindi.
Student A: You can say मुझे पानी चाहिए when you are thirsty and want a formal yet simple request. In this sentence, मुझे marks the experiencer (to me) and चाहिए is the polite verb phrase for 'need'.
Commentary: Translate the English explanatory sentences into the target language, but keep the Hindi example मुझे पानी चाहिए verbatim. Do not attempt to translate or transliterate the Hindi example into the target language vocabulary or script.

Dialogue 2 (politeness formulas):
When introducing daily phrases, many handbooks include short words like धन्यवाद in a glossary alongside equivalents such as 'thank you.' Leave those entries unchanged. For example: the course glossary lists धन्यवाद — a short, high-frequency expression used in everyday exchanges. Your translation should render the surrounding sentence and the explanatory gloss into the target language, but do not modify the Hindi token itself.

Formatting and placement rules
- Preserve the original Devanagari spelling, diacritics, and whitespace for the Hindi spans. Do not normalize these spans into Latin characters or alter their case.
- Maintain the Hindi spans in place: if a Hindi phrase occurs mid-sentence, it must remain mid-sentence in the translated output, with appropriate surrounding punctuation retained.
- If the English surrounding text contains parentheses, quotation marks, or em dashes that bracket a Hindi fragment, keep those punctuation marks in the translated text as they contribute to structure. Only the Hindi letters inside should remain unchanged.

Practical suggestions for ambiguous cases
Sometimes the English narrative paraphrases or comments on a Hindi example (for instance, describing grammatical roles or phonetic details). Translate that commentary normally. If a Hindi example appears with a literal gloss in English, translate the gloss into the target language but leave the Hindi term intact. If you encounter transliteration or romanized Hindi in the source, verify whether a native-script equivalent is present; only sections in Devanagari in this document are protected and must remain exactly as-is.

Short exercises for the translator
1. Translate the English instructions and commentary into the target language while leaving each Hindi fragment untouched in its original location. 2. Verify punctuation: ensure commas, colons, and parentheses align sensibly in the translated sentence after you insert an unchanged Hindi span. 3. Preserve line breaks and list formatting where the original uses bullet points or short glossary entries.

Glossary (do not alter entries)
मुझे पानी चाहिए — example sentence used to illustrate 'need' constructions
धन्यवाद — common formula meaning 'thank you'

Final note
Consistency matters: use the same approach for every protected Hindi substring in a document. If the source contains multiple identical Hindi tokens, treat each occurrence the same way. The translator should ensure that the final translated document reads naturally in the target language while retaining all Devanagari fragments exactly as they appear here.
translation_notes: Translate only the English text; leave the listed Devanagari Hindi spans unchanged in place.
Preserve original spelling, diacritics, and punctuation around protected Hindi substrings.
expected_challenges: Integrating unchanged Hindi spans into grammatically correct target-language sentences when word order differs.
Maintaining register and explanatory nuance when the Hindi fragment carries culture-specific meaning not easily rendered in the target language.

title: Field Survey Summary: Riparian Vegetation and Soil Profile (FS-055)
source_text: Site Overview Site ID: FS-055 GPS: 45.1234 N, -122.1234 W This field survey report summarizes a riparian transect sampled on 2025-03-14. The study focused on vegetation cover, dominant species, and near-surface soil depth across three transects. Observers documented cover estimates and soil depth at standard quadrats, and photographed representative sections for later annotation (see Figure 1 and Figure 2). The photographs are saved as placeholder paths and must be preserved during translation: images/site-photo-055.jpg and images/soil-profile-055.jpg. Materials and Methods Sampling followed the standard protocol described in the project manual. Each transect (A, B, C) contained five 1 m^2 quadrats spaced at 2 m intervals along a 10 m line. For each quadrat, the following data were recorded: Quadrat ID, dominant species, percent ground cover, and soil depth measured to the first impermeable layer. Voucher specimens were collected for ambiguous taxa and annotated in field notes. Table 1. Quadrats and Observations | Transect | Quadrat ID | Species Dominant | Coverage (%) | Soil Depth (cm) | |---------:|:----------:|:----------------:|-------------:|----------------:| | A | A-01 | Salix exigua | 65 | 12 | | A | A-02 | Carex obnupta | 80 | 8 | | A | A-03 | Poa pratensis | 40 | 15 | | A | A-04 | Mixed | 55 | 10 | | A | A-05 | Salix exigua | 70 | 9 | | B | B-01 | Carex obnupta | 90 | 6 | | B | B-02 | Typha latifolia | 30 | 20 | | B | B-03 | Salix exigua | 50 | 11 | | B | B-04 | Mixed | 60 | 7 | | B | B-05 | Poa pratensis | 35 | 13 | | C | C-01 | Carex obnupta | 85 | 5 | | C | C-02 | Salix exigua | 55 | 10 | | C | C-03 | Mixed | 60 | 9 | | C | C-04 | Typha latifolia | 20 | 18 | | C | C-05 | Poa pratensis | 45 | 14 | Notes on Table 1: Table headers 'Species Dominant', 'Coverage (%)', and 'Soil Depth (cm)' are essential for correct interpretation of the dataset. Keep header order and labels unchanged in translation. Summary and Interpretation Overall, Carex obnupta exhibited the highest average cover across transects, particularly in low-elevation zones near the watercourse. Salix exigua was the most frequent woody dominator in upper banks. Soil depths were shallow in vegetated mats dominated by sedges and deeper where Typha formed tussocks. Observers recorded a high frequency of mixed-species quadrats, indicating patchy transitions between sedge-dominant and willow-dominant microhabitats. Figure 1. Site overview with transect lines marked (placeholder). Image path: images/site-photo-055.jpg Caption: A wide-angle view looking northeast; Transect A is visible in the foreground. Translate caption text and preserve the image path string exactly as written. Figure 2. Representative soil profile from Transect B (placeholder). Image path: images/soil-profile-055.jpg ![Figure 2: Soil profile showing organic mat and underlying mineral layer](images/soil-profile-055.jpg) Caption: Soil horizon developed over compacted alluvium; depth measurement shown at 20 cm. Translate the caption and preserve parentheses and numeric labels. Recommendations 1. Repeat sampling during late-summer low-flow conditions to compare seasonal shifts in cover and emergent species. 2. Submit voucher specimens for Poa and mixed assemblages to corroborate field identifications. 3. When translating, ensure numerical values and units remain identical and that table separators (|) and header spellings are kept consistent with the original. Field Observers: J. Morales, L. Chen, and A. Patel Report compiled: 2025-03-20
translation_notes: Translate all figure captions, table headers, and surrounding prose; preserve image file paths and figure labels verbatim.
Keep table header order and pipe-separated Markdown table layout unchanged; numeric values and units (e.g., %, cm, m^2) must remain exact.
expected_challenges: Rendering of Markdown table separators and header alignment must be preserved during translation.
Translating scientific names vs. common names: maintain Latin binomials (e.g., Salix exigua) unchanged while translating common names.
Preserving image path strings and figure references exactly as they appear.
Handling parenthetical numeric labels and units without introducing spacing or punctuation changes.

title: Acme Platform v2.3.1 Release Summary
source_text:
# Release Summary: Acme Platform v2.3.1

This document summarizes the key changes, upgrade instructions, and known issues for Acme Platform release v2.3.1. It is intended for system administrators, site reliability engineers, and developers responsible for upgrading production environments.

## Highlights

- Security patch for the authentication subsystem that closes a token replay vulnerability.
- Performance improvements to the job scheduler, reducing average queue latency by ~18% under high load.
- New CLI option `--safe-mode` to run maintenance commands without executing destructive actions.
- Updated documentation, including a new troubleshooting section in `README.md`.

> Note: If you are maintaining older clusters, see the compatibility notes under **Upgrade Notes** before proceeding.

## Key Changes

### Authentication

- Replaced the legacy token store with a hardened implementation that enforces per-token rate limits.
- Fixed an edge case where short-lived tokens could be reused if clocks were not synchronized.

### Scheduler

- Rewrote the hot path for task dispatch to use a lock-free queue, which reduces contention on multi-core nodes.
- Added telemetry counters for queue depth and average dispatch time. These appear under the `scheduler.metrics` namespace.

### CLI and Tooling

- `acme-cli` now supports the `--safe-mode` switch that simulates operations and prints what would change.
- The upgrade script `scripts/upgrade.sh` includes a dry-run mode and outputs a pre-check report.

### Documentation

- Added a troubleshooting appendix to `README.md` with common error messages and recommended actions.
- Added an architecture diagram to the docs site: https://example.com/release-notes/arch-diagram

## Upgrade Notes

Before upgrading, review the following checklist and run the pre-upgrade checks:

1. Backup current configuration and database snapshots.
2. Ensure node clocks are synchronized (NTP) to avoid token reuse issues.
3. Verify that custom plugins are compatible with the new scheduler API.
4. Test the upgrade path in a staging environment using `scripts/upgrade.sh --dry-run`.

Known compatibility considerations:

- Plugins that rely on scheduler internals must be updated to the new `TaskDispatch` interface. See the API changelog in the developer docs.
- If you use third-party monitoring integrations, ensure they map the new `scheduler.metrics` counters correctly.

For quick reference, jump to the specific section: [Upgrade Notes](#upgrade-notes).

## Known Issues

- Under certain high-throughput scenarios, telemetry aggregation may lag by several seconds. We recommend tuning the aggregation window in `config/telemetry.yaml`.
- In rare cases, the web UI may show stale job status after scheduler failover. A manual refresh resolves the display inconsistency.

If you encounter these or other issues, collect logs and file a report at https://example.com/support with the following attachments:

- System logs from the affected node
- Output of `acme-cli diagnostics` (use `--safe-mode` to avoid side effects)
- A copy of your `config/telemetry.yaml` if telemetry issues are present

## Deprecations

- The legacy HTTP healthcheck endpoint `/v1/health` is deprecated and will be removed in the next major release. Use `/v2/healthz` instead.
- Old plugin hooks `onTaskStart` and `onTaskFinish` are deprecated; migrate to the `TaskLifecycle` hooks.

## Rollback Strategy

If an upgrade fails, follow these steps:

- Stop incoming traffic to the cluster.
- Restore the database snapshot taken before upgrade.
- Re-deploy the previous release artifacts and restart services.
- Validate with a full integration test run before re-enabling traffic.

## Contributors and Credits

Thanks to the following contributors for this release:

- Engineering: Priya N., Mateo R., and Chen L.
- Security: D. Alvarez and K. Morgan.
- Documentation: S. Ito and M. Green.

© 2026 Acme Corp. For full details, including the complete changelog and API diffs, visit: https://example.com/release-notes

title: Handout: Intermediate Multilingual Communication Workshop — Week 3
source_text:
Course: Intermediate Multilingual Communication
Instructor: Dr. Lina Patel
Duration: 3 hours
Format: Interactive seminar with breakout activities and peer feedback

Overview
This handout prepares participants for Week 3 of a semester-long workshop on multilingual communication strategies. The session emphasizes pragmatic language use in classroom settings, code-switching awareness, and how to design short lessons that include non-English target-language prompts. We will analyze short dialogues, practice designing warm-up activities, and assess micro-teaching lessons. Wherever Hindi examples are used in this document they are included as native-script references and should be treated as language items rather than content to be translated.

Learning objectives
- Identify moments in classroom discourse where a single-word greeting or classroom direction in another language can increase student rapport and clarity.
- Produce a two-minute warm-up activity incorporating a non-English greeting, a student response, and a transition to English instruction.
- Evaluate peers’ micro-lessons for effective code-switching and cultural sensitivity.

Example (greeting practice)
Start with a brief greeting in the students’ home language to model inclusion. For instance, say the greeting: "नमस्ते" to acknowledge Hindi-speaking learners before switching into English instructions. Discuss with your group why a one-word greeting may be more effective than an extended monologue in the students’ L1 when time is limited.

Weekly schedule — Week 3
- 00:00–00:20: Introduction and review of Week 2 reflections
- 00:20–01:00: Mini-lecture on pragmatic code-switching and the pedagogy of brief L1 use
- 01:00–01:30: Group activity — design a 3-minute warm-up
- 01:30–02:15: Micro-teaching sessions (3×10 minutes) with peer feedback
- 02:15–02:40: Whole-class debrief: transferability of strategies to different languages
- 02:40–03:00: Assessment rubric calibration and final Q&A

Activity instructions
Break into groups of four. Each group creates a 3-minute warm-up that includes:
1) a greeting in a target language (one line only),
2) a comprehension check in English,
3) a smooth transition into the main English-language task.

Use the following practical prompt in the warm-up: One student says a short instruction in their designated target-language, for example: "कृपया कार्यपत्र पूरा करें" and another student repeats the instruction and asks one clarification question in English. The instructor should time each group and provide immediate feedback focused on clarity and inclusivity.

Assessment and rubrics
Micro-teaching is assessed on three dimensions: clarity (40%), linguistic sensitivity (30%), and classroom flow (30%). Clarify whether non-English insertions are intentional pedagogical choices and note their function (e.g., greeting, direction, cultural reference). Provide written comments that specify which moments enhanced rapport and which caused confusion.

Suggested readings and resources
- Selected articles on code-switching in classroom pedagogy (see course packet)
- Practical checklist: when to use a single-word insertion versus extended L1 explanation
- Video samples of short warm-ups demonstrating best practices

Glossary (for instructor reference)
- code-switching: deliberate alternation between languages for communicative effect
- L1 insertion: using a student’s first language briefly to scaffold understanding
- micro-teaching: short teaching segment used for practice and feedback

Notes to translators
This document will be localized into other languages for distribution to non-English-speaking instructors. When translating, translate all English content while leaving any included native-script examples in Hindi unchanged. Preserve formatting for headings, bullet lists, and quoted strings.

Contact
If you have questions about implementing these activities in your context, email the course coordinator at lina.patel@university.edu or bring sample materials to the next seminar for review.
translation_notes: Translate all English content; do not alter or translate the Hindi substrings listed in protected_spans.
Preserve formatting such as headings, bullet lists, and quoted phrases exactly; leave quoted Hindi phrases verbatim.
expected_challenges: Maintaining clarity around which items are pedagogical examples versus translatable narrative.
Preserving non-English script integrity and avoiding accidental transliteration of Hindi spans.
Rendering pragmatic terms (e.g., code-switching, L1 insertion) in target language without losing instructional function.

title: Effects of Buffer Composition on Microbial Biofilm Growth: Imaging and Quantitative Analysis
source_text: Abstract This report examines the influence of buffer composition on early-stage biofilm formation by Pseudomonas aeruginosa over a 48-hour period. We combine time-lapse microscopy, image analysis, and quantitative assays to correlate structural features with measured growth rates. Key observations are illustrated in Figure 1 and summarized in Table 1. Introduction Biofilm development is sensitive to ionic strength, pH, and nutrient availability. Previous studies have reported variable attachment and maturation dynamics depending on buffer formulation. Here we compare three commonly used buffers at physiological and near-neutral pH to determine their impact on microcolony expansion. Methods Experiments were performed with a standardized inoculum (optical density OD600 = 0.05) and incubated at 37°C. For imaging we used a phase-contrast microscope at 40x magnification and acquired images every 30 min for 48 h. Image file names follow the convention images/fig_01.png and images/fig_02.jpg for representative frames. Quantitative growth rates were computed from segmented colony area versus time using linear regression over the initial 12 h window (R2 > 0.95). Biological replicates: n=24 per condition. Results Representative micrographs are shown in Figure 1 (single frame at 12 h) and Figure 2 (frame at 36 h). Visual inspection indicates more compact, densely packed colonies in Buffer B compared with Buffer A and Buffer C. Table 1 lists mean growth parameters with standard deviations and significance compared to Buffer A (one-way ANOVA, post hoc Tukey test). Figure 1. Phase-contrast micrograph of P. aeruginosa biofilm at 12 h in Buffer B (see images/fig_01.png). Scale bar = 50 μm. Image emphasizes dense peripheral expansion and multiple daughter microcolonies. Table 1. Initial growth metrics for Pseudomonas aeruginosa biofilms under three buffer conditions (mean ± SD; n per condition shown). | Buffer | Mean growth rate [μm2/h] | Standard deviation [μm2/h] | n | p vs Buffer A | |--------|--------------------------:|---------------------------:|---:|--------------:| | Buffer A (PBS, pH 7.4) | 12.4 ± 2.1 | 2.1 | 24 | — | | Buffer B (MOPS, pH 7.4) | 18.9 ± 3.4 | 3.4 | 24 | p < 0.001 | | Buffer C (Tris, pH 7.4) | 10.7 ± 1.8 | 1.8 | 24 | p = 0.02 | Caption: Mean growth rate measured as change in segmented colony area per hour. Statistical comparisons performed relative to Buffer A. All buffers were adjusted to pH 7.4 and maintained at 37°C during imaging. Discussion Buffer composition had a statistically significant effect on early expansion: Buffer B increased mean growth rate by ~52% relative to Buffer A (p < 0.001). The table header units [μm2/h] are essential for interpreting magnitude; translators should preserve unit symbols and superscripts. The consistent pH (pH 7.4) across conditions isolates the effect of buffer chemistry and ionic composition. Image analysis highlighted two morphological modes: compact microcolonies with high local density (Buffer B) and loose, filamentous structures (Buffer C). These differences can be correlated to nutrient diffusion and surface charge effects specific to the buffer ions. Conclusions The study demonstrates that buffer selection, independent of pH, alters early biofilm growth kinetics and structure. For reproducibility, image filenames (e.g., images/fig_01.png) and table headers must be preserved in translations. Future work will probe ionic strength systematically and include fluorescence labeling to resolve matrix composition. Figure 2. Representative frame at 36 h for Buffer C (images/fig_02.jpg). Scale bar = 100 μm. The image shows dispersed colonies with elongated tendrils.
translation_notes: Do not translate file paths or image filenames (e.g., images/fig_01.png).
Preserve table headers, units (e.g., [μm2/h]), and statistical notation (e.g., p < 0.001, n=24).
expected_challenges: Rendering of table alignment and numeric formats while translating header text and units.
Preserving image tags and captions exactly, including file paths and scale bar units.
Handling scientific abbreviations and statistical symbols (ANOVA, p-values, ±, μm²/h) without altering meaning.

title: Study Guide: Collaborative Git Workflows for Teams
source_text:
# Study Guide: Collaborative Git Workflows for Teams

## Overview

This study guide outlines essential Git concepts and hands-on practices for developers working in small-to-medium teams. It focuses on workflows, branch management, conflict resolution, and integrating with CI/CD. The aim is to provide a concise reference you can use when preparing for code reviews, pair programming sessions, or a technical interview.

## Learning Objectives

- Understand common branching models (feature branching, Git Flow, trunk-based development).
- Apply safe practices for rewriting history and force-pushing.
- Resolve merge conflicts and rebases with minimal disruption.
- Integrate Git operations with CI/CD and code review tools.

## Core Concepts

### Branching Models

- Feature branching: short-lived branches named like `feature/issue-123`.
- Git Flow: a structured model with `develop`, `master`, `release/*`, and `hotfix/*` branches.
- Trunk-based development: small, frequent merges to `main` or `trunk`.

Consider writing a short team guide that includes naming conventions and branch lifetime expectations.

### Rewriting History: When and How

Rewriting history is sometimes necessary to clean up a feature branch before merging. Use interactive rebase (`git rebase -i`) to squash or reorder commits. When you must update a remote branch after rewriting history, prefer safer force-push strategies and coordinate with teammates.

Inline examples:

- Amending the last commit message: `git commit --amend`
- Force-push with lease: `git push --force-with-lease`

### Merge vs Rebase

- Merge preserves the chronological merge history and produces a merge commit.
- Rebase creates a linear history by replaying commits onto a base, which can make `git log` easier to read.

Best practice: use rebase for local cleanup and merge for public branches unless your team agrees on a linear history.

## Practical Exercises

[Jump to this section](#practical-exercises) to practice the commands below.

### Exercise 1: Create and Merge a Feature Branch

1. Create a branch: `git checkout -b feature/auth-improvements`.
2. Make two small commits.
3. Push the branch and open a pull request.
4. Simulate a review change, make another commit, and merge via the platform.

### Exercise 2: Rebase and Resolve Conflicts

1. Update your local `main`: `git checkout main && git pull`.
2. Rebase your feature branch: `git checkout feature/auth-improvements && git rebase main`.
3. Resolve any conflicts, continue with `git rebase --continue`.

## Tips & Pitfalls

> Avoid using `git push --force` on shared branches without coordination. Accidental overwrites are a common source of lost work.

- Keep commits small and descriptive.
- Rebase only local, private branches when rewriting history.
- Use `--force-with-lease` instead of `--force` to reduce the risk of clobbering someone else's work.

## Assessment Checklist

- [ ] Can you create and push a feature branch?
- [ ] Can you rebase safely and resolve conflicts?
- [ ] Do you know when to merge vs rebase according to your team's policy?

Complete the checklist by performing each task in a real repository or sandbox environment.

## Integrating with CI/CD and Code Review

Configure CI to run on pull requests and fail the build on lint or test regressions. Protect important branches (e.g., `main`) in your hosting service and require status checks to pass before merging. If your organization exposes an internal service or external API, be mindful of versioning—document whether your project targets `API v2` or another specific endpoint.

Useful links:

- Official Git documentation: https://git-scm.com
- GitHub flow reference: https://guides.github.com/introduction/flow/

## FAQ

Q: When should I rebase a public branch?

A: Prefer not to rewrite history on branches other developers depend on. If necessary, communicate and ensure teammates are aware so they can synchronize their clones.

Q: How do I recover from an accidental force-push?

A: Use `git reflog` to find the commit and, if needed, create a new branch from that commit to restore history.

---

For more practice, clone a sample repository and follow the exercises above. Keep this guide handy as a quick reference during collaborative work sessions.

title: Audio Guide: Modern Narratives Gallery
source_text:
Welcome to the Modern Narratives Gallery. This audio guide is intended to accompany your self-paced visit; each track corresponds to a single installation or cluster of works. Please use the gallery map to locate the numbered plaques on the walls and pause the guide when you wish to examine details.

Track 1 — Introduction to the Gallery (2:10)
The Modern Narratives Gallery explores the relationship between personal memory and collective history. As you enter the space, notice the layout: works are grouped thematically rather than chronologically to encourage cross-references. Take a moment to view the centerpiece directly ahead, then proceed clockwise to follow the curator's suggested route.

Track 2 — Central Installation: A Dialog of Frames (4:25)
This installation uses layered frames and translucent fabrics to evoke the idea of seeing through multiple generations. The artist employed found photographs, archival prints, and fragile textiles to create depth. Look for the small handwritten notes tucked into the frames; they are original marginalia from donors and family members. One plaque lists the title in its original language as follows.
सत्य की तलाश
This is the title the artist provided in Hindi; it refers directly to the central motif of the installation, the quest for truth as interpreted by layered family narratives.

Track 3 — Artist Statement (1:40)
On the wall nearby you will see a short quote from the artist. It is presented in the artist's native tongue as an intentional element of the piece and should remain as written.
कला मन की आवाज़ है
The artist insists on preserving the phrase to emphasize that the listening experience is part of the artwork. When you hear the spoken interpretation later in the recorded tour, remember that the original wording is integral to the piece's identity.

Track 4 — Interactive Kiosks (3:00)
Proceed to the right-hand wall where three interactive kiosks allow visitors to compare translations and contextual notes. The kiosks are optional; they provide background on donors and technical details about the textile restoration. Use the touch slider to view close-ups and magnified stitch work. If you prefer audio-only, stay on this guide and continue to the next track.

Track 5 — Didactic Panel and Glossary (2:45)
We provide a short glossary of terms used throughout the gallery to help non-specialist visitors. Words like provenance, palimpsest, and restitution appear in panel text. Important multilingual elements, including titles and quoted utterances, are deliberately left in their original scripts and are marked on the map. When reading the printed guide, take care not to substitute transliterations for originals; the original script carries cultural and semantic weight.

Track 6 — Visitor Recommendations (1:30)
For an engaged visit, spend at least ten minutes with the central installation, and then return to works 4 through 6 where interactive labels allow you to toggle archival layers. Photography without flash is permitted for personal use, but please do not touch the textile surfaces. If you have questions, the gallery attendants at the entrance can provide further reading lists and catalog references.

Thank you for visiting the Modern Narratives Gallery. This concludes the main audio guide. If you would like an extended curator talk, proceed to the auditorium where a scheduled discussion will begin on the hour.

title: TrailMate X2 Product Catalog Page
source_text: TrailMate X2 — All-Terrain Daypack Overview The TrailMate X2 is a versatile daypack designed for hikers, cyclists, and urban commuters who need a lightweight bag with high organization capacity. It combines a reinforced external frame with breathable padding and modular accessory points. Typical use cases include multi-hour hikes, overnight bivouacs with light gear, and daily commuting. See Figure 1 for the primary view of the pack. Specifications Below is the summary table of the current TrailMate family. This inset highlights the triple-stitch seam used on higher-load points. Product variations and ordering The TrailMate X2 ships with a removable sternum strap, a rain cover stored in the base pocket, and a detachable accessory pouch. Sizing guidance: for torso lengths 40–50 cm we recommend the X2 for optimal load distribution. Sample serial and batch information for quality tracking is printed on the inner label (for example: S/N: TMX2-2026). Care and maintenance Always close zippers before washing. Spot-clean the outer fabric with a soft brush and mild detergent; do not tumble dry. The rain cover may be machine washed on a gentle cycle. For repairs, retain original stitching samples for color and thread matching. Warranty and returns The standard limited warranty covers manufacturing defects for two years from the date of purchase. Proof of purchase (invoice or receipt) is required for warranty claims. For abrasions or wear from regular use, our repair service offers parts replacement at a nominal fee; see the Repair & Parts section on our website for instructions and forms. Use cases and styling notes The TrailMate X2 bridges the gap between ultralight daypacks and heavier expedition packs. For light alpine objectives, pair the X2 with a minimal sleeping system and compressible jacket; a 28 L capacity accommodates a 20 L internal stuff sack and additional external carry. For urban commuters, the padded laptop sleeve fits devices up to 15.6" and the front pocket organizes chargers and transit cards. Comparisons When choosing between models, refer to the table above. title: Local Development Tutorial: Docker Compose + Make Workflow
source_text:
# Local Development Tutorial: Docker Compose + Make Workflow

This tutorial explains a practical local development workflow using Docker Compose and a small Makefile. It targets developers who want a repeatable, team-friendly way to start services, run tests, and iterate quickly.

## Table of contents

- [Prerequisites](#prerequisites)
- [Quickstart](#quickstart)
- [Common tasks](#common-tasks)
- [Debugging tips](#debugging-tips)
- [FAQ](#faq)

## Prerequisites

Before you begin, ensure you have the following installed:

- Docker Engine and Docker Compose. See the official docs: https://docs.docker.com/compose/
- GNU Make (optional but recommended)
- A text editor and command-line access

Note: this guide assumes a Unix-like shell. Windows users can follow along using WSL2 or Git Bash.

## Quickstart

Follow these steps to get a simple app running locally.

1. Clone the repository and change into the project directory.
2. Inspect the service definition file named docker-compose.yml.
3. Start the stack with a single command:

```bash
# build images and start containers in the foreground
docker-compose up --build
```

4. Open your browser at http://localhost:8080 or the port defined in docker-compose.yml.

If you prefer detached mode, run `docker-compose up -d` and use `docker-compose logs -f` to stream logs.

## Common tasks

Use the included Makefile for shortcuts. Typical targets include:

- `make dev`: builds images and starts services with live reload if configured
- `make test`: runs the test suite inside a dedicated test container
- `make clean`: removes created containers and images

Example Makefile fragment:

```makefile
.PHONY: dev test clean

dev:
	@echo 'Starting development stack'
	docker-compose up --build

test:
	docker-compose run --rm app pytest -q

clean:
	docker-compose down --rmi local -v
```

Tip: keep development-only dependencies out of production images. Use multi-stage builds for compiled assets.

## Debugging tips

> If something fails to start, check container logs first. Use `docker-compose logs <service>` or `docker logs <container>`.

Common issues and how to resolve them:

- Port conflicts: verify no other process is bound to the port. Use `lsof -i :8080` or change the host port mapping.
- Database migrations: ensure the migration scripts run on startup or execute them manually from the container.
- File permissions: bind mounts can inherit host permissions. Adjust ownership with `chown` or use named volumes.

When you need to run an interactive shell inside a container, use:

```bash
docker-compose run --rm app /bin/bash
```

Replace `/bin/bash` with the shell available in your image.

## Workflow suggestions

- Use `make dev` while developing. It standardizes the command and makes it easy for new contributors.
- Commit a minimal, well-documented docker-compose.yml that describes service names and dependencies, not every developer preference.
- Use environment files (`.env`) for configuration and include an `.env.example` in the repo.

## FAQ

Q: How do I run tests in CI with this setup?

A: Create a separate CI job that uses `docker-compose -f docker-compose.ci.yml up --build --abort-on-container-exit` and collects exit codes. Alternatively, run tests in a single container image built specifically for CI.

Q: Will my local volumes leak into production?

A: No. Use different compose files for development and production. Keep persistent volume definitions explicit in production manifests.

## Further reading

- Docker Compose overview: https://docs.docker.com/compose/
- Makefile basics: https://www.gnu.org/software/make/manual/make.html

If you need a trimmed down starting point, copy the `docker-compose.yml` from this project, replace image names with local builds, and experiment with one service at a time.

---

Anchors used above make it easy to share links to sections. Happy coding and iterate safely.

title: Guidance for Completing a Bilingual Patient Intake Form
source_text:
Overview
This document explains how to complete a bilingual patient intake form that contains both English instructions and a few protected Hindi labels. The form is primarily English; however, three Hindi field labels appear on the form and are intentionally left in Hindi to aid Hindi-speaking registrants. When translating this document into another language, translate every English sentence and interface instruction, but leave the Hindi labels exactly as they appear.

Purpose
The intake form collects basic demographics, contact details, and consent. It is used at clinics where staff may serve patients who read Hindi. To avoid confusion, certain critical labels are preserved in Hindi on the printed form.

Section 1 — Basic Information (How to fill)
- Enter your legal name in the "Name" field.
- For the label printed as "पूरा नाम", write the same legal name exactly as entered in English, or in Devanagari if you prefer. The presence of "पूरा नाम" is to help Hindi readers locate the name field quickly.
- Record your date of birth in the Date of Birth field; where the paper form shows "दिनांक" next to the date box, write the date in the format YYYY-MM-DD to prevent ambiguity.

Section 2 — Contact Details
- Provide a current mailing address and a phone number where you can be reached during business hours.
- The label "पता" appears next to the address block on bilingual forms. If you complete the form online, the same label is displayed beside the address input box.
- If you have a temporary address, state that clearly in the address line and include start and end dates for that temporary residency.

Example entries (do not modify the Hindi labels)
- Name: John A. Patel    "पूरा नाम"
- Address: 123 Elm Street, Apt 4B, Springfield    "पता"
- Date of Visit: 2026-05-20    "दिनांक"

Consent and Signature
- Read the consent paragraph in full. If any sentence is unclear, ask a staff member for clarification. The staff can provide verbal interpretation for Hindi speakers but will not change the printed Spanish or other language text.
- Sign and date the consent area. For the signature line, write your full name and the date in the box marked "Signature". If the printed form includes the Hindi label "दिनांक" next to the signature date box, ensure the date is written clearly.

Common pitfalls and how to avoid them
1) Translating protected labels: Translators must not translate the three Hindi labels. They serve as fixed anchors on the physical form and must match the printed version exactly. 
2) Date formats: Different locales use different date orders. Even if you translate the surrounding English, preserve any explicit format instruction (for example, "YYYY-MM-DD").
3) Field alignment: Keep examples aligned with their labels so that a translated manual still clearly points to the same form field.

Frequently Asked Questions
Q: Should I translate the Hindi labels into the target language?
A: No. The Hindi labels "पूरा नाम", "पता", and "दिनांक" must remain unchanged. Translate all other English text, including instructions, examples, and the consent paragraph.

Q: What if a target language requires different punctuation or spacing conventions?
A: Use the target language's punctuation norms for translated English sentences, but do not insert or remove spaces within the Hindi labels.

Closing notes
This guidance is intended to ensure that translated instructions map directly to the bilingual source form without altering the protected Hindi labels. When checking the translation, verify that the three Hindi substrings appear verbatim and in the same positions relative to the example entries.
translation_notes: Translate all English text; do not translate or alter the Hindi spans.
Preserve formatting and relative positions of examples and labels; keep date formats like YYYY-MM-DD unchanged.
expected_challenges: Ensuring the Hindi labels remain verbatim while translating surrounding English that references them.
Maintaining alignment of examples and labels across languages with different word lengths.
Preserving explicit formatting instructions (dates, signature blocks) that could be interpreted differently in the target language.
Handling mixed-script layout so the Hindi spans are not inadvertently re-tokenized or normalized.

title: Roman Household: Mosaic of the Seasons and Glassware
source_text: Exhibit Note: Roman Household Objects Overview This gallery presents two related domestic categories excavated from a villa site near the Tiber: a floor mosaic depicting seasonal motifs and a group of household glassware used for serving and storage. Labels and the catalogue table below provide concise information for display. Curators recommend referring to the full accession file for measuring techniques and provenance documentation. Provenance and Significance The mosaic (see Figure 1) is a polychrome tessellated floor panel recovered in situ from Room VIII of the villa complex. Its iconography — a central medallion surrounded by seasonal personifications — suggests a domestic program celebrating agricultural cycles and household abundance. The piece's stylistic parallels place its manufacture in the early Antonine period. Figure 1: Mosaic of the Seasons (detail). Photo: images/mosaic_seasons.jpg. Photograph orientation corresponds to the plan view in the conservation report. The accompanying glassware (see Figure 2) includes blown and mold-pressed vessels typical of Mediterranean trade networks. Their residues and wear patterns inform use-wear analysis and reconstruct daily routines in a middling Roman household. Figure 2: Roman glass jar with iridescence. Photo: images/glass_jar.jpg. Catalogue Summary (selected items) | Catalogue No. | Object | Material | Provenance | Date | Condition | |---------------|--------------------------------|-----------------|----------------------|-------------|------------------| | Catalogue No. 58 | Mosaic: central medallion | Stone tesserae | Villa, Room VIII | AD 120–150 | Fragmentary, stable | | 59 | Glass jar, ovoid body | Soda-lime glass | Villa, Service area | 2nd century | Surface iridescence | | 60 | Glass beaker, ribbed | Soda-lime glass | Villa, Dining room | 2nd century | Hairline crack | Description and Materiality The mosaic's tesserae are predominantly local limestone with imported colored marbles for the blue and red palette. Mortar analysis indicates a lime-based binder with organic admixtures consistent with techniques described in contemporaneous construction manuals. The stone selection and color arrangement reinforce the seasons' iconography: green for spring vegetation, ochre for summer harvests, brown for autumnal yields, and dark blue for winter skies. Glass pieces exhibit varying degrees of weathering. The jar in Figure 2 bears a fine iridescent patina due to long-term burial under fluctuating moisture and salt conditions. Residue sampling from the jar's interior revealed traces of olive oil and pine pitch, consistent with culinary or lamp-related uses. Conservation Notes - The mosaic has been stabilized in situ where feasible; several loose tesserae were set on conservation-grade reversible adhesive during the 2019 intervention. - Glass objects were desalinated, consolidated around hairline fractures, and housed in inert foam mounts for display. Interpretive Remarks Visitors should consider household objects as active agents in social practice rather than mere background décor. The mosaic's centrality within Room VIII indicates focal social gatherings, while the diversity of glass forms points to a range of dining and storage practices. Comparative parallels in urban houses suggest the villa's owners participated in broader Mediterranean exchange networks. References and Further Reading Selected conservation reports, stratigraphic drawings, and full accession records are available upon request. The accession label for the mosaic and related finds is filed under "Catalogue No. 58" in the main register.
translation_notes: Translate captions and table headers fully; preserve image file paths and HTML tags unchanged.
Maintain exact punctuation and numeric ranges (e.g., AD 120–150) as they appear; do not localize catalogue identifiers.
expected_challenges: Rendering museum-specific terminology (tesserae, iridescence, desalinated) into target language equivalents without loss of technical meaning.
Preserving table layout and exact catalogue identifiers while translating headers and cell contents.
Handling date ranges and en-dashes correctly (AD 120–150) and maintaining typographic characters.
Translating captions embedded near HTML image tags without altering the src paths or tag syntax.

title: FAQ: Data Export, Accessibility & Troubleshooting
source_text:
# FAQ: Data Export, Accessibility & Troubleshooting

Welcome to the FAQ page for data export, accessibility features, and common troubleshooting steps. This document is organized by topic and includes quick links to related sections.

- Quick links: [Getting started](#getting-started) • [Export formats](#export-formats) • [Accessibility](#accessibility) • [Troubleshooting](#troubleshooting) • [Contact](#contact-and-feedback)

## Getting started

### What can I export?

You can export reports, user lists, and activity logs. Supported file types include `CSV`, `JSON`, and `XLSX`.

- Small exports (under 10 MB) are generated instantly.
- Large exports are queued and emailed when ready.

Tip: For multilingual data, ensure your export character encoding is set to `UTF-8` before opening the file in a spreadsheet application.

### How do I request an export?

1. Navigate to Settings > Data > Export.
2. Select the report type and date range.
3. Choose the output format and click **Export**.

If the process fails, see [Troubleshooting](#troubleshooting) below.

## Export formats

### CSV

CSV files are suitable for lightweight datasets and spreadsheet imports. Remember:

- Fields containing commas or newlines will be quoted.
- Use `UTF-8` encoding for non-Latin scripts.

### JSON

JSON preserves nested structures and is recommended for programmatic ingestion. Each export uses an array of objects; timestamps are ISO 8601 strings.

### XLSX

XLSX is preferred for users who need formatting and multiple sheets. Large exports are split across sheets when necessary.

> Note: If you plan to import exported data into another system, validate the schema against the target system's expected fields.

## Accessibility

We are committed to inclusive design. Key accessibility features include:

- Keyboard navigation across export dialogs.
- Screen reader-friendly labels and ARIA attributes.
- High-contrast mode and resizable fonts.

Keyboard shortcuts: Use `Ctrl+Shift+F` to open the global search, then type "export" to find the export page.

### Reporting accessibility issues

If you encounter an accessibility problem, please report it with:

- A brief description of the issue.
- Steps to reproduce.
- Browser and assistive technology versions.

Send reports to privacy@example.org with the subject line "Accessibility Issue".

## Troubleshooting

### Export failed or hangs

Common causes and fixes:

1. Network interruption: Retry the export. If the problem persists, try a different network or a wired connection.
2. Permissions: Confirm that your role has export privileges under Admin > Roles.
3. Data size: For very large exports, request a scheduled export during off-peak hours.

If you see an error code, include it when contacting support. You can also visit our support center: https://www.example.com/support

### Incorrect character encoding

If exported text displays garbled characters:

- Ensure the file was opened with `UTF-8` encoding.
- Try importing the CSV into your application and explicitly select UTF-8 during import.

### Missing fields

If expected fields are not present in the export:

- Check the selected report template and filters.
- Verify that the fields are enabled for export in Settings > Export Templates.

For step-by-step troubleshooting, see the [Troubleshooting](#troubleshooting) anchor or contact the team.

## Contact and feedback

If you need additional help:

- Use the in-app Help widget for guided assistance.
- Email support at privacy@example.org for account-sensitive inquiries.
- File a bug or feature request via https://www.example.com/support/feedback

We welcome feedback about documentation, export behavior, and accessibility improvements. When submitting, include the steps to reproduce and screenshots if possible.

---

Last updated: 2026-03-15

title: April Community Newsletter — Neighborhood Programs and Language Notes
source_text:
Community Corner

Welcome to the April edition of our neighborhood newsletter. This month we have several updates about local programs, volunteer opportunities, and a short language corner aimed at helping volunteers and visitors communicate more effectively with long-time residents who prefer Hindi. Please read the sections below and sign up for activities that interest you.

Upcoming Events

1) Spring Park Clean-Up (Saturday, April 16, 9:00 AM)
Join neighbors at Riverside Park for trash pickup, native planting, and bench repairs. Tools and gloves will be provided; volunteers should bring water and comfortable shoes. Meet at the park kiosk near the playground. For clarity of signage during the event, we will post the welcome banner reading: स्वागत है at the kiosk entrance so that longstanding residents who read Hindi feel included.

2) Free Tax Help Clinic (Wednesday evenings)
Certified volunteers will offer basic tax-filing assistance in the community center. Appointments are limited; please register online or call the center desk. If you work at the desk and someone asks for quiet space, use the standard library-style sign: कृपया शोर न करें posted outside the consultation room to keep the atmosphere calm.

3) Neighborhood Potluck and Story Exchange (Sunday, April 24, 5:00 PM)
Bring a dish to share and a short story or memory about the neighborhood. This event is a chance for intergenerational exchange; young people are encouraged to come and record elders' stories (with permission). We will provide translation support primarily in English, but we will respect and leave intact short statements or proverbs that residents choose to keep in Hindi.

Volunteer Spotlight

This month we highlight Aisha Khan, a bilingual volunteer who has coordinated language access at events for three years. Aisha organizes interpreter schedules, prepares bilingual signs, and runs a crash-course for new volunteers on basic greeting phrases, respectful forms of address, and how to note when a speaker prefers Hindi. In Aisha's words: 'Small gestures like greeting someone in their preferred language often open doors to meaningful conversation.' Her outreach has measurably increased attendance at seniors' programs.

Language Corner: Practical Phrases and Guidelines

We encourage volunteers to use simple, high-frequency phrases rather than attempting literal translation of long sentences. A small glossary is included here for event signage and courtesy phrases; the Hindi entries should remain in Devanagari if used on printed materials to preserve recognition by native readers.

- Welcome (banner): स्वागत है
- Please do not make noise (door sign): कृपया शोर न करें
- Thank you (closing remarks): धन्यवाद

When translators or volunteers prepare materials, translate the surrounding English context into the target language but leave these Hindi items unchanged. If you include the Hindi phrases in printed materials, keep their order and punctuation exactly as shown so that older readers familiar with the script can easily find them.

Safety and Accessibility

We continue to remind attendees to watch for wet paint, uneven pavement, and to report any hazards to event staff. All public meeting spaces have wheelchair access; if you require additional accommodations, contact the community center at least 72 hours before the event. For those who will assist elders who speak primarily Hindi, consider pairing with a bilingual volunteer or using a short, clear written note that includes the Hindi phrases above.

Closing Notes

Thank you for staying connected and for supporting an inclusive neighborhood. If you have announcements for next month's issue, please submit them by the 20th. We welcome suggestions on how to broaden language support and to make events more accessible to everyone. In community partnership,

Neighborhood Association Board

धन्यवाद

title: Hydraulic Actuator Maintenance — Procedure Page
source_text: Hydraulic Actuator Maintenance — Procedure Page Overview This training manual page describes the routine maintenance steps for the Type-A hydraulic actuator used in assembly lines. Follow the safety instructions in Section 1 before starting any work. Refer to the diagrams (Figure 1 and Figure 2) for part locations and sequence flow. Image files are referenced for clarity and must be preserved exactly as filenames during documentation updates. Safety Warnings - Lockout/tagout the primary power source and bleed hydraulic pressure before disassembly. - Wear safety glasses and gloves rated for hydraulic fluid contact. - If a pressure relief is present, verify isolation with a pressure gauge. Tools and Consumables - Torque wrench, 5–50 Nm range - 10 mm and 13 mm hex sockets - Hydraulic fluid: HFD-46 or equivalent - Clean lint-free rags Parts and Torque Specifications The table below lists critical fasteners and nominal torque values. These headers and values should be translated carefully to preserve unit notation and tolerances. | Component ID | Component Description | Torque (Nm) | Tolerance (+/-) | |--------------|-----------------------------|-------------:|----------------:| | ENG-CTRL-3 | Mounting flange bolts (M8) | 25 | 2 | | PISTON-01 | Piston rod clamp screw (M6) | 12 | 1 | | VALVE-ASSY | Valve cover bolts (M5) | 8 | 0.5 | See Figure 1 for the location of the Mounting flange and Piston rod clamp. The Component ID column must remain intact if referenced by parts ordering systems. Procedure — Disassembly 1. Isolate the actuator from hydraulic supply and relieve internal pressure. 2. Remove the mounting cover using a 10 mm socket. Keep fasteners in a labeled tray. 3. Detach the piston rod clamp screw (PISTON-01) and gently extract the piston assembly. Figure Reference and Image Captions Figure 1 shows the actuator front view with labeled components. Caption text conveys positional context. ![Figure 1: Front view of Type-A actuator with labelled components](images/diagram_step1.png) Figure 2 illustrates the recommended maintenance flow and decision points in a standard HTML-rendered flowchart. Caption: Figure 2. Maintenance flowchart: Step A — Bleed system; Step B — Inspect seals; Step C — Replace worn parts. Ensure that the sequence is followed and that decisions are documented on the maintenance log. Procedure — Inspection and Replacement 4. Inspect seals for scoring, cuts, or hardening. Replace seals that fail visual or tactile inspection. 5. Check piston rod for straightness and surface pitting. Replacement is required if corrosion exceeds 0.1 mm depth or if scoring is continuous over 5 mm length. 6. Reassemble in reverse order using torque values in the table. Apply threadlocker to ENG-CTRL-3 only if specified by revision note. Troubleshooting Tips - Symptom: Excessive leakage at the valve cover. Likely cause: Damaged valve O-ring or improper seating. Action: Disassemble VALVE-ASSY and replace O-ring; verify seating surface is clean. - Symptom: Abnormal noise during actuation. Likely cause: Air in hydraulic lines. Action: Perform bleed procedure as in Figure 2, Step A. Documentation and Reporting Record all actions in the maintenance log. Use the exact Component ID from the table when ordering replacement parts. If a non-standard part is found, tag the unit and escalate to engineering with photographs attached. Preserve image filenames in reports to link back to original diagrams. Revision History Rev 2.1 — Added torque tolerance column and updated bleed sequence in Figure 2. End of page.
translation_notes: Translate all narrative text, table headers, and figure captions; preserve punctuation and unit symbols (e.g., Nm).
Do not translate filenames, Component ID codes, or other protected spans; keep table layout and Markdown/HTML image tags unchanged.
expected_challenges: Rendering of table cell alignment and preservation of numeric formatting (decimal points, tolerance signs).
