title: Bilingual Form Completion Guide (English with Hindi examples)
source_text:
Introduction
This guide explains how to complete forms that include both English labels and occasional Hindi examples or fields. Translators: translate only the English text of the form. Do not alter any Hindi examples, field labels, or quoted Hindi responses. The purpose of leaving Hindi spans unchanged is to preserve original names, legal phrases, or respondent-provided samples that must remain verbatim.

When this guide applies
Use this document when you encounter administrative forms, application instructions, or help pages that contain: bilingual prompts, sample entries in Devanagari, or short quoted Hindi utterances that represent user-supplied data. The layout below mirrors a typical online form help page and includes sample filled entries.

How to complete bilingual fields
1. Translate headings and instructions into the target language, but do not translate Hindi text embedded in examples.
2. If a field includes an explicit Hindi example, render the English surrounding it in the target language and leave the Hindi span exactly as shown.
3. If a sample value is provided in Hindi and is clearly marked as an example (for instance, as a suggested name or a quoted utterance), keep it unchanged.

Important Hindi examples (leave intact)
- Example for entering a name in Devanagari: "नाम: शर्मा, सीमा". Translators should not alter this example.
- To request a brief self-introduction in Hindi, the original text may appear as: "कृपया हिंदी में अपना संक्षिप्त परिचय लिखें:". Preserve this entire string wherever it appears.

Sample form sections (do not translate Hindi spans)
Personal Information
- Name (English): Enter your full name as it appears on official documents.
- Name (Devanagari example): "नाम: शर्मा, सीमा". This is provided as an example only.
- Date of birth: Use DD/MM/YYYY format. Translate date-related help text but leave any Hindi date examples unchanged.

Contact Details
- Email: Provide a current email address.
- Phone: Include country code, e.g., +91-9876543210.
- Address: Translate the field instruction; for city or locality examples in Hindi, keep the script identical to the source.

Statement and consent
- Consent checkbox label: Translate this into the target language.
- Example consent statement in Hindi (user-supplied sample): "कृपया हिंदी में अपना संक्षिप्त परिचय लिखें:" which prompts users to write a short introduction in Hindi. Do not modify the Hindi prompt.

Examples of acceptable translations
- Original English instruction: "Enter legal name in the Name field."
  Translated: "[Target language equivalent]"
- Original bilingual label: "Preferred name (if different) / नाम" — translate the English portion and retain the Hindi label if present.

Checklist before finalizing translation
- Have you translated every English sentence, heading, and label?
- Have you left Hindi spans like "नाम: शर्मा, सीमा" and "कृपया हिंदी में अपना संक्षिप्त परिचय लिखें:" unchanged?
- Have you preserved punctuation and line breaks around preserved spans so examples remain recognizable?

Accessibility and formatting notes
- Keep bullets, numbered steps, and example blocks intact. If an example block contains Hindi, do not rephrase it.
- For items that mix English and Hindi on the same line, translate only the English substrings and ensure spacing and punctuation around the Hindi substrings remain exactly as in the source.

Contact for clarification
If unsure whether a short Hindi example should be preserved, consult the project lead. When in doubt, prefer leaving short user-provided Hindi text unchanged rather than attempting to translate or transliterate it.

End of guide
This document is intended to reduce ambiguity when translating bilingual form instructions. Follow the rules above and double-check protected spans before submitting your translation.
protected_spans: कृपया हिंदी में अपना संक्षिप्त परिचय लिखें:
translation_notes: Translate only the English portions; leave the exact Hindi spans unchanged.
Preserve punctuation, spacing, and line breaks around protected Hindi spans.
expected_challenges: Identifying mixed lines where only part of the line should be translated (English + Hindi).
Maintaining exact Devanagari script and punctuation without accidental normalization or transliteration.

title: Bronze Ritual Vessel: Exhibit Note and Provenance
source_text:
Object label

Bronze Ritual Vessel (circa 8th century BCE)
Inventory: INV-1874-BV

Introduction
This exhibit note accompanies a late Bronze Age ritual vessel recently conserved and displayed in Gallery 3. The object is notable for its incised geometric decoration, tripod form, and evidence of repeated repair. Visitors are guided to interpret both function and social significance by reference to material, wear patterns, and associated finds from the same horizon.

Figure and image
![Bronze Ritual Vessel](images/bronze_vessel.jpg)

Figure 1. Bronze Ritual Vessel, side view. INV-1874-BV. Photograph by A. Smith, 2019. For publication use consult museum image policy.

Provenance and acquisition details
The following table summarizes the known provenance, dating, and acquisition history relevant to interpretation and legal status. Translators should preserve the column headers and inventory code formatting, as these elements carry administrative and legal weight in translation.

| Provenance stage | Description | Date or period | Source document |
|------------------|-------------|----------------|-----------------|
| Findspot | Probable ritual pit at Tell Qarim, Layer IV | 8th century BCE | Field report FR-2007-08 |
| Initial owner | Local collector, documented sale | 1893 | Sales ledger SL-1893 |
| Museum acquisition | Donated to National Museum under accession terms | 1912 | Accession record AC-1912-54 |
| Recent conservation | Stabilisation and X-ray analysis | 2018-2019 | Conservation file CF-2019-07 |

Conservation summary
Conservators identified multiple solder patches applied in antiquity and more recent fills used in the early 20th century. X-radiography revealed a repaired crack that extends from the rim to the shoulder, consistent with structural failure from repeated use. Surface sampling yielded trace residues consistent with organic offerings, which support an interpretation of ritual use rather than domestic cooking.

Interpretation and context
The tripod shape and elevated rim distinguish this type from purely culinary vessels and align it with votive or libation contexts in nearby contemporaneous settlements. Iconographic parallels include incised chevrons and concentric circles, which appear across several assemblages in this region and period. While a definitive ritual function cannot be proven by form alone, the combination of residue analysis and deposition context strengthens the hypothesis of ceremonial use.

Display recommendations
Label text should include: brief description, secure provenance summary, inventory number, and a note on recent conservation. When translating label text, maintain the inventory format exactly as presented here and do not translate the inventory code or file names. Figure captions and the table header labels should be rendered clearly and concisely in the target language to preserve administrative clarity and interpretive intent.

References and further reading
- Field report FR-2007-08, Excavation at Tell Qarim, stratigraphic report and objects catalogue
- Conservation file CF-2019-07, National Museum Conservation Department
- Comparative examples: see catalogue entries for nearby sites, particularly context layers dated to the 8th century BCE

Curator's note
This object remains a compelling case study for discussions of ritual practice and the lifecycle of material culture. The inventory number, photographic reference, and provenance table together form the administrative backbone supporting ethical display and research access.
translation_notes: Preserve inventory codes and file names verbatim (do not translate INV-1874-BV or images/bronze_vessel.jpg).
Translate table headers, figure captions, and label text, maintaining their function and brevity in the target language.
expected_challenges: Rendering administrative terms and inventory codes without altering format or meaning.
Translating technical conservation terminology and residue analysis results while preserving precision.

title: Riverside Community Newsletter — April Edition
source_text:
Riverside Community Newsletter — April Edition

From the Editor
Spring is a busy season for our neighborhood, and this month’s newsletter highlights several initiatives, volunteer opportunities, and a special neighborhood pledge. Please read through the sections below; translations should leave certain Hindi terms and the pledge verbatim as they appear.

Upcoming Events
- Saturday, April 10: Park Beautification Day. Join neighbors at 9:00 AM to restore garden beds, repaint benches, and plant native perennials. This activity is part of our ongoing "स्वच्छता अभियान" and includes a short safety briefing for new volunteers.
- Sunday, April 18: Community Potluck & Story Swap at Riverside Hall, 5:00–8:00 PM. Bring a dish and a short story about your family’s arrival in the neighborhood. We will provide name tags and a sign-up table at the door.
- Friday, April 24: Youth Mentoring Night, 6:30–8:30 PM. High-school volunteers will host study sessions and career talks for middle-school students.

Volunteer Spotlight
This edition we celebrate Aisha Khan, who coordinated last month’s donation drive to restock the free pantry shelves. Aisha’s approach combined logistics with outreach: she organized schedules, recruited volunteers via social media, and arranged delivery of healthy food items to families in need.

Neighborhood Pledge
On April 10, at the conclusion of Park Beautification Day, we will ask neighbors to read aloud a simple pledge to reaffirm our commitment to community care. The pledge will include the following lines, which are intentionally printed in Hindi to respect the multilingual character of our neighborhood and to encourage participation by native speakers: "हम अपने पड़ोस को स्वच्छ रखेंगे". Translators: do not alter or translate this pledge text.

Guidelines for Public Spaces
Please respect the following guidelines to keep shared spaces welcoming:
- Dispose of trash in the designated bins; excess recyclables should be flattened before placing them in the blue containers.
- Do not leave personal items unattended on benches or playground equipment for extended periods.
- If you are organizing a neighborhood event that expects more than 30 attendees, reserve the community tent at least two weeks in advance.

Special Note: Cultural Celebration
We will hold a small cultural celebration to honor the diversity of our block. The program will include a song performance, short plays, and a children’s art show. A few items from cultural groups will be displayed with bilingual labels. For groups wishing to use Hindi event titles, please ensure you register the title exactly as you want it printed—examples include the festival name "संकल्प दिवस" which several families have proposed for a future annual pledge and community service day.

Resources & Contacts
- Community Coordinator: Marco Diaz — marco@riverside.org
- Volunteer Sign-up: volunteers@riverside.org (please indicate availability and skills)
- Emergency Contacts: see the back page for a full list, including the nearest clinic and language assistance numbers.

Closing Notes
We rely on clear communication and mutual respect. If translating this newsletter into another language, please translate only the English content and leave the Hindi phrases and pledge exactly as printed. If you need clarification about how to handle bilingual headings or culturally specific terms, contact the Community Coordinator before publishing translated materials. Thank you to everyone who contributes time, skills, and creativity to make Riverside a vibrant place to live.
protected_spans: हम अपने पड़ोस को स्वच्छ रखेंगे
translation_notes: Translate only the English text; do not alter or translate strings in protected_spans.
Preserve punctuation and line breaks around protected Hindi spans and keep them verbatim in the translation.
expected_challenges: Ensuring Hindi event names and the pledge remain untranslated while fitting grammatically into the target language layout.
Maintaining the newsletter tone and formatting (bullet lists, dates, headings) when English and Hindi appear adjacent.

title: Hydraulic Pump: Routine Disassembly and Inspection
source_text: Hydraulic Pump: Routine Disassembly and Inspection Purpose This page provides step-by-step instructions for routine disassembly, inspection, and reassembly of the HP-42 hydraulic pump. Follow the torque values, safety warnings, and visual checks to ensure reliable operation after maintenance. Figures and tables are included for reference. Safety Precautions - Depressurize the system and verify zero pressure at the pump inlet and outlet before beginning work. - Wear eye protection, chemical-resistant gloves, and hearing protection when operating nearby equipment. - Note: residues of hydraulic fluid may be combustible or toxic. Clean spills immediately. Tools and Materials - 14 mm box wrench, torque wrench (0–150 Nm), pick set, soft mallet - Clean rags, lint-free towels, inspection magnifier (5×) - Replacement O-rings (see Table 1) and 15W-40 hydraulic oil Procedure 1. External Cleaning and Access Panel a. Remove the two M6 hex screws securing the access panel. Use a 4 mm Allen key and apply 4.5 Nm torque on reinstallation. b. Inspect the sealing gasket for tears. If the gasket shows cracking or elongation, replace before proceeding. 2. Rotor and Vanes Removal (Figure References) Refer to Figure 1 for the exploded view of the pump assembly and Figure 2 for the recommended vane extraction sequence. ![Exploded assembly diagram](images/diagram_step3.png) Figure 1: Exploded view of HP-42 pump. Note orientation of the drive key and timing marks. a. Remove the retaining circlip with pliers and slide out the shaft assembly. Keep components in order for reassembly. b. Remove vanes sequentially, alternating positions to prevent spring loss. Place vanes in a numbered tray. 3. Inspection Use the magnifier to inspect surfaces for pitting, scoring, or heat discoloration. Refer to Table 1 for allowable wear limits. Table 1: Fastener Torque Specifications | Fastener | Thread | Torque (Nm) | Notes | |----------|--------|-------------:|-------| | End Cover Bolt | M8 x 1.25 | 28 | Use medium-strength threadlocker | | Access Panel Screw | M6 x 1.0 | 4.5 | Hand-start, torque with wrench | | Drive Flange Bolt | M12 x 1.75 | 60 | Tighten in star pattern | 4. Surface Measurements and Acceptance Criteria - Rotor runout: max 0.12 mm at 50 mm from flange face. - Vane thickness: minimum 1.45 mm. Replace vane if below limit. - Cavity wear: observe for ovality; reject housing if more than 0.20 mm deviation. Inspection Photo Figure 2: Close-up inspection showing acceptable micro-surface finish; note discoloration band at the inner radius. Reassembly Notes - Reassemble in reverse order. Ensure each vane spring seats fully in its pocket. - Apply light film of hydraulic oil to O-rings; do not use lubricants with solvents. - Torque values in Table 1 must be followed precisely. Use the torque wrench to avoid over-torquing. Troubleshooting Tips - Symptom: intermittent pressure drop under load. Possible causes: worn vanes, air ingress past seals, or cavitation. Check vane wear and inlet screen for blockage. - Symptom: excessive noise at start-up. Possible causes: improper preload, loose drive flange bolts, or foreign debris in housing. Record Keeping Document pump serial number, date of service, parts replaced, and measured values for rotor runout and vane thickness in the maintenance log. Retain photos of any rejected components. References - HP-42 Service Manual, Section 3: Hydraulic Components - Safety Data Sheet: Hydraulic Oil 15W-40
translation_notes: Translate all figure captions and table headers; preserve image file paths and Figure numbers verbatim.
Keep measurement units (mm, Nm) and thread designations (e.g., M8 x 1.25) unchanged; translate surrounding unit words as appropriate.
expected_challenges: Technical abbreviations and thread specifications require consistent, domain-accurate rendering.
Maintaining numeric formats and unit symbols while adapting decimal separators for target locale.
Preserving imperative instructions and safety warnings without altering procedural meaning.
Translating table headers and keeping column alignment and meaning intact.

style_label: Documents with multilingual input and selective translation
title: Teacher's Note: Teaching Everyday Dialogues with Embedded Hindi Examples
source_text:
Overview
This note is intended for language teachers preparing classroom materials that mix English explanations with short Hindi utterances. The practice aims to help learners recognize natural code-switching and to focus on pragmatic functions (greeting, asking after health, expressing gratitude) without forcing students to transliterate. In this document, English is the primary narrative language; the Hindi phrases are presented as in-class examples that should remain unchanged when the material is adapted or translated into other target languages.

Learning objectives
- Identify and respond to basic social formulas in Hindi.
- Observe how intonation, politeness markers, and context affect meaning.
- Produce classroom prompts and feedback in the students' target language while retaining authentic Hindi examples.

Classroom dialogue examples
Below are short dialogues meant to be presented verbatim during lessons. Teachers should translate the surrounding English instructions for students but keep the Hindi lines as written.

Example 1: A simple greeting exchange to model intonation
Teacher: Introduce the scene in your target language: a student enters the room and greets the teacher.
Student A (live example): "नमस्ते"
Teacher: Model a warm response and invite a follow-up question.
Student B (live example): "क्या हाल है?"
Notes: The two Hindi lines above perform different conversational functions: the first is a greeting formula, and the second is an inquiry about well-being. Teach learners to notice the short pause and rising intonation on the question form.

Example 2: Expressing thanks and confirming understanding
Context: After a student receives help, encourage them to respond politely.
Student: "धन्यवाद"
Teacher: Prompt learners to say thank you in their target language and then reflect on how formality changes the response in Hindi. Discuss the register differences compared to English expressions such as 'thanks' vs. 'thank you'.

Grammar and pragmatic notes
- Fixed formulas: Many everyday Hindi utterances are fixed phrases. Treat them as single pragmatic units in classroom input rather than sentences to be parsed word-by-word. For example, "नमस्ते" functions as a unitary greeting with cultural weight.
- Ellipsis and abbreviated responses: Short responses like "क्या हाल है?" often omit subject pronouns when context makes them obvious. Highlight equivalent elliptical patterns in the learners' target language.
- Politeness and verb forms: When modeling longer replies in Hindi (not included here), be careful about using formal verb endings; the three protected phrases above do not include complex morphology but point to different politeness registers.

Exercises for learners
1) Listening discrimination: Play recordings of the three Hindi utterances in different intonations and have students mark which perform greeting, inquiry, or gratitude.
2) Role-play: Each group stages a short scene in the target language but must include the exact Hindi lines provided above at the indicated moments. Emphasize that the Hindi spans are kept unchanged.
3) Reflection writing: Ask learners to write, in the target language, a brief paragraph about how including short Hindi units affects comprehension and cultural authenticity.

Glossary (for teacher reference only; keep Hindi unchanged in student-facing materials)
- नमस्ते — traditional greeting used both formally and informally
- क्या हाल है? — colloquial inquiry meaning 'How are you?'
- धन्यवाद — expression of thanks, comparable to 'thank you'

Formatting and presentation recommendations
- Display Hindi phrases on their own lines and in quotation marks so students can clearly see the boundaries of the protected spans.
- When translating accompanying instructions, preserve the Hindi phrases' original script, punctuation, and line breaks.

Final remarks
These notes are designed to help teachers maintain linguistic authenticity while producing translated classroom resources. Keep the English explanatory text fluid and idiomatic when translating, but leave the Hindi utterances exactly as shown: their form and placement are pedagogically significant.
translation_notes: Translate only the English text; leave the Hindi spans exactly as they appear.
Do not transliterate or alter the Hindi script, punctuation, or line breaks around those spans.
expected_challenges: Maintaining clear boundaries between English instructions and embedded Hindi lines during translation.
Preserving pragmatic functions (greeting, inquiry, gratitude) when translating surrounding explanatory text without modifying the Hindi examples.

style_label: Documents with code and doc strings
title: Sessionization and Funnel Analysis Note
source_text:
Overview

This note explains a common approach to sessionization and funnel analysis using SQL window functions and CTEs. The goal is to produce readable, translatable explanatory prose alongside runnable SQL snippets and configuration examples. Translate all explanatory sentences, docstrings, and comments; preserve SQL identifiers and syntax exactly as shown.

Design considerations

- A session is defined by a sequence of events for the same user with gaps no larger than a specified threshold.
- We use LEAD/LAG and SUM(...) OVER windows to compute boundaries and assign session ids.
- For large event tables, consider partitioning and predicate pushdown; keep the analytic window as narrow as possible.

SQL example

Below is a step-by-step SQL example that computes sessions and a simple funnel conversion rate. Comments (lines starting with -- and block comments) should be translated, but do not change table or column names.

```sql
-- Compute sessions by user using a 30-minute inactivity threshold
WITH ordered_events AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    event_time,
    event_type,
    -- event properties JSON is stored in the payload column
    payload,
    LEAD(event_time) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY event_time) AS next_event_time
  FROM analytics.user_events
  WHERE event_time >= CURRENT_DATE - INTERVAL '30 days'
),
session_flags AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    event_time,
    event_type,
    payload,
    -- mark boundary when the gap to the next event exceeds threshold
    CASE
      WHEN next_event_time IS NULL THEN 1
      WHEN EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (next_event_time - event_time)) > 1800 THEN 1
      ELSE 0
    END AS is_session_end
  FROM ordered_events
),
sessions AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    event_time,
    event_type,
    payload,
    -- cumulative sum to assign a session number per user (session_id_offset)
    SUM(is_session_end) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY event_time ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS session_id_offset
  FROM session_flags
)
SELECT
  user_id,
  session_id_offset AS session_id,
  MIN(event_time) AS session_start,
  MAX(event_time) AS session_end,
  COUNT(*) AS events_in_session,
  -- preserve instrumentation tag as-is
  payload->>'INSTRUMENTATION_TAG' AS instrumentation_tag
FROM sessions
GROUP BY user_id, session_id_offset
ORDER BY user_id, session_start;
```

Python docstring example

"""
Builds a derived table of sessions for downstream funnel queries.
This function returns a SQL string; translate the description but do not change the embedded SQL.
Parameters:
  start_date (str): inclusive start date for the window in ISO-8601 format.
  end_date (str): inclusive end date for the window in ISO-8601 format.
"""

Shell and YAML snippets

# Run the query using psql. Translate the comment to match local conventions.
psql -h db.example.local -U analytics -d events_db -f session_query.sql

# Example CI job configuration that schedules the query once per day
pipeline_job:
  schedule: "0 3 * * *"  # daily at 03:00 UTC
  task: run-sessionization
  retries: 2
  timeout_minutes: 30

Performance tips and localization notes

- Time zones: event_time is stored in UTC. Convert to local time only in presentation layers.
- When translating, keep technical terms such as CTE, partition, and window function as-is if no equivalent exists in the target language, or provide a concise localized term.
- Preserve numeric literals, interval specifications (e.g., '30 minutes' or INTERVAL '30 days'), and ISO formats like ISO-8601.

Common pitfalls for translators

1. Do not translate code identifiers such as table names, column names, or JSON keys (for example, analytics.user_events or INSTRUMENTATION_TAG).
2. Keep SQL syntax and punctuation identical to avoid accidental changes that could make the sample invalid.
3. Ensure comments remain clear and consistent with the translated prose; maintain any examples of date/time formats exactly.

End of note.
translation_notes: Translate all explanatory prose, comments, and docstrings; do not alter code, identifiers, or syntax.
Keep date/time literals and format examples (e.g., ISO-8601, INTERVAL specifications) unchanged.
expected_challenges: Distinguishing between translatable comments/docstrings and non-translatable code identifiers.
Preserving SQL syntax, punctuation, and indentation while producing fluent translated prose.
Handling technical terms (CTE, partition, window function) that may have no direct equivalent in the target language.
Maintaining accuracy of date/time formats and interval expressions during translation.

title: Field Survey Summary: Riparian Vegetation and Soil Moisture (FS-90)
source_text: Field Survey Summary Site ID: FS-90 Date: 2025-03-12 Survey Team: A. Ramirez, T. Cole, J. Patel Introduction This document summarizes the field survey conducted at the riparian corridor adjacent to Meadow Creek (Site ID: FS-90). The objective was to quantify vegetation cover, dominant species composition, and soil moisture gradients across three transects. The survey combined visual estimates, point-intercept vegetation sampling, and soil probe measurements at 0–15 cm and 15–30 cm depths. Map reference and site photography are included in Figures 1 and 2. Location and Methods Latitude/Longitude reference: 45.1234 N, -121.2345 W (WGS84). Transects A, B, and C run perpendicular to the creek at 50-m intervals. At each 10 m along a transect we recorded: canopy cover (%), dominant species, groundcover percentage, and volumetric soil moisture (VSM, %). VSM was measured with a handheld probe (Model: MoistSense-200) and cross-checked with gravimetric sampling at selected points. Survey Map and Site Photo Figure 1 shows the survey layout, creek position, and transect locations. Figure 2 is a representative site photo taken at Transect B, 10 m from the bank. Figure 1: Survey map for FS-90 showing transects A–C, sampling points, and scale bar. (Map projection: WGS84 UTM Zone 10N) ![Site photo: Transect B - images/survey_site_FS-90.jpg](images/survey_site_FS-90.jpg) Figure 2: Site photo, Transect B at 10 m from creek bank. Photo filename: images/survey_site_FS-90.jpg Results Table 1 summarizes averaged measurements by transect. Note that canopy cover is the averaged estimate across all 6 sampling points per transect. Dominant species are listed by common name followed by scientific name in parentheses when available. | Transect | Canopy Cover (%) | Dominant Species | Groundcover (%) | VSM 0–15 cm (%) | VSM 15–30 cm (%) | |----------|------------------:|------------------|----------------:|----------------:|------------------:| | A | 62 | Black Cottonwood (Populus trichocarpa) | 78 | 24.5 | 19.1 | | B | 48 | Red-osier Dogwood (Cornus sericea) | 66 | 28.2 | 22.7 | | C | 35 | Reed Canarygrass (Phalaris arundinacea) | 55 | 31.0 | 27.4 | Table 1: Averaged transect metrics for FS-90. VSM units are volumetric water content by percentage. Observations and Notes - Transect A (closest to upstream wooded section) exhibited the highest canopy cover and dense sapling recruitment under the cottonwoods. - Transect B showed mixed shrub dominance with patches of invasive reed canarygrass encroaching at the 30–50 m range from bank. - Transect C, downstream of a cattle access point, had lower canopy cover and the highest soil moisture values, likely due to localized compaction and reduced surface drainage. - Several point observations indicated small scour features on the bank near Transect C; GPS waypoint labels for these points are: WP_FS90_01, WP_FS90_02. Discussion The contrasting VSM profiles between transects suggest hydrological variability over short spatial scales, which is correlated with land use impacts (e.g., cattle access) and vegetation type. Reed canarygrass dominance at C aligns with higher VSM and lower canopy shading, indicating a feedback that may inhibit tree recruitment. Canopy reduction from historical clearing is likely a contributing factor. Recommendations 1. Implement a targeted riparian exclusion fence at the cattle access point (near Transect C) and monitor VSM quarterly for 12 months. 2. Prioritize planting of native willow and cottonwood seedlings in zones identified by lower VSM and canopy gaps (Transects B and C), using native stock sourced from local nurseries. 3. Establish photo points at the existing sample locations and retain file naming conventions: images/survey_site_FS-90.jpg and images/survey_map_FS-90.png for longitudinal comparison. Appendix: Raw point data and GPS tracklines are stored in the project folder under: - /data/field/FS-90/raw_points.csv - /data/field/FS-90/gps_track.gpx End of report.
translation_notes: Do not translate file paths, GPS codes, or site IDs (e.g., FS-90, WP_FS90_01).
Preserve table header order, numeric formats, and units exactly (e.g., % and VSM 0–15 cm).
expected_challenges: Rendering and translating HTML image tag alt text and Markdown image captions without altering file paths.

title: Kinetics and Morphology of Biofilm Formation on Titanium Alloys
source_text: Abstract This report summarizes laboratory observations of Pseudomonas aeruginosa biofilm formation on two titanium alloy surfaces, Ti-6Al-4V and Ti-13Nb-13Zr, under controlled conditions. Emphasis is placed on comparative growth kinetics, measured enzyme activity as a proxy for metabolic state, and SEM imaging of surface morphology. Key variables include nutrient concentration, pH, and shear stress in the flow cell. Introduction Biofilms on implantable medical devices are a major clinical concern. Differences in alloy composition can influence initial adhesion and maturation. We performed parallel experiments to quantify kinetic parameters and to document morphological differences with scanning electron microscopy (SEM). All experiments were performed at pH 7.4 and 37 °C to mimic physiological conditions. Methods Materials: Discs (12 mm diameter) of Ti-6Al-4V and Ti-13Nb-13Zr were polished to a 0.05 µm finish and sterilized by autoclaving. Media: LB medium diluted to 0.5× for slow-growth conditions. Inoculum: P. aeruginosa strain PAO1 grown overnight and diluted to OD600 = 0.05. Flow cell protocol: Discs were mounted in parallel flow cells with a constant flow rate producing a shear stress of 0.1 Pa. Samples were taken at 6, 12, 24, and 48 h. Enzymatic proxy: Cellular esterase activity was measured using fluorescein diacetate hydrolysis and reported as relative fluorescence units (RFU). Table 1 reports kinetic parameters derived from Michaelis–Menten fits to substrate uptake assays and an empirically determined inhibition at a fixed competitor concentration. Table 1: Kinetic parameters and inhibition metrics for surface-associated cells | Surface | Km (µM) | Vmax (nmol·min⁻¹·mg⁻¹) | Inhibition (% at 100 µM inhibitor) | |---|---:|---:|---:| | Ti-6Al-4V | 35 ± 4 | 12.5 ± 0.8 | 22 ± 3 | | Ti-13Nb-13Zr | 48 ± 6 | 9.1 ± 0.6 | 41 ± 5 | Results Kinetic fits indicate a lower apparent affinity (higher Km) and reduced Vmax on Ti-13Nb-13Zr compared to Ti-6Al-4V, suggesting either reduced cell density or altered metabolic state. The inhibition assay shows a substantially higher percent inhibition on Ti-13Nb-13Zr, consistent with increased susceptibility to the competitor molecule used. Morphology: SEM imaging revealed distinct architectures. Ti-6Al-4V surfaces developed thicker, more homogeneous extracellular polymeric substance (EPS) layers by 48 h, whereas Ti-13Nb-13Zr exhibited patchy colonization with pronounced cell aggregation. Figure 1 illustrates representative SEM images taken at 10,000× magnification. Image filenames are provided for reference and should be preserved in translation. ![Figure 1](images/figure1_placeholder.png) Figure 1: SEM images of biofilms at 48 h. Left: Ti-6Al-4V (dense EPS mat). Right: Ti-13Nb-13Zr (aggregated clusters). Scale bar = 2 µm. Figure 2 shows a schematic of the flow cell used; the HTML tag below is included for layout fidelity in the original manuscript and must be retained. Figure 2: Flow cell schematic. Flow enters at the inlet valve, passes over the mounted disc, and exits through the waste port; arrows indicate flow direction and measured shear plane. Discussion The combination of kinetic and morphological data suggests that alloy composition modulates both adhesion and metabolic performance. Translators should pay particular attention to table headers and figure captions, which carry quantitative conclusions: "Km", "Vmax", and "Inhibition" entail unit and symbol fidelity. The difference in Km values (35 µM vs. 48 µM) underlines a biologically meaningful shift in substrate handling. Conclusions Ti-6Al-4V supports higher apparent metabolic throughput and more uniform biofilm coverage under the tested conditions, whereas Ti-13Nb-13Zr shows reduced Vmax and increased inhibition, with heterogeneous morphology. These observations inform material selection for implants where biofilm resistance is a concern.
translation_notes: Translate all figure captions and table headers; preserve image paths and HTML/Markdown image tags exactly as in source_text.
Maintain units and scientific notation (e.g., µM, nmol·min⁻¹·mg⁻¹, RFU) and Greek letters; do not localize numeric formats unless instructed.
expected_challenges: Preserving table alignment and special characters (µ, °, ±, superscripts, and subscripted units) in the target language.
Translating technical terms (e.g., extracellular polymeric substance, Michaelis–Menten) while keeping concise captions and unit semantics intact.

title: Unit 3 Handout: Writing, Peer Review, and Assessment
source_text:
Overview:
This handout supports a two-week unit on academic writing and peer review for intermediate-level students. The primary focus is developing clear argumentative essays, practicing constructive feedback, and understanding assessment criteria. In-class activities combine individual drafting, small-group workshops, and reflective revision. Instructors should adapt timing to class size and learner needs.

Learning objectives:
- Produce a 700–900-word argumentative essay that states a clear thesis, uses relevant evidence, and incorporates academic citation conventions.
- Demonstrate peer-review techniques: summarizing a partner's argument, identifying strengths and areas for improvement, and giving actionable revision suggestions.
- Complete a self-assessment using the provided rubric and reflect on revision choices.
- Practice bilingual classroom instruction by introducing an example task labeled 'निबंध लिखना' so students can connect vocabulary in both English and Hindi when appropriate.

Schedule (suggested):
Week 1
- Day 1: Topic selection, thesis development workshop, and mini-lesson on paragraph cohesion.
- Day 2: Research strategies and citation basics; formative quiz on source evaluation.
- Day 3: Drafting session with targeted prompts; instructor conferences for 10–15 minutes per student.

Week 2
- Day 1: Peer-review clinics in triads; focus on thesis clarity and evidence use.
- Day 2: Revision lab with guided exercises and instructor feedback.
- Day 3: Final submission and reflective self-assessment.

In-class activities (detailed):
1. Thesis bingo: Students circulate and find peers with different types of theses (causal, evaluative, argumentative). This encourages familiarity with thesis forms and concise explanation.
2. Feedback sandwich practice: Model giving feedback with positive comment, constructive critique, and suggested revision. Emphasize specificity: say what and where, not only general impressions.
3. Comparative reading: Read two short sample introductions and annotate differences in scope, evidence, and tone.

Assessment and rubrics:
All assessment should be transparent and aligned to learning objectives. Use the following components:
- Argument and organization (40%): clarity of thesis, logical flow, paragraph structure.
- Evidence and analysis (30%): relevance of sources, interpretation, integration of evidence.
- Style and mechanics (15%): sentence variety, grammar, and citation formatting.
- Reflection and revision (15%): quality of revision based on peer and instructor feedback, documented in a revision log.

Include the phrase 'मूल्यांकन मापदंड' in your printed rubric header where you present the categories and weightings, to support bilingual comprehension among students who read Devanagari script. This label identifies the rubric section for Hindi-reading students without changing the scoring language.

Peer-review script (example prompts):
- Summary: In two sentences, what is the author's claim and the main reasons offered?
- Strength: Point out one specific passage that does persuasive work, and explain why.
- Question: Identify one place where more evidence or clarification is needed.
- Suggestion: Offer one revision the author can try in the next draft.

Glossary (select terms):
- thesis statement: A concise sentence expressing the main claim of the essay.
- revision log: A chronological record of changes made between drafts and reasons for those changes.
- निबंध लिखना

Resources and further reading:
Provide links to a university writing center, a short bibliography on argumentation strategies, and exemplar essays at varying grade levels. Recommend an instructor checklist for completing conferences and a printable one-page rubric for students.

Instructor notes:
Allow flexibility for multilingual classes. When Hindi spans are used, maintain their original script in all handouts and digital materials. Encourage students to annotate in the language they find most productive for planning and revision.
translation_notes: Translate only the English portions of the document into the target language; do not alter the listed Hindi spans.
Preserve headings, bullet structure, and rubrics formatting; keep Hindi spans exactly as they appear, including script and spacing.
expected_challenges: Preserving Devanagari script spans verbatim while translating surrounding sentences into a different language.
Maintaining clarity of rubric percentages and alignment language when sentence structure changes in translation.
Ensuring that bilingual labels (short Hindi spans) remain readable and correctly positioned in translated layout.

style_label: Documents with code and doc strings
title: Shell runbook: deploy and troubleshoot local Docker Compose service
source_text:
Overview

This runbook explains how to start a local stateless service using Docker Compose, how to inspect logs, and how to recover from common failures. The prose and inline comments are intended for translation; keep all code, file names, and command tokens unchanged.

Prerequisites

- Docker and docker-compose (v1 or v2 CLI) installed.
- A user account with permission to run Docker commands.
- A copy of the repository with docker-compose.yml in the project root.

Quick start

1. Bring the stack up in the foreground so you can watch the startup sequence:

```bash
# Start services and stream logs to the terminal
docker-compose up
```

2. To run in detached mode (background), use:

```bash
# Run containers in background
docker-compose up -d
```

3. If a service fails to obtain necessary secrets, check environment variables in your shell and the .env file at project root.

Configuration example (docker-compose.yml)

The following YAML defines a simple web service that exposes port 8080 and mounts a config file. Translate comments but do not change keys, values, or indentation.

```yaml
version: "3.8"  # Compose file version
services:
  web:
    image: myorg/web:latest  # image to deploy
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"  # host:container mapping
    environment:
      - ENV_VAR_API_KEY  # API key provided from environment or .env
    volumes:
      - ./config:/etc/myapp/config:ro  # bind-mount local config directory as read-only
```

Bash helper script

Include a small script to restart a single service and wait for it to become healthy. The docstring and comments are translatable instructions for operators.

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
"""
Restart a Docker Compose service and wait until it reports status "healthy".
Usage: ./restart-service.sh <service_name>
"""

# Fail fast on errors
set -euo pipefail

SERVICE=${1:-web}
TIMEOUT_SECONDS=60

# Stop and start the target service
docker-compose stop "$SERVICE"
# Ensure the container is removed so a fresh one is created
docker-compose rm -f "$SERVICE"
# Start the service in detached mode
docker-compose up -d "$SERVICE"

# Wait loop: poll container health until success or timeout
SECONDS_PASSED=0
while [ $SECONDS_PASSED -lt $TIMEOUT_SECONDS ]; do
  # Query health status from docker inspect
  HEALTH=$(docker inspect --format='{{.State.Health.Status}}' "${SERVICE}_1" 2>/dev/null || echo "unknown")
  if [ "$HEALTH" = "healthy" ]; then
    echo "Service $SERVICE is healthy"
    exit 0
  fi
  sleep 2
  SECONDS_PASSED=$((SECONDS_PASSED + 2))
done

echo "Timed out waiting for service $SERVICE to become healthy" >&2
exit 1
```

Troubleshooting

- If logs show `connection refused`, verify that dependent services (datastore, cache) are running and reachable. Translate the explanation but keep error literals and status codes as-is.
- If the container crashes with exit code 137, it likely ran out of memory. Consider increasing container memory limits or host resources.
- For misconfigured mounts, validate that the local path exists: `/etc/myapp/config` inside the container must be present and readable.

Helpful commands

# Show last 200 lines of logs for a specific service
`docker-compose logs --tail=200 web`

# Bring down the stack and remove anonymous volumes
`docker-compose down --volumes`

Notes for translators

- Translate all prose, comments, and docstrings into the target language.
- Do not translate code tokens, file paths, image names, environment variable names, or literal command flags (for example: `-d`, `--volumes`).
- Preserve YAML and shell indentation and quoting exactly as shown.
translation_notes: Translate prose, comments, and docstrings; keep code, flags, file paths, image names, and variable names unchanged.
Preserve indentation, quoting, and fenced code blocks exactly; do not alter YAML keys or shell syntax.
expected_challenges: Distinguishing between translatable comments/docstrings and non-translatable code tokens or paths
Handling embedded inline code and command snippets within prose without altering syntax
Translating short error literals or status codes contextually while preserving exact tokens when necessary
Maintaining YAML indentation and shell quoting while translating adjacent descriptive text

style_label: Documents with code and doc strings
title: YAML-based Service Configuration with Examples
source_text:
Overview

This document explains a simple pattern for configuring services using YAML and a small Python loader. The prose and comments are intended to be translated, but the code, configuration keys, and filenames must remain unchanged. Read each section to understand how keys like "DATABASE_URL" and service identifiers are referenced by the loader.

Minimal service YAML

Below is a minimal service configuration in YAML that demonstrates required keys and optional timeouts. Translate the explanatory text, but preserve the YAML syntax and keys exactly as shown.

```yaml
# service name used by orchestration tools
app.service:
  image: "example/myapp:latest"  # container image identifier
  env:
    DATABASE_URL: "postgres://user:pass@db:5432/mydb"  # connection string
  resources:
    cpu: 0.5
    memory: 256MB
  service_timeout: 30  # seconds before the platform restarts the service
```

Explanation

The top-level key app.service identifies the unit of deployment. The loader expects a mapping with keys image, env, and resources. The DATABASE_URL value is a placeholder in many environments — translators should not localize placeholder names or connection strings. The numeric value for service_timeout is an integer measured in seconds.

Python loader example

This Python example shows a loader function that reads the YAML and applies basic validation. Translate the docstring and inline comments, but do not change function or variable names.

```python
'''Load and validate service configuration.

This function reads a YAML string and checks for required fields.
It raises ValueError if mandatory keys are missing or if types are incorrect.
'''

import yaml

REQUIRED_KEYS = ['image', 'env', 'resources']

def load_service_config(yaml_text):
    # Parse the YAML into a Python dictionary
    cfg = yaml.safe_load(yaml_text)

    # Ensure the top-level key exists
    if 'app.service' not in cfg:
        raise ValueError('Missing top-level "app.service" key')

    svc = cfg['app.service']

    # Validate presence of required keys
    for key in REQUIRED_KEYS:
        if key not in svc:
            raise ValueError(f"Missing required key: {key}")

    # Coerce and validate the timeout
    timeout = svc.get('service_timeout', 60)
    if not isinstance(timeout, int):
        raise ValueError('service_timeout must be an integer')

    return svc
```

Shell snippet: validate and deploy

Translate the descriptive comments below. Commands and filenames must remain unchanged.

```sh
# Validate the YAML file against basic rules
python -c "import sys, yaml; yaml.safe_load(open('docker-compose.yml'))"

# Deploy using a fictional CLI that reads the config
deployctl apply --file docker-compose.yml
```

Best practices and translation notes

- Keep configuration keys (for example, DATABASE_URL, app.service, service_timeout) unmodified in translation.
- Translate user-facing messages, comments, and docstrings to maintain clarity for local developers.
- Preserve code blocks, indentation, and inline code formatting exactly as presented.

Migration tips

When translating configuration documentation, avoid changing punctuation that affects parsing (colons, parentheses, quotes). If a translation requires additional explanation, add it as a separate paragraph or comment, not by altering the original key names or literal values.

End of document

This completes the sample tutorial. Translators should focus on conveying intent and usage while protecting identifiers and file names that the loader expects verbatim.
translation_notes: Translate all prose, comments (lines starting with #), and docstrings ('''...''').
Do NOT translate code, YAML keys, identifiers, filenames, or placeholder values like connection strings.
expected_challenges: Distinguishing between prose to translate and code or keys that must remain unchanged.
Maintaining YAML and code indentation and punctuation during translation.
Handling technical terms and acronyms while preserving identifiers like DATABASE_URL.
Translating inline comments without altering quoted strings or identifiers.

title: Main Hall Audio Guide: Threads of Resistance
source_text:
Welcome to the Main Hall. This audio guide will direct you through a focused walk highlighting three clusters of objects: the Independence Textiles, the Folk Narratives, and the Modern Memory installation. Speak softly and allow other visitors to enjoy the space. If you are listening with a group, pause at the end of each section to allow for questions.

Section 1 — Independence Textiles (Gallery A)
You are now facing a large textile banner hung on the south wall. The banner was hand-embroidered in 1947 and served as a rally standard during city demonstrations. Please note the central stitched phrase: सत्य और अहिंसा. This phrase appears in block script on the original cloth and was intentionally left in its vernacular to maintain political and cultural specificity.

Listen carefully to the ambient recording played next to the banner. It reproduces crowd calls and the cadence of songs from the period. Take thirty seconds to absorb the sound and then proceed clockwise to the case with the travel-worn shawl.

The shawl to your left was mended repeatedly; conservation staff have kept visible repairs because they tell a story of repeated use and care. Look for the patched corner where different fiber types meet — linen patched with cotton — and note the handwritten label: ownership was passed through three generations.

Section 2 — Folk Narratives (Gallery B)
In this gallery we display painted scrolls, oral recordings, and a painted frame that contains a short verse reproduced exactly as the artist wrote it. The verse is printed on the inner frame and begins with the line 'तुम ही चले आओ'. Because the line is part of the artist's title for the piece, we preserve the original script as found on the object.

When you approach the listening station for the scrolls, you will be offered a choice of two audio tracks: a professional narration in English and a field recording of the poet reciting in the original dialect. For translation purposes, the museum has chosen to present both side-by-side: an English interpretation, and the preserved sound in the original language. If you choose to listen to both, note the difference in rhythm and emphasis.

Section 3 — Modern Memory Installation (Gallery C)
This installation reconstructs a small street corner from the mid-20th century with replica signboards, reproduced posters, and a projection of archival footage. Walk around the corner slowly; the curator recommends spending at least two minutes observing the projections to appreciate the layering of image and text.

A plaque adjacent to the projection contains a short curator note in English explaining the sourcing and ethical considerations. The note points out which fragments are originals and which are reconstructions, and explains why certain fragments retain their original script. Wherever a non-English script appears on an object, the museum chooses to present the original element unchanged alongside a descriptive panel in the visitor's language.

Accessibility and visitor interaction
If you need a transcript or a large-print version of any audio track, ask at the information desk; the museum staff will provide printed translations of the English narration. Audio descriptions for visitors with visual impairments are available for each gallery and include verbal descriptions of layout, color, and material condition.

Conservation note
Please refrain from touching objects. Many textiles and pigments are sensitive to oils from the skin. For some works we allow supervised handling of facsimiles; these are indicated by a small sign reading "Handling Replica". If you are working on a translation for classroom use, photograph the object label and consult the curatorial bibliography for primary source citations.

Glossary (items left in original script on objects will remain unchanged)
- सत्य और अहिंसा: phrase embroidered on the Independence banner.
- 'तुम ही चले आओ': opening line reproduced on the painted scroll's frame.

Thank you for visiting. Continue to the gift shop at the east exit if you wish to view reproductions and published catalogs. For further research inquiries, speak with the curatorial assistant at the reception desk; they can provide references to archival collections and oral history recordings used in this exhibition.
translation_notes: Translate only the English text; leave the Hindi spans in Devanagari exactly as they appear.
Keep exhibit titles, directions, and audio instructions natural in the target language while preserving object inscriptions verbatim.
expected_challenges: Deciding how to render museum-specific voice and imperative instructions fluidly in the target language.
Preserving the typographic and script distinction between translated labels and original Hindi inscriptions.
Maintaining the tone differences between factual curator notes and visitor-facing audio narration.
Handling single-quoted lines (e.g., 'तुम ही चले आओ') so quotes are preserved in the translated layout.

title: Portable Projector Product Page - Model X7-Pro
source_text: Portable Projection System — Model X7-Pro Overview The Model X7-Pro is a lightweight portable projector designed for on-the-go presentations, home cinema, and creative installations. With an integrated battery and wireless streaming, the X7-Pro balances brightness and portability. See the front profile in Figure 1 and the rear I/O panel in Figure 2 for physical layout and connector locations. ![Front view](images/product_X7-Pro_front.jpg) Figure 1. Front profile of Model X7-Pro showing lens alignment and speaker grille. Features - Compact chassis with all-aluminum housing for heat dissipation and durability. - Onboard 6,500 mAh battery for up to 3.5 hours continuous playback at 50% brightness. - Dual 5W stereo speakers with passive radiator for improved bass response. - Native 1080p projection with adaptive keystone correction. - Built-in Wi-Stream™ wireless technology supporting Miracast and AirPlay. Figure 2. Rear I/O: USB-C power, HDMI 2.1, USB-A for media, 3.5 mm audio out, and microSD slot. Technical Specifications The table below summarizes the key measurements and pricing for the current product line. Headers must be preserved during translation as they determine specification mapping. | Model | Dimensions (mm) | Weight (g) | Battery (mAh) | Brightness (ANSI lumens) | Price (USD) | |-------|-----------------|------------:|--------------:|--------------------------:|------------:| | Model X5 | 120 x 85 x 60 | 420 | 4,000 | 200 | 249.00 | | Model X7-Pro | 140 x 95 x 68 | 580 | 6,500 | 450 | 499.00 | | Model X9 | 160 x 110 x 80 | 920 | 10,000 | 900 | 899.00 | Note: Brightness is measured in ANSI lumens. Battery runtime varies by content and brightness settings. Comparison and Use Cases The X7-Pro occupies the middle of the lineup, offering a clear step up from the X5 in brightness and battery capacity while remaining significantly lighter than the full-size X9. Recommended use cases include: - Field sales demonstrations where portability and reliable battery life are essential. - Small conference rooms and classrooms for up to 80-inch screens. - Travel filmmakers and photographers needing a compact client review solution. Ordering and Accessories Accessory options include a compact tripod, protective carrying case, and a USB-C power bank optimized for pass-through charging. | SKU | Item | Included | Warranty | |-----|------|----------|---------:| | 982-TR | Compact tripod | No | 1 year | | SKU: 983-XL | Protective case (X7-Pro) | Yes | 2 years | | 984-PB | USB-C power bank 20,000 mAh | No | 1 year | Warranty and Support All models come with a standard 12-month limited warranty that covers manufacturing defects. The protective case for the X7-Pro is registered under SKU: 983-XL and carries an extended 24-month warranty when purchased with the projector. For repair and service, contact our support center; retain your sales receipt and model label. Installation Notes - Place the projector on a stable surface and allow 10 cm clearance around vents. - For best image uniformity, align the projector perpendicular to the screen plane and enable auto keystone in settings. - When using Wi-Stream™, verify both source and projector are on the same local network and follow on-screen pairing instructions. Figure References Refer to Figure 1 for front-facing controls and lens alignment. Refer to Figure 2 for connector types and layout when planning cable runs for a fixed installation. Legal and Safety Keep vents clear of obstructions and do not expose the lens to direct sunlight for extended periods. The product complies with applicable safety standards; battery disposal should follow local regulations.

title: Bilingual Form Assistance: How to Complete the Application with Hindi Examples
source_text:
Overview
This guide explains how to complete the online application form for a municipal grant program. The primary text is in English, but a few Hindi examples and phrases are embedded and must remain unchanged when the rest of the document is translated. Those Hindi spans are used as authoritative examples: a document name and an applicant declaration.

Before you begin
Read all sections in English. If you encounter a Hindi example, treat it as a fixed string that represents either an official document title or a legally significant declaration. Do not translate or alter those Hindi spans.

Section A: Personal details
1. Full name: Enter your legal name as it appears on your identity documents.
2. Date of birth: Use the format YYYY-MM-DD.
3. Address: Provide your current postal address. If your address contains local administrative names written in Devanagari script, keep those script forms exactly as provided by the applicant.

Section B: Required documents (upload)
When uploading supporting files, label each file clearly. Below are examples of required document names; the Hindi entry is an official document title used by some applicants and should remain unchanged:
- Proof of identity: passport, national ID, or "आधार कार्ड"
- Proof of residence: utility bill or rent agreement
- Income statement: latest salaried payslip or self-employment certificate

Section C: Project description
Briefly describe the proposed project in plain English. Include the objective, timeline, and expected beneficiaries. Keep each description to 150 words or fewer.

Section D: Sample bilingual entries (for reference)
The following examples show how applicants can include Hindi phrases without changing the rest of their submission. Translators should leave the Hindi substrings exactly as shown.
Example 1: Employer name: "S. Kumar Engineering"; Identification provided: "आधार कार्ड".
Example 2: Local landmark in address: Near "मुख्य चौक"; if an applicant writes that landmark in Devanagari, do not convert it to any other script.

Section E: Declaration and signature
Applicants must provide a signed declaration. Some applicants choose to use their local language for this declaration. An example Hindi declaration that is commonly used appears below and should be treated as a protected, unchanged text if present:
"मैं सत्यापित करता हूँ कि ऊपर दी गई जानकारी सटीक है।"
After the declaration, the applicant must provide the date and an image of their signature in JPEG or PNG format.

Section F: Contact information
Provide daytime phone number and an email address. For country codes, use + followed by digits (for example +91 for India). If applicants include transliterated names or local legal terms in Hindi script in the contact fields, maintain those forms verbatim.

Tips for translators
- Translate all English interface text, instructions, and field labels into the target language.
- Do not translate or modify any Hindi spans in Devanagari script; they are considered authoritative text elements.
- Preserve date formats and numeric conventions unless the receiving system requires localization.

Troubleshooting
If an applicant reports that a Hindi document name was altered during translation, revert the translated version to the exact original Hindi substring. Ensure that quotes, punctuation, and whitespace around the Hindi spans remain as in the source.

Appendix: Quick checklist
- Translated: All English instructions and labels
- Unchanged: "आधार कार्ड"
- Unchanged: "मैं सत्यापित करता हूँ कि ऊपर दी गई जानकारी सटीक है।"
- Confirm file names match uploads and that any Devanagari script remains intact
protected_spans: मैं सत्यापित करता हूँ कि ऊपर दी गई जानकारी सटीक है।

Maintain original punctuation, quotes, and whitespace surrounding protected Hindi substrings.

Preserving exact Devanagari script substrings, including punctuation and quotation marks, while translating surrounding text.

title: database connection utilities with retry logic
source_text:
'''Module: db_utils.py

This module provides a small set of utilities for establishing and maintaining
connections to a remote database service. The code samples below include
connection helpers, simple retry/backoff logic, and a reusable context manager.
Translators: translate the explanatory prose and all docstrings and comments.
Do not translate code identifiers, function names, or literal configuration
keys within fenced code blocks (they are protected tokens used by the runtime).
'''

from contextlib import contextmanager
import time
import socket

DEFAULT_TIMEOUT = 5  # default connection timeout in seconds

@contextmanager
def connection_context(conn):
    """Context manager that ensures the connection is closed on exit.

    Parameters:
      conn: an object with a close() method representing a live connection.

    Usage:
      with connection_context(conn):
          # perform queries
          pass

    Translate this docstring and any inline comments.
    """
    try:
        yield conn
    finally:
        # Ensure we always attempt to close the connection. This may raise
        # an exception if the underlying socket is already closed; callers
        # should be prepared to handle such errors.
        conn.close()


def connect_to_database(host, port, timeout=DEFAULT_TIMEOUT, retries=3, backoff=1.5):
    """Attempt to establish a TCP connection to the given host and port.

    Parameters:
      host (str): hostname or IP address of the database server.
      port (int): TCP port number.
      timeout (float): connection timeout in seconds.
      retries (int): number of retry attempts before giving up.
      backoff (float): multiplicative backoff factor between retries.

    Returns:
      socket.socket: a connected socket object on success.

    Raises:
      socket.error: if a connection could not be established after retries.

    Notes:
      - This function performs a blocking connect and will sleep between
        attempts following an exponential backoff schedule.
      - Translate this description, parameter notes, and exception notes.
    """
    attempt = 0
    current_timeout = timeout
    last_exc = None

    while attempt <= retries:
        try:
            # Create a new socket per attempt. We use AF_INET and SOCK_STREAM
            # for a standard TCP connection.
            s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
            s.settimeout(current_timeout)
            s.connect((host, port))
            # Successful connection
            return s
        except socket.error as exc:
            # Record the exception and prepare to retry.
            last_exc = exc
            attempt += 1
            if attempt > retries:
                # Exhausted retries: raise the last encountered exception.
                raise
            # Sleep with an increasing delay before next attempt.
            time.sleep(backoff ** attempt)
            # Optionally adjust timeout for subsequent attempts.
            current_timeout = min(timeout * (1 + attempt * 0.5), 30)


# Example usage snippet. Translators should translate comments and
# any descriptive lines, but not alter code tokens or placeholders.
#
# Example: simple script invocation
#
# ```sh
# python -c "from db_utils import connect_to_database; conn = connect_to_database('db.example.local', 5432)"
# ```

# Configuration example (YAML). Keys like 'database_url' should be left
# untranslated in the configuration example; translate only surrounding
# explanatory prose.
#
# ```yaml
# database:
#   database_url: "postgresql://user:password@db.example.local:5432/mydb"
#   connect_timeout: 5
#   max_retries: 3
# ```

# Small integration example that demonstrates the context manager.
# This inline code is illustrative and does not require execution.

def example_integration():
    """Illustrative example showing how to call the helper and use the
    connection_context to ensure cleanup.

    The translator should render this explanation in the target language,
    but keep the code block verbatim when presented in translated output.
    """
    host = 'db.example.local'
    port = 5432
    # TODO: handle retries with backoff and add logging
    conn = connect_to_database(host, port)
    with connection_context(conn):
        # perform a lightweight health check; real code would send a
        # protocol-specific ping or simple query here.
        pass

title: Gallery Note: Ritual Objects from the Eastern Delta
source_text: Exhibit Overview This small gallery presents ritual and votive objects excavated from sites in the Eastern Nile Delta, dating from the Late Bronze Age to the Early Iron Age. The labels combine archaeological description with interpretive comments intended for a general audience and for academic visitors. Translators should note that cataloging codes and measurements are authoritative; these should be preserved verbatim in translations where indicated. Key Objects (See Table 1) | Artifact | Date | Material | Provenance | Accession No. | |---|---:|---|---|---| | Bronze statuette of a worshipper | 12th century BCE | Bronze | Tell el-Amarna, Layer II | Accession No. 1997.45 | | Faience amulet (ankh) | 11th century BCE | Faience | Port deposit, Site K | Accession No. 2001.12 | | Painted pottery bowl | 10th century BCE | Ceramic | Canal-side workshop, Unit 7 | Accession No. 1999.03 | Table 1: Selected highlights of the current rotation. Table headers identify crucial catalog information and should be translated precisely. Detailed Descriptions Bronze statuette of a worshipper. This figurine, shown in Figure 1, depicts a standing individual with raised hands in the traditional gesture of adoration. Incised details on the wig and kilt suggest a synthesis of local and coastal metalworking styles. Note the inscription on the base, which remains partially legible and is under study by epigraphers. Figure 1: Bronze statuette of a worshipper, Accession No. 1997.45, H. 42 cm. Photographed under raking light to emphasize surface tooling. Faience amulet (ankh). The small ankh amulet was recovered in a burial deposit adjacent to a domestic shrine. Its bright blue glaze is characteristic of the region's faience workshops during this period. The museum display emphasizes both the original symbolic function and the technical processes used in faience production. ![Faience amulet](images/faience_ankh.png) Figure 2: Faience ankh, Accession No. 2001.12. Scale bar = 2 cm. Painted pottery bowl. This bowl bears a repeating chevron motif rendered in a resilient iron-rich slip. Microscopic sampling indicates multiple episodes of repair in antiquity, suggesting the object's continued value within a household setting. Provenance records list the findspot as Canal-side workshop, Unit 7; translators should retain this locality phrase when possible. Historical Context These objects were curated together to illustrate changing ritual practices in delta communities as trade networks shifted. Where inscriptions or catalog cards contain untranslated fragments in the original language, translators must indicate the presence of untranslated text and provide a gloss rather than attempting conjectural restoration. Conservation and Display Notes All three items are displayed in low-UV vitrines with controlled humidity. Conservation labels include practical instructions for handling and cleaning; these procedural notes are not intended for the general public and should be flagged for specialized translation if included in a public-language label. References and Credits Photographs: Museum Photographic Studio (file names above correspond to archival image records). Cataloging: Department of Near Eastern Antiquities. Funding acknowledgments and donor names appear on a separate credit panel and should be translated with attention to proper names and donor-provided wording. Visitor Information The exhibit rotation runs through the end of the calendar year. Guided tours focus on material culture and methods; please refer to the audio guide script for extended interpretive material. For research inquiries, contact the curatorial team with the accession number provided on each label.
translation_notes: Preserve accession numbers and measurements (e.g., 'Accession No. 1997.45', 'H. 42 cm') verbatim; do not translate or reformat them.
Translate captions and table headers precisely; retain image file paths like 'images/bronze_statuette.jpg' unchanged.

Handling bilingual fragments, catalog codes, and measurement units that must remain unchanged or require specific formatting conventions.

title: Maplewood Neighborhood Association — May Newsletter
source_text:
From the Editor:

Welcome to the May edition of the Maplewood Neighborhood Association newsletter. This month we focus on engagement, safety improvements, and the small victories that keep our neighborhood vibrant. Many residents requested multilingual resources at our meetings; we include short Hindi examples below so translators and volunteers know what to preserve. Please translate all English text into the target language but leave the Hindi phrases exactly as they appear.

Upcoming Events:

- Community Town Hall: Saturday, May 14, 6:30–8:30 PM at the Community Center. We will host a session labeled "खुली बैठक" to encourage open floor discussion. Simultaneous childcare and light refreshments will be provided. The agenda includes: an update on the traffic-calming pilot, a brief report from the parks committee, and a Q&A with city council representative Maria Torres.

- Neighborhood Clean-Up: Sunday, May 22, 9:00 AM. Volunteers will meet at the north parking lot; gloves and tools supplied. Families and student volunteers earn community service credit. If you can lead a team, reply to volunteers@maplewood.org.

Volunteer Spotlight:

This month we highlight Saira Patel, who coordinated the winter food drive and helped launch our after-school tutoring program. Saira often greets new neighbors using our informal motto, "हमारा समुदाय", and she organizes language-access sessions for non-English speakers. Her leadership has increased attendance at meetings and broadened our volunteer base.

Budget and Facilities Update:

The board approved minor capital improvements to the playground and irrigation system. The finance committee reports a modest surplus driven by increased membership dues and an outdoor concert fundraiser. A detailed line-item budget will be published online; translators should note that numeric figures, dates, and technical terms must remain in their original format unless the target language convention differs for numerals.

Safety & Accessibility:

After reviewing resident feedback, the traffic task force recommends installing two additional crosswalks and repainting curb edges near Lincoln School. We remind cyclists and motorists to respect speed limits and to stop for pedestrians. Accessibility upgrades include a new ramp at the north entrance and tactile signage at the library doors. Please maintain the original order of instructions when translating.

How to Help:

We need volunteers for translation, outreach, and event logistics. If you speak a second language, consider joining our Language Access Team. Volunteers are asked to attend one training session and to use the templates provided by the communications coordinator. For translations specifically: do not translate embedded Hindi phrases such as community labels or quoted greetings; preserve their script exactly as shown.

Glossary (for volunteers):

- Our informal greeting/motto — "हमारा समुदाय"
- Town hall session name — "खुली बैठक"
- Closing thanks used by staff — "धन्यवाद"

Closing Remarks:

Thank you for reading and for your ongoing participation. Small acts—picking up a litter, knocking on a neighbor's door, offering a translation—make a measurable difference. We will circulate minutes and action items after the May town hall; please submit agenda items by May 10. Reach out to the editor at editor@maplewood.org for corrections or to offer a short column in the June issue.

Warm regards,
Maplewood Neighborhood Association
translation_notes: Translate only the English portions; leave the Hindi substrings in protected_spans unchanged and in Devanagari script.
Preserve dates, times, numeric figures, and punctuation; retain headings and list structure in the translated output.
expected_challenges: Handling code-switching where Hindi spans are embedded in English sentences without altering sentence flow.
Preserving proper nouns, community-specific terminology, and the exact Devanagari script of protected spans.
Maintaining the tone and imperative structure of safety instructions while following target-language grammar.
